AGENDA

SEE END OF AGENDA FOR OPTIONS AND INSTRUCTIONS ON HOW TO PARTICIPATE IN THE MEETING.

To join via telephone:  (408) 638-0968

Any unheard items will automatically move forward to the next regular meeting.

Rethink Waste 2025 Trash to Art Contest Winners - Recognition
CONSENT CALENDAR
All matters listed under the Consent Calendar are considered by the Council to be routine and will be enacted by one motion without
discussion. If discussion is desired, that item may be removed and considered separately.
 
4.
City Council Meeting Minutes – Approval
Approve the minutes of the City Council meetings as follows: the special and regular meetings held on May 5,
2025, May 19, 2025, June 2, 2025, and June 16, 2025; and the special meetings held on May 6, 2025, May 7,
2025, May 10, 2025, May 15, 2025, May 27, 2025.
CEQA: This minutes approval is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative
activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section
15378(b)(5).)
1 of 983

--- page 2 ---
5.
Zoning Code Amendments and Below Market Rate Program Update — Ordinance Adoption
Adopt an Ordinance amending the Zoning Code (Title 27) to address affordable housing compliance options,
planning application procedures, and set minimum densities for residential mixed-use development projects; and
find that the Ordinance is exempt from further environmental review pursuant to California Environmental
Quality Act (CEQA) Section 15183.
CEQA: The Environmental Impact Report (EIR) for General Plan 2040 analyzed new residential development of up
to 19,764 new housing units and growth around the City through 2040. On March 18, 2024, the City Council
certified the EIR.
6.
Small Wireless Facility Permit Support Services — Agreements
Approve professional consultant services agreements for small wireless facilities permit review support services
through May 31, 2027, with up to two optional one-year extensions, with: Anchor Engineering, Inc. d/b/a
ANCHOR, in an amount not to exceed $250,000; and Gunnerson Consulting & Communication Site Services, LLC
in an amount not to exceed $250,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreements in
substantially the form presented and issue amendments within the budget amount including no-cost term
extensions.
CEQA: These agreements are not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that they will
not cause a physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)(CEQA Guidelines Section
15378(b)(4).)
7.
Citywide Custodial Services – Agreement
Adopt a Resolution approving an alternative purchasing procedure to award a three-year agreement to Impec
Group, LLC for Citywide Custodial Services in the amount of $3,581,907.00, with two optional one-year
extensions subject to future Council approval; establish an annual contingency in the amount of $110,000 per
agreement year; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form
presented and issue amendments within the contingency amount.
CEQA: This project is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a
physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
8.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Upgrade Construction Services – Contingency Increase
Approve an increase in the amount of $19,500,000 to the City’s contingency budget for its contract with Sundt
Construction, Inc. for construction of the Wastewater Treatment Plant Upgrade and Expansion Project; and
authorize the Public Works Director to issue change orders within the contingency amount.
CEQA: The City prepared a Final Programmatic Environmental Impact Report (Final PEIR) in accordance with the
California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) (SCH No. 2015032006). Council on June 6, 2016, certified the PEIR. In
accordance with CEQA, the Final PEIR serves as the primary environmental compliance document for the
implementation of the Clean Water Program
9.
Hillsdale High School/31st Avenue Pedestrian Safety Improvement Project – Grant Acceptance, Appropriation,
and Supplemental Agreement
Adopt a Resolution to accept a San Mateo County Transportation Authority Cycle 7 Measure A and Measure W
Pedestrian and Bicycle Program grant in the amount of $351,648 and appropriate the grant funding and $39,072
of the City’s Measure A funds as a local match to the Hillsdale High School/31st Avenue Pedestrian Safety
Improvement Project; approve a supplemental agreement with San Mateo County Transportation Authority to
receive the grant funding; and authorize the City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form
2 of 983

--- page 3 ---
presented and any necessary future no-cost term extensions.
CEQA: This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because the adoption of a resolution approving a Supplemental
Agreement with the TA and approving local matching funds is a government fiscal activity which does not involve
any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the
environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
10.
Wastewater Treatment Plant Upgrade Engineering Services During Construction – Amendment and Contingency
Increase
Approve Amendment No. 13 to the agreement with HDR Engineering, Inc. for engineering services during
construction for the Wastewater Treatment Plant Upgrade and Expansion Project extending the term of the
Agreement from August 31, 2025 through May 31, 2026; increase the contingency reserve by $1,500,000; and
authorize the Public Works Director to execute the amendment and issue change orders within the contingency
amount.
CEQA: The City prepared a Final Programmatic Environmental Impact Report (Final PEIR) in accordance with the
California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) (SCH No. 2015032006).
11.
Humboldt Street Paving with the City of Burlingame — Revised Cost Sharing Agreement
Approve a Cost Sharing Agreement with the City of Burlingame for the Paving of Humboldt Street in conjunction
with Burlingame’s 2025 Street Resurfacing Project with an estimated cost to the City in the amount of
$150,000.00; and authorize the City Manager to execute the Agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: Approval of this Cost Sharing Agreement is categorically exempt from CEQA, because the underlying
project within the City of San Mateo consists of the operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing,
or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical
features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
12.
Citywide Elevator Maintenance Services – Agreement
Adopt a Resolution approving an alternative purchasing procedure to award a three-year agreement to Advanced
Elevator Solutions, Inc. for Citywide Elevator Maintenance Services in the amount of $232,200, with two optional
one-year extensions subject to future Council approval; establish an annual contingency in the amount of $8,000
per year of the initial three-year agreement; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in
substantially the form presented and issue amendments within the contingency amount.
CEQA: This City Council action is exempt from CEQA review as an “existing facility,” because inspections, testing,
and maintenance of the City’s elevator and wheelchair lifts consists of the operation, repair, maintenance,
permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, mechanical
equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA
Guidelines Section 15301.)
13.
Poplar Creek Tenant Improvements – Agreement
Approve and authorize the City Manager to execute an agreement with Jon K Takata Corporation d/b/a
Restoration Management Company to provide contractual services in an amount not to exceed $1,265,263.23;
increase the construction contract contingency to $50,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to issue
change orders within the contingency amount.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures,
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of use. (CEQA
3 of 983

Guidelines Section 15301.)
14.
Citywide Street Rehabilitation Package 6B-1 — Agreement
Approve an agreement with G. Bortolotto & Company, Inc. in the amount of $2,588,392.25; establish a
contingency reserve in the amount of $389,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the
agreement in substantially the form presented and issue change orders as allowed by the contract documents
and within the contingency amount.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures,
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or
former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
15.
Main Library Lighting Controls Equipment – Purchase Agreement
Adopt a Resolution approving a purchase agreement with Graybar Electric Company, Inc. to provide lighting
controls equipment to the Main Library in an amount not to exceed $183,911.28; establish a contingency of
$18,000.00; and authorize the Public Works Director to issue change orders within the contingency and execute
the agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures,
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or
former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
16.
Citywide Surface Treatment Package 4A-1 Project — Agreement
Approve an agreement with American Asphalt Repair & Resurfacing Co., Inc. for Citywide Surface Treatment
Package 4A-1 Project in the amount of $3,110,671; establish a contingency reserve in the amount of $311,000;
and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and issue
change orders as allowed by the contract documents and within the amount of the contingency reserve.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,
repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures,
facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or
former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
17.
North Shoreview Flood Assessment District - Compensation Agreement
Approve a compensation agreement with County of San Mateo for levying of the North Shoreview Flood
Assessment District fee and authorize the City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form
presented.
CEQA: This approval of the compensation agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government
fiscal activity which does not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially
significant physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
18.

term through June 30, 2026; establish a contingency reserve in the amount of $15,000; and authorize the Public
Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and issue change orders within the

contingency amount.
CEQA: This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation,

19.
City Hall Council Chambers Audio-Visual Upgrades – Construction Contract
Award a contract to Mark Latimer & Associates, Inc. dba Coda Technology Group for the City Hall Council
Chambers Audio-Visual Upgrades project in the amount of $562,389.08; establish a contingency of $56,000;
authorize the City Manager to execute the contract in substantially the form presented; and authorize the Public
Works Director to issue change orders within the contingency amount.
CEQA: This City Council action is exempt from CEQA review under the “common sense” exemption, because it can
be seen with certainty that there is no possibility that upgrades to the audio-visual system located in City Council
chambers may have a significant effect on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3).)
20.
Juvenile Diversion Program Case Management Services – Contract and Amendment
Approve a three year contract with the Youth Service Bureau of the YMCA of San Francisco for Juvenile Diversion
Program case management services in the total amount of $623,308; approve Amendment No. 6 to the
agreement with the County of San Mateo to increase the reimbursement amount by $270,892 for case
management services for the San Mateo Police Department Juvenile Diversion Program; approve a change order
in the amount of $38,576 to cover the final costs for juvenile diversion case management services rendered in
FY24/25; approve a contingency reserve of $50,000 and authorize the Chief of Police to issue change orders
within that reserve; and authorize the Chief of Police to execute the documents in substantially the form
presented.
CEQA: This action is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a
physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
21.
Police Recruit Training Officer Assignment - Agreement
Approve an agreement with Alameda County Sheriff's Office for the assignment of one police officer to the
Alameda County Sheriff's Office Regional Training Center, and authorize the Chief of Police to execute the
agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause
a physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
22.
Building Inspector, Development Review Technician, and Housing Specialist Series; and Salary Schedule: Senior
Housing Specialist – Job Specification Updates
Adopt a Resolution to approve updates to the job specifications for three series: 1) Building Inspector I-II-Senior,
2) Development Review Technician I-II-Senior, and 3) Housing Specialist I-II-Senior; and to approve the salary
schedule for the classification of Senior Housing Specialist.
CEQA: The job specification updates are not subject to CEQA because it is a government fiscal activity which does
not involve any commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on
the environment.(CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
23.

the Waste Management division of the Public Works Department to Solid Waste Technician.

24.
Finance and Human Resources Services for San Mateo Consolidated Fire Department – Agreement
Approve an agreement between the City of San Mateo and the San Mateo Consolidated Fire Department for
Finance and Human Resources services for a five-year term beginning August 1, 2025, and ending June 30, 2030,
with estimated compensation to the City in the amount of $6,147,039.18; and authorize the City Manager to
execute the Agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: Approval of this agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because the provision of finance and human
resources services to SMC Fire is an administrative activity which will not result in direct or indirect physical
changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
25.
Congregate Nutrition Program — Interagency Agreement
Approve an interagency agreement with the County of San Mateo for Fiscal Year 2025-2026 to partially fund a
congregate nutrition program through the Older Americans Act for up to $48,096; and authorize the Director of
Parks and Recreation to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity
that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)
(5).)
26.
Central Park Phase 1 Playground Improvements Project – Agreement Amendment No. 3

agreement from December 31, 2025 to December 31, 2026.
CEQA: This agreement amendment is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or
administrative activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA
Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).) I
27.
Climate Adaptation Plan for Sea Level Rise – Agreement
Approve an agreement with Environmental Science Associates (ESA) for the development of a Climate Adaptation
Plan for Sea Level Rise in the amount of $420,000 for a term from August 1, 2025, through August 1, 2028; and
authorize the City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause
a physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.) CEQA review will be included as
part of the Climate Adaptation Plan.

28.
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy – Adoption
Adopt a Resolution to adopt the Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy establishing policy direction for

CEQA: This plan adoption is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative

15378(b)(5).)
PUBLIC COMMENT
Members of the public wishing to comment on any item not appearing on the agenda may address the City Council at this time. State
law prevents Council from taking action on any matter not on the agenda; your comments may be referred to staff for follow up.
Public comment is limited to a total of 15 minutes; however, an opportunity for additional public comment may be provided later in
the agenda.
 
OLD BUSINESS
29.
Amendment to Contribution Limits for City Candidate Campaigns – Ordinance Introduction and Public Financing
Briefing
Introduce an Ordinance to amend San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 2.80: "Contributions and Loans to City
Candidate Campaigns” to adjust contribution limits for individuals to $1,000, thereby aligning with organizational
contribution limits, link future contribution limits adjustments to the CPI, and enhance incentives for
participation in the voluntary expenditure limits program, all in accordance with Council direction. Receive an
informational briefing on Public Financing of City Candidate Campaigns and provide direction on future action.
CEQA: This ordinance introduction is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or
administrative activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA
Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NEW BUSINESS
30.
Emergency Rental Assistance Program - Adoption and Agreement
Adopt a Resolution to establish an Emergency Rental Assistance Program, appropriate $168,000 from the Low
and Moderate Income Asset Fund for FY 2025-26, approve an agreement with Samaritan House to administer the
program, and authorize the City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
CEQA: This resolution is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity
that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)
(5).)
REPORTS AND ANNOUNCEMENTS
City Manager, City Attorney and Council Members report on their various assignments and liaison roles and Council requests for
scheduling future items.
 
ADJOURNMENT
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
AGENDAS: Agendas and material are posted on the City’s website on the Friday preceding each Council Meeting and can be viewed on the City's
website at www.cityofsanmateo.org . Any supplemental material distributed to the Council after the posting of the agenda will be made part of the
official record.

--- page 8 ---
WATCHING A MEETING ON TV: City Council meetings are broadcast live on Comcast/channel 27, Wave/channel 26, or AT&T/channel 99.
For transmission problems during the broadcast, please call (650) 522-7099. For all other broadcast comments, call (650) 522-7040, Monday-Friday,
8 a.m. - 5 p.m.
WATCHING A MEETING ON A COMPUTER: There are three ways to stream.
1) Public Meeting Portal www.cityofsanmateo.org/publicmeetings
2) City YouTube channel and stream it on YouTube: http://youtube.com/CityofSanMateo
3) Watch TV live stream: https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/193/Channel-San-Mateo-Live-Stream 
PUBLIC COMMENTS/REQUEST TO SPEAK
Prior to the Meeting 
Send comments to: clerk@cityofsanmateo.org until 4 p.m. the day of the meeting.
During the meeting
By Zoom: Click the link at the top of the agenda and you’ll be added to the meeting. All attendees are muted by default. When the item of interest
is open for consideration, select the “Raise Your Hand” icon and you will be called on at the appropriate time.
By telephone: Call (408) 638-0968 and enter the conference ID found at the top of the meeting agenda. When the item of interest is open for
consideration, select *9 to raise your hand. When called upon, press *6 to unmute, state your name and provide your comments.
In Person: At the meeting complete a “Request to Speak” form.
ACCESSIBILITY: In compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act and to ensure meaningful access for all participants, including those with
limited English proficiency, individuals requiring special accommodations or language assistance to participate in this meeting may contact the City
Clerk’s Office at (650) 522-7040 or clerk@cityofsanmateo.org . Notification 48 hours prior to the meeting will enable the City to make reasonable
arrangements to ensure accessibility to this meeting.

Regular Meeting
 
CALL TO ORDER at 7:00 p.m.
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll Call
Present:
Mayor Newsom, Deputy Mayor Loraine, Council Members: Fernandez, Cwirko-Godycki, and Diaz Nash
AB 2449 Requests and Considerations for Meeting Participation by Teleconference due to Just Cause or Emergency 
Circumstances – there were no requests. 
CEREMONIAL
1.
Aragon High School – 4.0 Grade Recognition - Students from Aragon High School were recognized for their 
scholastic achievements.
2.
Asian American Pacific – Islander Heritage Month – Proclamation - Kenneth Szeto (ZEE toe), Self-Help for the 
Elderly, San Mateo Program Manager; Wesley Taoka, San Mateo Japanese-American Community Center; and Tolifili 
Fa – Mosquito & Vector Control District Trustee, received the proclamation.
3.
Jewish American Heritage Month – Proclamation - Scott E. Hartley, President at Peninsula Temple Beth El, received 
the proclamation.
4.
National Police Week – Proclamation - Ed Barberini, Chief of Police, received the proclamation.
CONSENT CALENDAR
The following items, 5 through 12, were considered to be routine by the City Council. After the titles of the items were read 
by the City Clerk, the public was invited to comment and there were two speakers: Mike Swire commented on his support 
of the Council’s letter in opposition to the 101/92 direct connector project but wants the Council to consider removing a 
particular statement from the letter. Giuliano Carlini is also concerned with the language in the letter regarding highway 
widening. Council discussed the language of the letter and decided to keep it in. Motion passed 5-0.
Moved:
Loraine, Seconded: Fernandez
Ayes: 
Newsom, Loraine, Fernandez, Cwirko-Godycki, and Diaz Nash
Noes: 
None
5.

Adopt a Resolution to approve an alternative purchasing procedure and approve a Master Services Agreement 
(MSA) with OpenGov for a three-year renewal of software subscription, cloud hosting, and technical support in the 
amount of $403,057.37; and authorize the City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form 
presented.

Enactment:  Resolution No. 39 (2025)

Downtown Business Improvement Area – Approval
Adopt a Resolution of Intent to Levy the Annual Assessments for the Downtown Business Improvement Area for 
fiscal year 2025-26; accept the fiscal year 2024-25 Downtown San Mateo Association Annual Report; and set a 
public hearing for the May 19, 2025, City Council meeting for the annual levy.

Side Letter of Agreement - Police Technical Services Administrator Classification Representation – Adoption
Adopt a Resolution to reflect the change in representation of the Police Technical Services Administrator (PTSA) 
from the San Mateo Management Employees’ Association (SMMEA) to the San Mateo Safety Management 
Association (SMSMA).
Enactment:  Resolution No. 42 (2025)
9.
Minimum Wage Enforcement – Agreement
Approve an agreement with the County of San Mateo’s Office of Labor Standards Enforcement (OLSE) to provide 
minimum wage enforcement services for the City’s minimum wage ordinance, in an amount not to exceed $60,000, 
and authorize the City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
10.
Electric Vehicle Fleet Program Terms and Conditions – Approval
Approve the PG&E Program Electric Vehicle Fleet Program Terms and Conditions and authorize the City Manager to 
execute the documents in substantially the form presented and to negotiate an easement agreement for the 
deployment of PG&E facilities to serve electric vehicle infrastructure, including relocation as needed, for future 
Council approval.
11.
385 2nd Avenue Lease Agreement Renewal – Reauthorization
Re-approve a lease renewal extending the term to March 31, 2026 with a one-year option for the property located 
at 385 2nd Avenue in the Main Street Garage with Elmer Vasquez and Anavela Vasquez with an initial base rent of 
$1,374.00 per month; and authorize the City Manager to execute the lease renewal in substantially the form 
presented.
12.

the City of San Mateo’s opposition to the current U.S. 101/SR 92 Interchange Direct Connector Project proposal.

signing the opposition letter.

City Council Regular Meeting Minutes – 5/5/2025

PUBLIC COMMENT
An organized presentation by Farzaneh Yountehi and Melissa Toboni on behalf of the Parkside neighborhood regarding 
the disruption to their lives caused by the noise and damage from the Parkside Montessori Construction. Kevin Simpson

with over 113 signatures to end the 14-day reservation window for VIP members at Poplar Creek Golf Course. Allison

to protect houses from fire and wants it to be an agenda item. Mike Swire concerned about bike lanes. Andrew Ryan 
expressed concerns regarding the historic district in Glazenwood’s lack of formal adoption as a historic district, costing 
residents thousands of dollars to remodel their homes. Trina Pierce wants the parking restored on Humboldt. Anna Lieb 
encouraged keeping the Humboldt bike lanes and increase safety. Robert Whitehair and John McKenna asked the Council

Mateo/Foster City School District. He also noted that staff would look into the noise issue raised by the public.

NEW BUSINESS
13.
Delaware Street Safe Routes to School Corridor Bike Lane Improvements – Update
Sang Hee Cho, Associate Engineer, gave an update on the Delaware Street Safe Routes to School Corridor Bike 
Lane Improvements project.
Public Comment:
Kevin Simpson urged support for the bike lane project, highlighting its alignment with 2040 goals, youth 
benefits, and strong usage even without perfect outreach. Dana Stoer expressed concern that narrowing 
Delaware could hinder emergency and event access, suggesting Pacific Boulevard as an alternate route. Allison 
McQueen supported the protected bike lane for its safety, climate, and connectivity benefits, encouraging San 
Mateo to keep pace with peer cities. Mike Swire praised project outreach, emphasized student safety, and 
opposed rerouting via Pacific due to access challenges. Kai shared that his family bikes Delaware but avoids 
parts due to traffic, and voiced strong support for protected lanes. Karvin Dassanayake described Delaware as 
unsafe for cyclists and said protected lanes would significantly improve safety. Phil King, with his daughter, 
urged the City to keep the Delaware route to ensure safe school commutes for students. Anna Lieb supported 
the project, citing safety concerns during pregnancy and the need for more accessible infrastructure. Leo Stoll 
endorsed the project as a complement to parking restrictions and a means to reduce speeding and car 
dependency. Debra Rosenfeld opposed the lane reduction, citing inadequate outreach, safety concerns, and 
poor road conditions near the Arco station. Giuliano Carlini strongly supported the project, urged prioritizing 
cyclist safety, and requested flex posts near 28th Street. Sschochet supported the project for safety and 
environmental reasons, advocating for buffered bike lanes and improved connections. Seema Patel, speaking 
personally, supported the bike lanes and suggested enhancements near Bermuda and Sunnybrae Elementary 
to close safety gaps. Max Mautner praised the plan’s cost-effectiveness and appeal to cautious riders, while 
raising concerns about emergency access. Maria opposed the project, arguing it would worsen congestion, 
limit mobility for working-class residents, and pose safety risks. David Hunt supported the project for 
improving school bike routes and urged extending protected lanes to 19th Avenue.
Council asked questions and made comments.
13 of 983

--- page 14 ---
City Council Regular Meeting Minutes – 5/5/2025
Page 4
City Council expressed general support for the proposed improvements, acknowledging the need to enhance 
safety for bicyclists along the corridor. Councilmembers emphasized the importance of hearing and 
incorporating concerns from residents on the west side of Delaware, where appropriate. As part of the next 
steps, staff will coordinate with the event center and the Fire Department to identify and address any potential 
issues related to the proposed southbound lane reduction before finalizing the project.
City Manager, Khojikian, received direction to return with a comprehensive overview of upcoming projects, 
categorized by priority levels, low, medium, and high. This overview should include details on the source of 
funding for each project, highlighting which are locally funded and which rely on grants or other external 
funding sources. No changes to existing policy were made at this time. 
Council recessed from 10:20 p.m. to 10:25 p.m.
14.
City Council Priority List – Adoption
Christina Horrisberger, Assistant City Manager, gave the presentation.
Public Comment
Kevin Simpson strongly supported prioritizing item #6, a traffic survey necessary for radar-based speed 
enforcement, emphasizing its urgency due to increasing traffic-related deaths. He also urged attention to item 
#12, citing safety and quality-of-life concerns caused by mobile food trucks operating late and creating hazards 
in residential neighborhoods. Mark voiced strong support for item #9, the regulation of gas-powered leaf 
blowers, citing their negative impact on neighborhood quality of life and the environment. He urged the city to 
keep the item at least a medium priority, ideally moving it to high.
Council asked questions and made comments.
Council gave direction at 11:00 p.m. to continue the meeting through 11:40 p.m.
City Council reviewed the Priority List and provided additional direction to staff. The next step in the adoption 
of the Fiscal Year 2025-26 Priority List will be through the Budget in June.
REPORTS AND ANNOUNCEMENTS
City Manager, City Attorney and Council Members reported on their various assignments and liaison roles and noted

investigate implementing speed limits for electronic bikes. City Manager Khojikian offered that this item could be 
brought forward at a future Council goal setting session. Chief of Police Ed Barberini provided some clarification on 
current regulations. Deputy Mayor Loraine requested Council action on Assembly Bill 306, City Manager Alex Khojikian 
shared that this bill will be included within the Council’s upcoming legislative briefing. Assistant City Manager 
Horrisberger reported on a staff coordinated community meeting on April 22, 2025 on the resumption of parking 
enforcement in Bay Meadows. Following Assistant City Manager Horrisberger’s report, the Council acknowledged 
community input.

STUDY SESSION 
1. 
Code of Conduct - Workshop 
Tim Dunkin facilitated a workshop on the City Council and Boards and Commissions Code of Conduct. The City 
Council received a presentation from City Attorney Prasanna Rasiah on a draft enforcement mechanism and 
provided clarifying questions and feedback. The Council directed staff to develop a formal enforcement framework 
to be presented for adoption at a future regular meeting. Council also directed staff to prepare related 
amendments to the Council Rules and Procedures addressing the use of electronic devices while on the dais, 
adherence to federal, state, and local laws, and the importance of avoiding the appearance of impropriety. The 
amendments are expected to return to the Council for consideration in June 2025.

1.
Legislative and Grant Opportunities Briefing – Study Session
California State Senator Josh Becker, California State Assemblymember Diane Papan, and Jacki Rigoni, Chief of 
staff, presented legislative updates on behalf of San Mateo County Supervisor Noelia Corzo (District 5). The City’s 
state legislative consultant, California Public Policy Group, and federal legislative consultant, Smith Garson gave 
briefings as well.
Public Comment:
Robert Whitehair urged the Council to oppose Assembly Bill 306, cautioning that it would undermine local 
electrification efforts and building reach codes under the pretext of expediting construction in certain areas of 
Southern California. Max Mautner expressed support for Senate Bill 63, citing its significance to Bay Area transit 
agencies such as Caltrain and BART, and encouraged the development of more housing near transit. Rick Bonilla 
supported SB 63, advocating for improved, equitable, and integrated regional transit. Jordan Grimes voiced 
support for both SB 63 and SB 79, emphasizing the importance of regional collaboration on transit and housing, 
and commended SB 79’s flexible, tiered approach to increasing affordable housing without placing undue burden 
on cities. Adina Levin spoke in favor of AB 1250 to improve paratransit access and supported SB 63, noting its 
consistency with San Mateo’s goals related to housing, equity, and climate. 
Council members called to separate votes for each bill.
Motion to support Senate Bill 63 (SB63):
Moved:      Loraine, Seconded: Newsom
Ayes:       Loraine, Cwirko-Godycki
Noes:       Newsom, Fernandez, Diaz Nash
 
Motion failed 3–2.

PUBLIC COMMENT
Michael Sevillia reiterated his request to eliminate the 14-day reservation window for VIP golf memberships at Poplar 
Creek, citing ongoing accessibility challenges for residents and concerns about revenue loss associated with the restaurant 
and event space closure. Andrew Reback expressed disappointment over the planned 18-month closure of the Marina 
Library without a temporary replacement, urging the City to consider establishing a modest interim facility to serve 
Parkside residents, particularly students and seniors. Andrew Ryan commented on the City’s administration of Mills Act 
contracts, citing a lack of inspections and significant tax breaks to affluent property owners, and called for contract 
cancellations and stronger enforcement measures before any expansion of preservation programs. Frank Elliott raised 
concerns about the financial and operational sustainability of the City’s historic preservation initiative and the Rincon 
contract, recommending that the Council reconsider or reduce the scope of the project due to anticipated costs and 
limited benefits. Rick Bonilla echoed concerns regarding Mills Act administration and the Rincon contract, calling for an 
immediate investigation, public disclosure of findings, and cancellation of the contract. Laurie Hietter expressed support 
for the City’s commitment to historic preservation, stating that the Rincon project is consistent with the General Plan and 
long-established goals, and clarified that the contract amount is $330,000, not $500,000 as previously stated.

Public Comment
Cathy Levitt praised the Downtown San Mateo Association (DSMA) for helping her bank engage with weekend

citing Boba Day and the Head West Market as examples that brought new vibrancy and business discovery. Robert

business celebrations. Lorry Morales expressed appreciation for the increased visibility and support DSMA has 
brought to her area through videos, new banners, and consistent advocacy. Rick applauded the transformation of 
downtown since the early 1990s, crediting the DSMA’s leadership and local merchants for creating a more vibrant, 
engaging environment and voiced his support for the resolution to renew DSMA’s funding.

volunteer outreach, strategic use of the $240,000 DSMA reserve, and consideration of staff salary increases. They 
also encouraged more diverse downtown events, targeted programming for teens and seniors, and praised new 
initiatives like the pedestrian scramble.

20.
Climate Action Plan – Annual Progress Report
Andrea Chow, Sustainability Analyst, gave a progress report on the implementation of the City's Climate Action 
Plan. The City Council gave feedback and encouraged staff to continue promoting rebates and resources to help 
residents save money while reducing their carbon footprint. Councilmembers also emphasized the importance of 
the City’s electric vehicle fleet goals and commended staff for being responsive to community concerns by 
implementing pedestrian safety improvements in Downtown. 
Public Comment – there were no speakers.
REPORTS AND ANNOUNCEMENTS

and future events.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Special Meeting Minutes
City Council
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
 
June 2, 2025
City Hall Council Chambers 5:30 PM
Special Meeting
 
CALL TO ORDER at 5:32 p.m.
Roll Call
Present:
Mayor Newsom, Deputy Mayor Loraine, Council Members: Fernandez, Cwirko-Godycki, and Diaz Nash
AB 2449 Requests and Considerations for Meeting Participation by Teleconference due to Just Cause or Emergency 
Circumstances – there were no requests. 
STUDY SESSION
1.
Historic Policy Update Effort and Preservation Ordinance — Update
Zachary Dahl, Director Community Development, gave a presentation on how to proceed with updating the City’s 
Historic Preservation Ordinance.
Public Comment: 
Mike Ethridge expressed opposition to the historic district designation and urged the Council to prioritize broad 
community input rather than the interests of a small group. Shawn Fahrenbruch raised concerns that historic 
designation could affect homeowners’ insurance and noted that staff had not yet addressed this issue. Kolja 
Schluetter stated that the historic preservation proposal does not align with community priorities, referencing a 
recent survey showing only 2.8% of residents identified it as a concern. Rich Hedges voiced concern about 
community division and supported including an opt-out provision to protect working-class neighborhoods, while 
also supporting collaboration with historic homeowners. Connie Weiss urged the Council to focus on adopting a 
strong ordinance that aligns with the General Plan, cautioned against misinformation from opponents, and called 
for a respectful, well-run process. Dave Cohen commended the Council’s commitment to fair governance and 
opposed assigning preservation oversight to the Planning Commission due to past bias. Anna Kuhre supported a 
consent threshold—up to 90%—to avoid spreading conflict into other neighborhoods and criticized city spending 
on preservation efforts without demonstrated community demand. Jay Kuhre clarified that he does not oppose 
preservation but criticized the state’s default-consent process as undemocratic. Frank Elliott supported recognizing 
San Mateo’s history but called for more clarity around the legal implications of state-mandated preservation 
processes and urged a deeper understanding before proceeding. Rick Bonilla cautioned against repeating 
exclusionary historical practices and emphasized the need for high standards of homeowner consent and inclusive 
policymaking. Zev Simon shared his experience with delays due to historic preservation policies, warning that such 
approaches could harm property owners and deter development, and urged strong consent requirements to 
prevent ongoing conflict. Eleanor Dogan stated that Baywood residents largely oppose the historic district and 
suggested using the city’s existing parking permit model to guide owner consent, also citing financial and 
insurance-related concerns. Andrew Ryan opposed residential historic districts, highlighted vague state criteria and 
irreversible impacts, and supported requiring at least 80% affirmative owner consent. Laurie Watanuki encouraged 
proceeding with the historic preservation ordinance update as envisioned in the General Plan, recommended 
deferring consent discussions until a draft is available, and supported oversight by a dedicated historic commission 
or qualified staff. Tom Lease opposed requiring owner consent, arguing that doing so would undermine 
preservation goals by introducing loopholes. Lisa Vander Voorde objected to giving property owners the power to 
31 of 983

--- page 32 ---
City Council Special Meeting Minutes – 6/2/2025
Page 2
determine historic designations, stating that preservation serves the public interest. Michael Weinhauer opposed 
assigning oversight to the Planning Commission. 
Council asked questions and made comments.
The City Council directed staff to proceed with drafting updates to the Historic Preservation Ordinance that 
establish enabling authority, designate the Community Relations Commission as the reviewing body, and outline 
procedures for resource designation, appeals, enforcement, and definitions. The Council supported aligning local 
designation criteria with the California Register of Historical Resources while exploring additional criteria to reflect 
San Mateo’s unique history. The updated ordinance should include a process for designating local historic districts, 
requiring majority support from affected property owners, and incorporate a requirement for affirmative, 
expressed owner consent for all local historic designations.
ADJOURNMENT –The meeting adjourned at 7:11 p.m.
APPROVED BY:                                                        
SUBMITTED BY:
Rob Newsom, Jr., Mayor                   
Martin McTaggart, City Clerk

City Council Regular Meeting Minutes – 6/2/2025
Page 5
NEW BUSINESS
City Manager Khojikian made a request to the Council that agenda item 23 be considered as the first discussion item due 
to public interest and attendance. City Council provided a consensus to approve this request.
23.     2025 Transportation Program Annual Review — Progress Update
Jay Yu, Engineering Manager, gave the presentation summarizing progress on ongoing transportation plans and 
initiatives, key project updates, and performance metrics including citywide mode share and safety data.
PUBLIC COMMENT: 
Kevin Simpson expressed frustration that the traffic safety presentation was not shared earlier, commenting on the city’s 
lack of urgency in addressing fatal traffic incidents, and questioned decisions like potentially removing key bike 
infrastructure while safety issues persist. Sean Schochet urged the city to address dangerous conditions on Alameda de 
las Pulgas, especially between 24th and 26th Avenues, and advocated for an elevated crosswalk and better bike 
infrastructure to protect students and families. Mike Swire called for prioritizing impactful safety projects over politically 
easier ones, criticized the removal of popular bike lanes, and emphasized that underutilized parking and reduced traffic 
show a disconnect between perception and data-driven needs. Michael Reagan emphasized the long-standing need for a 
traffic light at Hillsdale and 31st Avenue, citing safety and traffic concerns, especially with increasing development. Max 
Mautner recommended the city focus on lower-cost, resilient traffic calming measures like speed bumps instead of 
expensive signal systems. Elaine Salinger emphasized that many cyclists avoid riding due to fear and lack of safety, calling 
near misses underreported and emotionally traumatic. She strongly advocated for protected bike lanes and urged the 
city not to remove existing ones in North Central. Anna Lieb supported prioritizing safety and accessibility, urging the city 
not to remove bike lanes despite some parking complaints. Andrea Stewart shared personal fears about safety and 
underscored the importance of protected bike lanes. She stated Bay Meadows feels isolated due to unsafe crossings and 
stressed that real change requires deprioritizing car dominance. Allison McQueen urged the council not to expand 
Highway 92 in response to school traffic congestion, arguing instead that investment in safe bike infrastructure near 
schools—like that in Palo Alto—can significantly reduce car trips. She called for full implementation of the 2020 Bike 
Master Plan.
Council recessed from 9:05p.m. to 9:17p.m.
City Council emphasized the need to fully implement the 2020 Bike Master Plan and Safe Routes to School, improve 
project outreach, increase protected bike lanes, enhance downtown parking and signage and accelerate projects while 
federal funding remains available. Council also called for better data tracking, filling key staff vacancies, and integrating 
delayed planning efforts. Council discussed the idea of creating a dedicated bike lane budget within the CIP and City 
Manager will explore the option.
PUBLIC HEARING
22.
Fiscal Year 2025-26 Proposed Budget and 2025-30 Capital Improvement Program – Review
Karen Huang, Finance Director, gave the presentation.
Public Comment – there were no speakers.
Councilmembers Cwirko-Godycki and Fernandez volunteered to join a revenue measure ad hoc committee to 
explore funding options for both capital projects and ongoing general services, with findings to be brought back to 
the full council. City Manager, Khojikian, noted that staff will review parking fees and hours, especially free 
evening parking, to ensure competitiveness and reduce subsidies.

AB 2449 Requests and Considerations for Meeting Participation by Teleconference due to Just Cause or Emergency 
Circumstances – there were no requests. 
CLOSED SESSION
Following the opportunity for public comment, there were no speakers, the City Council convened into Closed Session at 
5:32 p.m. to consider:
1.
Conference with Legal Counsel – Existing Litigation
(Paragraph (1) of subdivision (d) of Section 54956.9)
Name of case: Housing Action Coalition v. City of San Mateo (San Mateo Superior Court Case No.: 24-CIV-03126)
Council reconvened at 5:42 p.m.
STUDY SESSION
2.
Prohibiting Lodging in Vehicles — Ordinance Enforcement
Zachary Reda, Senior Management Analyst, gave a presentation on enforcement of Section 10.04.140 of the 
Municipal Code, entitled “Prohibiting Vehicle Use for Habitation and Sleep”.
Public Comment: Laurie Watanuki urged the city to resume enforcement of the ordinance and emphasized the 
need to act promptly to prevent an influx of RVs from neighboring cities tightening their regulations.  Julia 
Bonfiglio and Sebastian Bonfiglio support adopting "option one" with outreach and deadlines, and emphasized the 
need for both enforcement and assistance for those living in the vehicles. David Light supports “option one” as the 
most compassionate and practical solution. He acknowledged alternatives like designated parking areas with 
facilities but believes they are less feasible for San Mateo. Jeremy Levine acknowledged the need for short-term 
solutions to RV encampments but urged the council to also consider longer-term strategies, such as collaborating 
with nearby cities and exploring a safe parking program. He emphasized that while RV living isn't a permanent 
housing solution, it could offer greater stability as the city processes new housing developments.
Council agreed to proceed with Option 1: enforce Municipal Code Section 10.04.140, but direct the Police 
Department to conduct outreach at least twice prior to issuing a 24-hour warning. After this warning, citations or 
other appropriate enforcement actions may be taken. A 45-day grace period will be observed before any citations 
are issued. The city will focus on educational outreach (in English and Spanish) and engagement by the HOT 
(Homeless Outreach Team). Council requested progress updates at six months and one year, and staff will explore 
options for a waste pump-out station and continued coordination with the county on long-term solutions.

PUBLIC COMMENT
Marjorie Ruiz, PG&E’s new Local Government Affairs Representative, offered ongoing support to the City of San 
Mateo, and proposed a wildfire safety presentation ahead of fire season. Michael Sevilla expressed concern over 
the legal complaint filed by the Par 3 restaurant owners, criticizing the city's handling of the San Mateo Municipal 
Golf Course and urging the city to end the 14-day VIP reservation window and restore the facility for public use. 
Jordan Grimes urged the City Council to publicly affirm that San Mateo and its police department will not 
cooperate with federal immigration enforcement, citing rising fear among immigrant communities and calling for 
leadership in upholding the California Values Act.
The City Manager reaffirmed that San Mateo and its police department do not engage in immigration enforcement 
or inquire about immigration status, in line with a 2017 City Council resolution. Council agreed to direct staff to 
draft a public statement in multiple languages to clearly communicate this policy to the community.
OLD BUSINESS
City Manager Khojikian made a request to the Council that agenda item 41 be considered as the first 
discussion item due to public interest and attendance. City Council provided a consensus to approve this 
request.
41.
Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable Building Strategy — Policy Feedback
Andrea Chow, Sustainability Analyst, gave an informational report on the "Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable 
Building Strategy" project. Staff is requesting direction on which policy pathways to include in the final strategy 
(Option 1: Business as Usual, Option 2: Reach Codes for Renovations, Option 3: Zero NOx equipment 
requirements, Option 4: Commercial Building Performance Standards, Option 5: Municipal Electric First Policy).
Public Comment:
San Mateo Climate Action Team: Terry Whitehair, Robert Whitehair, Michelle Hudson, and Vivian Scott gave a 
presentation and strongly supports all five decarbonization policy options under consideration. Harry Moody 
emphasized climate threats and praised the city for leadership. Advocated for options 2 and 3. Fernando Peña (San 
Mateo County Association of Realtors) opposed Option 3, which would accelerate the Bay Area’s proposed gas ban 
to 2026. Argued it’s unrealistic due to aging homes and infrastructure challenges. Expressed concerns about 
equity, rebates, and hidden costs (panel upgrades, rewiring). Rhovy Lyn Antonio (California Apartment Association) 
urged thorough discussion with property owners before enforcing electrification mandates and asked for clear 
distinction between residential and commercial regulations and warned against rushed implementation.
Dashiell Leeds (Sierra Club – Loma Prieta Chapter) supports all proposed options, with emphasis on Option 2 (AC-
to-heat pump replacement). Sam Fishman (SPUR Policy Think Tank) strongly supports the full decarbonization plan 
and encourages early implementation of regulations to stay ahead of 2027/2029 state mandates. Wendy Chow 
47 of 983

--- page 48 ---
City Council Regular Meeting Minutes – 6/16/2025
Page 8
(Acterra) supports all five strategies. Elaine Salinger (Citizens Climate Lobby – San Mateo County) supports all five 
options. Mike Swire supports all five electrification strategies.
The City Council provided direction to support Option 1, Business as Usual and Option 5 Municipal Electric First 
Policy. City Council also supported Option 2, Reach Codes for Renovations, and Option 4, Commercial Building 
Performance Standard, with the understanding that additional analysis will be reviewed by Council prior to 
Ordinance development. The Sustainable Building Strategy will be presented for Council review at a future City 
Council meeting.
Council recessed from 9:47 p.m. to 10:01 p.m.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  5
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 25-3112
File ID: 25-10146
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
City Clerk's Office
MEETING DATE:
July 28, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Zoning Code Amendments and Below Market Rate Program Update — Ordinance Adoption
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt an Ordinance amending the Zoning Code (Title 27) to address affordable housing compliance options, planning 
application procedures, and set minimum densities for residential mixed-use development projects; and find that the 
Ordinance is exempt from further environmental review pursuant to California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Section 
15183.
BACKGROUND:
On June 16, 2025, City Council introduced the attached proposed Ordinance (Attachment 1) to amend several chapters of 
Title 27 (Zoning) of the San Mateo Municipal Code (SMMC) to implement programs to support the goal of housing 
production within the Housing Element. These updates include the following: 
•
Allow for alternative compliance options for required onsite affordable dwelling units in housing development 
projects (Policy H1.3)
•
Add provisions for appeal procedures for processing planning applications (Policy H1.6)
•
Modify provisions for approved planning application expiration and extensions (Policy H1.6)
•
Modify language for approval authority of development projects that exceed height thresholds (Policy H1.6)
•
Set minimum densities for residential mixed-use development projects (Policy H1.9)
If adopted, this Ordinance will be effective 30 days after its adoption. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The Zoning Code Amendments to implement the Housing Element and General Plan are fully funded in the Community 
Development Department’s annual operating budget, utilizing the Advanced Planning Fund (Fund 225), and does not 
impact the City’s General Fund.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The Environmental Impact Report (EIR) for General Plan 2040 analyzed new residential development of up to 19,764 new 
housing units and growth around the City through 2040. On March 18, 2024, the City Council certified the EIR.  
In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Guidelines Section 15183, the zoning amendment to 
modify housing development provisions and development standards (“Project”) is exempt from further environmental 
review because the Project, which generally allows for more housing types to be permitted uses in specific zoning districts 
and remove regulatory barriers from the zoning code, is consistent with the anticipated land uses under the land use 
designations adopted as part of General Plan 2040 for which an EIR was certified. There are no specific proposed changes 
50 of 983

--- page 51 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
to residential development densities, and it is consistent with the development densities established by General Plan 2040.  
The Project is also consistent with the implementation policies of the adopted and certified Housing Element and pursuant 
to certain mandates in state law. 
Applying Section 15183, there are no project specific effects that are peculiar to the project; there are no project specific 
impacts that the General Plan EIR failed to analyze as significant effects; there are no potentially significant off-site and 
cumulative impacts that the EIR failed to evaluate; there is no substantial new information which results in more severe 
impacts than anticipated by the EIR; and projects will be required to comply with mitigation measures contained in the EIR.  
Furthermore, any future development of sites where these code amendments would apply would be evaluated as a 
potential “Project” under CEQA and if necessary, be further assessed for their potential environmental impacts prior to 
City entitlement.
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 – Proposed Ordinance
STAFF CONTACT
Chantal Eaton, Deputy City Clerk
ceaton@cityofsanmateo.org

CITY OF SAN MATEO 
PROPOSED ORDINANCE 
Amending Several Chapters of Title 27, Zoning, of the San Mateo Municipal Code to Implement the 2023-2031 
San Mateo Housing Element (Sixth Housing Cycle) 
WHEREAS, the State Housing Element Law (Government Code Section 65580 et seq.) requires that the 
City Council adopt a Housing Element for the 2023-2031 period to accommodate the City of San Mateo (“City”) 
regional housing need allocation (RHNA) of 7,015 housing units; and  
WHEREAS, on May 20, 2024, the City Council adopted a resolution to adopt the revised Housing Element 
of the General Plan for the period of 2023-2031 (Housing Element); and 
WHEREAS, under Housing Element Goal 1 – Production and the Policies H1.3, H1.6, and H1.9, the City 
commits to reviewing and amending the City’s Zoning Code to update the City’s Inclusionary Housing 
requirements to allow for alternative compliance options to increase affordable housing production, update 
standards to support housing production, and create minimum residential densities for mixed-use projects; and 
WHEREAS, the Environmental Impact Report (EIR) for the San Mateo General Plan 2040 analyzed new 
residential development of up to 19,764 new housing units and growth around the City through 2040; and  
WHEREAS, on March 18, 2024, at a duly noticed public hearing, the City Council certified the EIR for the 
San Mateo General Plan 2040, pursuant to the California Environmental Quality Act of 1970, together with 
related State implementation guidelines promulgated thereunder (CEQA); and 
WHEREAS, on May 13, 2025, the Planning Commission held a duly noticed public hearing, received all 
written and oral public comments, and recommended that the City Council approve the proposed ordinance to 
amend several Chapters of Title 27, Zoning, of the San Mateo Municipal Code to Implement the 2023-2031 San 
Mateo Housing Element (Sixth Housing Cycle); and 
WHEREAS, on June 16, 2025, at a duly noticed public hearing, the City Council introduced an ordinance 
to adopt the amendments to several Chapters of Title 27, Zoning, of the San Mateo Municipal Code to 
Implement the 2023-2031 San Mateo Housing Element (Sixth Housing Cycle).   
NOW, THEREFORE, THE COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO ORDAINS AS FOLLOWS: 
Section 1. Legislative Findings. At a duly noticed public hearing on June 16, 2025, the City Council of the City of 
San Mateo reviewed and considered the accompanying staff report and attachments thereto, the exemption 
from environmental review, together with comments received during the public review process, and hereby 
finds and determines that the proposed ordinance is consistent with the adopted General Plan in that it 
supports the development of housing and achieves the General Plan’s stated goals and policies. 
Section 2. Section 27.02.230 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is added to Chapter 27.02 as follows: 
52 of 983

--- page 53 ---
27.02.230 RESIDENTIAL MIXED-USE DEVELOPMENT PROJECTS. 
 
(a) Within a 1/2 mile radius of a Caltrain Station as shown on the General Plan Land Use Map, a mixed-
use development project with a residential component that includes five or more residential units shall comply 
with the following minimum residential densities if the development project is within one of the following 
General Plan Land Use Designations. If the project area is within more than one General Plan Land Use 
Designations, the lowest density requirement applies. 
 
(1) Mixed-Use Low and Mixed-Use Medium I – 30 dwelling units per acre 
(2) Mixed-Use Medium II and Mixed-Use High – 50 dwelling units per acre 
 
(b) A mixed-use development project with a residential component that includes five or more residential 
units outside of a TOD area are required to comply with minimum residential densities when the development 
project is within one of the following General Plan Land Use Designations. If the project area is within more than 
one General Plan Land Use Designations, the lowest density requirement applies. 
 
(1) Mixed-Use Low – 10 dwelling units per acre 
(2) Mixed-Use Medium I – 15 dwelling units per acre 
(3) Mixed-Use Medium II – 30 dwelling units per acre 
(4) Mixed-Use High – 50 dwelling units per acre 
 
(c) The City Council may consider a request to reduce the required minimum residential density in a 
mixed-use development project with a residential component for a project that provides a greater percentage of 
affordable housing units than required by Section 27.16.050 Affordable Housing or by providing public 
community benefits.  
Section 3. Section 27.04.130 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is amended as follows: 
27.04.130 SITE PLAN AND ARCHITECTURAL REVIEW (SPAR). 
(a) The following projects, as well as other projects that may be designated in this code, require a site 
plan and architectural review and no such project shall commence until the approval body has approved a 
planning application for site plan and architectural review: any building, new parking lot, fence over seven (7) 
feet in height, or an extension, alteration, or addition of or to an existing building or parking lot; historic 
buildings within the Downtown Specific Plan area as specified in Chapter 27.66. Single family and accessory 
buildings that conform to the standards contained in Chapter 27.18, or minor facade modifications as defined in 
Section 27.04.313, and which conform with Section 27.08.031, are exempt from this requirement, unless they 
are specifically designated by this section as requiring review. In making its review, the Zoning Administrator, 
Development Review Board, and Planning Commission shall be guided by the standards adopted by the Planning 
Commission and City Council. The application shall be approved if the Zoning Administrator or Commission finds 
all of the following to exist: 
(1) The structures, site plan, and landscaping are in scale and harmonious with the character of 
the neighborhood; 
53 of 983

--- page 54 ---
(2) The development will not be detrimental to the harmonious and orderly growth of the City; 
(3) The development will not impair the desirability of investment or occupation in the vicinity, 
and otherwise is in the best interests of the public health, safety, or welfare; 
(4) The development meets all applicable standards as adopted by the Planning Commission and 
City Council, conforms with the General Plan, and will correct any violations of the zoning ordinance, 
building code, or other municipal codes that exist on the site; 
(5) The development will not adversely affect matters regarding police protection, crime 
prevention, and security. 
(b) All buildings, structures, landscaping, and other establishments shall be constructed in accordance 
with the approved drawings. 
(c) The City Council shall review and make the final determination on all buildings that exceed the height 
limit in the General Plan.

Section 5. Section 27.08.035 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is amended as follows: 
27.08.035 COMPLETION OF PLANNING APPLICATION. 
(a) A planning application is determined to be complete when all documents, reports, data, maps, fees, 
and other information prescribed in Section 27.08.010 are submitted and are determined to be adequate to 
allow the approval body to determine if the required findings can be made.  
(b) For any application determined to be incomplete, an applicant may appeal the incompleteness 
determination to the Planning Commission within 14 days of the date of the incomplete letter. Any appeal shall 
be submitted in writing, and be accompanied by payment of the filing fee. Pursuant to California Government 
Code Section 65943, the Planning Commission shall make a final written determination on the appeal not later 
than 60 calendar days after receipt of the applicant’s written appeal. The appeal process shall toll any timing 
requirements to resubmit incomplete application materials.  
(c) The Zoning Administrator may close out an application, if information is requested in writing by the 
Zoning Administrator, and the information has not been submitted to the Zoning Administrator or the action has 
not occurred within 120 days. Applications which are closed out are considered to be withdrawn and any 
unspent fees from the application will be refunded. An applicant for a housing development project, as defined 
in California Government Code Section 65905.5(b)(3) shall follow timelines and provisions outlined in California 
Government Code Section 65941.1 to maintain preliminary application eligibility.

(d) The Zoning Administrator's action may be appealed to the Planning Commission within ten (10) days 
of the Zoning Administrator's action closing out an application, or of determining that a new planning 
application is required. 
Section 6. Section 27.08.085 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is amended as follows: 
27.08.085 PLANNING APPROVAL EXPIRATION. 
Final approval of a planning application shall expire two years from the date of approval, with the 
following exceptions: 
(a) A completed building permit application is filed before the expiration date and a building permit is 
issued prior to or within six months after the two year expiration date. 
(b) The property within two years has been used in conformance with the final order.

are budgeted in whole or in part for the project by the City or Agency. 
(d) Projects for which an extension has been obtained in accordance with Section 27.08.087 below. 
Section 7. Section 27.08.087 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is amended as follows: 
27.08.087 PLANNING APPROVAL EXTENSION. 
The following extensions to planning approvals are available: 
(a) Extensions required by state or federal law. Where state or federal law requires that extension of a 
planning approval be considered, the Zoning Administrator shall approve an extension if they find that the 
project complies with all applicable zoning, planning, and general plan regulations and conditions. 
(b) City extensions. The Zoning Administrator may approve up to two extensions of a planning approval, 
each for up to two years, subject to the following provisions: 
(1) Application required. To apply for a planning approval extension, an applicant must submit 
the application prior to the expiration date of the planning approval, pay the application fee and provide 
the reason(s) for the extension request. 
(2) Findings required. To approve a time extension, the Zoning Administrator must find all of the 
following: 
(A) The approved project is still consistent with the City's General Plan; and 
(B) The approved project is still consistent with the City's Zoning Ordinance; and 
(C) The extension will not result in any new environmental impacts or an increase in 
severity of previously identified environmental impacts. 
55 of 983

--- page 56 ---
(c) Notice of Zoning Administrator Decision on Extension. Notice will be provided to the neighborhood 
by the same process followed for the original project approval. Notice will also be provided to the Planning 
Commission and City Council within ten days of the Zoning Administrator's decision. 
(d) Appeals. The decision of the Zoning Administrator is appealable to the Planning Commission within 
ten days of the Zoning Administrator's decision by filing a written appeal with the Planning Division and payment 
of applicable fees. When an appeal is filed, the expiration date of a planning approval is tolled until a final action 
is taken. The provisions of Municipal Code Chapter 26.48 govern all projects with associated subdivision maps. 
Section 8. Section 27.16.050 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is amended as follows: 
27.16.050 AFFORDABLE HOUSING. 
To implement the affordable housing goals and policies in the General Plan, the City Council adopted the 
Below Market Rate (BMR) Program. Specific requirements for the development of affordable units under the 
BMR Program, including the alternative compliance option of providing offsite affordable units and other 
proposed alternatives, are regulated by resolution adopted by the City Council. 
Section 9. Environmental Determination In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) 
Guidelines Section 15183, the zoning amendment to streamline the processing of development applications,  
and establish minimum densities for mixed-use housing development projects is exempt from further 
environmental review because the Project is consistent with the anticipated land uses under the land use 
designations adopted as part of General Plan 2040 for which an EIR was certified by the City Council on March 
18, 2024.  Also, there are no specific proposed changes to maximum residential development densities and is 
consistent with the development density established by the General Plan.  The Project is also consistent with the 
implementation policies of the adopted and certified Housing Element and pursuant to certain mandates in 
state law.  There are no project specific effects that are peculiar to the project or the sites; there are no project 
specific impacts that the General Plan Update EIR failed to analyze as significant effects; there are no potentially 
significant off-site and cumulative impacts that the general plan EIR failed to evaluate; and there is no 
substantial new information which results in more severe impacts than anticipated by the general plan EIR. 
Section 10. Severability. In the event any section, clause or provision of this ordinance shall be determined 
invalid or unconstitutional, such section, clause or provision shall be deemed severable and all other sections or 
portions hereof shall remain in full force and effect. 
Section 11. Publication. This Ordinance shall be published in summary in a newspaper of general circulation, 
posted in the City Clerk's Office, and posted on the City's website, all in accord with Section 2.15 of the City 
Charter. 
Section 12. Legislative History and Effective Date. This ordinance was introduced on Clerk to complete., and 
adopted on Clerk to complete., and shall be effective 30 days after its adoption.

PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
July 28, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Small Wireless Facility Permit Support Services — Agreements
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve professional consultant services agreements for small wireless facilities permit review support services through 
May 31, 2027, with up to two optional one-year extensions, with: Anchor Engineering, Inc. d/b/a ANCHOR, in an amount 
not to exceed $250,000; and Gunnerson Consulting & Communication Site Services, LLC in an amount not to exceed 
$250,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreements in substantially the form presented and 
issue amendments within the budget amount including no-cost term extensions.
BACKGROUND:
The Public Works Development Section is responsible for providing review and permitting for Small Wireless Facility (SWF) 
Permits within the public right-of-way. Over the last 5 years, staff have seen an increase in SWF permit applications being 
submitted to the City. Public Works received 178 wireless permit applications in 2023 and 11 wireless permit applications 
in 2024. As required by the Federal Communications Commission (FCC), City staff must review SWF applications within a 
review timeframe also known as the "shot clock” (90 days for installations on new structures and 60 days for installations 
on existing structures). The shot clock starts once the applicant submits the application and stops when the permit is 
approved, which includes the appeal process and decision. If staff cannot complete the review within the shot clock, the 
application is automatically deemed approved by existing federal law.
In 2024, Public Works amended the San Mateo Municipal Code (SMMC) Chapter 17.10 "Wireless Communication Facilities 
in the Public Right-of-Way” and adopted a new City policy for regulating small wireless facilities  within the public right-of-
way. As part of that adoption process, the City Council directed staff to pursue additional third-party consultant services 
for application review.
FCC RF Emission Compliance:
Pursuant to 47 U.S.C 332(c)(7), the City may not regulate the placement, construction, and modification of wireless service 
facilities to the extent that such facilities comply with the FCC’s regulations concerning radio frequency (RF) emissions. 
SWF applications are required to include an RF compliance report prepared by an RF engineer to certify the proposed 
facility, as well as any collocated facilities and any cumulative emissions from adjacent areas, will comply with applicable 
FCC regulations. These reports are prepared and stamped by licensed Electrical Engineers; however, City staff typically 
does not have the technical expertise to verify the calculations and results of these reports. 
Consultant Proposals:
In November 2024, Public Works issued a request for proposals (RFP) to multiple firms capable of providing 
comprehensive SWF review services on an on-call basis. In total, Public Works received responses from two (2) firms and 
57 of 983

--- page 58 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
evaluated those submissions based on the firms’ experience in SWF application review, RF Report preparation and/or 
review, the proposed firms’ staff qualifications, and the pricing structures. Ultimately, Public Works utilized those criteria 
to consider awarding agreements for on-call SWF review services to both firms. The firms selected by Public Works are:
 

Anchor Engineering, Inc. d/b/a ANCHOR

Gunnerson Consulting & Communications Site Services, LLC
 
Conflicts of Interest:
Gunnerson Consulting & Communications Site Services, LLC (Gunnerson Consulting) has no known conflicts of interest with 
the City. ANCHOR includes EBI Consulting as a sub-consultant, which has or currently provides services to wireless service 
providers (such as AT&T, Crown Castle, T-Mobile, and Verizon). EBI Consulting has confirmed that they will cease working 
for providers within the City while under contract with the City and will not review any submitted reports that were 
previously created by their firm. These two firms are recommended for award to allow staff to assign RF Report reviews to 
prevent a firm from reviewing their own reports.
 
Contract Terms:
These firms will each enter into separate agreements with the City for approximately two-year terms ending on May 31, 
2027, with up to two optional one-year extensions (with no contract value increases) pending performance review by staff.
 
Based on the above, staff recommends executing professional consultant agreements with ANCHOR and Gunnerson 
Consulting to provide SWF review services for a term of two years, with two optional one-year extensions, for not-to-
exceed amounts of $250,000 for each agreement. 
 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The expenses of these small wireless facility review services will be fully cost recoverable as they will be charged to the 
small wireless facility permit deposit accounts as set forth in the most current adopted fee schedule.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
These agreements are not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that they will not cause a 
physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Att 2 – Agreement with Gunnerson Consulting & Communications Site Services, LLC

SECTION 19 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

EXHIBIT A
SCOPE OF SERVICES
SWF PERMIT REVIEW SERVICES
•
Invoicing
o
Consultant shall track the total amount of work performed for each Small Wireless 
Permit under that permit’s specific wireless permit number (SWF# or WC#).
•
Application Packet Review
o
Perform complete review of all submittal documents for compliance with the City’s 
Wireless Ordinance and the City Policy for Small Wireless Facilities in the Right of Way. 
This may include, but is not limited to, the following documents:
▪
Project Narrative and Justification
▪
Public Noticing proof
▪
Construction Drawings
▪
Photo-simulations
▪
Alternative Sites Analysis
▪
Environmental Impact Assessments
▪
Exceptions Requests
o
Perform complete technical review of technical reports for compliances with applicable 
City code, FCC regulations, and/or other applicable health and safety regulations. This 
may include:
▪
RF/EME Compliance Reports (pre-construction and post-construction)
▪
Structural Analysis
▪
Noise Compliance Analysis
o
Utilize the City’s Permitting Portal (Tyler’s EnerGov). City Staff will provide access and 
training if necessary.
o
Attend site visits as necessary to review proposed SWF locations.
o
Attend Coordination Meetings with City staff for clarification or direction on reviews.
o
Attend applicant meetings when requested by applicants.
o
Draft comment letters for Notices of Incomplete during the application review or for the 
post-construction RF certification.
o
Draft Director Decision Letters and Notices for approval/denial of applications.
o
Send/communicate NOIs, comment letters, or Decision Letters to applicants.
•
Deliverables
o
Reviews to be completed within seven (7) calendar days to meet shot clock deadlines.
o
Acceptance or Comment letters for NOIs or Post-Construction RF certs.
o
Director’s Decision Letters and Notices of Director’s Decision
