SUBJECT:
Hillsdale High School/31st Avenue Pedestrian Safety Improvement Project – Grant Acceptance, Appropriation, and 
Supplemental Agreement 
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to accept a San Mateo County Transportation Authority Cycle 7 Measure A and Measure W Pedestrian 
and Bicycle Program grant in the amount of $351,648 and appropriate the grant funding and $39,072 of the City’s Measure 
A funds as a local match to the Hillsdale High School/31st Avenue Pedestrian Safety Improvement Project; approve a 
supplemental agreement with San Mateo County Transportation Authority to receive the grant funding; and authorize the 
City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and any necessary future no-cost term 
extensions.
BACKGROUND:
In August 2024, the City submitted a grant application to the San Mateo County Transportation Authority (TA) for Cycle 7 
Measure A and Measure W Pedestrian and Bicycle Program funding. The application sought funding for the design phase 
of the Hillsdale High School/31st Avenue Pedestrian Safety Improvement Project (Project). This project aims to enhance 
pedestrian safety and accessibility for students, staff, and the community near Hillsdale High School by addressing safety 
concerns, including speeding vehicles and limited pedestrian visibility. Proposed improvements include, but are not limited 
to, curb extensions, raised crosswalks, and a new traffic signal at the intersection of 31st Avenue and Alameda de las 
Pulgas.
In December 2024, the TA Board formally notified the City that its grant application for $351,648, with a required 10% 
local match of $39,072, was approved for the Project.  
On February 4, 2025, the City Council adopted a resolution authorizing the execution of a new Primary Agreement with the 
TA to facilitate future funding allocations from Measure A and Measure W program.
To proceed with the disbursement of the approved Cycle 7 Measure A and Measure W funds, a Project-specific 
Supplemental Agreement between the City and the TA must be executed. Staff recommends that the City Council 
authorize the execution of the Supplemental Agreement to secure and appropriate $351,648 in grant funding and $39,072 
in the City‘s Measure A funds as the required match for the Project.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Adoption of this Resolution will establish the Hillsdale High School/31st Avenue Pedestrian Safety Improvement Project 
[46R032] as a project in the 2025-26 Capital Improvement Program budget. The total project funding includes $351,648 
from the TA Cycle 7 Measure A and Measure W Pedestrian and Bicycle Program and a local match of $39,072 from the 
City’s Measure A funds, resulting in a total project budget of $390,720 for the design phase.
141 of 983

--- page 142 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because the adoption of a resolution approving a Supplemental Agreement with 
the TA and approving local matching funds is a government fiscal activity which does not involve any commitment to any 
specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15378(b)(4).)  In addition, the Hillsdale High School/31st Avenue Pedestrian Safety Improvement Project is exempt from 
CEQA review as an “existing facility,” because the proposed pedestrian and traffic safety related work 
consists of the operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or 
private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of 
existing or former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Project Description and Scope of Work:  
31st Avenue is a busy, multimodal corridor providing direct access to Hillsdale High School, the 
Hillsdale Shopping Center, Hillsdale Caltrain Station, and Rail Corridor TOD area. 
Approximately 60% of Hillsdale High School students use 31st Avenue to access school, and 
31st Avenue is served by two SamTrans bus routes to bring students from Foster City to 
Hillsdale High School and a San Mateo Union High School District to transport students by bus. 
The Project aims to address pedestrian safety concerns voiced by Hillsdale High School 
students, parents, staff, and nearby residents, including pedestrian visibility issues, illegally 
parked vehicles at crosswalks, and speeding.  
The Project includes design, environmental, community outreach, and planning phases to make 
improvements in the school parking lot and at the driveway. The outcome is expected to be a 
construction-ready design with a number of pedestrian safety improvements, including curb 
extensions and raised crosswalks at major crossings, high-visibility crosswalks at all 
145 of 983

--- page 146 ---
Page 3 of 6
21752589.2 
intersections, ADA compliant curb ramps, and a new signal and protected intersection at 31st 
Avenue and Alameda de las Pulgas. The improvements will provide added safety and mobility 
benefits for students and community members walking, biking, and accessing transit on 31st 
Avenue.   
This Project Supplement No. 1 provides funding for the completion of the PLAN, PA/ED, and 
PS&E phases of the Project.

SUBJECT:
Wastewater Treatment Plant Upgrade Engineering Services During Construction – Amendment and Contingency Increase
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Amendment No. 13 to the agreement with HDR Engineering, Inc. for engineering services during construction for 
the Wastewater Treatment Plant Upgrade and Expansion Project extending the term of the Agreement from August 31, 
2025 through May 31, 2026; increase the contingency reserve by $1,500,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to 
execute the amendment and issue change orders within the contingency amount.
BACKGROUND:
The City is currently under a Regional Water Quality Control Board Cease and Desist Order requiring improvements to its 
sewer collection system and Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) to eliminate sanitary sewer overflows and 
unauthorized discharges of untreated or partially treated wastewater to San Francisco Bay. The Clean Water Program 
(CWP) includes several capital improvement projects that are currently under construction and intended to comply with 
the Cease and Desist Order. The WWTP Upgrade and Expansion Project (Project) is the largest of these projects, which will 
result in a new treatment plant for processing wastewater. The project designer is HDR Engineering Inc. (HDR). As the 
project designer, HDR has also been contracted by the City to provide engineering services during construction (ESDC). 
Construction of the Project by Sundt Construction Inc. has been underway since May 2019. The Project is being delivered 
in three construction packages: 

Package 1 - Site preparation: Work was substantially completed in July 2022. 

Package 2 - Foundation piles: Work was substantially completed in January 2022. 

Package 3 - WWTP process facilities, Administration Building, and Maintenance Warehouse: In progress.
 
Below is the history of the HDR ESDC agreement and the amendments to date:

June 2019, City Council approved an agreement with HDR to provide ESDC services for Package 1 of the Project in 
the amount of $450,221.

June 2020, City Council approved Amendment No. 1 to include ESDC for Package 2 in the amount of $419,830.

November 2020, City Council approved Amendment No. 2 to include ESDC for Package 3 in the amount of 
$12,273,376 and authorized a contingency reserve of $2,244,000 to address future scope needs. 

Using the contingency budget from Amendment 2, the Public Works Director executed six additional amendments 
(Nos. 3 through 8) to HDR’s agreement.

July 2023, City Council approved an increase in the contingency reserve by $1,100,000 to address additional scope 
needs. 

Using the increased contingency budget, the Public Works Director executed two additional amendments (Nos. 9 
and 10) to HDR’s agreement.
150 of 983

--- page 151 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO

September 2024, City Council approved Amendment No. 11 to extend ESDC services for Package 3 in the amount 
of $1,607,808 and authorized an increase to the contingency reserve of $320,000 to address future scope needs. 

Using the increased contingency budget, the Public Works Director executed Amendment 12 to HDR’s agreement.

The total contract value awarded to HDR for Packages 1, 2, and 3, including the additional amendments using 
contingency funds is $18,233,097.

Currently, the remaining contingency budget is $182,138. 
On February 18, 2025, staff presented an update to Council for the WWTP Project including construction progress updates, 
causes for delays and budget increases, and updated schedules. Construction has been challenging while still progressing 
forward. The Project has required several significant changes, including changes related to the emergency generators fuel 
storage system, the fire suppression system in the new Administration Building, the chemical piping system, the aeration 
piping system, piping in the Biological Nutrient Removal Basin due to corrosion from dissimilar metals, timing issues 
related to the completion of leak testing of the concrete basins, and delays in remote monitoring system programming. 
 
In the February 2025 Council meeting, staff also noted that due to risks, changes, delays, and unknowns identified over the 
past year, the WWTP Project’s estimate at completion has increased by approximately $25 million from $572.5 million to 
$597.5 million, which is still subject to change. To ensure there is sufficient funding to complete the WWTP Project, staff 
proposed a $25 million budget increase in the Fiscal Year 2025-26 Capital Improvement Program to fund the completion of 
the project. The proposed budget increase was approved by Council on June 16, 2025. This $25 million increase includes 
costs associated with the construction contract ($19.5 million) and extended soft costs ($5.5 million). Under the extended 
soft costs, $1.5 million is associated with additional ESDC services – mainly project management, resident engineer, 
startup and commissioning assistance, design modifications, cost estimating, and change order cost reviews. The $1.5 
million budget increase is based on information known today and is subject to change as the project progresses closer to 
completion. 
 
Overall, construction of Package 3 is approximately 96% complete and in its final stages. Construction of the new 
Administration Building and the Maintenance Warehouse is completed and occupied. Construction of the new treatment 
facilities is anticipated to be complete in mid-summer 2025. Clean water testing began in January 2025 and will be ongoing 
until the whole new plant is ready for process startup. Process seeding, startup, and performance testing is expected to 
occur in the July - August 2025 timeframe. The new WWTP is anticipated to be in full service by September 2025. Once 
liquids treatment operations are fully transitioned from the existing to the new WWTP, the remaining scope of work 
includes conversion of the existing aeration basins to wet weather flow equalization basins, and miscellaneous sitewide 
improvements including site lighting, landscaping, paving, signage, site security, etc. Currently, final completion and 
project closeout is forecasted to be at the end of May 2026. 
 
The current term of HDR’s ESDC agreement is through August 31, 2025. As a result of the construction contract extension 
(approved and potential), extension of associated ESDC services is also necessary. Staff requests approval of Amendment 
No. 13 extending the term of HDR’s ESDC Agreement through May 31, 2026.
 
As noted above, the remaining contingency budget at this time is $182,138. Staff requests approval for increasing the 
contingency reserve by $1,500,000 for extension of HDR’s ESDC services to cover potential modifications to scope items 
that may be necessary to successfully complete the Project. Over the remaining project duration, staff will continue to be 
thorough in review and negotiations of potential amendments to manage the overall cost impact within the approved 
contingency budget.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available in the WWTP and Flow Management Upgrade and Expansion Project No. 46t003 to award 
Amendment No. 13 and increase the contingency reserve for HDR’s agreement for engineering services during 
construction.

PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
July 28, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Humboldt Street Paving with the City of Burlingame — Revised Cost Sharing Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve a Cost Sharing Agreement with the City of Burlingame for the Paving of Humboldt Street in conjunction with 
Burlingame’s 2025 Street Resurfacing Project with an estimated cost to the City in the amount of $150,000.00; and 
authorize the City Manager to execute the Agreement in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
Both the City of San Mateo and the City of Burlingame administer pavement management programs and construct annual 
pavement rehabilitation projects. The City of Burlingame has selected North Humboldt Street, between Peninsula Avenue 
and Amphlett Boulevard, as part of its 2025 Street Resurfacing Project. The city limit between the two cities lies within the 
center of the roadway in this portion of Humboldt Street. This joint effort aligns with both cities’ goals to leverage 
resources, promote regional cooperation, and improve infrastructure in a fiscally responsible manner. 
This Cost Sharing Agreement will provide the City of Burlingame funds to include the City of San Mateo’s portion of 
Humboldt Street for pavement rehabilitation as part of their 2025 street resurfacing project. The agreement proposes that 
the City of Burlingame will serve as the lead agency for project delivery, including design, bidding, construction 
management, and inspection. The estimated fiscal obligation for the City of San Mateo is $150,000. This amount will be 
funded from the Citywide Street Rehabilitation Program budget. 
Update: The original Cost Sharing Agreement was adopted by City Council on June 16, 2025, but was not executed. The 
previously attached version did not include minor revisions made by Burlingame staff and agreed upon with the City of San 
Mateo. The updated agreement, incorporating those changes, is now being submitted for approval.
The work will take place on North Humboldt Street, between Rollins Road and Peninsula Avenue, and will consist of a 
standard 2-inch mill and overlay of the top layer of asphalt. In addition to this pavement rehabilitation, a Class III bike 
facility will be implemented, which includes shared lane markings. The project will not alter travel lanes or parking. All 
work will be confined to North Humboldt Street, with no construction occurring on Peninsula Avenue. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available in the Citywide Street Rehabilitation Program (469997) to fund this Agreement. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
158 of 983

--- page 159 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Approval of this Cost Sharing Agreement is categorically exempt from CEQA, because the underlying project within the City 
of San Mateo consists of the operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing 
public or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no 
expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)

This Cost Sharing Agreement for the paving of Humboldt Street in conjunction 
with the City of Burlingame’s 2025 Street Resurfacing Project, Project No. 86970 
(“Agreement”) is entered into by and among the CITY OF BURLINGAME, a municipal 
corporation in the County of San Mateo, State of California, hereinafter called 
“Burlingame”, and the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation in the County of 
San Mateo, State of California, hereinafter called “San Mateo” (each a “Party” and 
collectively the “Parties”), and is made effective as of _________________, 2025 and is 
based on the following: 
 
RECITALS 
 
 
WHEREAS, the Parties desire to enter into an agreement to provide for the 
allocation of costs for the construction and construction management of street resurfacing 
on Humboldt Street (between Rollins Road and Peninsula Avenue), including staff costs, 
in conjunction with the City of Burlingame’s 2025 Street Resurfacing Project, Project No. 
86970 (“Project”), as further described below, and to establish the roles and 
responsibilities of the Parties for completion of construction and construction management, 
and to enter into this Agreement to establish the terms, conditions, and obligations thereon; 
and, 
 
 
WHEREAS, certain elements incorporated into the Project may provide benefit to 
only one of the Parties to this Agreement and each Party agrees to pay the full costs of any 
element(s) which is/are to its sole benefit and to share the cost of those elements that are 
of mutual benefit to the Parties; and,

AGREED by and between Burlingame and San Mateo as follows: 
 
Section 1.   
Recitals. 
 
The foregoing Recitals are true and correct and incorporated into this Agreement by 
reference. 
 
Section 2. 
Definitions. 
 
The following definitions apply to terms used in this Agreement: 
 
“Line-Specific Costs” means the actual cost of materials, labor and all associated 
incidentals to construct the improvements on Humboldt Street, including the billings of the 
contractor and any consultants. 
 
“Shared Costs” means the costs of labor and materials that are for the mutual benefit of 
the Parties including mobilization, storm water control, traffic control, and construction 
inspection. 
 
“Project Costs” includes all costs incurred by the Lead Agency specifically to implement 
and complete construction of the Project, including construction contractor costs and 
consultant construction inspection.  In addition, Project Costs include any liability the Lead 
161 of 983

--- page 162 ---
Page 3 of 8 
 
City of Burlingame and City of San Mateo 
 
 
Cost Share Agreement –Humboldt Street Paving 
 
Agency incurs in prosecuting the Project and any claims adjusting, claims investigation, 
and costs incurred in defending claims (including reasonable attorney’s fees) brought by 
any contractor or third party for damages arising out of the prosecution of the Project.   
 
“Lead Agency” means the agency responsible for performance of tasks required to 
implement and complete construction of the Project, or a portion thereof, on behalf of all 
Parties, including tasks such as obtaining and overseeing construction contractors and 
professional services.  Burlingame shall be the Lead Agency for the Project. 
 
Section 3. 
City of Burlingame 2025 Street Resurfacing Project. 
 
Burlingame performs an annual Street Resurfacing Project on various roadways in need of 
repair or treatment. Humboldt Street (between Rollins Road and Peninsula Avenue) is 
scheduled for resurfacing during the 2025 Street Resurfacing Project and has been selected 
for a mill and overlay treatment with pavement section repairs. This portion of Humboldt 
Street between Rollins Road and Peninsula Avenue is half within the jurisdictional 
boundary of Burlingame and half within the jurisdictional boundary of San Mateo. In 
addition, striping improvements will be made in conformance with Caltrans Standard 
Specifications and Standard Details.  
 
The improvements are shown on Exhibit “A”, and estimated costs associated with this 
construction are shown on Exhibit “B”. 
 
Section 4. 
Party Responsibilities. 
 
 
A.  
As Lead Agency, Burlingame will prepare bid documents, conduct the 
public bidding process, award the contract for the Project, and cause the Project to be 
constructed, all in compliance with applicable law.  Burlingame will be the primary contact 
with the contractor who is awarded the contract, subject to San Mateo’s right to initiate 
change orders or other reasonable directives with respect to San Mateo’s line-specific 
work.  To the furthest extent permitted by law, Burlingame shall issue a categorical

--- page 163 ---

exemption for the Project pursuant to Public Resources Code § 21000 et seq. and California 
Code of Regulations, Title 14, Sections 15000 et seq. (collectively, “CEQA”).  Burlingame 
shall include, in its contract with the contractor on the Project, a requirement that the 
contractor include San Mateo, its officials, officers, employees and agents as additional 
insureds and indemnify, defend, and hold harmless San Mateo, its officials, officers, 
employees and agents from any liability, claims, demands, suits or actions which may be 
made by any person resulting from the Project. 
 
 
B. 
San Mateo shall review the design documents, bid documents and contract 
to be awarded.  Upon opening of the bids, San Mateo may decline to participate in the 
Project if costs exceed San Mateo’s expectations and may terminate this Agreement, with  
at least five (5) business days’ written notice to Burlingame.  In the event of termination, 
San Mateo shall be responsible for its Line-Specific Costs which were incurred prior to 
termination.  This Agreement is subject to San Mateo obtaining authorization from its City 
Council for the Expenditure Limit as defined in Section 5(C) below.  San Mateo shall issue 
any categorical exemption or other CEQA notice which San Mateo may determine to be 
required for its line-specific work in addition to the categorical exemption noted in Section 
4(A) above.  
 
Section 5. 
Financial Participation.  
 
A. 
Burlingame and San Mateo shall each be responsible for the independent funding 
of their respective portions of the Project Costs.   
 
B. 
Each Party shall pay its own Line-Specific Costs, which are estimated in Exhibit 
“B.”  In addition, Burlingame and San Mateo shall pay Shared Costs as identified in this 
Agreement and on Exhibit “B.”  However, Burlingame and San Mateo understand and 
agree that the amounts in Exhibit “B’ are estimates and that actual, total amounts will be 
unknown until the Project is completed.  It is understood that the total sum to be reimbursed 
to Burlingame by San Mateo shall be determined from the Project Costs reasonably 
incurred by Burlingame to construct those certain improvements on behalf of San Mateo

Section 7. 
Limitations. 
 
This Agreement shall not affect, modify, amend, or replace the allocation of costs for 
treatment, capital improvements, or other services under any prior agreement between the 
Parties.  The Parties shall continue to share costs for those services in accordance with the 
procedures set forth in prior agreements until they are amended or replaced. Except as 
modified herein, the terms of previous agreements shall continue in full force and effect.

Section 8. 
Indemnification. 
 
Each Party shall indemnify, defend and hold the other Party, its officials, officers,

injuries, in law or in equity, to property or persons, including wrongful death, (“Claims”) 
in any manner arising out of or incident to any negligent acts or omissions or willful 
misconduct of the indemnifying Party, its officials, officers, employees, agents, consultants 
or contractors in the performance of the indemnifying Party’s obligations under this 
Agreement, including the payment of interest, penalties, reasonable attorney’s fees and 
expert fees, and amounts paid in settlement of any Claims. 
 
Each Party shall give the other Party prompt written notice of any Claim coming within 
the scope of this Section 8.  Each Party will cooperate in the investigation, defense, and 
settlement of any such Claim.

CITY OF SAN MATEO

Agenda Number:  12
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR

Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
July 28, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Citywide Elevator Maintenance Services – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution approving an alternative purchasing procedure to award a three-year agreement to Advanced Elevator 
Solutions, Inc. for Citywide Elevator Maintenance Services in the amount of $232,200, with two optional one-year 
extensions subject to future Council approval; establish an annual contingency in the amount of $8,000 per year of the 
initial three-year agreement; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form 
presented and issue amendments within the contingency amount.
BACKGROUND:
The City of San Mateo is the largest city on the Peninsula, with a population exceeding 100,000 residents. The City has over 
19 conveyances, such as elevators and wheelchair lifts, across its facilities and parking garages, all of which require 
ongoing repair and maintenance. To ensure public safety and protect workers in the elevator industry, elevators and 
wheelchair lifts must undergo monthly inspections to comply with State Division of Occupational Safety and Health permit 
requirements, as well as pass annual inspections by the State of California. As such, certified specialists are required to 
perform regular inspections, testing, and maintenance of the elevators and lifts in City-owned facilities. The City’s existing 
elevator maintenance services agreement expires on July 31, 2025.  
In May 2025, the City issued a Request for Proposals (RFP) outlining specific criteria for performing elevator maintenance 
services for a three-year term with two (2) optional one-year extensions. An RFP was issued rather than a using 
competitive bidding process, as explained below. Staff reviewed and assessed each submitted proposal based on the 
following criteria: Organization and Experience, Key Personnel, Approach and Understanding, and Cost Proposal. These 
factors were then assessed and weighted according to the RFP guidelines. The results of the scoring were:
Based on the results of the evaluation, in addition to checking references with other municipal agencies, staff identified
Advanced Elevator Solutions, Inc., to be the recommended firm. The breakdown of Advanced Elevator Solutions, Inc.’s 
annual elevator maintenance services cost proposal is shown below:
Company
Avg Score
(200 Max)
Ranking
Advanced Elevator Solutions, Inc.
175
1
Metro Elevator Northern California, Inc.
170
2
TK Elevator
145
3
Peninsula Elevator Company
140
4
171 of 983

--- page 172 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
The initial agreement period will be in place for three years with an option to extend the agreement for up to two (2)
additional one-year periods, bringing the total potential agreement term to a maximum of five (5) years. For the proposed
three-year term, the agreement amount results in a total of $232,000. The two additional one-year periods may only be 
granted through an amendment to the agreement, which will require Council approval.
To address any market fluctuations and any unknown additional expenses, staff also recommends establishing an annual
contingency in the amount of $8,000 renewing each agreement year to be utilized within the agreement term for
which it is reserved. If the service provider's initial three-year term performance warrants staff to recommend pursuing 
any of the optional term extensions, staff will return to Council to request an amendment for the term extension and
associated increase in fee.
San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.030 generally requires competitive bidding for agreements of $100,000 or more. 
San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.070(h)(3) authorizes the City Council to approve an alternative purchasing 
procedure when it finds doing so is in the best interest of the City. In this case, utilizing the RFP process is more beneficial 
to the City because the City is not required to accept the lowest bid, but rather may evaluate other factors in addition to 
price such as Organization and Experience, Key Personnel, and Approach and Understanding. In addition, an RFP allows the 
City to consider proposals where the purpose and/or performance is known but for which no unique specification exists. 
This process is in the best interest of the City as proposals are considered based upon ability along with cost savings to the 
City.
Staff recommends awarding the Citywide Elevator Maintenance Services agreement to Advanced Elevator Solutions, Inc. 
to ensure a service provider is in place for the next three fiscal years, and as the proposal received the highest scoring 
based on the established rating criteria. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Funding for Citywide elevator maintenance services is budgeted annually through the operating budgets of the Fleet and 
Building Maintenance Fund. Sufficient funding is available to cover the cost of this agreement.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This City Council action is exempt from CEQA review as an “existing facility,” because inspections, testing, and 
maintenance of the City’s elevator and wheelchair lifts consists of the operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, 
licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical 
features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

RESOLUTION NO. ____ (2025)
APPROVING AN ALTERNATIVE PURCHASING PROCEDURE TO AWARD A THREE-YEAR AGREEMENT FOR 
CITYWIDE ELEVATOR MAINTENANCE SERVICES WITH ADVANCED ELEVATOR SOLUTIONS, INC. IN THE AMOUNT 
OF $232,000, WITH TWO OPTIONAL ONE-YEAR EXTENSIONS SUBJECT TO FUTURE COUNCIL APPROVAL
WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo currently has over 19 conveyances, such as elevators and wheelchair 
lifts, across its facilities and parking garages which require ongoing inspections, repair and maintenance services 
to ensure public safety; and
WHEREAS, the City has contracted these elevator services since 2017 and the term of the current 
elevator maintenance services agreement expires on July 31, 2025; and
WHEREAS, in May 2025, staff issued a request for proposals (RFP); and
WHEREAS, staff received inquiries from multiple qualified participants, conducted a mandatory facilities 
walk through, and received proposals for a three-year term; and
WHERAS, staff analyzed each submitted proposal and evaluated each on the following factors: 
Organization and Experience, Key Personnel, Approach and Understanding and Cost proposal; and 
WHEREAS, staff selected Advanced Elevator Solutions, Inc. as the recommended firm based on a review 
of its proposal; and
WHERAS, sufficient funding is available in the Building Maintenance Fund for these elevator 
maintenance services; and 
WHEREAS, San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.030 generally requires competitive bidding for 
contacts of $100,000 or more; and
WHEREAS, San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.070(h)(3) authorizes the City Council to approve an 
alternative purchasing procedure when it is in the best interest of the City; and 
WHEREAS, utilizing the RFP process is more beneficial to the City because the City is not required to 
accept the lowest bid, but rather may evaluate other factors in addition to price such as Organization and 
Experience, Key Personnel, and Approach and Understanding; and 
WHEREAS, an RFP allows the City to consider proposals where the purpose and/or performance is 
known but for which no unique specification exists; and
WHEREAS, the RFP process is in the best interest of the City as proposals are considered upon ability 
along with cost savings to the City; and
WHEREAS, based on these circumstances, staff recommends award of a three-year agreement to 
Advanced Elevator Solutions, Inc. in the amount of $232,000.00 for elevator maintenance services.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES 
that: 
1.
Adoption of this resolution approving an alternative purchasing procedure to award an agreement 
to Advanced Elevator Solutions, Inc. is exempt from CEQA review as an “existing facility,” 
because inspections, testing, and maintenance of the City’s elevator and wheelchair lifts 
174 of 983

--- page 175 ---
consists of the operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor 
alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or 
topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. 
(CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.)
2.
In accordance with San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.070(h)(3), the award of an agreement to 
Advanced Elevator Solutions, Inc. based on an RFP process is in the City’s best interest for these 
reasons: 
a.
The RFP process is more beneficial to the City for these services because the City is not 
required to accept the lowest bid, but rather may evaluate other factors in addition to 
price such as Organization and Experience, Key Personnel, and Approach and 
Understanding.
b.
The RFP process allows the City to consider proposals where the purpose and/or 
performance is known but for which no unique specification exists.
c.
The RFP process is in the best interest of the City as proposals are considered upon 
ability along with Cost savings to the City. 
3.
The award of a three-year elevator maintenance services agreement to Advanced Elevator 
Solutions, Inc. in the amount of $232,000, with a contingency reserve in the amount of $8,000 per 
contract year, is approved.

The Public Works Director is authorized to issue change orders within the contingency amount and 
execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.

Agreement.

the services of CONTRACTOR.

perform public work pursuant to Section 1725.5 of the Labor Code.  For the work performed for the

to compliance monitoring and enforcement by the Department of Industrial Relations.  CONTRACTOR 
agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in Municipal Code Chapter 5.92, 
which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer Price Index escalator.

SECTION 15 - WAIVERS
The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this

term, covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of

Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this provision that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.

subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act.
SECTION 18 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

EXHIBIT A
SCOPE OF SERVICES
The work under this section includes elevator maintenance, repairs and emergency response services. 
There are twenty-one (21) operable elevators in fifteen (15) locations across the City.
Contractor’s Duties:
a) The work to be performed by the Contractor under this agreement will consist of furnishing all
material, labor, tools and equipment necessary to provide full preventative maintenance on the
equipment described.
b) Any work not specifically mentioned but which is needed to make the maintenance complete with the 
intent of this agreement will be performed after quoting the additional work and receiving City 
authorization.
c) The contractor will perform, coordinate and complete the requirements of this agreement in
cooperation with any other contractors or trades doing any work on the property.
d) The contractor will guarantee that a member of their supervisory personnel regularly engaged in 
inspection and supervision will visit each elevator semi-annually to ensure the quality of
maintenance meets the specified and intended standards. The Contractor’s supervisor will
schedule site visits with the Public Works Facilities Maintenance Supervisor or designee.
e) The Contractor agrees that they can maintain this equipment to its original design capabilities
based on the equipment conditions as surveyed and all the technical information available at the
182 of 983

--- page 183 ---
Contract for Services-Estero SB854 (PW)
Page 8 of 15
Rev 9/22
time of the award of this agreement.
f) Provide routine service of equipment, time expended on routine service will consist of
examination, minor adjustment, cleaning and lubricating the equipment. All lubrication
frequencies and types of lubricants will meet the manufacturers’ specifications.
g) At the City’s reasonable request, the Contractor will provide a hall call traffic analysis report on
any or all elevators. The analyzer will be capable of gathering and printing information about the
elevator performance and group operation. 
h) Contractor will notify Public Work Facilities Maintenance Supervisor if hydraulic fluid is added to a tank 
due to an unaccountable loss of fluid. The contractor will notify the City of the implication of possible 
underground leakage and provide a proposal with a complete description of the procedure for 
individually testing the integrity of the cylinder and underground to/from oil line if applicable.
Exclusions
The following work is excluded and will not be the responsibility of the Contractor:
a) Power supply feeders, switches and fuses
b) Repair or replacement of products of combustion detectors for fire recall.
c) Car enclosure finishes and lighting lamps, hoistway enclosures, door panels and frames
d) Other items caused by vandalism or negligence by persons other than the Contractor, its
representatives and employees, excluding normal wear and tear. Contractor will obtain the
Public Works Facilities Maintenance Supervisor or his designee’s written approval to repair vandal related 
problems. Payment will not be made for any unauthorized work.
e) New attachments may be recommended or directed by insurance companies or by Federal,
State, Municipal or other Government Authorities.
Conditions of Service
Contractor will maintain all elevator equipment in good operating condition in accordance with
manufacturer’s specifications and should conditions warrant, repair or replace the equipment,
including, without limitation, the following:
Traction Elevators
a) Machine, drive sheave, drive sheave shaft bearings, brake pulley, brake coil, brake contact,
linings and component parks, gears, worms, thrusts.
b) Motor and motor generator windings, rotating element, commutator, brushes, brush holders
and bearings.
c) Controller, selector and dispatching equipment, all relays, solid state components, resistors,
condensers, transformers, contacts, leads, dashpots, timing devices, computer devices, steel
selector tape or cable and mechanical and electrical driving equipment.
d) Governor, governor sheave and shaft assembly, bearings, contacts and governor jaws.
Deflector, car and counterweight sheaves, bearings, car and counterweight buffers, car and
counterweight guide rails and brackets, top and bottom limit switches, governor tension sheave
assembly, compensating sheave assembly, counterweight and counterweight guide shoes
including rollers and gibs. Contractor is not responsible for alignment when affected by
building compression or shifting hoistway enclosures.
e) Hoistway door interlocks, door hangers, bottom door guides and auxiliary door closing
devices. Automatic power operated door operator, car door hanger, car door contact, door
protective devices, load-weighting equipment, car frame, safety mechanisms, platform,
platform sub-flooring, elevator guide shoes, gibs and rollers, signal and operating fixtures
183 of 983

--- page 184 ---
Contract for Services-Estero SB854 (PW)
Page 9 of 15
Rev 9/22
including lights, buzzers and gongs in all signal and operation fixtures.
f) Renew all wire ropes as needed, equalize the tension on all hoisting ropes, repair or replace
conductor cables, hoistway and elevator machine room to maintain adequate factor of safety.
g) Shorten, reshackle and retention hoist cables if stretching of ropes makes this necessary.
• Contractors will check the condition and operation of detectors, safety edges and of
light rays on cars at every visit. If they are inoperative, the contractor will repair within one business 
day. Seismic triggers and/or derailment devices; collision switches.
• Fire related elevator controls.
• Respond to discrepancies identified by the local elevator enforcing authorities.
Hydraulic Elevators
a) Complete pumping plan, valves, exposed piping fittings, piston (less damaged by cylinder
failure), packing tank, heaters and mufflers.
b) If flexible hose and fitting assemblies are used, they will be changed as required by California
Code of Regulations, Title 8, Safety Orders, sooner if necessary.
c) Materials and services covered as applicable by Contractor.
d) Furnish all oil, lubricants, packing and other materials required.
Wheelchair Lifts
a) Materials and services covered by Traction Elevators or Hydraulic Elevators as applicable.
Performance
a) The Contractor will maintain the original contract speed in feet per minute and the
performances for elevators as indicated under basic performance requirements.
b) If the performance of the elevator does not meet the times established for the elevators of
speed and type of control, the Contractor will restore the elevator to its optimal potential.
c) If there are no exceptions taken, performance will be as provided as specified hereinafter.
d) Basic Performance Requirements: Electric elevators will be adjusted to meet the following
performance standards and will maintain these standards for the life of the agreement.
Operating Characteristics
a) Starting, acceleration, stopping and leveling will be smooth and free from jars or bumps.
b) Full speed riding will be without swaying or vibration.
c) Elevator and door operation will be quiet.
d) Emergency stop by way of emergency stop switch will be sudden but not violent.
e) Door pressure will be maintained below 30 pounds closing.
f) Keep group control systems operating at design criteria for the lifetime of the maintenance
agreement. If systems check requires test/corrections on overtime hours, Contractor will
provide at no additional costs.
g) Door standing times. Car Call – 5 seconds. Hall Call – 5 seconds.
Special Tests
a) Elevators provided with fire service, derailment devices, seismic switches or other special
circuitries will be checked once every year to make certain these devices operate correctly as
designed. The Contractor and Public Works Facilities Maintenance Supervisor or his designee will
arrange for mutually acceptable dates to perform the tests. The City will test the emergency
power operation and if the elevator system fails, Contractor will make corrections and retest.
b) The Contractor will examine periodically each car safety devices and governors and conduct
an annual no load test and will, during the term of this agreement, or more if required by
184 of 983

--- page 185 ---
Contract for Services-Estero SB854 (PW)
Page 10 of 15
Rev 9/22
applicable law, ordinance or regulation but no less than once every five years, and perform one
full load, full speed test of the safety mechanism, over-speed governors, car and counterweight
buffers. The car balance will be checked electrically and the governor set. If required, the
governor will be recalibrated and sealed for proper tripping speed. The City will witness these
tests and the Contractor will furnish a written report indicating the results of such tests. All testing will 
conform to the requirements of AMSE A17.1 and/or local code testing requirements.
c) Hydraulic elevators will have a load test performed during the term of this agreement at least
once every five years, or more often if required by applicable law, ordinance or regulation and
will conform to the requirement of the State.
d) Contractor will create a form for each car describing tests and deliver a signed copy to the
Public Works Facilities Maintenance Supervisor after successful tests are concluded. The forms will
also describe any malfunctions along with corrective actions taken.
Housekeeping
a) Within the first three (3) months after this agreement is in effect, the Contractor will
thoroughly clean all elevator hoistways, pits, car tops, controller interiors including filters and
machine rooms. In addition, during the same period of time, all cars and hoistway door tracks,
hangers, interlocks and closers will be cleaned, lubricated and adjusted. Continuing cleaning
must be ongoing and at the following minimum intervals or sooner where conditions warrant:
• Quarterly: Car tops, pits, and machine rooms
• Semi-Annually: Hoist ways and door equipment
b) Exterior of machinery and other parts of equipment subject to rust will be properly painted and 
presentable at all times. The motor windings and controller coils will be periodically treated
with insulating compound. Contractor will maintain cleanliness of elevator room.
c) All debris such as wiping rags, empty oil cans, trash from pits, etc. resulting from this work
will be promptly removed by Contractor.
Stock of Materials
Contractor will maintain an adequate supply of contact in each machine room, switch parts, coils,
conductors, springs, holders, resistors, relays, lamps, condensers, tubes, transformers, car and hall buttons, 
fuses and other parts which are required for prompt replacements together with an adequate supply of 
lubricants and wiping rags. All supplies will be stored in metal cabinets or shelves and oily rags in closed metal 
containers. All cabinets, shelves and containers to be provided by the Contractor for use during the term of the 
contract. In addition, keep on the premises or in a warehouse within fifty (50) miles of the project or reasonably 
obtainable within 24 hours:
• One set of motor brushes and holders for each type of motor and generator
• One door operator for each type used
• Hanger sheaves for car and hoistway doors
• Two complete door interlocks
• One set of generator bearing for each type of generator
• One set of brake linings for each type of brake
• Parts for door protective devices
• Power supplies
• Coils, relays, motor brushes and all other parts as needed to ensure prompt replacement in 
the event of the shutdown of any elevator
• SCR drive components
• One spare control board of each type installed. Cylinder head packing and pump motor belts
Wiring Diagrams and Digitization
A complete set of all wiring diagrams for the elevator systems covered under this agreement will be
185 of 983

--- page 186 ---
Contract for Services-Estero SB854 (PW)
Page 11 of 15
Rev 9/22
maintained in their respective machine rooms. All changes in circuitry made by the Contractor will
be properly recorded on the City’s and the machine room copies of diagrams, including the date of
change and the name of the person making the change. The Contractor is responsible for digitizing
all existing wiring diagrams into a PDF format. This will include all relevant details and annotations.
The Contractor will ensure the accuracy and completeness of the digitized diagrams. The digitization
process will be completed within the first 12 months of contract execution and will be provided to the
Public Works Facilities Maintenance Maintenance Supervisor. The wiring diagrams are the property of the
City and are to remain in their respective machine rooms. The City will supply wiring diagrams if not
in the machine rooms.
Telephone Monitoring
Contractor will provide 24-hour telephone monitoring for each elevator and be able to dispatch both
service technicians for trouble/entrapment calls and relay calls to emergency services (911) as
appropriate.
Schedules and Records
a) Contractor will provide a work schedule for each machine room.
b) These work schedules will be designed for each type of equipment to be serviced, such as
geared elevators, hydraulic elevators, etc., and will conform to the manufacturer’s
recommended practice for the particular equipment concerned. They will show the kind and
frequency of service and lubrication proposed by the Contractor for the duration of the
agreement.
c) Minimum routine service all inspections, lubrication, adjustments, tests, cleaning, routing
repairing and other preventative maintenance activities will be performed in accordance with
schedules submitted by the Contractor.
d) Schedules posted will be of the chart type, which will be initialed by the service technician
when each scheduled inspection is performed.
e) Contractor will also maintain an accurate and complete log of all work performed in addition
to routine service at each location. These logs will include call back service describing the
nature of all complaints and the resolution. The logs will be kept in the equipment rooms at
each location. In addition to the specified machine room log, Contractor’s staff or supervisor
will log in and out of each facility/building on each and every visit. This includes, but is not
limited to, routine maintenance, trouble calls, repairs and supervisor’s visits. These logs will
remain the property of the City.
f) The Contractor, on a monthly basis, will deliver to the Public Works Facilities Maintenance Supervisor, a 
copy of the callback and repair log.
Performance Guarantee
a) All repairs will be pre-scheduled in writing. If an elevator is out of service for longer than two
consecutive workdays for a non-scheduled repair, the monthly maintenance cost of that unit
will be credited to the next monthly billing.
b) If the Contractor does not respond in the time frames listed below under “Trouble/Entrapment
Calls,” the following month’s billing will be credited $500 for each extended trouble call.
c) Contractors will be contacted on a rotating basis at the discretion of the City based on factors
such as cost, expertise, and availability.
Trouble/Entrapment Calls
a) Call back service will be furnished upon request at the Contractor’s expense during regular
working days. If a callback during overtime hours is required, the Contractor will furnish all
travel time, expenses and time on the job. Contractor may bill the City for the bonus portion
186 of 983

--- page 187 ---
Contract for Services-Estero SB854 (PW)
Page 12 of 15
Rev 9/22
of labor for overtime hours, but only a maximum of one hour travel time will be billed per
overtime callback.
b) In the event an elevator is shut down with trapped passengers (entrapment), Contractor will
guarantee thirty (30) minute response time between 8:00 am and 5:00 pm, Mondays through
Fridays and a one (1) hour response time between 5:00 pm and 8:00 am, Mondays through
Fridays, Saturdays, Sunday, and holidays.
c) In the event an elevator shuts down without trapped passengers, Contractor will guarantee a
one (1) hour response time between 8:00 am and 5:00 pm, Mondays through Fridays and a two
(2) hour response time between 5:00 pm and 8:00 am, Mondays through Fridays, Saturdays,
Sunday, and holidays.

EXHIBIT C
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate limit 
applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or the 
general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if Contractor 
has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no less than 
$1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury 
or disease. 
If the contractor maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and shall be 
entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the contractor.
Other Insurance Provisions
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
Additional Insured Status
The City of San Mateo and the Estero Municipal Improvement District, their elected and appointed 
officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on the auto policy for liability arising 
out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf of the Contractor; and on the 
CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations performed by or on behalf of the 
Contractor including materials, parts or equipment furnished in connection with such work or 
operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an endorsement to the 
Contractor’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 
forms if later revisions used).
Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this contract, the Contractor’s insurance coverage shall be primary insurance 
as respects the City of San Mateo and the Estero Municipal Improvement District, their elected and 
appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City of 
San Mateo and the Estero Municipal Improvement District, their elected and appointed officials, 
employees, or agents shall be excess of the Contractor’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.
Notice of Cancellation
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City.
Waiver of Subrogation
189 of 983

--- page 190 ---
Contract for Services-Estero SB854 (PW)
Page 15 of 15
Rev 9/22
Contractor hereby grants to the City of San Mateo and the Estero Municipal Improvement District a 
waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said Contractor may acquire against the City by 
virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  Contractor agrees to obtain any endorsement 
that may be necessary to effect this waiver of subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of 
whether or not the City has received a waiver of subrogation endorsement from the insurer.  
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the Contractor to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof of 
ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within the 
retention.  
Acceptability of Insurers
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City.
Verification of Coverage
Contractor shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies of 
the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the Contractor’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall

Agenda Number:  13
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 82-4681

TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
July 28, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Poplar Creek Tenant Improvements – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve and authorize the City Manager to execute an agreement with Jon K Takata Corporation d/b/a Restoration 
Management company to provide contractual services in an amount not to exceed $1,265,263.23; increase the 
construction contract contingency to $50,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to issue change orders within the 
contingency amount.
BACKGROUND:
Poplar Creek Golf Course has been in operation for over 90 years, with the current clubhouse built in 2000. The City and 
PAR3SM, LLC (PAR 3) entered into a lease agreement on April 3, 2019. In October 2024, an environmental hygienist 
conducted an air test which revealed high levels of bacteria, rendering the space unsafe for occupancy. Therefore, the 
restaurant tenant space was evacuated and has since remained closed to PAR 3 staff and the public during repairs.
In October 2024, the City engaged Jon K Takata Corporation dba Restoration Management Company (RMC) to develop the 
following phases of work:
•
Phase 1: Establish containment, decontaminate the area, remove and store equipment and furniture, and 
document existing building conditions. Investigate the source of water infiltration by removing finish materials and 
inspecting the drain lines. Identify the remaining tasks required to complete Phase 1 and incorporate them into a 
subsequent change order. Develop a detailed scope, schedule, and fee of repairs for Phase 2.
•
Phase 2: Restoration work to remove contaminants, repair or replace structural components, and install new finish 
materials. Reinstall equipment and furniture.
•
Phase 3: Coordinate with County Health officials to recommission the restaurant for operations. Provide a 
plumbing report and remediation report.
On November 21, 2024, the City entered into a $79,606.90 construction contract with RMC to initiate Phase 1. Subsequent 
change orders totaling $1,185,656.33 were issued to cover Phases 2 and 3, bringing the overall contract amount to 
$1,265,263.23. This included Change Order No. 1 for $1,072,649.41 approved on February 3, 2025 by the City Council, 
which also authorized a construction contract contingency of $115,300.  However, City staff inadvertently allowed the 
original construction contract to expire, thus necessitating the proposed agreement attached to this staff report in the 
amount of $1,265,263.23.  This agreement will cover all work performed since January 1, 2025 and going forward.  
Additionally, an increase in the remaining contingency from $2,292.18 to $50,000 is to address the additional work not in 
the original scope to ensure the project meets aesthetic standards.  
191 of 983

--- page 192 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
BUDGET IMPACT:
This work is funded by the Capital Improvement Program budget through the Poplar Creek Tenant Improvements Project 
(46f053).
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15301.)

This Agreement, made and entered into this day of ____________________, by and between 
the CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California 
("CITY"), and JON K TAKATA CORPORATION DBA RESTORATION MANAGEMENT COMPANY, a

RECITALS:
A.
CITY desires certain contractual services hereinafter described.
B.
CITY desires to engage CONTRACTOR to provide these contractual services by 
reason of its qualifications and experience for performing such services and CONTRACTOR has offered 
to provide the required services on the terms and in the manner set forth herein.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows:
SECTION 1 - SCOPE OF SERVICES
The scope of services to be performed by CONTRACTOR under this Agreement is as described in 
Exhibit A to this Agreement, which is attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 2 - DUTIES OF CONTRACTOR
CONTRACTOR shall be responsible for the professional quality, technical accuracy and 
coordination of all work furnished by CONTRACTOR under this Agreement.  CONTRACTOR shall, 
without additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in its work.
CONTRACTOR represents that it is qualified to furnish the services described under this 
Agreement.
CONTRACTOR shall be responsible for employing or engaging all persons necessary to perform 
the services of CONTRACTOR.
CONTRACTOR and his subcontractors shall comply with State’s Prevailing Wage Laws. 
CONTRACTOR agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in 
Municipal Code Chapter 5.92, which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer 
Price Index escalator.

CONTRACTOR or subcontractor shall not be qualified to bid on, be listed in a bid proposal, or 
engage in the performance of any contract for public work unless currently registered and qualified to 
perform public work pursuant to Section 1725.5 of the Labor Code.  For the work performed for the 
City, the CONTRACTOR and his subcontractors shall pay the workers at the prevailing wage rate as 
determined by the California Department of Industrial Relations, for this locality.  This project is subject 
to compliance monitoring and enforcement by the Department of Industrial Relations.
SECTION 3 - DUTIES OF CITY
CITY shall provide pertinent information regarding its requirements for the project.
CITY shall examine documents submitted by CONTRACTOR and shall render decisions pertaining 
thereto promptly, to avoid unreasonable delay in the progress of CONTRACTOR's work.
SECTION 4 - TERM
The services to be performed under this Agreement shall commence upon execution of this 
Agreement and be completed on December 31, 2025.
SECTION 5 - PAYMENT
Payment shall be made by CITY only for services rendered and upon submission of a payment 
request upon completion and CITY approval of the work performed.  In consideration for the full 
performance of the services set forth in Exhibit A, CITY agrees to pay CONTRACTOR in an amount of 
$1,265,263.23.
SECTION 6 – TERMINATION
Without limitation to such rights or remedies as CITY shall otherwise have by law, CITY shall have 
the right to terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the Project for any reason, upon ten (10) days' 
written notice to CONTRACTOR.  CONTRACTOR agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon 
receipt of said written notice.
SECTION 7 - OWNERSHIP OF DOCUMENTS
All documents prepared by CONTRACTOR in the performance of this Agreement are and shall 
be the property of CITY, whether the project for which they are made is executed or not.
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY
All reports and documents prepared by CONTRACTOR in connection with the performance of 
this Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONTRACTOR shall not make any 
such documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed by 
CONTRACTOR or CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release.

out of CONTRACTOR’s performance of this Agreement, except for those claims arising out of CITY’s sole 
negligence or willful misconduct.  CONTRACTOR agrees to defend City, its elected and appointed 
officials, employees, and agents against any such claims. The CONTRACTOR’S duty to indemnify shall 
survive expiration or early termination of this Agreement.

in Exhibit B to this Agreement.
SECTION 13 - NONASSIGNABILITY

and cannot be transferred, assigned, or subcontracted by CONTRACTOR without the prior written 
consent of CITY.

It is mutually understood and agreed by and between the parties hereto that CONTRACTOR is 
skilled in the performance of the work agreed to be done under this Agreement and that CITY relies 
upon the skill of CONTRACTOR to do and perform the work in the most skillful manner, and 
CONTRACTOR agrees to thus perform the work.  The acceptance of CONTRACTOR's work by CITY does 
not operate as a release of CONTRACTOR from said obligation.

SECTION 15 - WAIVERS
The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this 
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such 
term, covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of 
any other term, condition, ordinance, or law.  The subsequent acceptance by either party of any fee or 
other money which may become due hereunder shall not be deemed to be a waiver of any preceding 
breach or violation by the other party of any term, covenant, or condition of this Agreement or of any 
applicable law or ordinance.
SECTION 16 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this provision that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.
SECTION 17 - NON-DISCRIMINATION
CONTRACTOR warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONTRACTOR nor any of its 
subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act.
SECTION 18 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

Other Insurance Provisions

of the Contractor; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the Contractor including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the Contractor’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both CG 
20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).
Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this contract, the Contractor’s insurance coverage shall be primary insurance 
as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any insurance or self-
insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or agents shall be 
excess of the Contractor’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.
Notice of Cancellation
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City.

SUBJECT:
Citywide Street Rehabilitation Package 6B-1 — Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with G. Bortolotto & Company, Inc. in the amount of $2,588,392.25; establish a contingency 
reserve in the amount of $389,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in substantially the 
form presented and issue change orders as allowed by the contract documents and within the contingency amount. 
BACKGROUND:
The Citywide Street Rehabilitation Package 6-B (Package) is part of the Public Works Pavement Management Program 
(PMP). The City entered into an Agreement with Nichols Consulting Engineers, Inc. in May 2024 to design Package 6-B, and 
the Package was split into two phases, PMP 6B-1 and PMP 6B-2. The first phase of the Package (PMP 6B-1) will repave nine 
street segments (see Attachment 2) with an Engineer’s Estimate for the Project of $3,800,000. The second phase of the 
Package (PMP 6B-2) is under design and is anticipated for bid in summer of 2026.
 
In accordance with Municipal Code section 3.60.030, a competitive public process was used to obtain bids for PMP 6B-1. 
PMP 6B-1 was advertised on May 22, 2025. On June 23, 2025, five bids were received. The bids received are listed below in 
order of increasing bid amount.
 
Contractor
Base Bid
G. Bortolotto & Company, Inc.
$2,588,392.25
O’Grady Paving, Inc.
$3,042,555.00
Graniterock
$3,130,888.00
Interstate Grading and Paving, Inc.
$3,211,862.00
McGuire and Hester
$3,344,757.00
 
After review of bid documents, staff determined that G. Bortolotto & Company, Inc. is the lowest responsive, responsible 
bidder. Therefore, staff recommends G. Bortolotto & Company, Inc. be awarded this construction agreement. In addition, 
staff recommends a contingency of 15% be established given potential unforeseen subgrade conditions.
 
Construction is anticipated for late summer and fall of 2025.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Currently, there are sufficient funds in the Citywide Street Rehabilitation Program (Project No. 469997) project budget to 
award this agreement and establish the contingency reserve.

Exhibit A 
Insurance Requirements for Construction Contracts
Contractor shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract, and for x years thereafter, 
insurance against claims for injuries to persons or damages to property which may arise from or in 
connection with the performance of the work hereunder by the Contractor, his agents, 
representatives, employees, or subcontractors. 
MINIMUM SCOPE AND LIMIT OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as: 
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 covering CGL 
on an “occurrence” basis, including products and completed operations, property damage, bodily 
injury and personal & advertising injury with limits no less than $5,000,000 per occurrence. If a 
general aggregate limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this 
project/location (ISO CG 25 03 or 25 04) or the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required 
occurrence limit.  
2. Automobile Liability:  Insurance Services Office Form CA 0001 covering Code 1 (any auto), with 
limits no less than $5,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage. 
3. Workers’ Compensation insurance as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, 
and Employers’ Liability insurance with a limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily 
injury or disease. 
4. Builder’s Risk (Course of Construction) insurance utilizing an “All Risk” (Special Perils) coverage 
form, with limits equal to the completed value of the project and no coinsurance penalty 
provisions.
5. Surety Bonds as described below.
6. Contractors’ Pollution Legal Liability and/or Asbestos Legal Liability and/or Errors and Omissions 
(if project involves environmental hazards) with limits no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or 
claim, and $2,000,000 policy aggregate.
If the contractor maintains broader coverage and/or higher limits than the minimums shown above, 
the Entity requires and shall be entitled to the broader coverage and/or higher limits maintained by 
the contractor. Any available insurance proceeds in excess of the specified minimum limits of insurance 
and coverage shall be available to the Entity.
Self-Insured Retentions 
Self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the Entity. At the option of the Entity, 
either: the contractor shall cause the insurer shall to reduce or eliminate such self-insured retentions 
as respects the Entity, its officers, officials, employees, and volunteers; or the Contractor shall provide 
a financial guarantee satisfactory to the Entity guaranteeing payment of losses and related 
investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses. The policy language shall provide, or be 
endorsed to provide, that the self-insured retention may be satisfied by either the named insured or 
Entity.
Other Insurance Provisions 
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:

1. The Entity, its officers, officials, employees, and volunteers are to be covered as additional

with such work or operations and automobiles owned, leased, hired, or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the Contractor. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an endorsement to

buildings, structures, processes, machinery and equipment.  The Property Installation Floater shall

impaired, broken, or destroyed during the performance of the Work, including during transit,

If any coverage required is written on a claims-made coverage form: 
1. The retroactive date must be shown, and this date must be before the execution date of the 
contract or the beginning of contract work.
2. Insurance must be maintained and evidence of insurance must be provided for at least five (5) 
years after completion of contract work. 
3. If coverage is canceled or non-renewed, and not replaced with another claims-made policy form 
with a retroactive date prior to the contract effective, or start of work date, the Contractor must 
purchase extended reporting period coverage for a minimum of five (5) years after completion 
of contract work. 
4. A copy of the claims reporting requirements must be submitted to the Entity for review. 
5. If the services involve lead-based paint or asbestos identification/remediation, the Contractors 
Pollution Liability policy shall not contain lead-based paint or asbestos exclusions. If the services 
involve mold identification/remediation, the Contractors Pollution Liability policy shall not 
contain a mold exclusion, and the definition of Pollution shall include microbial matter, including 
mold.

Best rating of no less than A: VII, unless otherwise acceptable to the Entity.

Contractor hereby agrees to waive rights of subrogation which any insurer of Contractor may acquire 
from Contractor by virtue of the payment of any loss. Contractor agrees to obtain any endorsement
