BOARD OF SUPERVISORS, ACTING AS THE GOVERNING BOARD OF THE AREA 
AGENCY ON AGING FOR THE COUNTY OF SAN MATEO, STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
*  *  * * * * 
RESOLUTION AUTHORIZING: A) THE SAN MATEO COUNTY HEALTH CHIEF OR 
THE CHIEF’S DESIGNEE TO ACCEPT OLDER AMERICANS ACT FUNDS FROM 
THE CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF AGING FOR PROGRAMS AND SERVICES 
UNDER TITLE III AND TITLE VII; AND B) THE SAN MATEO COUNTY HEALTH 
CHIEF OR THE CHIEF’S DESIGNEE TO EXECUTE OLDER AMERICANS ACT 
COMMUNITY PROVIDER AGREEMENTS, DURING OR FOR THE TERM OF JULY 1, 
2025 THROUGH JUNE 30, 2026, IN A TOTAL AMOUNT NOT TO EXCEED $5,561,829 
RESOLVED, by the Board of Supervisors of the County of San Mateo, State of 
California acting as the Governing Board of the Area Agency on Aging for the County of 
San Mateo, that 
WHEREAS, this Board has appointed the County’s Aging and Disability Services 
(ADS) division as the Area Agency on Aging (AAA) of San Mateo County to provide 
services pursuant to the Older Americans Act (OAA); and 
WHEREAS, on May 7, 2024, under Resolution No. 080316, this Board, through 
the AAA, executed an MOU (#AAA-2425-08) with the California Department of Aging (CDA) 
for the term of July 1, 2024, through June 30, 2029, to administer OAA programs for San 
Mateo County; and 
WHEREAS, CDA will present to the County for its consideration and acceptance 
a Funding Request Memo (FRM 2526-03) to provide funds for OAA Title III and VII 
programs, and ADS will work with community contractors to provide these programs 
during, or for the term of, July 1, 2025 through June 30, 2026; and 
WHEREAS, in February 2022, ADS conducted a Request for Proposals for the 
provision of OAA services to older adults and adults with disabilities for the term July 1, 
2022, through June 30, 2026, and 21 providers were selected.

SUBJECT:
Central Park Phase 1 Playground Improvements Project – Agreement Amendment No. 3
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Amendment No. 3 to the agreement with RRM Design Group (RRM) to extend the term of the agreement from 
December 31, 2025 to December 31, 2026.
BACKGROUND:
On March 29, 2022, the City entered into an agreement with RRM for the preparation of final construction documents, 
bidding assistance, and construction administration services for the Central Park Phase 1 Playground Improvements 
Project (No. 610027). Under Section 4 – Term, the services to be performed under this agreement commenced on April 1, 
2022, with a contract expiration date of December 31, 2025.
 
On June 5, 2024, Amendment No. 1 was made to the agreement with RRM to provide additional construction 
administration services at no cost for the Fallen Heroes Memorial project, a sub-project within the Phase 1 Playground 
Improvements Project. 
 
On December 9, 2024, City Council approved Amendment No. 2 to the agreement with RRM for additional scope of 
services to reduce the cost of the original playground design, to provide revised construction documents and to negotiate 
a lower contract price with the low bidder.
 
On March 3, 2025, City Council awarded a negotiated construction contract to Suarez & Munoz Construction, Inc., (SMC) in 
the amount of $8,485,784.
 
The proposed Amendment No. 3 to RRM’s agreement will extend the expiration date  from December 31, 2025 to 
December 31, 2026, to provide uninterrupted construction support services from RRM for the duration of the construction 
phase of the project.
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no budget impact for Agreement Amendment No. 3. There are sufficient funds allocated to complete the Central 
Park Phase 1 Playground Improvements Project.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
601 of 983

--- page 602 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
This agreement amendment is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that 
will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).) In addition, 
an Initial Study and Mitigated Negative Declaration (IS/MND) was prepared for the Central Park Phase 1 Playground 
Improvements Project. The IS/MND concluded that the project would not have any significant effects with the 
incorporation of mitigation measures that have been included in the project’s Conditions of Approval to ensure 
compliance during project implementation.
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

SUBJECT:
Climate Adaptation Plan for Sea Level Rise – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with Environmental Science Associates (ESA) for the development of a Climate Adaptation Plan for 
Sea Level Rise in the amount of $420,000 for a term from August 1, 2025, through August 1, 2028; and authorize the City 
Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
In January 2024, the City applied for grant funding through the California Department of Transportation (Caltrans) 
Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant Program for the development of a Climate Adaptation Plan for Sea Level Rise. 
The development of a Climate Adaptation Plan will build on regional efforts such as the County of San Mateo Sea Level 
Rise Vulnerability Assessment to produce a planning document specific to the City of San Mateo. The Climate Adaptation 
Plan will include an existing conditions assessment and technical analysis to develop criteria for evaluating vulnerable 
areas, hydrologic and hydraulic modeling to establish minimum elevations required to address projected sea level rise in 
2050 and 2100 and other technical analysis. The Climate Adaptation Plan will result in a prioritized list of facilities and 
infrastructure which may require site-specific adaptation strategies and will include funding and financing analysis that are 
intended to provide next steps toward implementation. 
In 2023, the California State Legislature passed SB 272 (Laird), Sea level rise: planning and adaptation. SB 272 addressed 
the need for coordinated standardized sea level rise adaptation by requiring local governments along the San Francisco 
Bay shoreline to develop Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plans. Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plans are required to 
meet the Guidelines established by the Bay Conservation and Development Commission (BCDC) Regional Shoreline 
Adaptation Plan (RSAP), ensuring effective and collaborative regional responses. Local governments that receive approval 
for their sea level rise plan are then prioritized for funding for the implementation of sea level rise adaptation strategies 
and recommended projects in approved plans. 
In July 2024, the City of San Mateo received a Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant Program conditional grant award 
letter from Caltrans. In September 2024, City Council adopted a Resolution identifying approval authority for the 
agreements between the City of San Mateo and Caltrans, in compliance with the Caltrans Sustainable Transportation 
Planning Grant Program requirement. 
On February 3, 2025, City Council adopted a resolution accepting grant funds in the amount of $360,500 from the 
California Department of Transportation (Caltrans) for the development of a Climate Adaptation Plan for Sea Level Rise 
and appropriating the grant funds to the Climate Adaptation Plan project and authorized the City Manager to execute the 
Restricted Grant Agreement with Caltrans. 
605 of 983

--- page 606 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
On March 21, 2025, staff released a Request for Proposals (RFP) to select a technical consultant to work with City staff to 
develop the Climate Adaptation Plan for Sea Level Rise that would meet Caltrans grant requirements and address BCDC 
RSAP requirements. The RFP closed on April 25, 2025 and five firms submitted proposals. 
Environmental Science Associates (ESA) was selected because of their local and regional experience working on sea level 
rise in the Bay Area, their thoughtful and customized approach to meeting both BCDC RSAP guidelines and Caltrans RGA 
requirements, and the ESA team’s qualifications. The project will kick off with Existing Conditions analysis, followed by 
vulnerability assessment and evaluation of adaptation strategies and pathways. Equitable outreach and community 
engagement is planned throughout the duration of the project. Based on the above, staff recommends City Council 
approve an agreement with Environmental Science Associates (ESA) for the development of a Climate Adaptation Plan for 
Sea Level Rise in the amount of $420,000 and authorize the City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the 
form presented.
BUDGET IMPACT:
On February 3, 2025, Caltrans Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant funds in the amount of $360,500 were 
appropriated to the Climate Adaptation Plan Project (46C005). Acceptance of the grant funding requires a local match of 
11.47 percent, or approximately $41,350. The project has sufficient funding to provide the local match and cover the 
anticipated project costs that may exceed the grant amount.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical 
change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.) CEQA review will be included as part of the Climate 
Adaptation Plan. 
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

SECTION 15 - WAIVERS

Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such 
term, covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of

other money which may become due hereunder shall not be deemed to be a waiver of any preceding 
breach or violation by the other party of any term, covenant, or condition of this Agreement or of any 
applicable law or ordinance. 
 
SECTION 16 - SEVERABILITY 
 
 
If any term or portion of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or otherwise 
unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall 
continue in full force and effect.

SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES 
 
 
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement. 
 
SECTION 18 - NON-DISCRIMINATION 
 
 
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONSULTANT nor any of its 
subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act. 
 
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION 
 
 
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw. 
 
 
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties. 
 
SECTION 20 - LITIGATION 
 
 
CONSULTANT shall testify at CITY'S request if litigation is brought against CITY in connection 
with CONSULTANT's services under this Agreement.  Unless the action is brought by CONSULTANT, or 
is based upon CONSULTANT's wrongdoing, CITY shall compensate CONSULTANT for preparation for 
testimony, testimony, and travel at CONSULTANT's standard hourly rates at the time of actual 
testimony.

EXHIBIT A 
 
SCOPE OF SERVICES 
 
ESA will prepare a Climate Adaptation Plan for Sea Level Rise consistent with the City’s Caltrans Restricted Grant 
Agreement (RGA)1 and San Francisco Bay Conservation and Development Commission (BCDC) Regional Shoreline 
Adaptation Plan (RSAP) Guidelines2. ESA will address the requirements and tasks of both the Caltrans RGA and 
the BCDC RSAP with an efficient and comprehensive approach. As described in detail in the Scope of Work 
below, ESA will draft Elements A through G of a Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plan as described by BCDC’s 
RSAP Guidelines. 
Task 1. Existing Conditions 
ESA will start the Project by developing a Project Management Plan, including draft content designed to address 
RSAP Element A. ESA will present the draft Project Management Plan at a kick-off meeting with City of San 
Mateo and ESA’s key personnel and the City and ESA will also walk through the contract, compensation and 
financial requirements, contract compliance, general terms and conditions, and the schedule. ESA will also 
facilitate one (1) virtual project kickoff meeting with the Technical Advisory Committee, discussed in more detail 
in Task 4, to review the scope of work, discuss big-picture goals, and define roles and responsibilities. 
 
Consistent with BCDC’s Element A requirements, the Project Management Plan will include a description of the 
plan type, planning project team, a description of efforts that will be taken to include diverse perspectives on 
the planning team, a description of how language services will be included in the planning effort, a list of 
affected and interested parties, a description of tribal consultation, and a description of multijurisdictional and 
county coordination. The plan will include a description of equitable outreach and engagement efforts and how 
the engagement process will include people from vulnerable communities, as well as Tribes. The plan will also 
include a map showing the boundaries of the planning area and a map showing Environmental Justice 
Communities, socially vulnerable communities and Tribal communities, as applicable to the City. 
 
BCDC’s RSAP requires that prior to initiating the process to prepare a Subregional Plan, a Notice of Intent to 
prepare a Subregional Plan must be submitted electronically to BCDC. The notice must include a detailed 
description of the process including local government staff contact, project scope, schedule, plan for the 
preparation of any necessary environmental documentation (i.e., compliance with the California Environmental 
Quality Act), an engagement plan describing the public process, and the anticipated timing of the two required 
consultations with BCDC staff. ESA will attend the preliminary consultation meeting with the City at which BCDC 
will advise the City on whether information contained in the notice aligns with the RSAP. After the meeting, 
BCDC will post notice on the agency’s website notifying the public that the City intends to initiate a process to 
prepare a Subregional Plan and the City will also post a notice notifying the public that the City intends to initiate 
the process of preparing a Subregional Plan. 
 
Regular coordination meetings are not assumed within this Project scope, but the ESA Project Management 
Team will coordinate with City staff by phone, email, or focused virtual meetings as needed to facilitate 
continuous progress. 
 
Under Task 1, the ESA team will also review relevant existing resources to understand the current state of SLR 
planning on the City of San Mateo shoreline. The review will consider existing and available documents to be 
provided by the City or otherwise readily available public information, including:

many of the assets and data layers that are required to be analyzed by BCDC’s RSAP in the Subregional 
Shoreline Adaptation Plans; 
•

for the San Mateo operational landscape units that includes the City’s shoreline;

City of San Mateo; and 
• 
Any additional available general and land use plans, hazard and emergency plans, climate and resilience 
plans pertaining to the City.

Conservation and Development Commission (BCDC)’s Regional Shoreline Adaptation Plan (RSAP).

and access plans, shoreline management plans, ecological management plans, economic development 
plans, hazard or contaminated sites plans, utility plans, and/or other similar applicable plans for relevant 
information.

BCDC’s RSAP Mapping Platform (expected to be released in 2025) and from FEMA (e.g., Letter of Map 
Revision Document8) which will capture additional fluvial flooding hazards.

Task 1 Assumptions: 
• 
This task will address BCDC’s RSAP Element A – Planning Process and Element B – Existing Conditions. 
However, the effort required to satisfy BCDC’s requirements cannot be determined with precision, as 
BCDC has not yet reviewed or approved a Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plan and compliance 
determinations are subjective and at BCDC staff’s discretion. Effort estimated for this task reflects ESA’s 
professional opinion, based upon past experience with similar projects. ESA will support the City in 
gaining BCDC approval to the best of ESA’s ability. However, ESA cannot guarantee successful 
procurement of BCDC approval of Element A and B, or the Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plan within 
a desired timeframe. Should BCDC require additional information or rounds of review that are not 
identified in this scope, additional budget may be required to complete the work described in this task.  
• 
ESA will attend one (1) kickoff meeting with City staff and one (1) in-person site visit with City staff and 
stakeholders. 
• 
ESA will participate virtually in the Preliminary Consultation meeting with BCDC. 
• 
ESA will provide a description of tribal consultation, but will not conduct the tribal consultation.  
• 
City staff will post the Notice of Intent on their respective websites and/or on the project website. City 
staff will submit the Final Notice of Intent to prepare a Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plan to BCDC. 
• 
Revisions to the project after initiation of analysis may cause schedule delays and require additional 
budget. 
• 
City staff will consolidate and deconflict all comments before sending to ESA for incorporation into the 
Draft Existing Conditions Report. ESA assumes one round of City staff review and comments for ESA to 
incorporate into the Final Existing Conditions Report.  
• 
BCDC’s RSAP Mapping Platform is expected to be available in September 2025. Delays in the completion 
of the platform may impact the completion of Task 1 and subsequent tasks. 
• 
Unless otherwise specified, all deliverables will be submitted electronically (MS Word and PDF versions). 
• 
The City shall arrange for access to and make all provisions for ESA to enter upon public and private 
property as required for ESA to perform its services. 
Task 2. Analysis 
Under Task 2, the ESA team will work with City staff to develop Elements C through G of the Subregional Plan. 
Task 2 will rely upon existing conditions information collected in Task 1 as described above. 
Task 2-1. Element C – Vulnerability Assessment 
Once the data and studies have been gathered and reviewed, the ESA Team will compile the inventory of 
existing assets and group the assets into broad categories that will facilitate the SLR risk vulnerability assessment 
and development of adaptation solutions in a simplified manner (e.g., transportation, water and wastewater, 
habitats, recreational, residential and commercial, etc.). Then ESA will conduct hazards mapping and asset 
exposure predictions, with the goal of gaining comprehensive insights into the vulnerability of shoreline assets 
617 of 983

--- page 618 ---
Professional Services  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Page 12 of 27 
Rev 12/23 
and resources to SLR, including their exposure, sensitivity, and adaptive capacity, and to identify threatened 
community assets, including habitats, across different SLR scenarios. 
 
The asset exposure predictions will detail key assets with high exposure to rising sea levels, storm surges, and 
flooding, focusing on natural areas and habitats, critical infrastructure that supports essential services (e.g., 
transportation, wastewater treatment, energy), as well as residential areas and communities with heightened 
social vulnerability, particularly disadvantaged and underserved populations. Because social factors influence a 
community’s adaptive capacity or ability to prevent or recover from a climate change−related event, ESA will 
also assess social determinants of health within the City. These determinants, such as race and ethnicity, age, 
gender, economic stability (e.g., cost of living, access to living wage), education level, housing and transportation 
options, and physical and economic access to critical services, will be evaluated by leveraging existing data such 
as BCDC’s environmental justice and social equity datasets, CalEnviroScreen 4.0 and the Centers for Disease 
Control and Prevention’s Social Vulnerability Index.  
 
Additionally, ESA will consider the vulnerability of critical infrastructure, such as the existing North Shoreview 
and Bayfront levees, US Route 101, CA Route 92, Caltrain stations and railroad tracks, Seal Point Park and its 
underlying landfill, and water and wastewater infrastructure, both with and without existing protective 
measures. Geosyntec’s geotechnical engineers will assist with seepage, slope stability, and settlement 
assessments of the levees and landfill. Paradigm will focus its assessments on surface water, including the City’s 
stormwater system and San Mateo Creek. Finally, ESA will analyze risks for key habitat areas in the City, 
including Coyote Point Recreation Area and Mariner’s Point Marsh. 
 
ESA will complete the following as part of the coastal hazards mapping and asset exposure predictions: 
• 
Analyze potential impacts of various SLR projections, as required by BCDC’s RSAP, including:  
o 2050 (0.8 ft); 
o 2100 Intermediate (3.1 ft); 
o 2100 Intermediate-High (4.9 ft); and 
o 2100 High (6.6 ft); 
• 
Assessment and description of shoreline flood risk conditions, including the likely timing of exposure of 
assets as a function of SLR projections; 
• 
Prioritization of risks based on the following criteria: 
o Exposure to the 0.8 ft scenario; 
o Any applicable BCDC RSAP Strategic Regional Priorities; 
o Vulnerable communities;  
o Baylands habitats; and 
o Locally identified significant high priority populations, assets and services; 
• 
Identification of highest priority areas / assets; and 
• 
Evaluation and summary of vulnerability using relative scoring that will be assigned to exposure, 
sensitivity, and adaptive capacity of assets and the consequences of exposure in highest priority areas. 
 
As described by BCDC’s RSAP Guidelines, the vulnerability assessment will include the maps and summaries of 
the exposure of assets to SLR projections for 2050 (0.8 ft) and 2100 Intermediate (3.1) and 2100 Intermediate-
High (4.9 ft) and 2100 High (6.6 ft) that will be developed as a part of this task. After completing the hazards 
mapping, ESA will conduct the SLR risk vulnerability assessment by using a combination of GIS tools and scripts 
and engineering assessment and judgment, including asset prioritization and the primary failure pathways for 
key facilities. Geosyntec groundwater experts will collaborate with ESA to develop an understanding of local 
618 of 983

--- page 619 ---
Professional Services  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Page 13 of 27 
Rev 12/23 
groundwater conditions for the City. This assessment will refer to regional existing and projected depth-to-
groundwater mapping9,10.  
 
This scope of work assumes the use of existing modeling and tools/framework and risk assessment 
methodologies to analyze the potential impacts of the SLR scenarios. ESA will strive to leverage the best 
available data, tools, and science to complete the coastal hazards mapping. As part of Task 2-1, ESA will also 
identify additional technical tasks or studies that to recommend completing to better facilitate SLR vulnerability 
and resilience planning as part of the recommended future projects (Element G).  
 
Central to ESA’s assessment of risk is an understanding of the vulnerability of each asset type. Where 
information exists and budget allows, this approach may entail translating hazard exposure into potential 
damages or direct impacts by establishing clear relationships between hazard likelihood, severity, and 
consequences across varying levels of impact. Where possible, the focus will be on generating consistent risk 
metrics that facilitate the comparison of risks across different hazards and asset types, enabling effective ranking 
and prioritization.  
 
As required by BCDC and SB 272, the Vulnerability Assessment (VA; Element C) will also include a high-level 
assessment of the potential cost of inaction. Previous studies by the Metropolitan Transportation Commission 
(MTC) have analyzed the potential cost of inaction for the San Francisco Bay region as exceeding $230 billion. 
These estimates will be tailored to the City of San Mateo.11 MTC estimates that the potential cost of regional SLR 
adaptation through 2050 could exceed $110 billion and the cost of SLR adaptation for San Mateo County was 
estimated at $11 billion. 
 
In summary, the SLR risk vulnerability assessment will include the following: 
• 
Hazard maps 
• 
Asset exposure predictions 
• 
High-level analysis of the potential cost of damages from inaction 
 
The draft VA will be shared with the City and the Technical Advisory Committee (TAC) for review and ESA will 
incorporate feedback. Then ESA will present the results of VA at the first community meeting, as described 
below in Task 3.  
Task 2-2: Element D – Adaptation Strategies and Pathways 
Following the completion of the draft VA, ESA will analyze potential adaptation solutions to address SLR and 
flooding risks for 0.8 ft and 3.1 ft SLR scenarios for the priority areas that were identified in the VA. Under this 
Task, ESA will help the City to frame its local vision and goals for the shoreline. This step will focus on identifying 
a suite of solutions that span green (nature-based), green-gray (hybrid), and gray (traditional engineering) 
infrastructure alternatives. The objective is to create a balanced approach to resilience that combines ecological 
benefits with effective flood protection. Green infrastructure solutions may include nature-based strategies like 
wetland restoration, floodplain reconnection, eelgrass beds, oyster reefs, and vegetated buffers. These nature-
based solutions create aquatic habitat as well as attenuate waves, support biodiversity, and improve water 
quality. ESA will also review the findings of SFEI’s Adaptation Atlas (2019), which indicates that tidal marsh 
restoration, polder management, ecotone levees, and reconnecting creeks to baylands may be appropriate 
strategies for certain locations on the City’s shoreline. Green-gray solutions will also be considered, including 
hybrid approaches that blend natural and engineered features, such as green stormwater management, living 
shorelines and reinforced levees with ecosystem enhancements (such as seaweed and cobble blankets), to 
combine flood resilience with environmental and social benefits. Gray solutions will be considered, including 
 
9 https://www.sfei.org/projects/shallow-groundwater-response-sea-level-rise 
10 https://ourcoastourfuture.org/hazard-map/ 
11 https://mtc.ca.gov/sites/default/files/documents/2023-07/SLR_Framework_Final_Report.pdf 
619 of 983

--- page 620 ---
Professional Services  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Page 14 of 27 
Rev 12/23 
traditional engineered methods, such as seawalls, levees, bulkheads, revetments and drainage systems, 
especially where nature-based solutions are less feasible or practical. Finally, ESA will explore potential policy 
and regulatory measures, described in more detail in Task 2-3: Element E below. 
 
The Adaptation Strategies and Pathways will include an evaluation of adaptation alternatives to determine 
which are the most effective strategies for the City. For each adaptation solution, ESA will assess physical 
feasibility, using existing resources such as SFEI’s Adaptation Atlas; high-level construction, maintenance and 
operational costs, using existing cost-estimating resources such as MTC’s SLR Adaptation Funding and 
Investment Framework Final Report; potential environmental impacts; and social acceptance, to be determined 
via community outreach; as well as alignment with BCDC RSAP’s Adaptation Strategy standards and the San 
Francisco Bay Plan. Structural and non-structural solutions that offer co-benefits—such as improved public 
access to green spaces, enhanced water quality, or biodiversity support—will be prioritized to maximize value 
for the community. The evaluation criteria will include a relative scoring system that allows the potential 
solutions for the 0.8 ft SLR scenario to be compared to each other. The Adaptation Strategies and Pathways will 
include maps of the conceptual designs, descriptions of the strategies and a description of potential adaptation 
pathways over time, as described in BCDC’s RSAP Guidelines. 
 
The Adaptation Strategies and Pathways will be developed with input from the local community, to reflect the 
needs and priorities of the people and ecosystems most affected by SLR. ESA will propose place-based 
adaptation solutions tailored to high-risk areas identified in the vulnerability assessment. These solutions will 
emphasize nature-based and hybrid infrastructure approaches, which offer resilience benefits while enhancing 
ecosystem health and public access. Community engagement will play a key role, with feedback at the second 
public outreach meeting (Task 3) from local residents, businesses, and other interested parties informing the 
selection and prioritization of adaptation measures. Finally, ESA recommends that one of the two required BCDC 
consultations should occur after the VA (Task 2-1) and adaptation strategies and pathways (Task 2-2) are drafted 
to check on progress and compliance with BCDC’s RSAP guidelines. 
Task 2-3: Element E – Land Use and Policy Plan 
For the Land Use and Policy Plan (Element E), ESA will describe potential land use and policy changes that may 
be necessary to enact the adaptation strategies and pathways that have been identified, particularly those sea 
level rise actions that are already described in the City of San Mateo’s General Plan 2040 and OneShoreline’s 
guidance, such as sea level rise and flood hazard monitoring; developing a community rating system and early 
flood warning system; developing a sea level and/or flood overlay zone; floodplain ordinance update; and 
creating a fee for new development to fund SLR and flood protection measures, such as a climate resiliency 
district. Additional non-structural solutions could include shoreline and creek setbacks or buffers; building codes 
and building retrofits; rebuilding and redevelopment restrictions; as well as other financial measures, such as 
conservation easements; tax incentives; special assessments; Geologic Hazard Abatement Districts (GHAD); 
transfer of development rights; OneShoreline’s / City of Burlingame’s sea level rise zoning code and/or buyouts.  
Task 2-4: Element F – Implementation Plan and Funding Strategy 
For the Implementation Plan and Funding Strategy (Element F), ESA will outline potential high-level costs for the 
selected adaptation strategies and potential funding and financing pathways, including public grants, private 
partnerships, and other financial mechanisms, as well as implementation leads or responsible entities. ESA has 
developed unit-costing methods to inform several cities’ shoreline planning efforts, which can be efficiently 
applied to this plan.  
 
ESA will specifically explore the high-level feasibility of establishing a Climate Resiliency District to provide 
sustainable, long-term funding for implementation and maintenance of the adaptation strategies. The City’s 
experience with the South Bayfront Flood Control Facilities Assessment District and the North Shoreview 
Assessment District can serve as templates for funding future sea level adaptations. To support the ongoing 
620 of 983

--- page 621 ---
Professional Services  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Page 15 of 27 
Rev 12/23 
effectiveness of these solutions, the action plan will include a strategy for monitoring and adaptive management 
that will allow for regular assessment and adjustments based on changing conditions. The monitoring program 
will also describe triggers, thresholds and/or decision points for adaptation pathways and responsible entities 
for monitoring. The implementation plan will also include a timeline and strategy for updating the plan as best-
available science and conditions change.  
Task 2-5: Element G – Project List 
As required by BCDC, ESA will also prepare a high-level list of adaptation projects (Element G) for consideration 
for funding prioritization and for inclusion in regionwide databases if the strategies are advanced enough to 
generate a list.  
 
Task 2 Deliverables  
• 
GIS Data Inventory  
• 
Hazards map, showing BCDC RSAP-required assets and SLR scenarios 
• 
Asset exposure predictions  
• 
Additional Data Analysis Memo, if data gaps are identified 
• 
Draft and Final Vulnerability Assessment Report (Element C) 
• 
Draft and Final Adaptation Strategies and Pathways (Element D) 
• 
Draft and Final Land Use and Policy Plan (Element E) 
• 
Draft and Final Implementation Plan and Funding Strategy (Element F) 
• 
Draft and Final Project List (Element G) 
• 
Draft and Final Technical Memorandum of Analyses, compiling Elements C-G, as required by the Caltrans 
grant 
 
Task 2 Assumptions  
• 
City staff and other partners will provide files, documents, surveys, maps, geospatial information/data, 
and other information not otherwise publicly available but on file with those agencies.  
• 
If GIS data for particular assets are not readily available, ESA will document the missing data in a brief 
data gaps memo. For historic, cultural and tribal cultural resources, ESA specifically will not prepare a 
cultural resources technical report but instead will rely on existing GIS data sources. 
• 
BCDC’s RSAP mapping platform was expected to be available in June 2025. Delays in the completion of 
the platform may impact the completion of Task 2 and subsequent tasks. 
• 
The coastal hazards maps will leverage existing data and may be augmented with other information and 
methods within the available budget. In the event that new data is released after completion of the task, 
our team could update the maps and analyses for an additional cost. 
• 
City staff will provide input on the selection of the SLR scenarios. 
• 
ESA will participate virtually in the BCDC consultation meeting. 
• 
Revisions to the project after initiation of analysis may cause schedule delays and require additional 
budget. 
• 
This task will address BCDC’s RSAP Elements C through G. However, the effort required to satisfy BCDC’s 
requirements cannot be determined with precision, as BCDC has not yet reviewed or approved a 
Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plan and compliance determinations are subjective and at BCDC 
staff’s discretion. Effort estimated for this task reflects ESA’s professional opinion, based upon past 
experience with similar projects. ESA will support the City in gaining BCDC approval to the best of our 
ability. However, ESA cannot guarantee successful procurement of BCDC approval of Elements C 
through G or the Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plan within a desired timeframe. Should BCDC 
require additional information or rounds of review that are not identified in this scope, additional 
budget may be required to complete the work described in this task.

• 
City staff will consolidate and deconflict all comments before sending to ESA for incorporation into the

Task 3. Public Outreach 
The ESA team will draft an Equitable Outreach and Engagement Plan as described in the RSAP Guidelines (A4) 
and assist the City with the establishment of an interested parties list and coordination of community meetings 
and information exchanges. The types of interested parties may include representatives from environmental 
advocacy groups, land trusts, and non-profits, recreational users of the San Mateo shoreline, as well as local 
residents and other interested parties, particularly from vulnerable communities. This task will include a 
minimum of three (3) community meetings, with potential meeting topics listed below.  
 
1. Existing Conditions (Task 1/Element B), Vulnerability Analysis (Task 2/Element C) & Draft Local 
Adaptation Vision (Task 2/Element D) 
2. Adaptation Strategies & Pathways (Task 2/Element D) 
3. Draft Plan, including land use and policy options, implementation plan and funding and the draft project 
list (Task 5/Elements E, F, & G) 
 
This task will also include one virtual presentation to the Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission and one 
virtual presentation to the City Council.  
 
ESA’s approach and recommended engagement techniques will take into consideration BCDC’s Environmental 
Justice and Tribal Consultation policies. This process will be designed to continue to cultivate existing 
relationships and grow new relationships through established community networks in the diverse communities 
along the City’s shoreline. To foster those discussions, the ESA team will coordinate with City staff to distribute 
relevant outreach materials for interested parties or “ad hoc advocates” to take back to their organizations and 
agencies in other meetings, thereby providing an additional method of public outreach from trusted community 
members. As a part of this task, ESA also proposes to leverage the City’s website to create project materials that 
can be added to the City’s project webpage for ongoing documentation and sharing. ESA will work with the City 
to determine the content of the webpage.  
 
Task 3 Deliverables  
• 
Draft and final Equitable Outreach and Engagement Plan 
• 
Interested party contact list 
• Two (2) in-person and one (1) virtual community meetings, with the following items for each 
workshop/session: 
o Presentation materials and handouts (e.g., PowerPoint slides, maps) 
o Meeting Agendas 
o Sign-in sheets 
o Community outreach flyer design 
• Documentation of community meeting outcomes 
• One (1) draft and final presentation to the Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission 
• One (1) draft and final presentation to the City Council 
 
Task 3 Assumptions:

by being available to present and/or answer questions.  
Task 4. Technical Advisory Committee (TAC) Meetings 
The ESA team will assist the City in convening a TAC comprised of staff from relevant agencies, which may 
include: 
 
• 
Local Agencies and Special Districts, including Cities of Burlingame, Foster City, and Belmont; San Mateo 
County Parks Department and OneShoreline; and the San Francisco Airport;  
• 
State Agencies, including BCDC, Caltrans, State Parks, California Department of Fish and Wildlife and the 
Regional Water Quality Control Board; 
• 
Federal Agencies, including U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and US Fish and Wildlife Service;  
• 
Native American Tribes to the extent that they are willing and interested in participating; 
• 
Environmental advocacy groups, land trusts, and non-profits; and 
• 
Additional technical experts. 
 
ESA will facilitate three (3) virtual TAC meetings during the Project. The TAC will review and provide direction for 
the project as precursor to the public outreach meetings (Task 3), as identified in the TAC Coordination Plan. In 
between TAC meetings, ESA can share project updates via email, the project website or other communication 
methods. 
 
TAC meeting topics could include: 
1. Discussion of the draft Existing Conditions and Vulnerability Assessment; 
2. Discussion of the draft Adaptation Strategies and Pathways; and 
3. Discussion of the draft Climate Adaptation Plan. 
 
Deliverables 
• 
TAC Coordination Plan 
• 
Preparation of three (3) virtual TAC meeting agendas, presentations and meeting minutes 
 
Assumptions 
• The ESA team will facilitate and attend three (3) virtual TAC meetings. Additional TAC meetings would be 
optional tasks for a separate cost. 
• 
Unless otherwise specified, all deliverables will be submitted electronically (MS Word and PDF versions). 
Task 5: Draft and Final Climate Adaptation Plan 
Building on the work of prior tasks, ESA will prepare the Draft and Final Climate Adaptation Plans that will 
provide a strategic roadmap for implementing the proposed adaptation solutions. The Final Plan will address the 
requirements of BCDC’s RSAP Guidelines for Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plans and will serve as the 
roadmap for the City’s future SLR adaptation and can be used to support future funding applications for 
planning, design, and implementation of SLR adaptation projects. The Plan will include the following sections 
and will address the Equity Assessment Standard, as required by BCDC’s RSAP: 
623 of 983

--- page 624 ---
Professional Services  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Page 18 of 27 
Rev 12/23 
 
• 
Element A: Planning Process (Task 1) 
o A1 - List of subregional plan partners including jurisdictions, planning project team 
members and affected parties, as well as tribal consultation. 
o A2 – Map of the Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plan area based on the 6.6 ft SLR 
scenario 
o A3 – Description of the multijurisdictional coordination process 
o A4 – Summary of equitable engagement efforts throughout the planning process, 
including vulnerable community identification and equitable outreach and engagement 
• 
Element B: Existing Conditions (Task 1) 
o B1 – List of existing plans, studies and/or other information that may be relevant to 
addressing and responding to coastal hazards 
o B2 – Map and description of physical and ecological characteristics of the landscape of 
the planning area 
o B3 – Map and description of existing populations, assets, sectors, services and land uses 
within the planning area 
• 
Element C: Vulnerability Assessment (Task 2) 
o C1 – Map and description of the exposure of people, assets, ecosystems and services to 
coastal flood hazards across a range of SLR scenarios 
o C2 – Summary of the vulnerability assessment for priority areas  
• 
Element D: Adaptation Strategies and Pathways (Task 2) 
o D1 - Local vision and goals for the planning area  
o D2 - Adaptation strategy alternatives for priority areas 
o D3 – Evaluation of adaptation alternatives  
o D4 – Conceptual plans and descriptions of selected strategies 
• 
Element E: Land Use and Policy Plan (Task 2) 
o E1 – Description of proposed land use and policy changes necessary to implement 
selected strategies and pathways identified in Element D 
o E2 – Description of policy and programmatic changes necessary to implement selected 
strategies and pathways identified in Element D 
• 
Element F: Implementation Plan and Funding Strategy (Task 2) 
o F1 – Implementation Plan that identifies next steps and responsible entities for 
implementing the selected adaptation strategies and pathways. 
• 
Element G: Project List (Task 2) 
o G1 – Priority Project List  
 
The Administrative Draft Plan will be shared with the City, TAC and BCDC for review and ESA will incorporate one 
round of comments. The Public Review Draft Plan, which will be shared in advance of the third public outreach 
meeting (Task 3). The Final Climate Adaptation Plan will include feedback received on the Public Review Draft. 
ESA suggests that the second of the two required BCDC consultations should occur after the Admin Draft Plan is 
shared with BCDC for their review and before the Public Review Draft is shared with the public and presented at 
the third public meeting.  
 
Deliverables  
• 
Admin Draft, Public Review Draft and Final Climate Adaptation Plan, which will credit Caltrans as the 
funder of this project on the cover page. 
 
Assumptions: 
• 
ESA will participate virtually in the BCDC consultation meeting.

• 
This task will address BCDC’s RSAP requirements for the development of a Subregional Shoreline 
Adaptation Plan (SSAP). However, the effort required to satisfy BCDC’s requirements cannot be 
determined with precision, as BCDC has not yet reviewed or approved a SSAP and compliance 
determinations are subjective and at BCDC staff’s discretion. Effort estimated for this task reflects ESA’s 
professional opinion, based upon past experience with similar projects. ESA will support the City in 
gaining BCDC approval to the best of our ability. However, ESA cannot guarantee successful 
procurement of BCDC approval of the Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plan within a desired 
timeframe. Should BCDC require additional information or rounds of review that are not identified in 
this scope, additional budget may be required to complete the work described in this task.  
• 
City staff will consolidate and deconflict all comments before sending to ESA for incorporation into the 
draft documents. ESA assumes one round of City staff / TAC review and comments for ESA to 
incorporate into Public Review Draft Plan. City staff will distribute draft documents for review. ESA 
assumes city staff will consolidate and deconflict public comments, which ESA will review and then 
incorporate into the Final Plan.  
•

plans and planning efforts. 
• 
As noted above, this scope assumes that ESA will not conduct formal Tribal consultation on behalf of the 
City, but the Tribes will be invited to participate in the process. 
• 
Unless otherwise specified, all deliverables will be submitted electronically (MS Word and PDF versions). 
• 
The City will be responsible for publishing legal notices and providing the costs for such posting. 
• 
This scope assumes no more than 50 individual comment letters will be received for the public review 
draft; however, if additional comments are received whose responses require greater effort than 
estimated herein, additional budget would be needed. 
Task 6: Board Review and Approval  
Once the Draft Climate Adaptation Plan has been prepared with community and TAC and BCDC input has been 
incorporated, ESA will present the Draft Plan to a joint meeting of the Sustainability and Infrastructure 
Commission (SIC) and Planning Commission. Then once SIC and Planning Commission input has been 
incorporated into the Draft Plan, the Final Draft Plan will be presented to the San Mateo City Council, which will 
provide another opportunity for additional public comment that will be incorporated into the  Final Plan. As a 
part of this task, ESA will advise the City on the level of California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) analysis that 
may be needed for the Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plan. 
 
Deliverables 
• 
Draft and Final CEQA and Approvals Strategy 
• 
One (1) draft and final presentation to a joint meeting of the Sustainability and Infrastructure 
Commission and Planning Commission 
• 
One (1) draft and final presentation to the San Mateo City Council for plan acceptance and approval  
 
Assumptions 
• 
ESA staff will attend the hearings virtually to support City staff by being available to present and/or 
answer questions.  
• 
If additional commission, board and/or public comments are received whose responses require revisions 
to the Final Plan, then additional budget would be needed. 
• 
This scope assumes that preparation for the submittal of the Subregional Plan to BCDC or assistance 
with BCDC’s review and/or BCDC’s hearing process would be optional tasks for a separate cost.  
• 
The City will be responsible for publishing legal notices and providing the costs for such posting. 
• 
As a part of Task 6, ESA will advise the City on the level of California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) 
analysis that may be needed for the Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plan, but this scope does not

Estimated Project Schedule  
The goal of the City’s Caltrans grant is to develop a Climate Adaptation Plan for Sea Level Rise. The grant work is divided 
into six (6) tasks. The City is required to submit final grant deliverables to Caltrans by June 30, 2027, it is assumed that City 
staff will work with Caltrans to extend the grant term. This schedule assumes an anticipated award of contract in August 
2025. The ESA team will strive to meet the schedule as outlined below and per grant requirements.

EXHIBIT C 
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS 
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE  
Coverage shall be at least as broad as: 
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal &
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate
limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or
the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if
CONSULTANT has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no
less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily
injury or disease.
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate.
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and 
shall be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT. 
Other Insurance Provisions 
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions: 
Additional Insured Status 
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used). 
Primary Coverage 
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it. 
631 of 983

--- page 632 ---
Professional Services  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Page 26 of 27 
Rev 12/23 
Notice of Cancellation 
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City. 
 
Waiver of Subrogation 
CONSULTANT hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said 
CONSULTANT may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  
CONSULTANT agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of 
subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of 
subrogation endorsement from the insurer.   
 
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions 
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof 
of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within 
the retention.   
 
Acceptability of Insurers 
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City. 
 
Verification of Coverage 
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. 86 (2024)

APPROVAL AUTHORITY FOR AGREEMENTS BETWEEN THE CITY OF SAN MATEO AND THE CALIFORNIA
DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORATION (CALTRANS) FOR THE DEVELOPMENT OF A CLIMATE ADAPTATION PLAN
FOR SEA LEVEL RISE THROUGH THE SUSTAINABLE TRANSPORATION PLANNING GRANT PROGRAM

WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo applied to the California Department of Transportation (Caltrans)
Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant Program, specifically, the Climate Adaptation Planning Grant for the
development of a Climate Adaptation Plan for Sea Level Rise; and

WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo received a conditional award letter for the 2024-25 Sustainable
Transportation Planning Grant; and

WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo is eligible to receive state funding for certain transportation planning
related plans through Caltrans; and

WHEREAS, a Restricted Grant Agreement is needed to be executed with Caltrans before such funds can
be reimbursed through the Caltrans Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant Program; and

WHEREAS, the Caltrans Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant Program requires the adoption of a
resolution identifying and authorizing the Restricted Grant Agreement signee prior to contract finalization; and

WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo wishes to delegate authorization to the City Manager to execute these
agreements and any amendments thereto.

NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES
that:
1. This Council action is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative
activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines
Section 15378(b)(5).)

2. The City Manager is authorized to execute all Restricted Grant Agreements and any amendments
thereto with Caltrans in furtherance of the development of a Climate Adaption Plan for Sea Level Rise
through the Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant Program.

RESOLUTION NO. 86 (2024) adopted by the City Council of the City of San Mateo, California, at a regular meeting
held on September 16, 2024, by the following vote of the City Council:

AYES: Council Members Diaz Nash, Newsom, Loraine, Hedges and Lee
NOES: None
ABSENT: None
ATTEST: Rx
Soni 5 lisa Diam) Nasl
Martin McTaggart, City Clerk Lisa Diaz Nash, Mayor

1.2. Grant Program Objectives and Considerations

Successful grant applications address and articulate how the project relates to the Caltrans
Mission, Grant Program Objectives, and Grant Program Considerations. The Grant Specific
Objectives identified in Chapters 2.1, 3.1, 4.1 and 5.1 indicate the specific purpose of the
Sustainable Communities, Climate Adaptation, and Strategic Partnership transportation planning
grants, respectively, and must also be considered when preparing an application.

Grant Program Objectives

The following Grant Program Objectives are focused on achieving the Caltrans Mission and are
intended to inform application development, including:

Sustainability

Preservation

Accessibility
Safety

Innovation

Economy

Social Equity

Promote reliable and efficient mobility for people, goods, and services, while
meeting the State’s GHG emission reduction goals, preserving the State's natural
and working lands, and preserving the unique character and livability of California's
communities.

Preserve the transportation system through protecting and/or enhancing the
environment, promoting energy conservation, improving the quality of life, and/or
promoting consistency between transportation improvements and State and local
planning growth and economic development patterns.

Increase the accessibility of the system and mobility of people, inclusive of older
adults, those with disabilities, and freight.

Prioritize the elimination of fatal and serious injury crashes and enhance safe use of
our roadways through the Safe System approach to realize a vision of zero road
fatalities and serious injuries by 2050.

Promote the use of technology and innovative designs to improve the performance
and social equity of our transportation system and provide sustainable transportation
options.

Support the economic vitality of the area (i.e., enables global competitiveness,
enables increased productivity, improves efficiency, increases economic equity by
enabling robust economic opportunities for individuals with barriers to employment
and for Disadvantaged Business Enterprise (DBE), etc.).

Decrease exposure to local pollution sources, reduce serious injuries and fatalities on
the transportation system, and promote physical activity across the lifespan, inclusive
of those with disabilities, especially through transportation means.

Promote transportation solutions that focus on and prioritize the needs of
underserved communities most affected by poverty, air pollution and climate
change, and promote solutions that integrate community values with transportation
safety and performance while encouraging meaningful public involvement in the
transportation decision making process.

Grant Program Considerations

The Grant Program supports related State and federal mandated initiatives. The Plans and
Programs listed below should be considered in grant application development. Definitions and
links to these resources can be found in Appendix A.

Caltrans Strategic Plan

The Strategic Plan is a declaration of the Caltrans vision and mission, core
values, strategic imperatives, and six goals to deliver a world-class
transportation system for one of the nation’s most populous, innovative, and
diverse states:

Caltran 0-202!
ST TEGIC PLAN

Safety first

Cultivate excellence

Enhance and connect the multimodal transportation network
Strengthen stewardship and drive efficiency

Lead climate action

Advance equity and livability in all communities

VVVVV V

This plan guides and informs the development of the Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant
Program. Competitive applications will articulate how they will assist in achieving the Caltrans
vision and mission, the six strategic goals above.

California Transportation Plan 2050

The California Transportation Plan (CTP) 2050 is a long-range transportation plan, updated every
five years pursuant to state and Federal law, and is statutorily required to be fiscally unconstrained:

> Defines a vision for the future transportation system, establishes
new statewide priorities, and guides Caltrans and partner

California

agencies in implementing its recommendations Transportation

> Identifies strategies and policies that are required to make iii Ney
transparent and transformational transportation decisions [ebay 2025

> Recognizes the unique transportation needs and interests of SAN
urban, suburban, rural, and Tribal communities ‘ez

> Guides transportation decision makers at all levels of wut oe LS

Bic:

government, while emphasizing the importance of shared
responsibility in meeting our transportation needs

The CTP does not contain projects, but policies and strategies required to close the gap between
what the regional transportation plans (RTP) aim to achieve and how much more is required to
meet 2050 goals. Competitive applications will discuss how proposed projects will assist in
achieving the CTP 2050 vision, goals, recommendations, and the modal plans that support it.

Modal Plans that Support the California Transportation Plan

The CTP is the umbrella plan that informs and pulls together the State’s long-range modal plans
listed below, to envision the future system:

Interregional Transportation Strategic Plan
California Freight Mobility Plan

California State Rail Plan

California State Bicycle and Pedestrian Plan
Statewide Transit Strategic Plan

California Aviation System Plan

Attachment III

Page 11 of 115

Title VI and Environmental Justice

Title VI of the U.S. Civil Rights Act prohibits discrimination on the basis of race, color, or national
origin in programs or activities receiving federal financial assistance. A similar prohibition applies to
recipients of state funds under California Government Code section 11135, which prohibits
discrimination on the basis of race, color or national origin, as well as ethnic group identification,
religion, age, sex, sexual orientation, genetic information, or disability.

Caltrans integrates Title VI as well as environmental justice in all activities. In the past, low-income
and minority communities disoroportionately bore many of the negative impacts of transportation
projects, which they continue to feel the consequences from today. It is the goal of environmental
justice to ensure that when transportation decisions are made, low-income and minority
communities have a full opportunity to participate in the decision-making process, and they
receive an equitable distribution of benefits and not a disproportionate share of burdens, which
contribute to poor health outcomes.

Climate Action Plan for Transportation Infrastructure

The Climate Action Plan for Transportation Infrastructure (CAPTI) details
how the state recommends shifting and investing billions of discretionary
transportation dollars annually to aggressively combat and adapt to
climate change while supporting public health, safety and equity. CAPTI
builds on executive orders signed by Governor Gavin Newsom in 2019 and
2020 targeted at reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in
transportation, which account for more than 40 percent of all emissions, to CAPTI 7
reach the state's ambitious climate goals. Competitive grant applications mo
will discuss how proposed projects will assist in implementing the CAPTI
Investment Framework, Guiding Principles, and align with the
Implementation Strategies and Key Actions.

California Climate Adaptation Strategy

The California Climate Adaptation Strategy, mandated by Assembly Bill 1482 (Gordon, 2015), links
the state's existing and planned climate adaptation efforts, showing how they fit together to
achieve California’s six climate resilience priorities. The Strategy is organized around outcome-
based priorities, enabling a coordinated, integrated approach to building climate resilience. The
Strategy shows how the state’s efforts fit together to deliver on six priorities for climate resilience
action in California: Strengthen Protections for Climate Vulnerable Communities; Bolster Public
Health and Safety to Protect Against Increasing Climate Risks; Make Decisions Based on the Best
Available Climate Science; Build a Climate Resilient Economy; Accelerate Nature-Based Climate
Solutions and Strengthen Climate Resilience of Natural Systems; Partner and Collaborate to
Leverage Resources. Competitive grant applications will discuss how proposed projects consider
climate resilience and align with the California Climate Adaptation Strategy.

668 of 983


--- page 669 ---
Master Plan for Aging

The Master Plan for Aging is a blueprint for aging across the lifespan, not
simply a plan for today’s older adults. California's over-60 population Is
projected to diversify and grown faster than any other age group. In
response, the Master Plan calls on all California communities to build a
California for All Ages: for older Californians currently living through the
many different stages of the second half of life; for younger generations
who can expect to live longer lives than their elders; for communities of alll
ages — family, friends, neighbors, coworkers, and caregivers — surrounding
older adults. Competitive grant applications will discuss specific
connections and help achieve the Master Plan’s five goals and twenty-
three strategies to build a California for All Ages by 2030.

City of San Mateo
Agreement Number 74A1675

2. SUSTAINABLE COMMUNITIES COMPETITIVE AND
TECHNICAL

Approximately $12 million in State Road Maintenance and Rehabilitation Account (RMRA) funds
and $5 million in State Highway Account (SHA) funds, or a combined total of $17 million will be
distributed through a competitive program to Metropolitan Planning Organizations (MPOs) with a
sub-applicant(s), Regional Transportation Planning Agencies (RTPAs), cities and counties, transit
agencies, and Native American Tribal Governments. MPOs must have a sub-applicant in order to
be eligible and apply to the Sustainable Communities Competitive and Technical grants.

Approximately $3 million will be set-aside for a technical project sub-category. In accordance with
the recent release of the guidance documents for the implementation of Senate Bill 743 (SB 743,
Chapter 386, Statutes of 2013), there is a current need for improved tools to measure VMT and
induced travel and to develop and implement VMT mitigation programs.

Approximately $500,000 will be set-aside for Native American Tribal Governments.

Funding distribution for the competitive program will depend on the quality and number of
applications.

2.1 Purpose and Specific Objectives

The purpose of the Sustainable Communities grants is to fund local and regional multimodal
transportation and land use planning projects that further the region's RTP SCS/APS (where
applicable), contribute to the State’s GHG reduction targets, and assist in achieving the Caltrans
Mission and Grant Program Objectives, which must be considered when preparing the grant
application.

A minimum threshold of 50 percent of Sustainable Communities Competitive and Technical Grants
has been identified for projects that benefit underserved communities, which includes Native
American Triobal Governments and rural communities (for transportation planning purposes, rural is
defined as all areas of the State that are not included in urbanized areas of 50,000 in population or
greater; refer to Appendix C. Caltrans/Regional Agency Boundaries Map, which indicates rural
areas). For purposes of the grant program, acceptable underserved communities definitions are
described in Appendix A and include:

e Rural communities of 50,000 or less and outside of urbanized areas

e Native American Tribal Governments

e Regionally/locally defined underserved communities

e Atorbelow 80% Assembly Bill 1550 (Gomez, Statutes of 2016)

e Ator above 75% California Department of Education, Free or Reduced Priced Meals Data
e Ator above 75% CalEnviroScreen Version 4.0

e Ator below 25% California Healthy Places Index (HPI)

Sustainable Communities Competitive and Technical applicants must demonstrate how the
project fits every aspect of the following Specific Objectives, as appropriate for the applicant and
project type:

e Encourage local and regional multimodal transportation and land use planning that
furthers the region’s RTP SCS/APS (where applicable). For applicants not in an MPO region,

Sustainable Communities Technical project types do not require extensive public engagement due

e How the public will be involved at later stages of the planning process

expertise to review and validate quantification assumptions and methods

e How stakeholders will be involved in the application and Scope of Work

Technical project applications are scored under the same criteria as all other project types, but
they are grouped with other technical projects, and they compete at the same level. Refer to
Chapter 2.2 for Example Technical Project Types. Applicants should contact their Caltrans District
listed in Appendix D to help decide which grant category is the best fit for their project.

3. SUSTAINABLE COMMUNITIES FORMULA

$12.5 million will be distributed to the MPOs on a formula basis. The formula funds for the MPOs will
reflect the same formula used to distribute Federal Highway Administration (FHWA) Metropolitan
Planning PL funds.

The FHWA PL formula has three components:

1. A base allocation

2. A two-part population component which distributes funds by the proportion of the total
population of each MPO based on California Department of Finance estimates each
January

3. An Air Quality component based on the proportion of federal Congestion Mitigation Air
Quality funds to total programmatic FHWA PL funds

3.1 Purpose and Specific Objectives

Annual Draft Overall Work Program (OWP) Development and Approval Process

Sustainable Communities Formula Grants are part of the annual draft Overall Work Program (OWP)
development and approval process. The draft OWP process includes meaningful consultation with
Caltrans district staff and the Headquarters Office of Regional and Community Planning (ORCP).
MPOs are responsible for including a draft Work Element(s) for Sustainable Communities Formula
Grant funds in the draft FY 2024-24 OWP and sending a list of activities using the provided template
to Caltrans, no later than March 1, 2024. Draft OWPs are submitted to the district Regional Planning
Liaison who will coordinate with ORCP. The draft Work Element(s) should include an explanation of
how the project supports the Sustainable Communities Grant Specific Objectives and provide the
same level of detail included in the grant application Scope of Work and Cost and Schedule for
the Sustainable Communities Competitive Grants. The Work Element name and number must
remain unchanged until the project(s) is completed. If Work Elements do not provide enough
detail, MPOs will need to submit the competitive grant application Scope of Work and Cost and
Schedule. More information and detailed requirements are outlined in the SB 1 Guidance for OWPs
and Requests for Reimbursements, available upon request.

3.3 Example Project Types

MPOs have flexibility for how the Formula Grant allocation is administered. For example, MPOs may
use these funds for a regional competitive grant program, integrated land use and transportation
planning activities related to developing their SCS/APS, carrying out the best practices cited in the
RTP Guidelines, or a combination thereof. If an MPO uses Formula Grant funds to administer a
regional grant program, the MPO must submit their grant program criteria and list of eligible
applicants and sub-applicants to the Caltrans district and ORCP. This step is to ensure the MPO’'s
grant program aligns with the Caltrans Sustainable Communities Competitive Grants, including city
and county housing element compliance. MPOs will also submit a list of awarded grants to the
Caltrans district and ORCP. MPOs should coordinate the submittal of this information with the
Caltrans district and ORCP to avoid delays for releasing the call-for-projects and grant awards. For
additional example project types, refer to Chapter 2.2.

4. CLIMATE ADAPTATION PLANNING

Climate change adaptation aims to anticipate and prepare for climate change impacts to
reduce the damage from climate change and extreme weather events. Adaptation is distinct
from, but complements, climate change mitigation, which aims to reduce GHG emissions. This
funding is intended to advance adaptation planning on California's transportation infrastructure,
including but not limited to roads, railways, bikeways, trails, bridges, ports, and airports.

The State-funded Climate Adaptation Planning grants ($31.9 million) will support tribal, local, and
regional identification of transportation-related climate vulnerabilities through the development of
climate adaptation plans, as well as project-level adaptation planning to identify adaptation
projects and strategies for transportation infrastructure. Applicants at all stages of adaptation
planning efforts including those who have not yet started adaptation work are encouraged to
apply.

Climate adaptation planning projects are defined by:

e = Identification of transportation system vulnerabilities and climate-related risks to existing
transportation infrastructure, including resilience improvement plans.

e = Identification of adaptation planning projects that address climate risk impacts to existing
transportation infrastructure including sea level rise, storm surge, changes in temperature
and precipitation.

e Planning for specific climate projects that can be programmed in existing local or regional
transportation plans.

4.1 Purpose and Specific Objectives

California's climate leadership has made the ambitious commitment to reduce greenhouse gas
emissions and facilitate a transition to a carbon neutral economy. However, as made clear by the
impacts already affecting California and the recent IPCC report, impacts of climate change are
occurring sooner than expected and they disproportionately affect the most vulnerable among us
(See Appendix A. for IPCC report). Many impacts, such as increased wildfires, droughts, landslides,
rising sea levels, floods, severe storms, heat waves, and impacts to wildlife, are already occurring
already and are expected to become more frequent and severe.

Climate change is increasingly impacting the State's transportation system. Adaptation efforts will
enhance the resiliency of the transportation system to better withstand climate impacts. The
overarching goal of this grant program is to support planning actions at local and regional levels
that advance climate change adaptation efforts on the transportation system, especially efforts
that serve the communities most vulnerable to climate change impacts. Taking steps now to
adapt to climate change will protect public health and safety, infrastructure, and the California
economy into the future.

Caltrans awarded three cycles of local adaptation planning grants allocated through the passage
of SB 1. This program operated from FY 2017-18 through FY 2019-20 and was able to fund
innovative tribal, local, and regional adaptation planning grants across California. Examples of the
completed projects supported by the SB 1 Climate Adaptation Planning Grant Program can be
found on the California Adaptation Clearinghouse’s case study4 page. The current funding in this
program aims to build upon the successful SB 1 program to further advance transportation
adaptation planning.

4 Adaptation Clearinghouse’s case study website: https://resilientca.org/challenges/

683 of 983


--- page 684 ---
684 of 983

--- page 685 ---

e Consistency with priorities and goals of the 2021 California State Adaptation Strategy, as
well as any applicable local/regional resilience planning, and that the effort will identify
ways to incorporate transportation-related climate adaptation needs into existing
transportation plans.

e Projects must include a multistakeholder process that provides an opportunity for
meaningful community engagement from communities potentially impacted by any
projects identified or developed as part of the planning grant.

Competitive applicants will demonstrate one or more of the following:

e Alignment with or augmentation of existing plans, including climate action
plans/adaptation plans, hazard mitigation plans, safety elements of general plans,
resilience improvement plans, and/or Certified Local Coastal Programs®, that will lead to
the identification and development of capital projects that can be programmed as part of
local or regional transportation plans. Projects should follow State Guidance on Adaptation
Planning (see Appendix A) or be aligned with local plans.

e Collaboration and partnerships with diverse external stakeholders such as businesses, non-
governmental agencies, community-based organizations, Federal, state, or local agencies,
and community residents.

e Consideration of the adaptation needs of environmental resources in proximity to the
transportation system such as coastal resources including but not limited to tidal marsh or
beaches, wildlife connectivity, wetlands, or fish passage needs.

e Consideration of public access and Complete Streets’ needs.

4.2 Example Project Types

Caltrans aims to fund a variety of project types from a diverse pool of applicants to respond to
these climate impacts. Applicants who have adaptation planning efforts underway, as well as
those who have not yet started adaptation planning, are encouraged to apply.

The examples below are provided fo illustrate the range of projects that may be appropriate for
the grant program, as well as to provide ideas to potential applicants. The project types range
from initial vulnerability assessment and broad planning efforts to project-level planning needs, for
instance through local studies on natural hazards to inform a future project. Proposed projects are
not constrained by the examples below.

e Climate Vulnerability and Risk Assessments (identification of climate vulnerabilities and risks
to transportation infrastructure)

e Planning for extreme weather events, which could include:

o Evacuation planning.

o Identifying communities and individuals with inadequate access to transportation
options (e.g., survey to determine number and location of transit dependent
residents).

o Planning for increased transportation options (e.g., multimodal) in evacuation
corridors.

o Planning for roadway warning systems for extreme weather events.

area can be found on the Coastal Commission's website and further verified by reaching out to
Coastal Commission staff at the applicable Coastal Commission District office.

o Planning and evaluating for decentralized energy storage needs for electric vehicle

Transportation Infrastructure Adaptation and Resilience Improvement Plans, and/or
integration of transportation adaptation planning into existing plans such as a climate
mitigation or adaptation plan, Certified Local Coastal Program, Hazard Mitigation Plan,
General Plan (including compliance with Senate Bill 379 requirements (Jackson, Chapter
608, Statutes of 2015)), or other related planning efforts, including project-level planning for
capital projects. For instance, this can include:

o Prioritization of adaptation needs and strategies in a given region/location.

o =©Projectlevel planning that can be programmed through an existing local or
