impacts, especially projects that integrate natural infrastructure or provide multiple
benefits. Specific examples could include: adaptation strategies related to
realigning/relocating transportation infrastructure impacted by sea level rise;
providing transit shelters with shade, water, or other means of cooling in locations
expected to see temperature increases; preparing for increased landslide risk; etc.

Natural and green infrastructure planning (e.g., wetlands restoration along transportation
corridors to protect transportation infrastructure from flooding and storm impacts,
identifying and removing barriers to habitat and wildlife connectivity.)

Technical feasibility studies required to advance project-level adaptation planning, for
instance coastal hazards analysis at a potential adaptation project location, or a
precipitation study that incorporates future climate risk.

Technical assistance projects for under-resourced and climate vulnerable communities to
perform climate adaptation planning.

Development of educational resources, trainings and workshops for local jurisdictions and
transportation service providers on any of the above listed adaptation planning activities.

5. STRATEGIC PARTNERSHIPS

$1.5 million in FHWA State Planning and Research (SPR) Part 1 funds and $3 million in Federal Transit
Administration (FTA) Section 5304 funds, or a combined total of $4.5 million, will be distributed
through a competitive program to MPOs and RTPAs. Funding distribution for the competitive
program will depend on the quality and number of applications.

5.1 Purpose and Specific Objectives

Strategic Partnerships are intended to fund planning projects that partner with Caltrans to address
needs on or connecting to the State Highway System (SHS), while the transit sub-category will
address multimodal planning projects that focus on transit.

The objectives of the Strategic Partnerships and Strategic Partnerships - Transit grants are to:

e Strengthen government-to-government partnerships
e Accomplish the Federal Planning Factors
e Achieve the Caltrans Mission and the Grant Program Objectives

5.2 Federal Planning Factors

—

Support the economic vitality of the metropolitan area, especially by enabling global
competitiveness, productivity, and efficiency

Increase the safety of the transportation system for motorized and non-motorized users
Increase the security of the transportation system for motorized and non-motorized users

Increase accessibility and mobility of people and freight

ak YW DN

Protect and enhance the environment, promote energy conservation, improve the quality of
life, and promote consistency between transportation improvements and State and local
planned growth and economic development patterns

6. Enhance the integration and connectivity of the transportation system, across and between
modes, for people and freight

Promote efficient system management and operation
8. Emphasize the preservation of the existing transportation system

Improve the resiliency and reliability of the transportation system and reduce or mitigate
stormwater impacts of surface transportation

10. Enhance travel and tourism

6. GRANT PROJECT ADMINISTRATIVE
REQUIREMENTS

The content of this chapter should be notably considered in the development of grant applications
as it lays the foundation for what to expect when applying for these grant funds. Upon award,
grantees will receive more specific guidelines including administrative and reporting requirements.

6.1 Coordination with Caltrans

Caltrans is committed to be an active partner. If awarded a grant, the applicant should include
Caltrans district staff when planning both technical advisory and community meetings. In addition,
Caltrans district staff will helo to ensure that the approved Scope of Work, Cost and Schedule, and
project funding will be maintained throughout the life of the contract. Applicants are also
recommended to engage Caltrans district staff throughout the entire grant life, wnen applicable.

Caltrans may contact grantees to participate in peer exchange forums to promote information
sharing and best practices.

If an agency does not demonstrate adequate performance and timely use of funds, Caltrans may
take appropriate actions, which can include termination of the grant.

6.2 Third Party Contracts

The agreements between a grantee and a sub-applicant/recipient, consultant, or sub-consultant
are often referred to as “third party contracts.” An eligible sub-applicant should be identified by
an eligible applicant at the onset of the application. Eligible sub-applicants/recipients may be
added to an application after award with prior Caltrans approval. If a grantee or a sub-recipient is
going to hire a consultant to perform work during the project, then proper procurement
procedures must always be used.

Grantees may use their agency's procurement procedures as long as they comply with the State
Contracting Manual, Chapter 5, the Local Assistance Procedures Manual, Chapter 10, and the
terms of the agreement with Caltrans. In addition, work can only be contracted if it has been
stated in the applicant's Scope of Work and Cost and Schedule. A grantee is fully responsible for
all work performed by its sub-recipient, consultant, or sub-consultant. Caltrans solely enters into a
contract directly with the grantee; therefore, the grantee is responsible to ensure that all third
parties adhere to the same provisions included in the contractual agreement between Caltrans
and the grantee.

All government funded consultant procurement transactions must be conducted using a fair and
competitive procurement process that is consistent with the State Contracting Manual, Chapter 5,?
the Local Assistance Procedures Manual, Chapter 10,'° and the terms of the agreement with
Caltrans. All documentation of third-party contract procurements must be retained, and copies of

? California State Contracting Manual Volume 1, Chapter 5, 2018,
httes://www.dgs.ca.gov/OLS/Resources/Page-Content/Office-of-Legal-Services-Resources-List-
Folder/State-Contracting

6.3 Quarterly Reporting

Quarterly Progress Reports (QPR) are required to be submitted for each State FY quarter after the
grant recipient has received a Notice to Proceed letter. The table below illustrates the State FY
timeframes for submitting the QPR to Caltrans district staff.

Quarterly Progress Report Timeframes

Quarter 1 Quarter 2 Quarter 3 Quarter 4

July —- September October —- December January — March April - June

For MPOs and RITPAs, the progress and expenditure of each awarded grant project must be
included as part of the OWP Quarterly Progress and Expenditure Report. If this method of reporting
is not adequately satisfied, Caltrans staff will require separate quarterly reports for each awarded
grant project.

All other primary grant recipients shall submit progress reports every quarter for each awarded
grant project. Caltrans district staff will provide the brief report form and due dates.

6.4 Integrated Climate Adaptation and Resiliency Program (ICARP)
Case Studies

Caltrans and the Governor's Office of Planning and Research are sharing the lessons learned from
this grant opportunity to inform other existing or new state grant programs. To this end, Climate
Adaptation Planning primary grant recipients will prepare a final case study/write-up upon grant
completion to be included in the ICARP Clearinghouse (described in Appendix A., Pages 70-71),
which was developed to share information, resources, and case studies with local and regional
jurisdictions. Previous case studies can be found on the ICARP Case Study website!!. These case
studies will be used to develop guidance for adaptation work in the future. Caltrans will share
progress reports with the ICARP Technical Advisory Council at regular intervals.

Climate Adaptation Planning grant recipients are also invited and encouraged to request
technical assistance support from the ICARP program, including bringing key challenges or lessons
learned to the Technical Advisory Council for their input. Caltrans staff will provide ICARP contact
information upon request.

Final Case Study Requirements:

e Include images and GIS files of maps, when applicable

e Emphasize processes or results that can be adapted or replicated for use in multiple
jurisdictions or in various parts of the state

e Document the underserved community's involvement throughout the entire process of the
proposed project and tools used to capture this information

6.5 Invoicing and Financial Requirements
Pre-Award Audit

The Sustainable Communities grants are available in amounts up to $700,000, Strategic Partnerships
grants are up to $500,000 and Climate Adaptation Planning grants are up to $3,000,000. However,
any awarded grant in excess of $250,000 may require a pre-award audit. The pre-award audit is to
ensure that recipients of State or federal funds maintain adequate financial management systems
prior to receiving the funds. Pre-award audits may be required of new grantees, agencies that
have not recently been audited, agencies that have undergone prior audits with significant
weaknesses or deficiencies in their financial management systems, or those determined to be a
higher risk to Caltrans. If a pre-award audit is needed, the local Caltrans district office will contact
the grantee to facilitate the appropriate action.

Accounting Requirements

Grantees and sub-applicants/recipients are required to maintain an accounting system that
properly records, and segregates incurred project costs and matching funds by line item. The
accounting system of the grantee, including its sub-applicants and subcontractors, must conform
to Generally Accepted Accounting Principles that enable the determination of incurred costs at
interim points of completion and provides support for reimbursement payment vouchers or invoices
sent to or paid by Caltrans. Allowable project costs must comply with 2 Code of Federal
Regulations (CFR), Part 200. It is the grantee’s responsibility, in conjunction with Caltrans district
staff, to monitor work and expenses to ensure the project is completed according to the
contracted Scope of Work and Cost and Schedule. Grantees must monitor work and costs to
ensure invoices are submitted on a regular and timely basis (monthly or quarterly as milestones are
completed). Grantees must communicate with their local Caltrans district office to ensure any
issues are addressed early during the project period.

Local Match

All grant project tasks/activities require a grant amount and a local match, except a grant amount
for administrative Tasks 01, 02 is optional. The local match is a financial requirement that
demonstrates the grantee/local agency is vested in the project.

Because the local match is part of the total project cost, the local match can only fund eligible
grant activities. Therefore, grant recipients must fund ineligible activities with funds other than the
local match.

The local match can be all cash, all third-party in-kind contributions, or a combination of the two.
The minimum local match is a percentage of the total project cost (i.e., minimum local match
amount plus the grant amount) and is identified in the Cost and Schedule at the Task level.

Beginning with FY 2021-22, grantees can provide a tapered local match, which allows grantees to
vary the required local match ratio for each task and with every Request for Reimbursement.
Applicants must identify if they will utilize the tapered match on the Cost and Schedule. Grantees
agree to satisfy the total local match amount by the grant expiration date.

6.6 Non-Discrimination Requirements

Title VI Non-Discrimination Requirement

The FHWA and the FIA each have requirements that recipients of Metropolitan Planning federal
funds must demonstrate continued compliance with Title VI. Compliance with Title VI includes
conducting meetings in a fair and reasonable manner that are open to all members of a
community. Compliance reflects not only the law but is also a good policy that builds the kind of
trust and information sharing upon which successful planning is done. Even where a city or county
may not be receiving federal funding for transportation, the Civil Rights Restoration Act of 1987 also
obligates that a city or county comply with Title VL, if it receives any other federal funding for any
program. Refer to the Caltrans Title VI website'8 for more information.

Disadvantaged Business Enterprises

Successful grant applicants are expected to market contracting opportunities to all small
businesses, including DBEs and Disabled Veteran Business Enterprises.

6.7. Final Product

All final reports funded through the Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant Program shall credit
the FHWA, FTA, or Caltrans’ financial participation on the cover or title page. An Americans with
Disabilities Act of 1994 (ADA)-accessible electronic copy of all final reports shall be forwarded to
the Caltrans district office responsible for the administration and oversight of the grant. There are
resources to assist with develooment of ADA compliant documents. 2°

Any technologies or inventions that may result from the use of these grants are in the public
domain and may not be copyrighted, sold, or used exclusively by any business, organization, or
agency. Caltrans reserves a royalty-free, non-exclusive, and irrevocable license to reproduce,
publish, or otherwise use and to authorize others to use for public purposes.

Applicants should thoughtfully develop the project title. Once a Caltrans grant agreement is
executed, changes to the original grant project title are not allowed. Grantees may use an “Also
Known As” (AKA), but both the AKA and the original title must be included in the final product.

Grantees must submit their final product with the final RFR to Caltrans for approval and
reimbursement. Grantees will not be reimbursed for activities that are not completed, and the
activity budget must be left unexpended.

6.8 Project Close-Out Survey

Once awarded grant projects are completed, grantees will complete a close-out survey to
describe the successes and challenges of their project. The survey will give the opportunity to (1)
highlight successes and obstacles in project implementation of the concepts identified by the
planning process, (2) identify best practices in transportation planning, with an emphasis in public
engagement, and (3) identify studies/plans that have been or will be funded for continued project
development. Information from the survey will be compiled into a report to illustrate the value of
the grant program and inform planning practitioners in their planning efforts. Caltrans’ goal is to
provide transparency and accountability for the program, as well as to use the survey feedback to
better serve future grant applicants.

7. APPLICATION SUBMITTAL PROCESS

The Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant Program is highly competitive. This section provides
applicants with supplemental information as well as details on required documents that must
accompany an application at the time of submittal. All applicants are strongly encouraged to
adhere to these requirements in order to score competitively during the application evaluation
process.

7.1. Applicant Eligibility

Primary applicants must have statutory authority to conduct transportation/transit planning for their
jurisdiction. Generally, the local and regional agencies have this specific authority. Native
American Tribal Governments must be Federally recognized to be eligible. Refer to the public
entity's bylaws to find this information.

7.2 Early Coordination and Technical Assistance for Primary
Applicants

Sub-applicants are encouraged to work far in advance of the application deadline with the
appropriate primary applicant to coordinate application development. It is also beneficial for
sub-applicants to be informed of the appropriate primary applicant process and schedule, as they
may differ slightly from those of Caltrans. RTPAs residing within MPO boundaries should also
coordinate application development with the MPO, as it is critical to ensure that proposed studies
align with the RTP SCS/APS for the entire MPO region and do not duplicate efforts being applied for
or already awarded to the MPO.

Caltrans district staff (See Appendix D) are available during the application period to answer
questions and help interested groups complete their applications.

For questions specific to the Grant Application Guide, applicants are also welcomed to contact:

Application Guide

8. APPLICATION REVIEW PROCESS

8.1 Two-Tiered Application Review Process

This chapter provides a brief overview of the grant application review process. Grant application
evaluation is a two-tiered process that consists of:

e = Caltrans District Review and Evaluation
e Caltrans Headquarters (HQ) Interagency Review Committees Evaluation

Caltrans district staff conducts the first-level review of all applications for content, submission of
proper documentation, overall relationship to regional and local planning efforts, and documents
their evaluations. Caltrans district staff scores, prioritizes, and recommends the most highly ranked
Sustainable Communities and all Strategic Partnerships applications for the next level of review with
the Interagency Review Committees. Grant applications from Native American Tribal
Governments, Transit Agencies, and proposed projects soanning multiple Caltrans districts or
projects having a statewide significance, may also move on directly from Caltrans districts to HQ
for review.

The HQ Interagency Review Committees conduct the second-level review and comprise staff from
Caltrans HQ, the Federal Highway Administration, the Federal Transit Administration, the California
Department of Housing and Community Development, the California Department of Public Health,
the Governor's Office of Planning and Research, the Strategic Growth Council, and the California
Air Resources. Caltrans HQ also coordinates with internal specialists, depending on the grant
application subject matter, to provide high-level reviews of the proposed project to avoid funding
duplicative efforts or efforts that are not supportive of State planning efforts. The committees for
each grant category convene to develop funding recommendations that are approved by every
level of Caltrans management and the California State Transportation Agency.

8.2 Application Evaluation/Scoring Process

Grant applications that address every aspect of the grant specific objectives will score higher
overall. Caltrans has diverse applicants and project types, which makes it difficult fo use a one-size
fits all scoring rubric that would not unintentionally put some applicant/project types ata
disadvantage. Therefore, applications will be scored based on how well they are able to describe
the project, justify need, incorporate the grant specific objectives, and develop a Scope of Work
and Cost and Schedule, all in accordance with this grant guide, samples and checklists provided,
as applicable and appropriate for the applicant and project type. Once the grant review
committees evaluate, rank, and select the best applications for grant funding, final
recommendations are presented to Caltrans management and California State Transportation
Agency for approval.

8.3 Past Performance Award Considerations

Previous Caltrans transportation planning grantee performance will be considered during the
evaluation process. Applicants with a history of inadequate performance and/or unresolved past
grant performance issues may be at a competitive disadvantage in the application review
process. Past performance issues could include the following:

Poor grant project management

Lack of communication/coordination with Caltrans
Failure to achieve grant project milestones
Untimely invoice submittals

9. APPLICATION AWARD PROCESS

9.1 Award and Non-Award

Successful grant applicants will receive an award letter via email. A list of award and non-
awarded grants will be posted to the Caltrans Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant Program
website.

Caltrans Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant Program Website:

httes://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/regional-
and-community-planning/sustainable-transportation-planning-grants

Conditional Award Teleconferences

Each grantee will receive a Conditional Award Letter that outlines the grant project cost, important
expiration and final invoice dates.

Caltrans district staff will schedule individual teleconferences to provide the specific and general
conditions of grant acceptance that are necessary to accept grant funding, including any
revisions to the grant application, Scope of Work and Cost and Schedule. Conditions may include
revisions to the project Scope of Work to bolster public participation, consider land use and
housing, and to coordinate with local housing and community development departments and
health departments. Awardees are required to submit all supporting materials and a signed
agreement or risk forfeiting the grant award.

Non-Award Teleconferences

Unsuccessful grant applicants are encouraged to request a debriefing from Caltrans. Applicants
typically receive specific comments from the District/Interagency Review Committee on how to
improve applications to re-apply in a future grant cycle. If the application advanced to the
second-level review, HQ provides the Interagency Review Committee score, as well as how close
the application was relative to the cut-off score for available grant funding. Applicants sometimes
apply two or three times before they are successful due to the competitiveness of the grant
program.

9.2 Contracting with Caltrans

All awarded grant funds must be under an executed agreement with Caltrans during the State FY
2024-25. The project start date depends on the method of contracting with Caltrans.

MPOs and Rural RTPAs

All MPOs/RTPAs with a current Master Fund Transfer Agreement (MFTA) must have the entire grant
award and local match programmed in the FY 2024-25 OWP within 90-days of receiving the signed
FY 2023-24 reconciliation letter. Due to the competitiveness of this grant program, failure to
program funds may result in forfeiture of grant funds. MPOs/RTPAs should include a place-holder
Work Element in the Draft FY 2024-25 OWP and satisfy specific grant conditions before the first FY
2024-25 OWP amendment. MPO/RTPAs can anticipate starting their projects in November 2024,
after the OWP amendment is processed and Caltrans issues a formal Notice to Proceed.

Non-MPOs/RTPAs

Grantees that do not have a current MFTA with the Caltrans Office of Regional and Community
Planning (i.e., cities, counties, transit agencies, Tribal Governments), Caltrans will contract directly
with the primary grantees through the Restricted Grant Agreement (RGA) process. For grantees

CalEnviroScreen Version 4.0

CalEnviroScreen is a screening methodology that can be used to help identify California
communities that are disproportionately burdened by multiple sources of pollution.
CalEnviroScreen uses environmental, health, and socioeconomic information to produce a
numerical score for each census tract in the state. For purposes of SB 535 (De Ledn, Statutes of
2012), disadvantaged communities are defined as the top 25 percent scoring areas from
CalEnviroScreen along with other areas with high amounts of pollution and vulnerable populations.

https://oehha.ca.gov/calenviroscreen/report/calenviroscreen-40

California Healthy Places Index (HPI)

The California Healthy Places Index (HPI) is an
interactive data and mapping tool that provides a
detailed snapshot of the social determinants of Lalisio
health across California, mapped down to the Eo
Census tract level. HPI provides comparison
rankings of Census tracts statewide and an
accompanying policy action guide. Therefore, the
HPI can be a useful tool in prioritizing areas with high
levels of social and economic disadvantage for Community
funding, policy, and planning interventions.HPI was Conditions
developed by the Public Health Alliance of
Southern California in collaboration with health
departments and data experts across the state.

Because the HPI focuses on the social and ond
environmental conditions that contribute to health, context

policy makers and local agencies can use it to
identify actionable policies that would improve
health in their community, such as improving
transportation access, housing affordability and quality, or access to parks and open space, HPI
also incorporates “decision support layers” that can be overlaid to show additional indicators such
as the California Department of Public Health's (CDPH) climate change and health vulnerability
indicators (see Appendix A. under Public Health Resources, CDPH Climate Change and Health
Vulnerability Indicators for more information.).

https://healthyplacesindex.org/

Understanding the HPI Score

The HPI includes a composite score for each Census tract in the State. The higher the score, the
healthier the community conditions. Each Census tract's score is converted to a percentile, which
allows it to be compared to other California Census tracts. For example, an HPI percentile of 79
indicates that a Census tract has healthier community conditions than 79 percent of the Census
tracts in California. HPI percentile rankings are further broken into quartiles, with percentiles below
25 typically used to indicate disadvantaged communities. Thus, lower scores can be used to
demonstrate a community, or project/service area, is underserved for purposes of qualifying for the
minimum threshold of 50 percent for underserved communities in this program.

In addition to the composite score and percentile ranking, applicants can review the individual
domain scores or indicators themselves and explain how their project will improve one or more of
these public health challenges. The numeric value and percentile ranking for these component
indicators can be found either by using the live map or by accessing the data directly. See the
following table for HPI examples.

Scope of Work Checklist

The Scope of Work (SOW) is the official description of the work that is to be completed

during the contract. Tasks 1-6 outlined in the SOW are for illustrative purposes only.

Applications with missing components will be at a competitive disadvantage. Please

use this checklist to make sure your Scope of Work is complete.
Scope of Work Checklist

(“) Ensure these items are completed prior to submitting to Caltrans

| ij Use the Fiscal Year 2024-25 template provided
Include the activities discussed in the grant application

List all tasks using the same title as stated in the Project Cost and Schedule

Include task numbers in accurate and proper sequencing, consistent with the
Cost and Schedule

Exclude sub-task numbers; only include sub-headings

Exclude tasks for project management and/or staff/consultant coordination;
these activities should be soread among relevant tasks

Include a thorough Introduction to describe relevant background, related
planning efforts, the project and project area demographics, including a
description of the underserved community involved with the project, if applicable

Include a thorough and accurate narrative description of each task
Task 01 is a required task. It must be titled “Project Administration”, it cannot
exceed 5% of the grant award amount, and only the grantee and sub-

recipient(s) can charge against this Task. This Task must only include the following
activities and deliverables:

e Caltrans and grantee Project kick-off meeting at the start of the grant

e Invoicing and quarterly reporting to Caltrans

e DBE Reporting (federal grants only)

nclude Task 02 for the procurement of a consultant (if needed). This task is for the
rantee and sub-recipient({s) only.

nclude detailed public participation and services to diverse communities in the
Public Outreach Task {excluding technical projects)

dentify public outreach strategies in a manner that provides flexibility and allows
for a diverse range of outreach methods (both in-person and on-line), excluding
technical projects

nclude a Task(s) for a Draft and Final product. The draft plan must include an
opportunity for the public to provide feedback (excluding technical projects).

nclude a summary of next steps your agency will take towards implementing the
project in the Final Product

List achievable project deliverables for each Task

EXCLUDE environmental, complex design, engineering work, and other ineligible
activities outlined in the Grant Application Guide

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  28
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 10-1311
File ID: 25-10192
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
City Manager's Office
MEETING DATE:
July 28, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy – Adoption
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to adopt the Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy establishing policy direction for 
decarbonizing existing buildings in San Mateo.
BACKGROUND:
Building energy use represents 38 percent of communitywide greenhouse gas emissions in San Mateo. Building 
electrification refers to the transition from gas equipment to electric equipment for space and water heating, clothes 
drying, cooking, and more. While natural gas is a fossil fuel, Peninsula Clean Energy (PCE) provides carbon-free electricity 
communitywide. Electrifying existing buildings is critical to reducing communitywide greenhouse gas emissions and is a key 
strategy in attaining the objectives contained in the City’s adopted Climate Action Plan. To continue to advance existing 
building electrification to meet the City Council decarbonization priority, City staff began working with Rincon Consultants 
to develop the Electrify San Mateo: Sustainability Building Strategy. The project team completed a building inventory and 
market segmentation analysis that was presented at the September 13, 2023, Sustainability and Infrastructure 
Commission meeting. After completing Phase I of community engagement, the project culminated in the identification of 
five potential policy pathways that were presented at the January 16, 2024 City Council meeting. City Council directed the 
project team to conduct community engagement on all five policy pathways to determine overall feasibility and support 
from the community.
On June 16, 2025, the project team presented community feedback and other information on the five policy pathways and 
City Council directed which of them to include in the final Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy. City Council 
directed staff to continue a “business as usual” approach which includes new construction reach codes and a continued 
emphasis on incentives and outreach. City Council also directed development of a municipal electric first policy with 
exception language for public safety and life cycle costs. City Council also requested more evaluation, data analysis, and 
community engagement for reach codes for renovations and building performance standards for commercial buildings. 
City Council did not recommend proceeding with Zero Nox standards ahead of the Bay Area Air District. 
On June 30, 2025, California Assembly Bill (AB) 130 was signed into law restricting local governments from amending 
residential state building codes between October 1, 2025 through June 1, 2031. This bill does not appear to impact the 
City’s currently adopted reach codes requiring energy performance margins for new construction. The final language in the 
bill includes some exceptions that allow for the adoption of reach codes after October 1, 2025. Staff is in the process of 
evaluating reach code approaches while ensuring compliance with AB 130.
Staff recommends adoption of the proposed resolution to adopt the Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy to 
formalize the   June 16, 2025 City Council direction on how to advance decarbonization policy efforts for existing buildings. 
773 of 983

--- page 774 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
This action would complete City Council Priority #1: Establish policies to decarbonize existing buildings and infrastructure 
and eliminate methane gas use by 2030. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The development of the Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy was funded by an appropriation from the 
available fund balance in the Advance Planning Fund (Fund 225) and had no impact on the City’s General Fund.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This plan adoption is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not 
result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. ____ (2025)
ADOPTING THE ELECTRIFY SAN MATEO SUSTAINABLE BUILDING STRATEGY
WHEREAS, building energy use represents 38% of communitywide greenhouse gas emissions; and
WHEREAS, in FY 2022-23, the City Council prioritized the evaluation and adoption of decarbonization 
policies for existing buildings to meet the City’s climate goals; and
WHEREAS, in November 2022, the City released a Request for Proposals (RFP) seeking an experienced 
team knowledgeable in decarbonization policy planning, analysis, and implementation, to develop a 
decarbonization strategy that centers equity and promotes a collaborative pathway to electrification; and 
WHEREAS, in March 2023, City Council approved an agreement with Rincon Consultants, Inc. for 
consultant services to develop the Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy; and
WHEREAS, City staff and Rincon Consultants, Inc. completed building inventory, market segmentation, 
building electrification cost analysis, policy pathways analysis and community engagement; and
WHEREAS, the final Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy summarizes the work completed 
and City Council direction on how to advance decarbonization policy for existing buildings. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES 
that: 
1.
This Council action is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that the 
adoption of the Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy will not cause a physical change in the 
environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.) 
2.
The Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy, as attached to this resolution, is approved.

1.
INTRODUCTION
The City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings 
Strategy provides a roadmap for the City of 
San Mateo to decarbonize the buildings where 
residents live, work, and play. Decarbonizing 
buildings through electrification  is a crucial 
step in the City’s work to reach its 2030 and 
2045 climate goals, comply with future regional 
air quality regulations, and provide healthy 
air and living spaces for all San Mateans. 
This report provides a summary of what 
building electrification is (Introduction), what 
electrification looks like in the City of San 
Mateo (Building Segmentation), the community 
engagement completed as part of developing 
this report (Community Engagement), and 
the final policy recommendations selected for 
further planning and eventual implementation.
WHAT IS ELECTRIFICATION 
AND WHY IS IT IMPORTANT? 
1.
https://gridworks.org/initiatives/cagas-system-transition/
2.
About 72 percent of residential new-construction line extensions were all-electric and 91 percent of 
non-residential projects were all-electric in 2024. https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/about-cpuc/divisions/
energy-division/building-decarbonization
The City of San Mateo is committed to reducing GHG emissions 
through the implementation of the Climate Action Plan. Residential 
and commercial buildings currently account for 38 percent of the City’s 
greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, making them a critical area for climate 
action. To meet the City’s Climate Action Plan (CAP) targets and align with 
California’s statewide climate goals, reducing emissions from residential 
and commercial buildings is imperative. While electricity emissions are 
expected to decrease to zero by 2045 due to Senate Bill (SB) 100 and the 
renewable portfolio standard, natural gas emissions will remain relatively 
constant. In San Mateo, Peninsula Clean Energy (PCE) already provides 
electricity that is 50 percent renewable and virtually 100 percent carbon 
free and is committed to delivering 100 percent renewable and carbon 
free electricity by 2025. Due to the low cost and availability of carbon free 
electricity, building electrification has been identified as the most cost-
effective strategy for reducing GHG emissions from our buildings.1 
Building electrification involves replacing fossil fuel-powered equipment 
– such as gas furnaces, water heaters, stoves, and dryers – with high-
efficiency electric alternatives. These electric systems can be powered
by clean, renewable energy, significantly reducing a building’s carbon
footprint. The City of San Mateo and the State of California have already
made significant strides in electrifying new construction and a majority of
buildings constructed in the state today are already all-electric2.
However, the City of San Mateo is already mostly built out, with a majority 
of buildings having been constructed before 1970    as shown in Appendix 
A. With new construction increasingly all-electric, nearly all the natural gas
emissions in 2030 will be from buildings which exist today. About 48 percent
of the City’s Climate Action Plans projected emissions reductions come
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
1
778 of 983

--- page 779 ---
from the electrification of existing buildings. Transitioning these 
existing buildings to all-electric systems is essential for reducing 
dependence on fossil fuels and achieving the City’s climate goals. 
THE BENEFITS OF BUILDING 
ELECTRIFICATION
While electrification is a critical climate action strategy, GHG 
reductions are not the only reason to electrify buildings. 
Electrification of buildings will improve health outcomes, increase 
energy efficiency, decrease utility bills, and provide additional 
resilience and comfort. 
Health and Air Quality 
Negative health impacts related to ambient air pollutants 
generated by the burning of fossil fuels in power plants, vehicles, 
and industrial operations are widely acknowledged.3 By contrast, 
sources of air pollution inside of buildings and related health effects 
are often overlooked. Commonly used natural gas appliances, 
including stoves, furnaces, and water heaters, emit substantial 
amounts of air pollutants, and if not properly ventilated can 
present significant indoor air quality impacts. Natural gas-powered 
appliances emit nitrogen dioxide (NO2), nitric oxide (NOx), sulfur 
oxides (SOx), particulate matter (PM), carbon monoxide (CO), and 
formaldehyde (CH2O). The potential health impacts related to 
combustion of natural gas can be serious but generally are not 
widely understood by consumers.4  
The United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) 
concluded that long-term exposure to NO2 is linked to the onset of 
asthma in children5. The US EPA has also reported that short-term 
exposure to NO2 can also lead to respiratory complications and 
3.
https://www.who.int/airpollution/ambient/health-impacts/en/
4. https://ehp.niehs.nih.gov/doi/10.1289/ehp.122-a27
5.
https://cfpub.epa.gov/ncea/isa/recordisplay.cfm?deid=310879
2
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
779 of 983

--- page 780 ---
trigger asthma attacks. A 2013 meta-analysis of prior research 
assessed the impact of indoor NO2 pollution on asthma in children 
living in suburban and urban homes. Results demonstrated that 
children in homes with gas stoves have a 42 percent higher chance 
of developing asthma symptoms.6   
Increased Energy Efficiency
Building electrification presents an opportunity to significantly 
increase the energy efficiency of our homes and other buildings. 
The development of electric heat pumps which move heat rath-
er than create it, can heat our water and provide air-conditioned 
spaces with 400 percent efficiency.7 This means that every 1 kWh of 
electricity can provide 4 kWh of heating capacity. This increase in 
efficiency means that most homes will see on-bill savings after elec-
trification when using PCE rates.8 Recent NREL studies have found 
that homes nationwide would see building energy use (natural gas 
and electricity combined) decrease by between 31 percent and 47 
percent on average after installation of heat pump technologies.9 
Utility Bill Savings
Analysis of Peninsula Clean Energy buildings has identified that an 
average single-family home can save up to $30 a month on average 
after installing a heat pump water heater and HVAC unit. These sav-
ings can be increased even further with the installation of solar and/
or building efficiency upgrades.10 While making some homes ready 
for electrification can carry increased upfront costs (Appendix A), 
when paired with available incentives these long-term cost savings 
can help offset upfront costs. 
6. 1Weiwei Lin, Bert Brunekreef, Ulrike Gehring. International Journal of Epidemiology, Volume 42, Issue 6, December 2013, Pages 1724–1737, https://doi.org/10.1093/ije/dyt150
7.
https://www.technologyreview.com/2023/02/14/1068582/everything-you-need-to-know-about-heat-pumps/
8. See Appendix A for more details on costs
9. https://www.nrel.gov/news/detail/press/2024/benefits-of-heat-pumps-detailed-in-new-nrel-report
10. https://www.peninsulacleanenergy.com/news-releases/savings-switching-gas-to-electric/
Figure 1.   Efficiencies of various space heating technologies 
(Rewiring America) Increased Resilience
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
3
780 of 983

--- page 781 ---
Increased Resilience
A city's resilience is defined by its community’s ability to survive, 
adapt, and thrive in the face of both sudden shocks and long-term 
challenges. All-electric buildings, especially when paired with solar 
panels and battery storage, can reduce the effects of power outag-
es and utility-led Public Safety Power Shutoff (PSPS) events during 
periods of high fire risk. Natural gas appliances such as furnaces 
and water heaters also rely on electricity to function, making them 
no more resilient during extended power outages. In addition, 
switching to a heat pump that provides both heating and cooling 
can provide much needed protection against extreme heat which is 
becoming more common in San Mateo.11 
THE POLICY CONTEXT OF 
BUILDING ELECTRIFICATION
The City of San Mateo, the Air District, and the State of California 
have adopted legislation and policies which are driving electrifica-
tion of new and existing buildings. The 2025 Title 24 Building Code 
will heavily favor all-electric construction due to the associated cost 
and energy savings associated with all-electric new construction. 
11.	 https://cal-adapt.org/tools/extreme-heat/#lat=37.5519&lng=-122.3150&boundary=place&climvar=Temperature
12.	 https://www.baaqmd.gov/en/community-health/building-appliances-rule-implementation
Regionally, the Bay Area Air District (Air District) has developed zero 
nitrous oxide (NOx) standards for space and water heating applianc-
es. Currently only electric appliances can meet the zero NOx stan-
dard, meaning that the upcoming zero NOx rules will effectively re-
quire all-electric water heaters to be installed in residential buildings 
by 2027 and all-electric HVAC units by 2029.12 Many of the strategies 
identified in the San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy work in 
tandem with the Air District regulations to prepare San Mateo for 
these upcoming changes and save money over time. 
Locally, the City Council has prioritized the decarbonization of exist-
ing buildings and elimination of methane gas use by 2030, reinforc-
ing the goals outlined in the CAP. CAP goals Building Electrification 
1 and Building Electrification 2 call for all-electric new construction 
and decarbonization of existing buildings, respectively. These two 
actions represent the largest GHG reduction opportunity identified 
by the CAP, demonstrating electrification of existing buildings as 
the cornerstone of climate action in the City.
4
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
4
781 of 983

--- page 782 ---
In May 2023, the City launched Building for the Future: City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy as part of the broader Electrify San Ma-
teo initiative. This strategy outlines a long-term roadmap to electrify the City’s existing residential and non-residential buildings. The Sustainable 
Building Strategy conducted a variety of research into the types of policies and actions which could increase building electrification while man-
aging costs and considering the equity impacts of building electrification as displayed in the timeline below. This report provides a summary 
of the building segmentation analysis, community engagement, analysis of primary challenges and solutions, and the final policy pathways 
completed as part of the City of San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy.

According to parcel data from San Mateo County, there are approximately 
19,350 single-family and 2,350 multifamily buildings within the City of San 
Mateo. Figure 2 shows the number of single-family and multifamily build-
ings by type.

To better understand the use of natural gas in these residential buildings, data provided by Peninsula Clean Energy based on the California En-
ergy Commission’s Residential Appliance Saturation Survey (RASS)13 for Climate Zone 3 was used to analyze the prevalent fuel types of existing 
appliances in buildings (i.e., water heater systems, space heating systems, stoves, clothes dryers, pools). While appliance data specifically for the 
City of San Mateo was unavailable, data for San Mateo County is used as a proxy. 
As shown in Figure 3, most of the residential water heaters in San Mateo County are standard gas-fired tank water heaters, especially in single 
family homes. These water heaters can be replaced with heat pump tank water heaters in most scenarios. Whole house tankless gas-fired water 
heaters are the next most common followed by standard electric resistance water heaters, tankless electric varieties, and then heat pumps.

Space heating in San Mateo County sees a broader range of fuel and appliance types, as shown in Figure 4. Gas-fired space heating systems are

condos, use more electric space heating systems. While some propane and wood are used across the County, these fuels are likely limited to 
unincorporated regions without access to the natural gas system.

gas if they are heated at all. 
Overall, natural gas consuming water heaters, stoves, and furnaces are the most common appliances of each type. However, a smaller yet sub-

--- page 791 ---
EQUIPMENT AVAILABLE 
TO ELECTRIFY
The building segmentation analysis demonstrated that most of the 
water heating systems, space heating systems, stoves, and pools in 
the City of San Mateo are gas-fired. The following subsections sum-
marize the types of electric equipment available to replace these 
gas-fired appliances.
Heat Pump Water Heaters
Most of the residential and commercial water heater systems in the 
City are standard gas-fired tank water heaters. These water heating 
systems can be replaced by heat pump tank water heaters in most 
scenarios. Rather than generating heat, heat pump tank water heat-
ers use electricity to move heat from the surrounding areas into the 
appliance to heat the water in the storage tank. These heat pump 
water heaters can be two to three times more energy efficiency 
than conventional electric resistance water heaters. The second 
most common residential and commercial water heater system 
in the City is gas-fired tankless water heaters. These water heating 
systems can also be replaced with heat pumps—specifically geo-
thermal heat pumps—which draw heat from the ground during the 
winter and from the indoor air during the summer to heat water. 
These geothermal heat pumps achieve higher energy efficiencies 
and can provide the majority of hot water needs during the sum-
mer.15 
Heat pump swimming pool heaters are also available to replace gas-
fired pool heaters. These devices use a small amount of electricity to 
transfer heat from the outdoors into the pool water.
While heat pump water heaters cannot heat a pool as fast as a gas-
fired heater, they provide even and consistent heat, operating with 
less energy demand and providing energy cost savings.16  
15.	 Department of Energy. Heat Pump Water Heaters. Available at: https://www.energy.gov/
energysaver/heat-pump-water-heaters.
16.	Department of Energy. Heat Pump Swimming Pool Heaters. Available at: https://www.energy.
gov/energysaver/heat-pump-swimming-pool-heaters.
14
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
791 of 983

--- page 792 ---
Heat Pump HVAC Units
The most common residential and commercial space heating sys-
tems in the City—gas-fired space heating systems—can be replaced 
by heat pumps as well. Heat pump HVAC units provide both heat-
ing and cooling with one appliance.17 This means that one unit can 
replace both a gas fired furnace and electric powered air condition-
ing unit. These heat pump space heating and cooling systems use 
electricity to transfer (or pump) heat from a cool space to a warm 
space to heat and cool buildings with up to 400 percent efficiency.18 
Heat pump water heating and space heating and cooling systems 
can also be combined for further energy and cost efficiencies.19 
Induction
Most residential stoves in the City are gas-fired. These stoves can 
easily be replaced by induction stoves. While traditional gas-fired 
stoves use a flame to radiate and conduct heat to cookware, induc-
tion stoves use an electromagnetic field powered by electricity to 
transfer currents directly to the cookware placed on its glass surface. 
These induction stoves only create heat through cookware surfaces. 
As such, they are up to three percent more energy efficient than gas 
stoves and ten percent more energy efficient than electric stoves.20 
They also provide faster cook times (i.e., up to 40 percent faster 
than gas and traditional electric stoves), do not cause burns like gas 
and electric stoves, and do not produce indoor air pollutants like 
gas stoves.   While most stoves operate using 240v circuits, some 
new stoves utilize batteries to provide fast cooking while using a 
standard 120v outlet. This reduces installation costs and lowers grid 
impacts. 
17.	 Peninsula Clean Energy. 2035 Decarbonization Feasibility Analysis and Plan. Available at: https://www.peninsulacleanenergy.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/01/2022.09-BOD-2035-De-
carb-Plan-Complete-Analysis-Draft-Plan-1.pdf.
18.	Department of Energy. Heat Pump Systems. Available at: https://www.energy.gov/energysaver/heat-pump-systems.
19.	 Department of Energy. Heat Pump Water Heaters. Available at: https://www.energy.gov/energysaver/heat-pump-water-heaters.
20.	Department of Energy. Making the Switch to Induction Stoves or Cooktops. Available at: https://www.energy.gov/articles/making-switch-induction-stoves-or-cooktops.
Electric Resistance
Electric resistance appliances are also an option to electrify gas-fired 
appliances. These appliances convert incoming electric energy into 
heat. Electric resistance appliances, however, are not as energy effi-
cient as heat pumps water and space heating systems or induction 
stoves which use electricity to move heat instead of create it. Thus, 
while they make up a considerable share of existing appliances in 
the City, they are not usually a preferable option for electrification. 
Buildings with existing resistance heating may want to consider up-
grading to a heat pump where feasible to gain considerable on-bill 
savings. 
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
15
792 of 983

--- page 793 ---
ELECTRIFICATION IMPACTS ON ELECTRICAL INFRASTRUCTURE
21.	
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301421524002581
One of the common concerns regarding electrification is the im-
pact that increasing electrical demand has on a buildings existing 
wiring, electrical panel, and service line (which runs from the util-
ityowned pole to the specific building). These important electrical 
components are shown in Figure 10. 
Figure 10.  Diagram of Building Electrical Equipment
While most natural gas equipment (including stoves, water heaters, 
and HVAC units) require 120v electrical connections, some all-elec-
tric appliances require a dedicated 240v circuit. This means that a 
new electrical circuit needs to be installed and there needs to be 
space on the existing panel. This added circuit can cause space or 
capacity constraints on the panel and service line.  However, new 
technologies and approaches are limiting the impacts of electrifica-
tion on these electrical components. New “retrofit ready” appliances 
such as heat pump water heaters and HVAC units are now available 
that only use 120v and can be plugged into existing outlets. New 
induction stoves are also available that use batteries to provide fast 
heating while only using 120v power. These new appliances can also 
be paired with smart circuits and smart panels which only allow 
one load (for example an EV charger OR electric dryer) to operate at 
one time. These new approaches are allowing homes to electrify on 
panels of 100amps.
A recent study conducted by UCLA added more detail to the pro-
portion of homes needing panel upgrades. The study found that 
only three percent of single family residential and ten percent of 
multi-family properties will need panel upgrades in order to fully 
electrify, while an additional 32 percent (single-family) and 59 per-
cent (multi-family) will require further load management or watt 
diet approaches to electrify on their current panel.21 
 
Courtesy of Emily Higbee, Redwood Energy Research Director

COMMUNITY 
ENGAGEMENT 
To guide equitable and practical policy creation, 
the City conducted extensive community 
engagement in two distinct phases reaching 
over 350  constituents during 16 individual 
engagement events. See Appendix B for a 
full summary of the methodologies, events, 
and results of the community engagement 
conducted as part of the engagement process.   
PHASE I ENGAGEMENT
During the first phase of community outreach, the City aimed to under-
stand the goals and concerns of the community as they relate to San 
Mateo’s buildings and decarbonization. Outreach events included a meet-
ing with the Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission, a City Coun-
cil meeting, five General Plan Town Hall meetings, and an online survey. 
Through the initial round of engagement, the City identified affordability, 
health and safety, resilience, and climate as the primary goals of the com-
munity. Figure 10 shows the results of the survey question asking commu-
nity members which qualities they value most from their buildings. Af-
fordability was the most important attribute followed by health and safety, 
resilience, and sustainability.

PHASE II ENGAGEMENT
In the second phase of community outreach, the City presented 
the five potential policy pathways and aimed to get specific feed-
back, concerns, and required support for each pathway. Phase II 
engagement included an in-person workshop and an additional 
virtual workshop for community members, a virtual information 
session on Building Performance Standards for commercial build-
ing owners, a hybrid information session with the San Mateo United 
Homeowners Association, presentations and feedback from City 
Commissions and City Council, and an online survey. 
A total of 25 residents attended the in-person workshop where they 
learned about the five potential pathways and participated in an 
exercise to discuss concerns and brainstorm solutions and required 
support to make building electrification feasible. Following the 
exercise, residents voted for which of the five policies they would 
be in favor of. Photos from the in-person workshop are shown in 
Figure 12. Overall, residents were excited about building electrifica-
tion policy and were eager to see the City pursuing climate action, 
evident in the zero votes for the “Do Nothing” or “Business as Usual” 
options during the vote. Despite this, many residents still had con-
cerns about cost, grid reliability, equity, technology and engineering 
challenges, and needed City education and support through the 
electrification process. To address these issues, residents said there 
should be continued education and technical support as well as 
connections to incentives and rebates to ease the potential cost im-
pacts of electrification. This sentiment was echoed throughout the 
various engagement events throughout Phase II. 
20
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
797 of 983

--- page 798 ---
Figure 13.  In-Person Community Engagement at the City of San Mateo Public Library 
In addition to the in-person and online workshops, the City con-
ducted a survey which received 217 responses from a wide range of 
socioeconomic backgrounds. Generally, the survey demonstrated 
polarized responses with many supporters and opposers for each 
policy. While Policy Options 1, 4, and 5 received overall positive sup-
port with more responses in support than not, Policy Options 2 and 
3 were more polarized with almost equal numbers of supporters 
and opposers. Policy Option 3 was the only option where opposition 
outweighed support by a small margin. 
The five policies were also presented to various interested parties 
including the San Mateo United Homeowners Association who were 
overall unsupportive of moving too quickly on building electrifica-
tion due to the potential costs and challenges around electrical in-
frastructure and grid resilience. Some attendees did express interest 
and/or support in low-cost opportunities for building electrification. 
A Building Performance Standard focus group was also held with 
commercial building owners. Attendees were generally supportive 
of a building performance standard and provided ideas to make 
Policy Option 4 more feasible. 
The policies were also presented to three City commissions/commit-
tees (Senior Advisory, Sustainability and Infrastructure, and Com-
munity Relations) who provided overall supportive feedback, but did 
note concerns regarding cost impacts. Several of the commissions/
committees identified infeasibility or hardship exemptions as a 
strategy to address the potential of uncertain costs impacts. 
Finally, a summary of the engagement results and policy pathways 
were presented to City Council who expressed similar feedback to 
the general community with a desire to drive climate action and 
decarbonization, but with concerns over cost and feasibility. City 
Council provided direction to staff to include policy options 1, 2, 4, 
and 5 into the final strategy, but expressed the need for continued 
engagement and refining before final adoption of any policy. A 
more detailed engagement summary is available in Appendix B.

PRIMARY OPPORTUNITIES 
AND CHALLENGES 
Through the robust community engagement process developed 
for the Sustainable Building Strategy the project team heard from 
a large cross section of community members and building owners. 
Throughout the engagement process community members were 
polarized in their responses relating to the building electrification 
policies discussed. While most constituents care about addressing 
the climate crisis and agree that the City should pursue existing 
building electrification in some form, many residents also expressed 
concerns especially over cost.  
The primary opportunities identified during Phase I and Phase II 
engagement that are motivating building electrification included: 
 
y
y	 The climate crisis is a priority issue 
 
y
y	 Health and air quality benefits of building decarbonization 
 
y
y	 Urgent need for the City to act now 
 
y
y	 Availability of electric equipment incentives and rebates to 
support property owners  
The primary concerns identified during Phase I and Phase II en-
gagement included:  
 
y
y	 Upfront costs of electric equipment  
 
y
y	 On-bill or operational costs of electric equipment 
 
y
y	 Installation and electrical infrastructure challenges 
 
y
y	 Grid reliability and resilience 
 
y
y	 Lack of education surrounding electrification  
These opportunities and concerns were driving forces behind the 
development of the final policy recommendations included in this 
Sustainable Building Strategy. The Electrification Challenges and 
Solutions section below highlights how community concerns were 
addressed through the policy development phase. 
22
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
799 of 983

--- page 800 ---
4.	
ELECTRIFICATION 
CHALLENGES 
AND SOLUTIONS
Based on feedback from the community, 
the project team analyzed the potential 
policy pathways to reflect constituent values 
and address their concerns. The concerns 
demonstrate common challenges involved 
with building electrification implementation 
including cost and grid impacts. However, 
these challenges can be mostly addressed 
through detailed policy development and 
targeted exemptions. The policies which 
have been included in the Sustainable 
Buildings Strategy seek to address each of 
these concerns specifically while setting the 
City up for future success. Solutions for each 
challenge are summarized below and covered 
in more detail under each policy in the Policy 
Pathways Section. 
UPFRONT COSTS OF 
ELECTRICAL EQUIPMENT 
For many building owners, making the switch from natural gas to electric 
appliances results in higher upfront costs without additional incentives or 
rebates. This is due in part to higher equipment costs, but also due to ad-
ditional electrical work including new electrical circuits, and in some cases 
electric panel upgrades, new sub-panels, or even a service upgrade.  As 
shown in Appendix A, the upfront costs of electrifying individual applianc-
es vary across appliance type and depend on the existing electrical equip-
ment in the buildings. While rebates can cover up to 100 percent of these 
costs in some scenarios and for some appliances, rebates are not always 
available. Therefore, the City has targeted specific conditions under which 
electrification becomes highly cost effective while avoiding additional elec-
trical infrastructure upgrades. While Appendix A provides detailed costs 
relating to several appliances, it should be noted that the policies ultimate-
ly included in the Policy Pathways section do not trigger specific electrifi-
cation upgrades and costs have been included for informational purposes. 
More details about each proposed policy’s costs are included in the Policy 
Pathways Section. 
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
23
800 of 983

--- page 801 ---
ON-BILL OR 
OPERATIONAL COSTS 
Ongoing operational costs (how much our monthly energy bills 
are) were another primary concern regarding building electrifi-
cation. However, recent studies by Peninsula Clean Energy show 
that the cost savings from building electrification are increas-
ing as natural gas rate increases outpace electricity rates. When 
paired with all-electric friendly rates from Peninsula Clean Energy, 
average savings were $29 per household per month after electrify-
ing both water and space heaters. These savings are demonstrat-
ed in Figure 14.  
Operational cost savings for electrification are consistent across 
each policy based on the equipment electrified.
GRID RELIABILITY 
AND RESILIENCE
Grid reliability and resilience concerns arose due to the perception 
that increased electrical use would put strain on the grid and that 
electricity was less reliable than gas. However, it has been nearly 20 
years since California had a power outage due to lack of electrici-
ty.22 Furthermore, the utilities, the CPUC, and the CEC, are all plan-
ning for increased electricity usage over time. Integrated Resource 
Planning conducted at the state level now assumes a 27 percent 
increase in peak demand by 2035 due to electrification of buildings 
and vehicles and is expanding the grid to meet that demand. The 
CPUC has approximately 20,000 megawatt hours (MW) of new gen-
eration resources currently under contract and in development.23  
22.	https://www.baaqmd.gov/~/media/files/community-health/building-appliance-implementation/grid-impacts-final-pdf.pdf?rev=14a3ea4d46704d19a48cf03e3984c90e&sc_lang=en
23.	https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/irp/
Figure 14.   Average Monthly Bill Savings from Electrification   
$19
HPWH
Note: Results include savings from electric rate optimization which lowers 
costs for all home usage. Therefore, savings do not stack linearly.
HP HVAC
HPWH + HP
HVAC
$20
$29

from Home Electriﬁcation

Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
24
801 of 983

--- page 802 ---
In addition, cities and individual building owners play a critical 
role in limiting electrification’s impact on the grid. By limiting 
the number of panel upgrades through smart circuits and oth-
er load management approaches, many buildings can electrify 
on 100amp panels or smaller. A recent study by Peninsula Clean 
Energy of over 700 all-electric homes found that 99% of homes 
never exceeded 100 amps during the course of a year. Limiting the 
number of service and panel upgrades will decrease strain on the 
grid and keep overall costs lower.24  
As noted above, neither gas water heaters nor most furnaces work 
without electricity, meaning gas appliances are no more resilient 
to power outages than their electric upgrades. On average, cus-
tomers in PG&E’s peninsula territory suffer less than 1 outage per 
year which lasts less than 2 hours.25 San Mateo County also did not 
experience one PSPS event in 2024.26  
LACK OF EDUCATION 
SURROUNDING 
ELECTRIFICATION 
Another concern across every community engagement event was 
the need for additional education resources to clearly explain new 
technologies, guide building owners through the installation pro-
cess, and connect residents to rebates and incentives. While elec-
tric technologies like heat pumps are not new, they have histori-
cally not been common in California due to low gas prices and an 
extensive natural gas system. This means that some of the appli-
ance specific considerations around installing heat pumps is new 
for many San Mateans. This education gap is already being closed 
through several sources and this Strategy will direct the City to 
continue to build on the resources that are currently available to 
suit the needs of the community. Ongoing and adaptive engage-
ment to contractors, homeowners, and other interested parties 
24.	Powerpoint Presentation
25.	https://www.pge.com/assets/pge/docs/about/pge-systems/CPUC-2023-Annual-Electric-Reliability-Report.pdf
26.	https://www.pge.com/assets/pge/docs/outages-and-safety/outage-preparedness-and-support/2024-post-season-report-1-narrative.pdf
will be critical to the successful adoption of electric appliances. 
The City has already started this process through the engagement 
efforts surrounding this Strategy as well as through their website 
and collaboration with the County of San Mateo to provide educa-
tion to contractors on upcoming requirements. For more informa-
tion on the resources currently available to San Mateans, see the 
Community Electrification Resources Section.

5.	  
POLICY   
PATHWAYS 
At the June 16, 2025 City Council meeting, 
the project team presented policy analysis of 
five potential existing building electrification 
policy pathways and the community feedback 
received on the five pathways. The City Council 
directed the project team to include four of 
the five pathways in the final Electrify San 
Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy. Detailed 
policy analysis can be found in Appendix C. The 
following section outlines each of the policy 
pathways and provides information on the 
electric infrastructure impacts, GHG emissions 
reductions, costs, implementation steps, and 
timelines for implementation.   
POLICY 1 - ELECTRIFY NEW 
BUILDINGS AND INCREASE 
EDUCATION, OUTREACH, 
AND INCENTIVES
Policy Description 
Policy 1 continues to implement the City’s adopted reach codes and identi-
fies opportunities to incentivize the electrification process using education, 
outreach, and connecting the community to rebates and technical support. 
The City’s adopted reach code requires energy compliance margins for new 
buildings will be effective through December 31, 2025, the end of the code 
cycle.  As part of this policy option, the City would investigate options around 
continuing the new building electrification reach code through either an 
updated performance standard which complies with the 2025 building code, 
or a zero NOx standard for new buildings.  
This option would also use staff time to conduct additional engagement 
and connect homeowners to incentives and technical information regarding 
building electrification. It would also promote existing programming and 
incentives run by partners (e.g. Peninsula Clean Energy’s Home Upgrade 
Program) to assist in a voluntary shift towards electrification while preparing 
the community to electrify space and water heating end uses in advance of 
upcoming Air District Zero NOx requirements. 
For example, this option could include an education campaign to encourage 
homeowners to prewire for water heaters and furnaces, so they are ready to 
make the switch once their appliance burns out. Another example incentive 
would be waiving permit costs for electric equipment upgrades. 
 
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
26
803 of 983

--- page 804 ---
Electric Infrastructure Impacts 
This policy focuses on new construction and voluntary upgrades to 
existing buildings. The 2025 California Building Code already re-
quires pre-wiring for all appliances and sufficient electrical capacity 
for all-electric buildings in new construction. Due to the voluntary 
nature of the existing building upgrades, while some upgrades will 
likely be needed for some homes to electrify, it will not be a require-
ment. Therefore, the electric infrastructure impacts of this policy are 
assumed to be low. 
GHG EMISSIONS REDUCTIONS – 1,770 MT CO2e per year by 2030
Policy Cost 
This policy approach would continue to incentivize all-electric new 
construction and direct the City to expand outreach efforts with the 
community to increase voluntary existing building electrification. 
New Construction reach codes have been shown to save money. 
Research from the New Buildings Institute (NBI) highlights that 
all-electric homes save between $7,500 and $8,200 in construction 
costs compared to mixed-fuel homes.27 These cost savings are pri-
marily due to avoiding installing fees for natural gas infrastructure 
like gas meters and pipelines. These homes also save money over 
time especially when paired with heat pumps and solar. 
Outreach, education, and sharing incentives incur no cost to com-
munity members and can help connect people to resources that 
will lessen costs for electrification. The City will need to provide 
adequate staff time to support building electrification engagement 
and other programs like peer-to-peer home tours for this approach 
to be effective. These engagement activities, when paired with 
other strategies listed below, can help the community feel more 
comfortable and informed on the benefits of electrification.  
27.	NBI. 2022. New Study on Electrification Costs Shows Benefits 
to Building Owners and Society. https://newbuildings.org/
new-study-on-electrification-costs-shows-benefits-to-building-owners-and-society/
Implementation Steps and Timeline 
 
y
y	 Staff is in the process of evaluating reach code approaches while 
ensuring compliance with AB 130. 
 
y
y	 If there is a path forward to adopt a local reach code that meets 
the new state law, staff will bring updated new construction 
ordinances to Council for consideration as cost-effectiveness 
studies are made available. 
 
y
y	 Staff will develop an annual engagement work plan for outreach 
and engagement leveraging partnerships and other connections 
to reach the community.
