--- page 805 ---
POLICY 2 – REACH CODES 
FOR RENOVATION
Policy 2 includes the evaluation of reach codes during renovations 
of existing buildings. Reach codes are typically evaluated on a 
three-year cycle in alignment with the new Building Code. As part of 
Policy 2, the Strategy focuses on two priority existing building reach 
codes which include a Two-Way Air Conditioning (AC) reach code or 
a Flexible Path reach code. These policies could be adopted individ-
ually or both adopted to complement each other. 
Policy 2a – Air Conditioning 
to Heat Pump  
Policy Description  
The Two-Way AC reach code requires all new central air condition-
ing units to provide both heating and cooling through the installa-
tion of a two-way heat pump or significantly increase the efficiency 
of a standard AC unit. A two-way heat pump is a versatile HVAC sys-
tem that can both heat and cool a building by reversing the flow of 
refrigerant within the system. In heating mode, it extracts heat from 
an external source, such as outdoor air or the ground, and transfers 
it indoors. In cooling mode, it reverses the process, removing heat 
from inside the building and releasing it outside. This dual function-
ality eliminates the need for separate heating and cooling systems 
which can save building owners money in the long term. Two-way 
heat pumps run completely off electricity and heat and cool spaces 
with high efficiency, making them a great way to reduce emissions 
coming from homes. 
This policy would be adopted as part of a local amendment to the 
building code and would only affect installations of air conditioning 
units in new or existing buildings. Standard AC units would need to 
upgrade efficiency through duct work, air sealing, and other means. 
While this code focuses initially on residential buildings, future work 
may also investigate the cost effectiveness of expanding the code to 
commercial buildings if feasible. 
Electric Infrastructure Impacts 
This policy only affects the installation of central AC units. AC units 
and heat pumps are structurally similar in their operations and foot-
prints; this means that no additional building or electrical changes 
are necessary compared to a standard AC installation. No additional 
electrical upgrades beyond those needed for a standard AC unit 
would be required, so the potentially high costs of panel or service 
upgrades are not applicable to this policy. 
GHG EMISSIONS REDUCTIONS – 2,200 MT CO2e per year by 2030
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
28
805 of 983

--- page 806 ---
Policy Cost 
The installation of a two-way heat pump is highly cost-effective as it 
can both cool and heat a space, effectively replacing both a central 
air conditioner and furnace within a home. This reduces the need 
for separate furnace replacements. According to Peninsula Clean 
Energy and statewide cost analysis, the cost difference between an 
AC and two-way heat pump ranges between no cost difference to 
$1,500. However, Peninsula Clean energy provides a $1,500 rebate 
to offset this cost. Peninsula Clean Energy also offers on-bill financ-
ing through the Zero Percent Loan program28 to cover the up-front 
costs of installing a heat pump, which leads to utility bill savings 
over time due to appliance efficiency. 
Implementing a Two-Way AC policy is particularly advantageous 
given the upcoming Air District Zero NOx requirements.  If a con-
stituent's air conditioner breaks before 2029 and is replaced with a 
standard AC which only provides cooling, they will need to purchase 
a Zero NOx alternative when their furnace eventually breaks after 
2029. Conversely, adopting a Two-Way AC policy in preparation for 
these requirements would ensure that all air conditioner replace-
ments in the next few years have two-way heating and cooling ca-
pabilities. This would prevent homeowners from needing to install 
an additional furnace with savings of up to $10,000 (Appendix C).  
Implementation Steps and Timeline 
 
y
y	 Staff is in the process of evaluating reach code approaches while 
ensuring compliance with AB 130.
 
y
y	 Staff will conduct engagement with the community and 
contractors, evaluate cost-effectiveness studies, and present at a 
City Council study session prior to ordinance development.
28.	Peninsula Clean Energy. 2025. 0% financing & rebates. Available: https://www.peninsula-
cleanenergy.com/residential/financing/
29
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
806 of 983

--- page 807 ---
Policy 2b – Flex Path Reach 
Code at Time of Renovation 
Policy Description  
Under the Flex Path policy the City will require additional energy 
efficiency or electrification actions at time of renovation. The Flex 
Path policy requires new renovations over a specific dollar amount 
to complete an efficiency checklist and includes one to two cost ef-
fective and energy saving approaches in their project. The flex path 
policies do not ban gas and provide flexible pathways for building 
owners to meet the decarbonization requirements.  Flex Path op-
tions include items like:
 
y
y	 Installing a heat pump water heater
 
y
y	 Installing additional insulation 
 
y
y	 Using higher efficiency windows
 
y
y	 Installing a heat pump HVAC unit
 
y
y	 Duct sealing 
 
y
y	 Cool roof
 
y
y	 Floor insulation
Each project would be provided a complete list of demonstrated 
cost savings approaches to choose from. Projects would select the 
most applicable and feasible approaches for their project (hence the 
Flex Path name). For example, a bedroom remodel would likely not 
elect to install a cool roof but may upgrade their floor or wall insula-
tion while a major remodel may elect to install a heat pump water 
heater to take advantage of rebates from Peninsula Clean Energy. 
The final list of measures and the thresholds for project inclusion 
(cost of permit, sq. ft. of project, etc..) would be determined in the 
final ordinance language. 
Electric Infrastructure Impacts 
Since this policy provides many options for compliance, many of 
which do not require electrification, the impacts on electricity infra-
structure are assumed to be low. However, conducting pre-wiring 
at a minimum during renovation is a great way to save money over 
time and prepare for upcoming Air District rules. 
GHG EMISSIONS REDUCTIONS – 730 MT CO2e per year by 2030
Policy Cost 
The Flex Path reach code is also cost-effective. By providing a range 
of retrofits and upgrades that building owners can choose from, this 
policy allows each project owner to pick the most cost-effective and 
efficient options. Based on a conducted cost analysis conducted by 
the Local Codes and Standards team, each check list item would 
need to be demonstrated to have cost savings over the lifespan of 
the upgrade. Costs range from approximately $300 to $4,000 and 
represent a small portion of the overall project costs. To be included 
in the final list each project would need to have a positive return on 
investment, meaning that it saves money overall during the life of 
the project. Furthermore, many homeowners may receive addition-
al savings through incentives provided by PCE, the State (Tech 
rebates) or the Inflation Reduction Act.  While the overall costs are 
expected to be low, the City will also work to develop a hardship 
exemption clause in the ordinance language out of an abundance 
of caution.
Implementation Steps and Timeline 
 
y
y	 Staff is in the process of evaluating reach code approaches while 
ensuring compliance with AB 130.
 
y
y	 Staff will conduct engagement with the community and 
contractors, evaluate cost-effectiveness studies, and present at a 
City Council study session prior to ordinance development.

POLICY 3 – ZERO NOX 
STANDARDS 
Based on community feedback and City Council direction, Zero NOx 
standards were not selected to be pursued at this time. This policy 
is already being pursued by the Bay Area Air District and will begin 
phasing in for covered appliances like water heaters in 2027. The 
costs as well as uncertainty around electric infrastructure upgrades 
like panels and services was too great a concern to speed up the 
timeline for electrification. Community members are still urged to 
follow the guidance in Section 6 Community Electrification Re-
sources, to make a plan as these requirements roll out.

Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy

--- page 809 ---
POLICY 4 - BUILDING 
PERFORMANCE STANDARDS
Policy 4 creates new standards for buildings (Building Performance 
Standards) aimed at increasing energy efficiency, and eventually, 
decarbonizing large buildings as a phased efficiency-focused ap-
proach. In comparison to relatively homogonous single-family and 
small residential buildings, commercial and large multifamily build-
ings can be highly variable in their systems and needs. Commercial 
and large multifamily building heating and cooling systems are also 
frequently oversized, meaning replacing existing gas equipment 
in a like for like manner with electric equipment is not economical. 
Process loads also vary significantly across multifamily and non-res-
idential building types. For these reasons, decarbonizing large 
buildings necessitates a flexible approach where building managers 
can first make strides towards understanding their buildings and 
cost-effective opportunities before committing to a specific ap-
proach. Typically, a BPS Program is implemented in phases where 
buildings first report energy use for several years (benchmarking), 
then perform a retro-commissioning process to identify opportuni-
ties before finally reducing emissions below an established thresh-
old (performance standard).    
 
y
y	 Phase I: Benchmarking (Years 1-2): This initial phase establishes 
a building’s energy performance baseline by comparing 
its energy use intensity (EUI) to similar buildings. Covered 
Buildings (buildings and properties with buildings totaling the 
specified size threshold or larger) report their annual energy 
usage in Energy Star Portfolio Manager. The data collected in 
the benchmarking phase improves building manager and City 
understanding of building energy usage and will inform GHG-
emissions performance standards. Benchmarking also identifies 
underperforming facilities and prioritizes improvements, with 
studies showing benchmarked buildings achieve average 
annual energy savings of 2.4 percent.29 
29.	National Association of State Energy Officials. 2025. Benchmarking and Building Performance Standards. Available: https://www.naseo.org/issues/buildings/benchmarking
 
y
y	 Phase II: Retro-commissioning (Years 3-4): Retro-commissioning 
is a process through which a building’s energy consuming 
systems are analyzed for efficiency and proper operation. The 
retro-commissioning process serves two primary purposes. 
The first is to uncover inefficient operations, increase energy 
efficiency, and save building owners money. The second is to 
identify cost-effective opportunities to reduce building GHG 
emissions through electrification and energy efficiency. 
 
y
y	 Phase III: Performance Standard (Year 4+): Jurisdictions set 
phased energy or emissions targets for GHG reductions over 
time on a square feet basis. The targets are based on data 
collected from the benchmarking phase and can be met 
through a variety of retrofits, such as upgrades, electrification, or 
renewable energy adoption. The most cost-efficient retrofits for 
each building were identified in the retro-commissioning phase. 
Standards often allow flexible compliance paths, such as 
achieving specific EUI thresholds or offsetting emissions via 
clean energy investments.  
Electric Infrastructure  Impacts
This policy does not establish specific requirements and therefore, 
specific infrastructure impacts cannot be identified. The phased-in 
approach of this program has been designed so that buildings own-
ers can collect the necessary data to more thoroughly understand 
their buildings prior to any project requirements. The City will work 
to understand the costs of increasing commercial building energy 
efficiency prior to setting thresholds in Phase III. 
GHG EMISSIONS REDUCTIONS – Variable based on Performance 
Standards Set in Phase III.
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
32
809 of 983

--- page 810 ---
Policy Cost 
The cost of decarbonizing buildings under a Building Performance 
Standard (BPS) policy varies significantly based on factors such as 
building age, equipment types, and energy end uses. This variabil-
ity necessitates a flexible, performance-based approach, allowing 
owners to prioritize cost-effective solutions tailored to their assets. 
The BPS framework’s phased implementation mitigates financial 
burdens: 
 
y
y	 Benchmarking identifies inefficiencies (e.g., leaks, malfunctioning 
systems) 
and 
establishes 
a 
baseline 
for 
improvement. 
Benchmarking reporting costs approximately $3,000 annually. 
 
y
y	 Retro-commissioning pinpoints low-cost operational optimizations 
(e.g., HVAC adjustments, controls recalibration), which typically 
yield 16 percent energy savings at an average cost of $0.30/sq. ft., 
often with payback periods under two years. 
 
y
y	 Performance 
standards are 
deferred 
for 4+ 
years 
post-
benchmarking, 
enabling 
strategic 
capital 
planning 
for 
equipment replacements and system upgrades. 
 
y
y	 Electrification of commercial building electric retrofits will likely 
have to be paired with a suite of demand management, energy 
efficiency, or renewable energy projects to make building 
electrification economical for building owners. Specific cost-
effective system upgrades include heat pump rooftop package 
units, which are the most promising electrification solution for 
commercial building electrification, offering a replicable model 
for a 1:1 equipment swap-out.30 
Implementation Steps and Timeline 
 
y
y	 Staff will conduct additional engagement with multifamily 
property and commercial building owners.
 
y
y	 Analyze available commercial performance data from the CEC
 
y
y	 Staff will present a workplan to City Council prior to ordinance 
development. 
30.	Rocky Mountain Institute. 2022. Medium-Size Commercial Retrofits. Available: https://rmi.org/
insight/economics-of-electrifying-buildings-medium-size-commercial-retrofits/

POLICY 5 - MUNICIPAL 
ELECTRIC-FIRST POLICY 
Policy Description 
This policy establishes a mechanism for local government renova-
tions to go electric first. The policy ensures financial and feasibility 
data is being collected for municipal facilities that will allow the City 
to compare costs to electrify versus replacing with gas equipment. 
This policy has a limited reach as it only applies to municipal build-
ings and GHG reductions potential would depend on the rate of 
equipment failure and availability of replacements. However, it 
demonstrates City leadership on hard-to-electrify existing buildings 
that mirror the commercial sector. Several other cities have adopted 
similar municipal electrification policies. Residents were largely in 
favor of this policy as it would not incur any costs to the community. 
Electric Infrastructure  Impacts 
Electric infrastructure impacts from this policy are expected to be 
variable from low impact to potentially high impact. While some 
appliances may be simple to electrify, others such as boilers or pool 
heaters may require significant upgrades to City owned facilities 
and local grid infrastructure. The City will address these challenges 
on a case-by-case basis. 
GHG EMISSIONS REDUCTIONS – 146 MT CO2e per year by 2030
Policy Cost
This policy enables the City to conduct a comprehensive feasibility 
assessment to evaluate the cost differentials between electrification 
and gas equipment replacement for municipal facilities. By incorpo-
rating exemptions for projects demonstrating technical or financial 
infeasibility, the policy ensures a targeted approach to identifying 
the most cost-effective energy upgrades, consistent with method-
ologies applied to commercial buildings. Implementation costs will 
vary based on building-specific factors, including system age, infra-
structure readiness, and operational requirements. 
As these facilities are City-owned, the municipality retains full 
decision-making authority over capital expenditures, eliminating 
potential equity concerns that might arise in privately owned hous-
ing or commercial properties. Furthermore, since many City owned 
facilities are used by the public such as libraries or park properties, 
creating healthier and more comfortable spaces while lowering 
operational costs provide an equity benefit to the community. These 
costs can be offset with grants, loans, and other incentives provided 
by Peninsula Clean Energy as well as the State and other sources.   
Implementation Steps and Timeline 
 
y
y	 Staff will work to develop a policy and provide an update to City 
Council in Spring of 2026.
 
y
y	 Staff will develop a reasonable cost exemption threshold based 
on lifecycle costs.
 
y
y	 Staff will maintain a database of project costs, hurdles, and 
successes for use in understanding other electrification policies. 
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy

6.	  
COMMUNITY 
ELECTRIFICATION 
RESOURCES 
The City of San Mateo has access to an extensive 
support program provided by Peninsula Clean 
Energy. Peninsula Clean Energy provides a 
customizable home upgrade support service 
that can walk you through the electrification 
process whether it’s due to a program identified 
in the Policy Pathways Section or through a 
voluntary action to improve your health, lower 
your bills, and help address climate change. The 
Home Upgrade Service can help connect you 
to rebates and incentives, provide one-on-one 
support and professional expertise to guide 
your decisions, and even connect you to trusted 
contractors. 
In addition to support, PCE also provides 
zero interest loans, unique incentives just 
for Peninsula Clean Energy customers, and 
electrification specific rates. There are also 
special programs including direct installation 
of electric appliances for low- and median-
income homeowners. These support systems 
can make building electrification easier and 
less expensive. 
Visit Peninsula Clean Energy to choose your 
level of support today. 
Making a plan to electrify can help you plan around future upgrades and 
avoid costly or challenging emergency repairs. Knowing what appliances you 
have that run gas, the size/space available on your panel, and the age/expect-
ed lifespan of each appliance can help you minimize stress and maximize 
savings. Peninsula Clean Energy can help you walk through these steps: 
1.	
Understand your electricity system – What size panel do you have? If 
your panel is smaller than 100amps or you have outdated or unsafe elec-
trical systems (knob and tube or a Zinsco panel)  you may want to start 
planning to replace it. If you have a 100amp panel, but no space, you can 
add a sub-panel instead of replacing the full panel. 
2.	 Consider “Retrofit Ready” appliances – If your panel is out of space or 
you want to save money on running a new 240V circuit (the wires that to 
go the outlet) you can opt for a 120V version which plugs into any outlet. 
There are even new induction stoves that utilize a battery to deliver high 
temperatures quickly without needing to upgrade your wiring.  
3.	 Plan out your appliance replacements – How old are your appliances? 
Water heaters tend to last around 10 years and furnaces 18 years. If have 
an appliance that is already questionable, that it’s a good time to start 
thinking about upgrading to a heat pump.
Electrify San Mateo Sustainable Building Strategy
33
812 of 983

--- page 813 ---
4.	 Get quotes – Work with Peninsula Clean Energy to find a con-
tractor and get quotes to complete your work. Try and get at 
least 3 quotes before moving forward. The data shows a wide 
range of costs for similar projects based on contractor experi-
ence. Peninsula Clean Energy can provide average costs and 
help you understand the quotes. 
5.	 Identify your rebates – Work with Peninsula Clean Energy or 
The Switch is On to see what rebates you qualify for. 
6.	 Consider solar + storage – Adding solar and storage could pro-
vide significant savings in conjunction with an all-electric home. 
While the cost of these systems is high, financing can provide 
on-bill savings from day one. 
While the Policy Pathways Section outlines key policies the City can 
enact to help target the most cost effective and feasible electrifica-
tion opportunities, these policies alone will not reach San Mateo’s 
GHG reduction targets. It will be up to the community to make 
the decisions that will help reduce the impacts of climate change. 
Over 42 percent of nationwide GHG emissions are the result of just 
a handful of kitchen table decisions. What water heater you have 
might not be noticeable in the temperature of your water, but the 
emissions benefits of electrification can be significant. These volun-
tary resources can fill the gap between the City’s identified policies 
and the climate action goals we need to achieve in order to address 
climate change.

Dear Ms. Chow: 
To develop an equitable, implementable, and effective Sustainable Building Strategy (SBS), the City of 
San Mateo (City) first needs to understand the existing building stock and energy usage and develop 
estimates for the various costs associated with building electrification.1 Part of this analysis includes 
investigating the up-front appliance costs2 for electrification, the cost of other upgrades that may be 
required to convert energy usage in buildings from gas to electricity, the financial incentives currently 
available for this work, as well as existing conditions of the building stock within the City and other 
practical opportunities or barriers to electrification of existing buildings. To answer questions about 
up-front costs, available incentives, existing conditions, and other opportunities and barriers, in 
collaboration with the City, Rincon completed a meta-analysis of existing cost studies and reviewed 
several electrification project case studies which have been completed within the City and County of 
San Mateo. This memo summarizes the information gathered from these reviews and case studies.  
This memo is intended to be used in developing educational materials for the community and 
informing future policy development. Data in this report was summarized from existing sources, some 
of which are not specific to the City of San Mateo. However, the findings are of sufficient scale and 
accuracy to help guide this process but not intended as an accurate interpretation of the final cost of 
electrification for any one building in the City. This memo includes the following pieces of analysis, 
which is organized according to San Mateo building stock categories: Single-Family Homes and Low-
Rise Multifamily and High-Rise Multifamily and Nonresidential Building Costs. 
• 
Part 1: Residential Building Segmentation Analysis 
o Building Stock  
o Building Vintage  
o Appliance and Fuel Source Analysis 
 
1 Building electrification refers to the replacement of gas-fired equipment or appliances in a building with electric equipment or appliances. 
2 Up-front costs include the cost to buy a new appliance and have it installed by qualified personnel. Up-front costs include additional costs 
that may come with other upgrades needed to make installation of the new appliance possible (e.g., installation of new wiring to install an 
electric stove, where there was previously only a gas hookup).

memo will also serve as a technical appendix to the final Sustainable Buildings Strategy. 
Part 1: Residential Building Segmentation Analysis 
Single-Family Homes and Low-Rise Multifamily Building Segmentation Analysis  
For this analysis, single-family homes and low rise (3 stories and under) multifamily are discussed 
together. This is because they generally utilize similar equipment and share similar costs for 
electrification. These building types include single-family, duplex, townhouse, rowhouse, and parcels 
with multiple single unit/duplex buildings. Commercial and high rise (over 3 stories) buildings are 
discussed in a later section.  
816 of 983

--- page 817 ---
City of San Mateo 
Building Inventory and Market Segmentation Memorandum (Task 1) 
Building for the Future – City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy (Version 2) 
3 
Significance of Building Vintage 
Building vintage3 can be used to provide a sense of the technologies employed in a particular building. 
While vintage does not tell us if updates have been made, it does provide some idea of potential 
hurdles that might be faced when completing electrification work. Based on anecdotal evidence, 
buildings constructed after 1970 are more likely to have electric panels of 100 amps or more instead 
of lower amp panels or fuse boxes which may require upgrades and may impede electrification work. 
A significant number (70%) of San Mateo’s single-family homes were built before 1970 and could 
potentially need panel upgrades or other electrical work prior to full electrification as shown in Figure 1. 
However, progress on and availability of circuit sharing technologies and retrofit ready (120 volt) heat 
pump products continues to lower the number of buildings which require panel upgrades. Adding an 
electric vehicle (EV) also increases the chance of needing a panel or service upgrade which should be 
considered when looking at whole home electrification.

vintage) are included in Figure 1.

Appliance and Fuel Source Analysis  
To better understand where natural gas is being used in San Mateo’s buildings, an appliance fuel 
source analysis was completed using existing data provided by PCE. The data provided by PCE 
leveraged the Residential Appliance Saturation Survey (RASS) for Climate Zone 3 for most appliance 
types with the exception of heating fuel source.4 Heating fuel source data was based on the American 
Community Survey which utilizes specific San Mateo County Data.5 Heating system types were pulled 
from the RASS Study but normalized using American Community Survey (ACS) fuel source data which 
is more specific to San Mateo County. This normalization process weighted fuel types more heavily 
towards the ACS results for fuel types but utilizes the relevant equipment types from the RASS. This 
data is not specific to the City of San Mateo but provides a good understanding of the fuel and 
appliance types common in the region. Based on this analysis, most gas consumption in residential 
buildings comes from space and water heating, as shown in Figure 3. 
 
4 The California Residential Appliance Saturation Study (RASS) is a comprehensive look at residential energy use. The California Energy 
Commission (CEC) manages the study, which is based on the California Home Energy Survey. The survey collects information from 
residents about appliances, heating and cooling equipment, electric vehicles, and energy. 
5 The American Community Survey (ACS) detailed population and housing information and is intended to helps local officials, community 
leaders, and businesses understand the changes taking place in their communities.

Water Heating Fuel Types 
In San Mateo County the vast majority of water heaters run on natural gas (though most gas water 
heaters also require electricity to operate) as shown in Figure 4.6 Multifamily buildings have the largest 
percentage of electric water heating. A small percentage of buildings within the county also run on 
bottled gas (propane), but these buildings are likely to be in the unincorporated areas away from 
natural gas infrastructure.

As shown in Figure 5, most of the water heaters in San Mateo County are standard gas fired water

common followed by standard electric resistance water heaters, tankless electric varieties and heat 
pumps. Different types of water heaters will have different space and cost impacts on operations. This

Space heating is the second largest gas consumption device in residential buildings. Space heating in 
San Mateo County sees a much broader use of electric appliances especially in multifamily rentals 
and multifamily condos, as shown in Figure 6. Single-family owned and rented buildings still use gas 
most commonly. Some wood and propane use is seen within the County, but is most likely in the 
unincorporated regions.

Air Conditioning Prevalence and Type  
Most residential buildings in San Mateo County do not have air conditioning. Those that do, mostly 
single-family owned homes, use central air conditioning. The lack of air conditioning within the County 
is important to note as projected increases in temperatures related to climate change may increase 
the need for air conditioning installations as a resilience measure. This represents an important 
opportunity for buildings to use a “two-way” air-conditioning system or heat pump. When considering 
the cost of both a furnace and air conditioner, a heat pump is significantly less expensive (see cost 
analysis below).  
Figure 8 
Air Conditioning System Counts by Building Type in San Mateo County  
 
Data Source: Residential Appliance Saturation Survey (RASS)  
Stove Types 
Within San Mateo County, gas and propane (bottled gas) fueled units make up approximately 60% of 
all stoves while electric units make up the remaining 40% as shown in Figure 9. Electric stoves are 
most common in multifamily rental units.

Clothes Dryer Types 
Most residential buildings in San Mateo County have a clothes dryer with dryers being most common 
in single-family units. Electric clothes dryers make up nearly 40% of all dryer use, while only 27% are 
gas. The remaining 34% of units do not have a dryer on site according to the RASS data.  
Figure 11 Clothes Dryer Counts by Building Type in San Mateo County 
 
Data Source: Residential Appliance Saturation Survey (RASS)  
Pools 
Approximately 849 residential buildings (4%) have pools within the City of San Mateo, according to 
PCE. Pools in the City of San Mateo are likely to be heated using gas.

Data on commercial building energy usage by appliance/end use specific to San Mateo or San Mateo 
County was not available at the time of this memorandum’s analysis. However, the California Energy 
Commission published the 2006 California Commercial End-Use Survey (CEUS) which details 
statewide commercial energy use. An updated 2018-2022 CEUS is likely to be released soon, though 
it is currently delayed due to COVID-19 related impacts. Therefore, the 2016 CEUS is the most up-to-
date information available on California’s commercial building energy consumption. CEUS filters 
results by utility which allows for some refinement, compared to the statewide results. The PG&E 
results are summarized below. The full report is no longer on the CEC website, but can be found here.7  
Within the PG&E service area there are just under 2 billion square feet of commercial floor space. The 
building types with the largest floor area are miscellaneous with 23%, large offices with 15% and retail 
with 14%. Total natural gas usage from these buildings is estimated at 565 Million therms per year 
with three building types representing over 54% of total natural gas usage: restaurants (21%) 
miscellaneous (20%) and health (13%). Total electricity and gas usage by end use are shown in 
Figure 13 and Figure 14, respectively.

Part 3: Residential and Commercial Buildings Electrification Cost 
Analysis 
Electrification Up-Front Costs - Single-Family and Low-Rise Multifamily Buildings  
Up-front costs include the costs to purchase an appliance, install it, and make it operational. Up-front 
costs to electrify were analyzed using the most recent and location specific data available. Third party 
vetted sources from local government and community choice aggregator (CCA) reports and data sets 
specific to the City and County of San Mateo were used for most data sources. Sources for each data 
set used in the analysis are summarized in Table 1 below. 
For most up-front costs, median install values were selected to inform the cost analysis. Using median 
costs helped remove outlier quotes from contractors, which would have influenced the average value. 
Quotes for some equipment, such as HVAC units, could vary by as much as $15,000 for the same 
project.  
Where there are exceptions to this data filtering process, due to missing data, data substitution 
methods will be clarified in the relevant cell of the analysis.

Low Rise Multifamily Applicability  
In general, the costs for single-family homes and the costs per unit for low rise multifamily homes (3 
stories and under) will be similar. Low rise multifamily homes tend to use similar equipment and one 
piece of equipment (such as a hot water heater or furnace can serve one or multiple units). In some 
low-rise multifamily units there may be a single larger piece of equipment shared between multiple 
units (an example would be an 80 gallon hot water heater being used by both units in a duplex). 
Therefore, the per unit costs for electrification in Table 3 are assumed to be conservative for 
multifamily buildings which use residential (as opposed to commercial) sized equipment. High rise 
multifamily homes (over 3 stories) look more like commercial buildings with a wide variety of 
equipment and the potential for larger centralized systems, which will be described further in High-
Rise Multifamily and Nonresidential Building Costs.

Up-Front Costs for Electrification 
• 
Key Figure Highlight. Whole home electrification could be less expensive for building owners in 
San Mateo than replacing appliances with gas once rebates are included. 
o Full-home appliance replacement with new gas appliances: $11,012 - $18,51212 
o Before incentives: Full-home appliance replacement with new electric appliances13 marginal 
cost: $13,291- $20,93614 
o After incentives: Full-home appliance (no AC) replacement with new electric appliances 
marginal cost: +$709 in cost savings – $4,83615 and get the benefit of air conditioning.  
o After incentives: Homes with AC + Furnace that need replacing could save up to $2,665 
To understand the up-front costs of electrification, Rincon gathered data through existing analysis 
described in Table 1 to determine the cost to install new gas appliances as well as new electric 
appliances. These costs were all-inclusive, incorporating wiring upgrades, labor costs, appliance costs, 
permitting, and contractor markup.  
Rincon assumed that in an electrification scenario for a home, switching any gas appliances to an 
electric appliance would only occur if the gas appliance no longer functioned or was due for 
replacement (‘burnout’), for other consumer preference reasons (ex. public health, climate 
consciousness), or was voluntarily upgraded to a modern electric option. Because of this electrify on 
replacement scenario, Rincon further determined the marginal cost of the electrification scenario by 
comparing:  
• 
Gas to gas “like for like” scenario- an old gas appliance is replaced with a new similar gas 
appliance.  
• 
Gas to electric “electrification” scenario- an old gas appliance is replaced with a new similar 
electric appliance.  
The marginal cost is the difference between the electrification scenario, and the gas to gas, or “like 
for like” scenario. By focusing on the time of replacement the cost of electrification is the difference 
between a standard gas replacement and an electric upgrade. While some building owners may opt 
to replace their appliances before burnout in order to gain the benefits of electrification (improved air 
quality, lower GHG emissions, bill savings) most people are expected to wait until burnout to replace 
their appliances. Therefore, while both gross and marginal costs are included in this memorandum, 
the marginal cost described in detail in Table 7 should be the focus for determining cost effectiveness. 
In reviewing the San Mateo existing data, there were four key factors that determine costs for home 
electrification that were apparent in the literature and data:  
• 
Avoiding panel upgrades through a watt diet16, panel management, or home electrification plan 
could result in significant savings ($3,700) for home electrification, though building owners may 
want to consider that installing future charging capacity for an electric vehicle may necessitate a 
panel upgrade anyway.17 
 
12 Low scenario does not include AC, high scenario does include AC (in addition to a gas powered furnace) 
13 Also known as ‘electrification’ 
14 This scenario does not include air conditioning, which would subtract $7,500 from both scenarios as a heat pump would displace the 
need to install an AC in addition to a gas fired furnace.  
15 Low scenario = Total costs with no panel upgrade with low-cost HVAC scenario and no AC, median HVAC scenario 
16 Watt Diet refers to the practice of minimizing the peak electricity demand through smart chargers and other technology to avoid a panel 
upgrade.  
17 https://www.smcsustainability.org/energy/decarbonizing-homes/

17 
• 
HVAC appliance costs and HVAC installation costs made up the largest single cost of electrifying a 
building. The cost range for HVAC installation was also the largest with quotes for the same HVAC 
system ranging by as much as $15,000. This variability emphasizes the need to get multiple 
quotes is very important.18 
• 
The large cost range for HVAC equipment necessitated the Project Team running both a low-cost 
and median cost for electric heat pump HVAC System, which had a $3,944 difference between 
scenarios.  
• 
Labor costs and quotes from contractors are likely the most expensive and variable factor in home 
electrification, not appliance cost.19  
Electrification Scenario: Gas to Gas “Like for Like” Upfront Costs 
In the gas ‘like for like’ scenario (base case), where it is assumed that the building owner replaces 
existing natural gas appliances with new gas-fired appliances, the typical whole-home cost in San 
Mateo, without air conditioning20 is $11,012. Once costs for air conditioning are added in, the total 
projected cost to replace all appliances that run on gas plus an AC is $18,512. This analysis 
demonstrates the amount of money that building owners would spend as a baseline if they planned 
on replacing their appliances.  
These costs describe all up-front costs for installation, appliance, and labor, by taking the average of 
collected median values for appliance types, while aggregating appliance types by end use (with the 
exception of AC, described in the note below Table 2).

Electrification Scenario: Gas to Electric – No Incentives Applied Upfront Cost 
In the gas to electric scenario (electric scenario) where it is assumed that the building owner replaces 
existing natural gas appliances with new electric appliances, the total whole-home electrification cost 
to replace all gas-powered appliances with all-electric, with no incentives or rebates applied, is:  
Assuming that home electrification does not require an electric panel upgrade22:  
• 
$24,303-$28,248 
Assuming that home electrification does require an electric panel upgrade (particularly for homes with 
electric panels less than 100 amps):  
• 
$28,003-$31,948 
The difference between both electric panel scenarios described above is the difference between the 
‘low’ and ‘median’ heat pump scenario, which was broken out to detail the significant difference in 
installation bids for electric heat pump HVAC systems. This discrepancy in installation bids from 
contractors stems from home-specific needs for ductwork upgrades, locations to house HVAC systems, 
and labor costs which are highly variable depending on the contractor. These two scenarios show that 
installation costs for HVAC and the potential need for an electric panel upgrade are two of the largest 
influencers of cost in home electrification for the City of San Mateo.  
These costs can be significantly or partially offset by stacked federal and local incentives as described 
in the Current Incentives for Electrification section below. Whole home costs for replacing gas 
appliances with electric with no incentives applied are shown below in Table 3. 
Table 3 
Electrification Scenario: Cost to Replace Gas Appliances with Electric 
Appliances (No Incentives, Total Costs)

22 As discussed in the BayREN Decarbonizing Single-Family Homes Case Study, potentially costly electric panel upgrades are frequently 
recommended by contractors without first understanding the many potential avenues to avoid this cost through maximum usage of 
existing service capacity. The amp cutoff for electric panel upgrade is widely understood to be 100 amps or higher- a building with below 
100 amps likely will need a panel upgrade to accommodate all-electric appliances. The BayREN Decarbonization Case Study recommends 
several ways for “panel optimization”, including making a whole home electrification plan, and investing in circuit sharing and pausing 
infrastructure instead of a panel upgrade.

Electrification Scenario: Marginal Costs (Difference Between Gas and Electric Install 
Costs) 
‘Marginal costs’ for electrification are at the core of this analysis as they describe the cost difference 
between the assumed investment needed to replace a gas appliance that would happen during 
‘burnout’ or equipment upgrade with all-electric technologies. Put simply, marginal costs describe how 
much more expensive it would be to go all-electric, rather than replace non-functioning appliances with 
gas. Zeroing out this difference between electric and gas install costs would mean that electrifying 
does not increase the cost burden on building owners in San Mateo when compared to the baseline 
‘gas for gas’ replacement, addressing a key cost and equity hurdle for developing existing building 
electrification policies.  
Assuming that the home electrification scenario replaced a combined furnace and AC with a heat 
pump23 , the marginal cost to electrify (without incentives) would range from:  
• 
$5,791-$9,736 
Assuming that the home electrification scenario does not have AC/does not want AC, and also does 
not require a panel upgrade, marginal costs to electrify before rebates would range from:  
• 
$13,291-$17,236 
o This cost range for both scenarios is based on the install difference between low and median 
heat pump HVAC systems  
o If an electric panel upgrade was added, $3,700 would be added to each cost range scenario 
Marginal costs for replacing gas appliances with electric, with no incentives applied are shown below 
in Table 4 by appliance type and end use, and with different full-home electrification scenarios with 
and without air conditioning cost savings, panel upgrades, and low and median cost heat pump 
scenarios included in Table 5.

Current Incentives for Electrification  
Depending on project availability, current appliance electrification incentives available through the 
Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), Peninsula Clean Energy (PCE), BayREN, TECH Clean California when 
combined make building owners eligible for $21,600 in rebates, which are summarized in Table 6. 
Incentives take the form of both rebates and tax incentives. Tax incentives through the IRA require the 
building owner to have some tax burden to reduce.

Federal Electrification Incentives under the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) & the High 
Efficiency Electric Home Rebate Act28 
The IRA was signed into law on August 2022, ushering in significant federal policy support and funding 
for building decarbonization. The High Efficiency Electric Home Rebate Act (HEEHRA) is the official 
name of the IRA’s rebate program for electric home technology including heat pumps, electrical wiring, 
and induction stoves, which will be available starting in 2023. Low-income households up to 80% of 
Area Median Income (AMI) can access 100% of upfront discount incentives, while moderate-income 
households from 80-150% AMI can access up to 50% of the upfront discount, in addition to tax credits 
that vary based on household income. Total HEEHRA discounts are capped at $14,000 across all 
electrification projects.  
Cost reductions under HEEHRA are not included in this analysis as details are still developing and the 
incentives may not be available to everyone, as City of San Mateo building owners are generally 
classified as high income. However, for low and moderate-income households up to 150% of AMI in 
San Mateo, IRA incentives may further increase the effectiveness of local electrification incentives 
available via PCE and BayREN, and help to close the gap for appliances where subsidies don’t always 
zero-out marginal costs, like HVAC systems, dryers, and induction ranges.  
Electrification Upfront Costs with Incentives Applied  
In the final piece of the cost analysis, we have applied the rebates described in Table 6 to the marginal 
costs described in Table 4 to see if rebates and tax incentives can make electric technologies the 
same, or less costly than the gas scenario. For this analysis, the referenced $5,500 total home 
electrification rebate provided by Energy Smart Homes California was not applied since it requires a 
complete home electrification project at one time. Since this analysis focuses on time of replacement, 
this rebate was not applied to the analysis.  
The analysis shows that with the low-cost scenario for HVAC applied, in a house without AC, when all 
incentives are stacked, total costs without a panel upgrade, electrification could save building owners 
in San Mateo $709. If a home has both a furnace and AC to replace, fully electrifying would save 
$2,665 in marginal costs even with a panel upgrade, since one heat pump would replace two 
appliances.  
On the high end of the marginal costs + incentives range, electrifying a home without AC, with a panel 
upgrade, under the Median HVAC cost analysis scenario, would be $4,836 more expensive than a gas-
for-gas scenario. These results are summarized in Table 7. 
PCE Zero Percent Loans  
In addition to these rebates, PCE offers up to $10,000 in zero percent loans to electrify gas water 
heaters, gas heaters/furnaces, panel upgrades, and efficiency upgrades (insulation, air sealing, duct 
replacements, and smart thermostats). These loans allow PCE-customers who are property owners to 
borrow up to $10,000 in 0% APR loans to electrify with a 2-10 year payback period.29

High-Rise Multifamily and Nonresidential Building Costs 
To better understand costs and other constraints related to multifamily and nonresidential building 
electrification, Rincon reviewed best-available existing resources on this topic, namely, the PCE 2035 
Decarbonization Plan (2022), and The Rocky Mountain Institute The Economics of Electrifying 
Buildings: Medium Size Commercial Retrofits (2022)30. This document review is also supplemented 
by original contractor interviews conducted by Rincon for the upcoming Sacramento Electric Buildings 
Strategy (2022). Though not strictly City of San Mateo specific, the technical considerations of 
challenges to electrify oversized buildings are consistent between Sacramento and San Mateo, so are 
referenced here as recent region-specific resources for commercial electrification in Northern 
California. 
Electrification Up-Front Costs and Other Considerations 
While single-family and low rise multifamily residential buildings tend to use similar equipment and 
operate in similar ways, commercial buildings and high-rise multifamily buildings tend to be highly 
variable. In original research conducted by Rincon for the City of Sacramento in the summer of 2022, 
all interviewees31 communicated that larger multifamily and nonresidential buildings are more 
complicated to electrify than single-family homes and estimating costs is typically only possible on a 
project-by-project basis. This is because for most larger buildings, replacing a gas system with a 
similarly sized electric system is cost-prohibitive and therefore, often requires building re-engineering 
to determine appliance replacement and sizing options. For this reason, a detailed cost analysis for 
larger multifamily and commercial buildings was not developed and included here. Smaller buildings 
 
30 https://rmi.org/insight/economics-of-electrifying-buildings-medium-size-commercial-retrofits/ 
31 To better understand costs and other constraints related to multifamily and nonresidential building electrification, Rincon conducted 
interviews with three energy engineering consultants working on large building decarbonization in Sacramento. Five additional 
interviewees were identified through the City’s contact network to speak to this topic, including energy managers and directors of UC Davis 
Health. Sacramento Municipal Utility District, Brighton Energy, Association for Energy Affordability, TRC Companies.

City of San Mateo

Building for the Future – City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy (Version 2) 
24 
with domestic-sized space and water heating end uses, such as small to medium-sized retail or service 
buildings, have similar electrification costs as seen for single-family homes.  
However, some equipment types are similar across building types and the cost to electrify can be more 
readily analyzed at a high level. The PCE analysis notes that packaged commercial HVAC equipment, 
namely, packaged Single Zone (SZ) Gas Furnace with Cooling, are easy to electrify, as existing building 
2-way A/C ordinances can cover most units without the requirement for incentives. The PCE Analysis 
also notes that the Packaged SZ Gas Furnace with Cooling offers a sizable opportunity to electrify, with 
5,441 Packaged SZ Gas Furnace with Cooling units across the County of San Mateo as shown below 
in Figure 17. Because of the relative cost parity and ease of adoption, there is already uptake of all-
electric packaged SZ heat pumps, accounting for nearly 20% of analyzed existing commercial HVAC 
Equipment, as shown previously in Figure 15. 
Figure 17 PCE 2035 Decarbonization Plan Commercial HVAC Equipment Analysis 
 
PCE analyzed commercial rooftop packaged unit costs, finding that rooftop heat pumps are nearly 
identical to gas-fired rooftop packaged units, even without additional incentives, as shown below in 
Figure 18. This indicates that local policy and potential new incentives to retrofit using heat pump 
RTUs could be an impactful and cost effective strategy to achieve widespread commercial building 
electrification.  
838 of 983

--- page 839 ---
City of San Mateo 
Building Inventory and Market Segmentation Memorandum (Task 1) 
Building for the Future – City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy (Version 2) 
25 
Figure 18 PCE 2035 Decarbonization Plan Commercial Rooftop Package Unit Cost 
Analysis 
 
The Rocky Mountain Institute (RMI) ‘The Economics of Electrifying Buildings: Medium-Size Commercial 
Retrofits’ Report notes that local policy, or expectations of new local building electrification policies, 
will be a key driver in advancing commercial energy electrification, as portfolio owners will want to 
avoid stranded assets unless they undertake electrification. The RMI report focused on the 
electrification of HVAC systems from rooftop units (RTUS) with Heat Pump Rooftop Package Units 
(RTUs), on a prototypical 50,000 sq ft office building.  
The RMI report modeled 5 cases to assess the economics of RTU retrofits: Case 1: No electrification, 
Case 2: Partial electrification, Case 3: Full electrification, Case 4: efficient electrification, Case 5: 
efficient electrification + demand management, Case 6: Efficient electrification + demand 
management and PV. These cases, found that in the cases of more temperate climates like Las Vegas 
and Seattle, building owners could achieve either neutral or positive 20-year net present value through 
full electrification (Seattle), or with full electrification with demand management and solar 
photovoltaics (Las Vegas), as shown in Figure 19. 
839 of 983

--- page 840 ---
City of San Mateo 
Building Inventory and Market Segmentation Memorandum (Task 1) 
Building for the Future – City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy (Version 2) 
26 
Figure 19  RMI Report Exhibit 3: the 20-year Net Present Value of Each Case by City32 
 
The results from the RMI study have three important San-Mateo-relevant takeaways, which is that 1) 
electrification policy, with electrification-specific prescriptive language for commercial building owners 
will be critical in actually electrifying commercial buildings, and 2) electrification of commercial building 
electric retrofits will likely have to be paired with a suite of demand management, energy efficiency, or 
renewable energy projects in order to make building electrification economical for building owners, 
and 3) heat pump rooftop package units are the most promising electrification solution for commercial 
building electrification, offering a replicable model for a 1:1 equipment swap-out.  
Case Studies 
In addition to the PCE analysis, Rincon identified all-electric retrofit case study projects related to 
offices, schools, and restaurants. Schools (primary and secondary), restaurants (full-service and quick-
service), and hospitals represent large commercial gas users in the City of San Mateo. Case studies 
were pulled from The Redwood Energy Pocket Guide to All-Electric Retrofits,33 the William J Worthen 
(WJW) Foundation Building Decarbonization Practice Guide Volume 5,34 and the California State 
University (CalState) Decarbonization Framework,35 and summarized here. Rincon will continue to look 
for more resources in these areas as the project progresses.

Building for the Future – City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy (Version 2) 
27 
Office 
IMMIX LAW OFFICE, PORTLAND, OR (11,615 SQUARE FEET; ALL-ELECTRIC RETROFIT) 
The building utilizes a highly efficient dedicated outside air system (DOAS) with heat recovery 
ventilation (HRV) for their HVAC system, which separates heating and cooling from the ventilation 
system to allow for optimal control of each of these critical building functions. The new HVAC system 
is comprised of a 16-ton Mitsubishi variable refrigerant flow (VRF) and four Ventacity VS1000RT HRVs. 
The HVAC system heats and cools 30 office spaces, five conference rooms, a lunchroom, exercise 
room, two restrooms, and open common spaces. The project resulted in increased occupant comfort, 
improved indoor air quality, lower energy bills (between $300 to $700 per month to heat, cool, and 
ventilate the building), saved roof space, and precise temperature and humidity control. The building 
has seen a 63% reduction in energy usage.36 
100 AVENUE OF THE AMERICAS, MANHATTAN, NY (>20,000 SQUARE FEET; ALL-ELECTRIC RETROFIT) 
This building was retrofitted to meet all winter space heating needed for exterior offices with waste 
heat from the air conditioning in the interior core of the building during the winter through an all-electric 
multizone heat pump HVAC system. Multizone systems are best for larger (20,000 square feet or 
more), more diverse buildings. While only 6% of HVAC systems in California are multizone systems that 
could be replaced with multizone heat pump technology, these buildings represent 37% to 75% of the 
GHG emissions from all buildings. They are technically complex to design and require highly skilled 
installers but also represent the largest GHG reduction opportunities.37 
SONOMA CLEAN POWER HEADQUARTERS, SANTA ROSA, CA (15,000 SQUARE FEET; ALL-ELECTRIC RETROFIT) 
The building was originally built in 1979 in Downtown Santa Rosa and retrofitted with all new 
insulation, all-new windows, all-new siding, and all-new mechanical systems. As part of the retrofit, the 
building installed Daikin rooftop units with variable air volume delivery and thermofusers at the zone 
level. The project also included its own microgrid and electric vehicle charging, which required an 
electrical service upgrade.38 
School 
CALIFORNIA STATE UNIVERSITY, CALIFORNIA 
The CSU Chancellor’s Office released a building decarbonization framework for the CSU system, which 
includes tools to evaluate central plan equipment, thermal overlap calculations for each campus, and 
a life cycle cost calculation tool for different decarbonization strategies. The framework provides all 23 
campuses with a technical roadmap for replacing fossil fuel infrastructure with clean, electrified 
alternatives. The associated heat recovery potential and natural gas reduction at each campus was 
analyzed. The Sacramento CSU campus currently uses 78,164 million British Thermal Units per hour 
(MBTU) for cooling and 52,739 MBTU for heating. The heat recovery potential estimated for the 
building after adding thermal energy storage on site is 64% and the estimated natural gas reduction 
potential is 422,000 therms.39

28 
CHATHAM UNIVERSITY EDEN HALL CAMPUS, PITTSBURGH, PA (ALL-ELECTRIC NEW CONSTRUCTION) 
The campus is the world’s first fully self-sustained and zero net energy (ZNE) university campus. The 
campus includes 46 geothermal wells, an on-campus water treatment site, and a 40-acre farm. The 
campus kitchen was designed based on extensive energy analysis focusing on commercial kitchen 
equipment and HVAC systems. The analysis showed that induction equipment would reduce total 
kitchen energy consumption by over 50% compared to a traditional gas kitchen. The campus also 
installed demand-based exhaust hoods with integrated heat recovery, a geo-exchange heat pump, and 
radiant heating and cooling systems for a low-energy HVAC system. The facility operates with an energy 
usage of approximately 60% below the typical full-service restaurant.40  
Restaurant 
OYSTERMAN SEAFOOD BAR & KITCHEN, LONDON, ENGLAND; RETROFIT (INDUCTION UNITS) 
The restaurant switched from gas to induction units, which allowed the restaurant to serve guests at 
a significantly faster rate. Gas ranges and fryers are half as efficient at transferring heat as electric 
ranges and fryers (35% vs. 75%), and electric ranges boil water twice as fast as gas ranges. Electric 
kitchens, therefore, use half or less as much heat to cook food and require half as much air 
conditioning. Electric equipment also takes less space to do the same amount of cooking, making it 
easier for a kitchen to expand its cooking capacity. In addition to these benefits, Oysterman also 
experienced fewer chef burns with their induction burners and found cleaning to be much easier, 
ultimately reducing staff turnover.41 
BENIHANA JAPANESE STEAK HOUSE, VARIOUS LOCATIONS, U.S.; RETROFIT (INDUCTION GRILL) 
The first Benihana steakhouse was started in New York City in 1964 and has since grown to 116 
restaurants. Benihana performs teppanyaki cooking on steel grills. Recently the restaurant chain 
converted its gas teppan grills to electric resistance and induction grills, which can cook meats faster, 
hotter, and more efficiently than gas while reducing the risk of uncontrolled fires.42 
HOTEL MARCEL, NEW HAVEN, CT; NEW ALL-ELECTRIC CONSTRUCTION 
The hotel has an all-electric commercial kitchen, which uses induction, including an induction wok 
setup.43 Induction woks are not common in American commercial kitchens, however, they are 
becoming increasingly popular in Mainland China, Taiwan, Hong Kong, and Macau. Many commercial 
options exist on the market for induction cookers that can accommodate concave pans (such as woks). 
15 million induction cookstoves were sold in Mainland China in 2020, with a growth of nearly 4% from 
the previous year.44  
MICROSOFT REDMOND CAMPUS CAFETERIA, REDMOND, WA; NEW ALL-ELECTRIC CONSTRUCTION 
The Microsoft Redmond Campus serves over 12,000 meals a day in 77,000 square feet of all-electric 
kitchens. The project had to overcome barriers such as equipment availability, throughput

considerations, and station design. Microsoft has noted a positive response from the industry in the 
past few years enabling the transition for them.45 
High-Rise Multifamily 
BAYVIEW TOWER, SEATTLE, WA 
The 100 unit high rise multifamily building was retrofit to be all electric using the Origin heat pump 
water heater system that provides all needed water heating components (heat pump, storage tank, 
valves, controls, etc..) on one skid to maximize ease of installation. The system is expected to reduce 
energy use by 59%. 46 
ELIZABETH JAMES HOUSE, SEATTLE, WA 
Elizabeth James House is a four story, 60 unit low income senior apartment building built in 1968. 
This project had a central plan of four ECO2 heat pumps, with three existing 120 gallon tanks in a 
series swing configuration. In addition, the system kept the three electric water heaters, and a 175 
gallon swing tank was installed.29 
Recommendations from Interviewees 
As part of the interview process for previous work with the City of Sacramento, Rincon asked 
contractors what recommendations they have for the City or utility provider for reducing barriers to 
electrification in the City. While some of these recommendations may be specific to the City of 
Sacramento, many of these recommendations hold for the City of San Mateo, to account for the 
viewpoint of contractors and building developers dealing with complex commercial building stock. 
These recommendations included: 
• 
Alleviate the burden on the customer for upgrading older, last-mile equipment such as 
transformers and services. Having to upgrade this equipment can kill a project. SMUD (in San 
Mateo, PG&E) could identify transformers that are already at peak capacity and deal with them 
pro-actively, or identify grant funds to help cover these costs. 
• 
More workforce training and work with the unions is needed to prepare the workforce for 
electrification work. 
• 
Encourage projects to build-in more hot water storage to help avoid an increased peak electrical 
load. 
• 
Develop policy to avoid over-estimation of panel needs, which can trigger larger-scale electrical 
upgrades that are prohibitively expensive. Default calculations on equipment tend to overestimate 
peak load and using real data from a building instead can help avoid ballooning expenses. 
• 
Adjusted rate structures could be an incentive for early electrification adopters, since this would 
help guarantee on-bill savings in many cases. Peak demand for commercial customers currently 
sets their billing rate. Many customers are on the edge of hitting their peak demand charge and 
electrification would bump them into a higher tier, creating a disincentive to electrify. A specific 
commercial electrification rate with a discount would help remove this barrier. This suggestion 
item would require coordination with PCE.  
 
45 William J Worthen Foundation. March 2022. The Building Decarbonization Practice Guide Volume 5: All-Electric Residential + 
Commercial Kitchens. 
46 https://www.redwoodenergy.net/publications/redwood-energys-pocket-guide-to-all-electric-commercial-retrofits 
843 of 983

--- page 844 ---
City of San Mateo 
Building Inventory and Market Segmentation Memorandum (Task 1) 
Building for the Future – City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy (Version 2) 
30 
• 
Expedited permits could create an incentive for commercial customers to electrify, especially on 
smaller jobs. Ideally, the permit could be filled out online over-the-counter with a simple set of 
questions with a permit turn-around rate of 24 hours. 
• 
A building performance standards approach could work well for commercial buildings. San 
Francisco’s program is recommended as a model. San Francisco’s program is self-reporting but is 
validated. Buildings over 10,000 square feet are required to complete a level 1 audit; buildings 
over 50,000 square feet are required to complete a level 2 audit, and reporting requirement is 
every five years. Setting up the reporting system online with simple report requirements and very 
low rigor at the start would help get the most buildings into the system. 
• 
Releasing a building performance standards program under something other than an 
electrification strategy may be the best tactic. This approach could appear more feasible, and avoid 
misunderstandings on potential for mandatory upgrades. 
Current Incentives

the City of San Mateo from PCE, TECH, and BayREN are summarized in Table 8. All residential 
appliance incentives described in Table 6 should also apply to multifamily residents if the applicant 
for rebates is also the owner of the apartment, in the case of PCE Incentives.47

On-Bill Costs  
On-bill costs are another important piece of the overall economics of electrification. Understanding on-
bill costs requires more complex modeling due to the change in electricity pricing during a day/season 
as well as the many different electricity rates that are available. However, Peninsula Clean Energy and 
Silicon Valley Clean Energy partnered to complete an in-depth analysis of their customers cost savings 
before and after electrification. The results of the analysis show that on average customers save 
between $20-$30 on their bills after electrification. These savings are driven primarily by heat pump 
HVAC and water heating.48 This is shown in Figure 20.  
 
48 Bill Impacts of Home Electrification Study - Feb 2025

Figure 20 Average On-bill Savings for Peninsula Clean Energy Customers 
 
 
In addition, Peninsula Clean Energy conducted case studies on real buildings which they had electrified 
in the City of San Mateo. Of those buildings one saved $11 per month on their energy bills and the 
other saved $42 per month. It is also important to note that these buildings did not have air 
conditioning before the switch. The results of these two case studies as well as several more from 
across the peninsula are included below in Figure 21. 
Figure 21 Cost Savings After Electrification Case Studies from Peninsula Clean Energy

Subject: Summary of Conducted Community Engagement for Electrify City of San Mateo 
A major component of the Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable Buildings Strategy is a robust community 
engagement process. This project aims to create an actionable roadmap with steps for City of San 
Mateo to electrify buildings based on effectiveness and equity criteria, cost-effectiveness, best 
available science and data, and community feedback. Community outreach and public input are 
fundamental to shaping effective, equitable, and successful decarbonization policies. This 
memorandum summarizes the engagement conducted by both City staff and Rincon Consultants 
between Spring 2023 and Spring 2025. 
Executive Summary of all Outreach  
Outreach has been conducted in two phases to understand community priorities, concerns, and 
needed support for successful policy implementation. During Phase I of engagement, the City 
presented at multiple meetings to gather feedback on the equity and effectiveness criteria and 
educate the community about costs and benefits of building electrification. This included a meeting 
with the Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission, a City Council meeting, five General Plan Town 
Hall meetings, and an online survey. After Phase I of engagement, the initial suite of policy pathways 
was narrowed down to five policy pathways to advance existing building electrification within San 
Mateo. Phase II of engagement aimed at further refining these priority policies for inclusion within the 
final Electrify San Mateo: Sustainable Buildings Strategy. During Phase II, the community engagement 
efforts included an in-person workshop and an additional virtual workshop for community members 
which focused on the five policy pathways, a virtual information session on Building Performance 
Standards for commercial building owners, presentations at City Commissions and community and 
stakeholder meetings, and an online survey.  
Throughout the community engagement process, there have been polarized responses to building 
electrification. While most constituents care about addressing the climate crisis and agree that the 
City should pursue existing building electrification in some form, many residents also expressed 
concerns.  
The primary constituent values identified during Phase I and Phase II engagement that are motivating 
building electrification included: 
• 
Urgent need to address the climate crisis is a priority issue 
• 
Health and air quality benefits of building decarbonization 
• 
Need for local action and the City to act now 
• 
Availability of electric equipment incentives and rebates to support property owners  
The primary concerns identified during Phase I and Phase II engagement included:  
848 of 983

--- page 849 ---
City of San Mateo 
Summary of Conducted Community Engagement 
2 
• 
Upfront costs of electric equipment  
• 
On-bill or operational costs of electric equipment 
• 
Installation and electrical infrastructure challenges 
• 
Grid reliability and resilience 
• 
Lack of education surrounding electrification  
In general, the results of the community outreach conducted by the City point to a general desire to 
address climate change and decarbonize buildings tempered by concerns about cost and feasibility. 
Based on these findings, a focus on policies that support decarbonization when cost effective and 
efficient while providing flexible alternatives for particularly challenging cases should be prioritized.  
Below is a detailed summary of outcomes and themes from each engagement event. A record of public 
comments and photos is listed at the end of this memorandum. 
Community Engagement Phase I: Community Concerns and Values  
During Phase I of engagement, the City held multiple events to gather feedback on the equity and 
effectiveness criteria and educate the community about costs and benefits of building electrification. 
The goal of this phase was to both provide information to the community on what building 
electrification is and why the City is pursuing it, as well as learn about the core values and concerns 
the community has around upgrading their homes and businesses. This included a meeting with the 
Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission, a meeting with the Equity and Inclusion Task Force, a 
City Council meeting, five General Plan Town Hall meetings, and an online survey. 
General Plan Town Hall Meetings 
In Fall of 2023, five General Plan Town Hall meetings were held to solicit feedback on the City’s General 
Plan Update. The General Plan Town Hall events were in-person events located throughout the 
community, one event in each of San Mateo’s five districts. At each outreach event, doors opened at 
5:30 pm to allow attendees to view poster boards (example at end of memorandum) on various topics 
(circulation, land use, safety, noise, etc.) with a formal presentation on the Draft General Plan at 7:00 
pm. An average of 60 community members attended each event. 
The project team had a poster board summarizing the Sustainable Buildings Strategy project effort. 
Community members were very engaged at the Sustainable Buildings Strategy poster board. Staff 
received a mix of both supportive feedback for working on electrification requirements as well as 
concerns about costs related to electrification.  
Diversity Equity and Inclusion Task Force Presentation  
On September 22, 2023, presented to the City’s Diversity Equity and Inclusion Task Force. Feedback 
from the Task Force included: the need to include education, data, and storytelling in outreach. The 
Task Force’s biggest concerns regarding electrification were related to the electric power outages and 
costs to homeowners. The Task Force also helped identify follow up opportunities for partnerships and 
project outreach. 
Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission Meeting 
At the September 13, 2023 Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission meeting, staff and the 
consultant team presented the building inventory and market segmentation analysis as well as 
849 of 983

--- page 850 ---
City of San Mateo 
Summary of Conducted Community Engagement 
3 
building electrification cost analysis. Over 120 written public comments were submitted for the item 
with the majority in support of decarbonization. 24 verbal comments were also provided at the 
meeting. Proponents of decarbonization support electrification policy efforts to reduce gas use in the 
community to combat climate change, improve air quality, and improve health. Proponents also 
encouraged the leveraging of incentives and rebates for electrification.1 For those opposed, the 
number one concern about electrification is the costs to the property owner. Other concerns include 
power outages, growth of the grid to accommodate future electric buildings, and passed on costs to 
renters.  
Survey 
The project team also launched an online survey to assess community priorities for building 
electrification that was open December 2023 through January 10, 2024. Results of the online survey 
were shared at the January 16, 2024 City Council Study Session. A summary of the survey results is 
included below. The survey had a total of 194 respondents from a wide range of socioeconomic 
backgrounds as shown in Figure 1 and Figure 2 .  
Figure 1 
Household Income of Survey Respondents

The Phase I survey primarily focused on what priorities and concerns the community had relating to 
buildings and building decarbonization. As shown in Figure 3, affordable energy costs was the highest-
ranking quality for San Mateo Buildings, followed by a tie between Health and Safety and Resilience. 
Climate and sustainability ranked fourth followed by Modern Appliances and Functionality.  
Respondents also provided feedback on their biggest concerns regarding building electrification in 
San Mateo. Figure 4 shows that increasing energy bills was the largest concern relating to building 
electrification. This concern was followed by concern over increased housing/rent costs as the second 
most common concern. Respondents also ranked difficulty in accessing rebates (3rd) and building 
electrification ignoring other more pressing building issues (4th) as primary issues that would need to 
be addressed.
