These survey results highlighted major concerns of building electrification of both upfront and on-bill 
cost, reliability concerns, and challenges accessing the funding and financing opportunities meant to 
decrease the cost of electrification. These topics were carried through into Phase II of outreach as the 
project team worked to refine the policy options based on community feedback.  
853 of 983

--- page 854 ---
City of San Mateo 
Summary of Conducted Community Engagement 
7 
City Council Meeting  
In January of 2024, Rincon Consultants and City staff provided a presentation to City Council on the 
Phase I results. The presentation focused on the results of the building segmentation analysis, cost 
analysis, engagement to date, and initial policy recommendations. During this meeting five policy 
options were provided for City Council feedback including:  
1. Continue adopted reach codes and focus on outreach and incentives (Business as Usual) 
2. Explore and adopt new reach codes to encourage and require electrification  
3. Require electric equipment upon replacement ahead of the Bay Area Air District’s Zero NOx 
requirements  
4. Establish Building Performance Standards for commercial buildings  
5. Adopt an electric-first policy for City facilities  
City Council provided feedback which included direction for City Staff to continue exploring the 
feasibility, cost, and community support for each of the five options.  
Community Engagement Phase II: Policy Pathways Feedback 
Following direction from City Council, City staff with support from Rincon Consultants, conducted 
additional outreach on the five policy strategies presented to City Council during Phase I. Phase II 
outreach events included an in-person community workshop, virtual community workshop, virtual 
meeting with commercial building owners, presentations to community and developer groups, and 
several upcoming Commission meetings. A second survey was also developed and distributed through 
these and other City channels. The second survey was developed to get more detailed information 
about support for/concern around the five policy pathways.  
Each community meeting and survey was advertised to the community through multiple avenues 
including the City’s social media channels, e-newsletter, and at City events, and to individuals who 
attended prior events.  
The following sections detail the feedback received from the community during each engagement 
opportunity.  
In-Person Community Feedback Meeting  
Feedback Meeting Summary  
The in-person community feedback meeting was advertised to the community through multiple 
avenues including the City’s social media channels, City e-newsletter, and direct emails. There were 
20 attendees.  
The in-person community feedback meeting took place on March 5th, 2025 from 7:00 p.m. to 8:00 
p.m. at the San Mateo Public Library. The goals of the meeting were for participants to: 
1) Understand the definition and importance of existing building electrification in the context of 
climate change and the City of San Mateo’s climate goals 
2) Note an overview and timeline of the City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy 
3) Recognize previously shared concerns and solutions about existing building electrification  
4) Be introduced to the five policy options and how each would impact the community  
854 of 983

--- page 855 ---
City of San Mateo 
Summary of Conducted Community Engagement 
8 
5) Share community feedback on the policy options through an interactive “Sailboat” Activity and 
Voting Activity  
A total of 25 community members attended the feedback meeting. City of San Mateo Sustainability 
Analyst, Andrea Chow, began the meeting and introduced Ryan Gardener, Director of Climate Action 
and Adaptation at Rincon Consultants, who gave a 25-minute presentation on goals one through four 
described above. Community members had the opportunity to ask questions a various points during 
the presentation.  
After the presentation, community members were split into three groups to participate in the Sailboat 
Activity facilitated by a City or Rincon staff member. Each group contained a variety of members 
including industry professionals like contractors, climate activists, students, and renters and 
homeowners who have electrified portions of their own homes. The Sailboat Activity helped community 
members brainstorm electrification challenges and support solutions to help the City achieve its 
decarbonization goals. The activity featured a poster board with a sailboat heading for an island while 
being supported by wind and held back by an anchor. The island represents the City’s decarbonization 
goals, the anchor represents barriers and challenges keeping the City from achieving its goals, and 
the wind represents various policy and support solutions to help the City reach its goals. Using post-it 
notes, participants were asked to first brainstorm various “anchors” or barriers to success. Then, 
participants were asked to brainstorm various solutions, or “wind”, to overcome such “anchors”. The 
activity lasted approximately 25 minutes. 
After the Sailboat Activity, community members were asked to participate in a final Voting Activity 
where they used post-its on a large posterboard to vote for as many of the five policy options that they 
would support the City adopt. There was also a “do nothing” option where participants could show that 
they did not support any electrification policy. Any community member who voted and left their email 
on a post-it was entered into a raffle for the chance to win one $50 visa gift card.  
Key results from each activity are included below, and a full record of all activity responses and photos 
are included in the Appendix.  
Sailboat Activity Themes  
Between the three groups, participants shared 30 “anchor” comments and 41 “wind” comments. 
Overall, the main concerns in the “anchor” category included costs, lack of education, grid reliability, 
equity, supply issues, and resistant social factors. The main support requested in the “wind” category 
included cost savings, education and outreach, grid reliability, equity programs, adequate supply, and 
a changing market. Below is a summary of comments in each section.

Virtual Community Feedback Meeting 
Feedback Meeting Summary  
The virtual community feedback meeting took place on March 13th, 2025 from 12:00 p.m. to 1:00

in-person event. 11community members participated in the virtual feedback meeting.

members participated in the same Sailboat Activity as the in-person meeting but in a virtual format. 
Participants remained in one large group using the engagement software Mural. Community members 
could enter comments into the Mural board themselves or leave comments in the chat or unmute 
themselves and leave verbal comments for Rincon staff to add onto the Mural board on their behalf.  
After the Sailboat Activity, community members were asked to take an exit poll to vote for as many of 
the five policy options that they would support the City adopt. There was also a “do nothing” option

San Mateo United Homeowners Association Feedback Meeting  
Feedback Meeting Summary 
The San Mateo United Homeowners Association (SMUHA) meets regularly to coordinate efforts 
between neighborhood and homeowner associations. The project team presented at the March 20th 
meeting at 7pm. There were 10 attendees. 
Results and Themes  
Similar to other feedback, most attendees voiced concern about the potential costs to property owners 
and the potential for costs to be passed on to renters. There was discussion of grid resilience and 
PG&E power safety shut offs. Most attendees were opposed to the City moving too quickly on building 
electrification policy.  
Commercial Building Performance Standard Information Session  
Feedback Meeting Summary  
The virtual Commercial BPS Information Session was advertised to building owners with buildings 
above 50,000 square feet through email. City staff spoke at the Chamber of Commerce Public Policy 
Group and Chamber of Commerce Developers Roundtable to promote this event. The City and Rincon 
Consultants created a BPS 1-pager explaining the relevance of the policy to distribute to building 
owners (available at end of this memorandum).  
The virtual Commercial BPS Information Session took place on April 10th, 2025 from 10:00 a.m. to 
11:00 a.m. over zoom. The goals of the meeting were for participants to: 
859 of 983

--- page 860 ---
City of San Mateo 
Summary of Conducted Community Engagement 
13 
1) Learn about the importance of a Building Performance Standard in the context of the City’s climate 
goals and legislation advancing a BPS across the state 
2) Note an overview and timeline of the City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy 
3) Learn about the phased implementation process of a Building Performance Standard 
4) Share feedback, concerns, and ask questions about the policy 
A total of six building owners attended the information session. City of San Mateo Sustainability 
Analyst, Andrea Chow, began the meeting and introduced Ryan Gardener, Director of Climate Action 
and Adaptation at Rincon Consultants, who gave a 25-minute presentation on goals one through three 
described above. After the presentation, building owners had the opportunity to share feedback, 
concerns, and ask questions about a potential BPS.  
Key results from the discussion are included below, and a full record of all activity responses and 
photos are included in the Appendix.  
Results and Themes  
Overall participants were thankful for the event as they were able to learn valuable information and 
share their feedback and desires for implementation of the policy. Several building owners from both 
retail and multi-family backgrounds shared insights as to how a BPS might affect them and the ways 
in which the policy thresholds could be structured to make implementation more successful. The main 
comments included: 
1) Setting a threshold based on an average of all commercial buildings (comparing old buildings to 
all-electric, new construction) could skew the threshold to require old buildings to convert to all-
electric which could be financially infeasible. One idea provided by an attendee was to set an 
individual threshold where buildings must reduce their emissions compared to their own historical 
emissions rather than meet a City or building type average. 
2) Converting to electric appliances also creates a possible space issue as electric rooms are 
increasingly taking up more space and old buildings may not have room for it. Relocating 
equipment due to space limitations poses increased engineering and financial burdens.  
3) For new construction that must meet the recent Title 24 requirements, can there be a vintage 
threshold to avoid unnecessary financial and time burden for buildings that are already well-
performing.  
4) For landlords with complicated tenant and lease structures, landlords would need City support 
to navigate conversations with tenants and financial responsibility. 
Survey  
Survey Summary  
An online survey was created to collect community feedback from those who were unable to participate 
in any of the other engagement events. It was shared broadly through City email listservs, social media, 
and during City events including the State of the City event. The survey was also advertised at the end 
of every engagement meeting so participants could share it with their networks.  
The Phase II survey aimed to gauge support for the five policy pathways while being brief and 
accessible. The survey provided a background on the project and its importance, defined the five policy 
options, and asked community members which of the policies they would support. Respondents could 
860 of 983

--- page 861 ---
City of San Mateo 
Summary of Conducted Community Engagement 
14 
choose options “Do not support”, “Neutral”, “Support”, or “Not sure.” Respondents also had the 
opportunity to leave other feedback in a comment box.  
Results and Themes

in Figure 5and Figure 6.

cost of some electrification projects.

stressed the importance of additional engagement and continued analysis as the program was 
developed in order to fully understand the impacts to building owners.

importance of managing costs to the City as well as collecting data/knowledge around the 
electrification process that could be leveraged for other programs.

APPENDIX C

Proposed Policy Summary


--- page 882 ---
Rincon Consultants, Inc. 
66 Franklin Street, Suite 300 
Oakland, California 94607 
510-834-4455 
 
www.rinconconsultants.com 
June 16, 2025 
 
Andrea Chow, Sustainability Analyst 
City of San Mateo 
330 West 20th Avenue 
San Mateo, California 94403 
Via email: achow@cityofsanmateo.org  
Subject:  Electrify San Mateo: Existing Building Electrification Policy Pathways Analysis 
In the City of San Mateo (City), the residential and commercial built environment make up 38 percent 
of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. To achieve emissions reduction targets consistent with state 
goals and the City’s Climate Action Plan, the City must decarbonize its residential and commercial 
buildings. This is strengthened by the 2022 City Council priority to “Establish policies to decarbonize 
existing buildings and infrastructure and eliminate methane gas use by 2030.” The decarbonization 
or electrification of buildings refers to the transition from gas equipment to carbon-free electric 
equipment for space and water heating, clothes drying, cooking and more. Decarbonization can also 
focus on increasing energy efficiency and the use of renewable energy. In San Mateo, Peninsula Clean 
Energy (PCE) provides electricity that is 50% renewable and virtually 100% carbon free and is 
committed to delivering 100% renewable and carbon free electricity by 2025. The City has already 
passed a New Construction Reach Code aimed at decarbonizing new buildings, however, electrifying 
existing buildings offers the largest opportunity and most cost-effective strategy for GHG emission 
reductions.  
In May 2023, the City, with the help of Rincon Consultants Inc. (Rincon), began developing the Building 
for the Future: City of San Mateo Sustainable Buildings Strategy project as a part of the larger Electrify 
San Mateo initiative. The goal of this project is to develop a long-term strategy document to electrify 
San Mateo’s existing building stock, including policies and actions for both residential and 
nonresidential buildings. This report provides Rincon’s final policy findings which are based on the 
cumulative technical analysis and community feedback completed to date.  
Project Timeline and Overview  
The City of San Mateo and Rincon began the initial research for this project in the Summer of 2023. 
The initial analysis included building segmentation analysis and an electrification cost study. Rincon 
also conducted research on the current best practices and a wide range of feasible policy options 
being deployed by other cities around the country. The project timeline is shown below in Figure 1. 
882 of 983

--- page 883 ---
City of San Mateo 
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
2 
Figure 1 
Project Timeline to Date 
 
Community Outreach (Phase I) 
During the first phase of community outreach, the City aimed to understand the goals and concerns 
of the community as they relate to San Mateo’s buildings and decarbonization. Through the initial 
round of engagement, the City identified affordability, health and safety, resilience, and climate as the 
primary goals of the community. The largest concerns related to building electrification were the 
potential for higher costs, increased energy bills, and a difficult installation process. Based on this 
feedback and direction from City Council, the initial policy scan list was narrowed down to five potential 
policy directions.  
Policy Pathways  
The results from the Phase 1 of outreach helped narrow the initial policy scan into a list of five policy 
pathways, which were presented to City Council in January of 2024. These policies were chosen based 
on the GHG reductions potential as well as cost-effectiveness. The five policy pathways include  
1. Continue adopted reach codes and focus on outreach and incentives (Business as Usual) 
2. Explore and adopt new reach codes to encourage and require electrification  
3. Require electric equipment upon replacement ahead of the Bay Area Air District’s (BAAD)1 Zero 
NOx requirements  
4. Establish Building Performance Standards for commercial buildings  
5. Adopt an electric-first policy for City facilities  
 
1 The Bay Area Air Quality Management District (BAAQMD) changed their name to the Bay Area Air District (BAAD) in 2025.  
883 of 983

--- page 884 ---
City of San Mateo 
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
3 
City Council provided feedback which included direction for City staff to continue exploring the 
feasibility, cost, and community support for each of the five options. 
Community Outreach (Phase II) 
In the second phase of community outreach (see Engagement Summary Memo for detailed results), 
the City aimed to get specific feedback about the five policy pathways. Phase II outreach events 
included both in-person and virtual workshops, presentations to the Senior Advisory Commission, 
Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission, and Community Relations Commission, as well as a 
second online feedback survey. Feedback received from Phase II of outreach is incorporated into an 
analysis of each policy pathway in the following sections.  
Addressing Community Concerns 
Throughout the community engagement process, there have been polarized responses to building 
electrification. While most constituents care about addressing the Climate Crisis and agree that the 
City should pursue existing building electrification in some form, many residents also expressed 
concerns. The primary concerns identified during Phase I and Phase II engagement included:  
• 
Upfront costs of electric equipment  
• 
On-bill or operational costs of electric equipment 
• 
Installation and electrical infrastructure challenges 
• 
Grid reliability and resilience 
• 
Lack of education surrounding electrification (addressed in Policy Option 1) 
Each of the pathways balance the City’s climate goals and the roughly half of respondents who strongly 
support building electrification with the concerns voiced during the engagement process in different 
ways. Each primary community concern is addressed below and further covered in the policy 
summaries.  
Upfront Costs of Electrical Equipment 
For many building owners, making the switch from natural gas to electric appliances results in higher 
upfront costs without additional incentives or rebates. While rebates can cover up to 100% of these 
costs, rebates are not always available. However, not installing electric equipment when it is most cost 
effective can have significant cost impacts in the future. Therefore, the City has identified flexible 
policies that allow building owners to choose electrification or other cost-effective strategies. Each 
policy below provides additional details of the upfront costs and savings.  
On-Bill or Operational Costs 
Ongoing operational costs (how much our monthly energy bills are) were another concern regarding 
building electrification. However, recent studies by Peninsula Clean Energy show that the cost savings 
from building electrification are increasing as natural gas rate increases outpace electricity rates. 
Average savings (when using electric rates like e-elec) were $29 per household per month after 
electrifying both water and space heaters. These savings are demonstrated in Figure 3.  
884 of 983

--- page 885 ---
City of San Mateo 
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
4 
Figure 2 
Average Monthly Bill Savings from Electrification 
 
Installation and Electrical Infrastructure Challenges 
While most electric appliance upgrades are relatively simple, some buildings present difficult edge 
cases. Some buildings may not have sufficient space or electrical infrastructure to easily upgrade. 
Upgrading electric panels and service lines can take months to complete. While all-building owners 
are encouraged to make a plan for electrification over time, many of the policies avoid these 
challenges by providing flexible paths to compliance or focusing on specific appliances which do not 
have these challenges. For example, the flex-path retrofit policy provides many options including 
insulation and other cost-effective efficiency upgrades at the time of major renovation. The AC to 
heat pump policy avoids these challenges because the heat pump upgrade uses the same electrical 
circuit as an AC, eliminating the need for upgrades. Grid Reliability and Resilience 
Grid Reliability and Resilience 
Grid reliability and resilience concerns arose due to the perception that increased electrical use would 
put strain on the grid and that electricity was less reliable than gas. However, it has been nearly 20 
years since California had a power outage due to lack of electricity.2 Furthermore, the utilities, the 
 
2 https://www.baaqmd.gov/~/media/files/community-health/building-appliance-implementation/grid-impacts-final-
pdf.pdf?rev=14a3ea4d46704d19a48cf03e3984c90e&sc_lang=en  
885 of 983

--- page 886 ---
City of San Mateo 
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
5 
CPUC, and the CEC, are all planning for increased electricity usage over time. The CEC now assumes 
a 27% increase in peak demand by 2035 due to electrification of buildings and vehicles and is 
expanding the grid to meet that demand. Furthermore, neither gas water heaters nor most furnaces 
work without electricity, meaning gas appliances are no more resilient to power outages than their 
electric upgrades. Therefore, the policies identified below focus on these appliances. 
Policy Findings Summary 
To analyze the feasibility and benefits of each policy pathway, Rincon provided a description of each 
pathway, pro’s and con’s, precedent legislation in other cities and the state, GHG reductions potential, 
community feedback, cost-effectiveness, and equity considerations. Based on the detailed analysis in 
the Policy Analysis section, the following summary highlights the major takeaways of the five policy 
pathways.  
1. Business as usual: The City could continue with adopting reach codes for new construction and 
expand outreach, education, and incentives. In the past, the City adopted local building code 
amendments to require more sustainable new construction than the State code. To continue to 
surpass State code, the City could consider other new construction reach codes including an 
energy performance or Zero NOx reach code. This approach has a limited impact because new 
construction accounts for a fraction of San Mateo’s development. However, this approach is 
already in place, cost saving up front and over time, and received community support based on 
our engagement efforts. There are no policy-related additional costs to building owners unless they 
voluntarily decide to electrify. A Zero NOx approach would save an estimated 1,770 MT CO2e per 
year by 2030 while a performance approach would save 20 MT CO2e per year by 2030.  
2. Reach codes for renovations: This approach would include the evaluation of additional reach 
codes to encourage and/or require electrification during renovations or at time of replacement for 
select appliances (air conditioners). New reach codes would vary depending on the updated State 
codes. One example of a reach code during renovations is requiring the installation of a two-way 
heat pump when an air conditioner (AC) is installed.  A Two-way AC time of replacement reach code 
could help residents save money by installing a single heat pump, rather than an AC and furnace, 
ahead of upcoming Bay Area Air District (BAAD) regulation which essentially mandates electric 
furnaces starting in 2029. The incremental cost of installing a heat pump instead of a traditional 
AC is the same (from $1,000 savings to $1,000 cost) and is covered by a $1,500 PCE rebate. 
Installing a heat pump today is alignment with future Air District regulations and thus could also 
save a building owner money  when their furnace needs replacement. Reach codes during 
renovations also help encourage electrification at an opportune time when a property owner is 
already considering home improvements. Another policy example is a Flex Path reach code which 
would require additional energy efficiency or electrification actions at time of renovation or 
replacement of certain appliances. The upfront costs of electrification improvements can vary 
depending on the project and unique circumstances of your home. These policies target the most 
cost-effective time to electrify and provide a variety of pathways for compliance. Cost-effectiveness 
studies must be completed to validate any adopted reach codes. Past studies have shown utility 
savings over time make electric equipment upgrades cost-effective compared to gas equipment 
replacements. Additionally, the Flex Path reach code allows residents to find the most cost-
effective retrofit for their unique situation. Two-Way AC reach code would reduce emissions by 
2,200 MT CO2e per year by 2030, while the Flex Path reach code would reduce emissions by 
approximately 730 MT CO2e per year by 2030. 
3. Require electric equipment upon replacement ahead of BAAD Zero NOx requirements: BAAD 
adopted zero nitrogen oxides (NOx) standards that will prohibit the sale and installation of gas 
water heaters starting in 2027 and gas furnaces in 2029 in the nine-county Bay Area. A local 
886 of 983

--- page 887 ---
City of San Mateo 
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
6 
ordinance ahead of these milestones would require the replacement of gas appliances with zero 
NOx versions when they fail or are voluntarily upgraded. This policy would have the most significant 
impact on greenhouse gas reductions with the ability to reduce emissions an average of 9,700 MT 
CO2e per year by 2030. However, this policy could incur large installation costs for residents in 
some cases which was the number one concern documented during both phases of community 
engagement. Due to the polarized community feedback, with some constituents being 
unsupportive of mandates that increase costs, it may be difficult to garner public buy in for a Zero 
NOx Equipment Requirement policy. This policy prioritizes GHG emissions reductions but does not 
address the community’s major concerns over costs and difficult installations.  
4. Building Performance Standards for commercial buildings: This pathway would establish a new 
set of policy standards for commercial buildings aimed at increasing energy efficiency, and 
eventually, electrifying commercial buildings as a phased approach. This is a well-established 
policy mechanism that has been adopted by many local governments across the country and builds 
off of AB 802 benchmarking requirements mandated by the state for buildings larger than 50,000 
SF. This approach can be customizable and lead to incremental reductions in energy use but not 
necessarily electrification. The flexibility of this policy and phased approach allows for building 
owners to plan for appliance replacement and decarbonize buildings in the most efficient way for 
each unique situation. Commercial building space makes up over 39 million square feet within the 
City and is a significant contributor to GHG emissions. However, due to the phased approach, this 
policy would likely not lead to significant GHG reductions before 2030. However, it could have 
significant reductions before 2045 as the thresholds are calculated and implemented. The 
development and operation of such a local program would necessitate significant staff time, so 
choosing a threshold that maximizes square footage covered while minimizing the number of 
building owners affected is crucial. Although this policy received majority community support, 
equity for renters is also a significant concern and guardrails would need to be set in place to avoid 
exacerbating the housing crisis especially for multifamily properties.  
5. Electric first policy for City facilities: This policy establishes a mechanism for local government 
renovations to go electric first. The policy ensures financial and feasibility data is being collected 
for municipal facilities that will allow the City to compare costs to electrify versus replacing with 
gas equipment. This policy has a limited reach as it only applies to municipal buildings and GHG 
reductions potential would depend on the rate of equipment failure and availability of 
replacements. However, it demonstrates City leadership on hard-to-electrify existing buildings that 
mirror the commercial sector. Several other cities have adopted similar municipal electrification 
policies. Residents were largely in favor of this policy as it would not incur any costs to the 
community. The City’s municipal buildings are estimated to generate approximately 586 MT CO2e 
per year of natural gas emissions, this policy was estimated to be able to reduce this by 25% by 
2030 for an estimated savings of 146 MT CO2e. 
Policy Analysis 
Policy Option 1: Continue adopted reach codes and focus on outreach and 
incentives (Business as Usual) 
Description  
Policy Option 1 continues to implement the City’s adopted reach codes and identifies opportunities to 
incentivize the electrification process using education, outreach, and connecting the community to 
rebates and technical support. The City’s adopted reach code requires energy compliance margins for 
new buildings will be effective through December 31, 2025, the end of the code cycle.  As part of this 
887 of 983

--- page 888 ---
City of San Mateo 
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
7 
policy option, the City would investigate options around continuing the new building electrification 
reach code through either an updated performance standard which complies with the 2025 building 
code, or a zero NOx standard for new buildings.  
This option would also use staff time to conduct additional engagement and connect homeowners to 
incentives and technical information regarding building electrification. It would also promote existing 
programming and incentives run by partners (e.g. PCE) to assist in a voluntary shift towards 
electrification while preparing the community to electrify space and water heating end uses in advance 
of upcoming BAAD (previously BAAQMD) Zero NOx requirements. For example, this option could include 
an education campaign to encourage homeowners to prewire for water heaters and furnaces, so they 
are ready to make the switch once their appliance burns out. Another example incentive would be 
waiving permit costs for electric equipment upgrades.  
Policy Pros 
• 
Policy already in place/ has received community support  
• 
No policy-related additional costs to building owners unless they voluntarily opt-in  
• 
Allows community to prepare for upcoming BAAD requirements through City-led outreach and 
education  
• 
Allows community to prepare for upcoming requirements through electric-readiness requirements  
Policy Cons 
• 
Limited impact and reach: The adopted reach codes do not affect the  existing building stock  
• 
Relies on engagement to reach community members which can be difficult 
• 
Outreach and education programs on their own are extremely variable in their effectiveness. 
Investment of staff time and city resources into outreach may not achieve substantial shifts in 
behavior that will be required to decarbonize buildings, even with major outreach campaigns.  
• 
Education and outreach campaigns can be very time/resource intensive with minimal 
programmatic results and unlikely to result in widespread behavior changes above baseline  
Greenhouse Gas Reductions Potential  
New construction reach codes have varying GHG reduction potential. For example, a new construction 
zero NOx requirement could save up to 1,770 MT CO2e per year by 2030 if all new buildings were built 
without natural gas. An energy performance reach code for new construction would lead to 
approximately 20 MT CO2e GHG reductions due to the stringency of the 2025 building code, however, 
precise reductions potential is unknown because the flexible nature of the pathway does not always 
guarantee electrification.  
As mentioned above, education and outreach and sharing incentives with residents is an important 
tool for the successful implementation of electrification policy but are not substantial enough to gauge 
GHG emissions on their own. It is difficult to predict what impact these voluntary programs would have 
on overall electrification rates, however, they are expected to be lower than the policy requirements 
identified in the other policy pathways.  
Community Feedback  
Across engagement events, this approach received broad community support. In the community 
survey, responses were 50% of respondents supporting this policy option, 11% neutral, and 38% do 
not support. At the in-person community event, many community members expressed that the City has 
888 of 983

--- page 889 ---
City of San Mateo 
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
8 
not done enough in terms of electrification policy beyond new construction and that a “business as 
usual” approach is not sufficient to meet the City’s climate goals. Those opposed to this approach are 
opposed to any local building electrification policy and recommend reliance on regional and state 
regulations. The Senior Advisory Commission, Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission, and 
Community Relations Commission were supportive of this policy pathway. 
Cost Effectiveness and Equity Considerations  
This policy approach would continue to incentivize all-electric new construction and direct the City to 
expand outreach efforts with the community to increase voluntary existing building electrification. New 
Construction reach codes have been shown to save money. Research from the New Buildings Institute 
(NBI) highlights that all-electric homes save between $7,500 and $8,200 in construction costs 
compared to mixed-fuel homes.4 These cost savings are primarily due to avoiding installing fees for 
natural gas infrastructure like gas meters and pipelines. These homes also save money over time 
especially when paired with heat pumps and solar. Outreach, education, and sharing incentives incur 
no cost to community members and can help connect people to resources that will lessen costs for 
electrification. The City will need to provide adequate staff time to support building electrification 
engagement and other programs like peer-to-peer home tours for this approach to be effective. These 
engagement activities, when paired with other strategies listed below, can help the community feel 
more comfortable and informed on the benefits of electrification.  
Policy Findings 
The adoption of reach codes while expanding education, outreach, and incentives to promote building 
electrification have been critical pathways for the City of San Mateo to support decarbonization to 
date. This approach is supportive of the other policy options because expanding education and 
engagement can potentially increase the number of cost-effective voluntary projects and expand 
community understanding of new technologies and regulations (like the upcoming Air District’s Zero 
NOx rules).  The community expressed the need education campaigns like helping to facilitate peer-
to-peer home tours and partnering with larger County initiatives like PCE’s helpdesk to ensure 
residents are receiving the guidance they need on their electrification journey. While this policy offers 
several benefits, it is important to acknowledge that this approach alone will not be sufficient to meet 
the City's climate goals.  .  
Policy Option 2: Reach Codes for Renovations  
Description 
Option 2 would include the evaluation of additional reach codes to encourage and/or require 
electrification. Reach codes are typically evaluated on a three-year cycle in alignment with the new 
Building Code. New reach codes options would vary depending on the updated Energy Code and Green 
Building Code. The City’s adopted reach codes apply to residential remodels. The City could consider 
a variety of reach codes that apply to different building types and/or equipment. The City focused on 
two priority existing building reach codes include a Two-Way Air Conditioning (AC) reach code or a 
Flexible Path reach code. These policies could be adopted individually or both adopted to complement 
 
4 NBI. 2022. New Study on Electrification Costs Shows Benefits to Building Owners and Society. https://newbuildings.org/new-study-on-
electrification-costs-shows-benefits-to-building-owners-and-society/ 
889 of 983

--- page 890 ---
City of San Mateo 
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
9 
each other. Other reach codes which were not covered with the community but may be important in 
the future are time of sale requirements similar to the policy adopted by the City of Berkeley.5 
Policy Option 2a: Two-Way Air Conditioning Reach Code  
The Two-Way AC reach code would require all new air conditioning units to provide both heating and 
cooling through the installation of a two-way heat pump. A two-way heat pump is a versatile HVAC 
system that can both heat and cool a building by reversing the flow of refrigerant within the system. In 
heating mode, it extracts heat from an external source, such as outdoor air or the ground, and transfers 
it indoors. In cooling mode, it reverses the process, removing heat from inside the building and 
releasing it outside. This dual functionality eliminates the need for separate heating and cooling 
systems which can save building owners money in the long term. Two-way heat pumps run completely 
off electricity and heat and cool spaces with high efficiency, making them a great way to reduce 
emissions coming from homes.  
Policy Option 2b: Flex Path Reach Code  
Under the flex path policies, the City could adopt one or more policies that require additional energy 
efficiency or electrification actions at time of renovation or replacement of certain appliances. One 
example would be an energy efficiency checklist in which renovation projects over $75,000 must 
include two cost effective energy efficient improvements in their project. The flex path policies do not 
ban gas and provide flexible pathways for building owners to meet the decarbonization requirements.  
Policy Pros:  
• 
The City has experience exploring, adopting and implementing reach codes 
• 
Two-Way AC is a cost-efficient and simple solution 
• 
Flex path offers variety of compliance pathways to reduce emissions and building owners could 
find the most cost-efficient pathways for their homes  
Policy Cons: 
• 
Community and builder resistance to new regulations. 
• 
Further analysis needed, dependent on reach code adopted. 
Other Cities and Legislative Drivers 
In May 2023, the Bay Area Air Quality Management District, now the Bay Area Air District (BAAD) 
adopted zero nitrogen oxides (NOx) standards that will prohibit the sale of gas water heaters starting 
in 2027 and gas furnaces in 2029 in the nine-county Bay Area. These standards are the most 
ambitious in the country and outpace the state’s goal of banning the sale of new gas furnaces and 
water heaters throughout the state starting in 2030. These policy efforts drive California closer to the 
ambitious climate goal of carbon neutrality by 2045. Reach codes during renovations present an  
opportunity to ensure that the City’s residential building stock is prepared for this upcoming mandate.  
Two-Way AC policies are relatively new, so no jurisdiction has yet to officially adopt this reach code, 
but many are expected to in the 2025 building cycle. The “Flex Path” reach code style is more common 
with several jurisdictions who have adopted this policy listed below:  
 
5 https://berkeleyca.gov/sites/default/files/documents/2025-04-15-Amendments-to-the-BESO.pdf   
890 of 983

--- page 891 ---
City of San Mateo 
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
10 
• 
City of San Rafael (Adopted 2024): San Rafael has adopted a "Flex Path" for single family remodels 
over 500 square feet. This policy requires homeowners to choose from a menu of energy-saving 
measures, such as insulation upgrades, HVAC improvements, or solar installations, to meet a 
target score based on their home's vintage. The program is designed to encourage energy 
efficiency and electrification while allowing homeowners to select measures that best suit their 
needs and budget. 
• 
City of San Luis Obispo (Adopted 2024): Starting January 1, 2025, single family major additions or 
alterations must install a set of energy efficiency measures to achieve a target score from a 
weighted menu of measures. Projects that include a new electrical panel and service upgrade to 
200A shall include electric readiness components. 
• 
Town of Corte Madera (Adopted 2024): Single family additions or alterations greater than 750 
square feet of floor area shall install a set of measures to achieve a target score from a weighted 
menu of measures; which varies based on building vintage. Projects that include a laundry room 
or kitchen remodel, or an electrical service panel upgrade, must also install electric readiness 
measures. 
Greenhouse Gas Reductions Potential  
The Two-Way AC reach code would reduce emissions by 2,200 MT CO2e per year by 2030. Two-way 
heat pumps tend to be two to four times more efficient than both one-way air conditioners and 
traditional heating sources, leading to large potential GHG reductions.7 While the Flex Path reach code 
would reduce emissions by approximately 730 MT CO2e per year by 2030. The Flex Path reductions 
are lower since they only capture remodels over a certain size which occur at a lower rate than other 
appliance replacements.   
Community Feedback  
Across community engagement events, constituents were supportive of this policy approach. 
Community members recognized the importance of electrifying during planned renovation projects. In 
the community survey, responses were mixed with 49% respondents supporting this policy option, 6% 
neutral and 45% do not support. Multiple constituents expressed a desire for an exception for edge 
cases that are financially or spatially infeasible. Those opposed are concerned about upfront costs or 
are generally opposed to all local building electrification policy. The Sustainability and Infrastructure 
Commission and Community Relations Commission were supportive of this policy pathway. 
Cost Effectiveness and Equity Considerations  
Although the Two-Way AC policy would mandate installation of a two-way heat pump at time of 
replacement, this retrofit is highly cost-effective as it can both cool and heat a space, effectively 
replacing both a central air conditioner and furnace within a home. This reduces the need for separate 
furnace replacements. Implementing a Two-Way AC policy is particularly advantageous given the 
upcoming BAAD Zero NOx requirements.  
If a constituent's air conditioner breaks before 2029 and is replaced with a gas HVAC system, they will 
need to purchase an electric furnace when their furnace eventually breaks after the 2029 BAAD Zero 
NOx implementation date. Conversely, adopting a Two-Way AC policy in preparation for these 
requirements would ensure that all air conditioner replacements in the next few years have two-way 
 
7 Canary Media. A new bill could speed up American electrification by 20 years. https://www.canarymedia.com/articles/heat-pumps/a-
new-bill-could-speed-up-american-electrification-by-20-years

heating and cooling capabilities and comply with BAAD’s ruling. This would prevent homeowners from 
installing outdated gas appliances for heating and cooling, which could be replaced by a single heat 
pump.  
Additionally, the difference between a central AC and a two-way heat pump is essentially zero (from a 
$1,000 savings to a $1,000 cost) especially after the applied $1,500 rebate from Peninsula Clean 
Energy. Peninsula Clean Energy (PCE) also offers on-bill financing through the Zero Percent Loan 
program8 to cover the up-front costs of installing a heat pump, which leads to utility bill savings over 
time due to appliance efficiency. This financing model can be particularly advantageous for low-income 
tenants who might not have access to traditional financing options. It removes the need for credit 
checks and upfront payments, making energy efficiency upgrades more accessible. Additionally, on-
bill financing can be an effective way for tenants and landlords to manage payment responsibilities 
for energy efficiency upgrades. Tenants could enjoy the benefits of lower utility costs, without being 
tied to a load if they move (because the payment is tied to the meter rather than a person) and the 
landlord can improve their building without needing to pay an upfront cost.9  
A Flex Path reach code is also cost-effective by providing a range of retrofits and upgrades that building 
owners can choose from, allowing them to pick the most cost-effective and efficient options. Based on 
a conducted cost analysis conducted by the Local Codes and Standards team, each check list item 
would need to be demonstrated to have cost savings over the lifespan of the upgrade. Figure 3 shows 
some of the cost-effective strategies for the City of San Mateo based on the 2022 Title 24 Building 
Code. Heat pumps provide the most cost-effective solutions, often paying back in 6-7 years. 
Furthermore, many homeowners may receive additional savings through incentives provided by PCE, 
the State (Tech rebates) or the Inflation Reduction Act.

Cost Effective Existing Building Upgrades (Cost Effectiveness Explorer) 
 
Both the Two-Way AC and Flex Path policies emphasize cost-efficient solutions for both the short and 
long term, ensuring that the City maintains equitable and sustainable practices.

Policy Findings 
Both a Two-Way AC policy and Flex Path policy promote cost-efficient and equitable solutions for 
building decarbonization. Implementing these policies would help the City achieve meaningful 
greenhouse gas reductions, get closer to climate goals, prepare the community for future regulations,  
and support residents in making energy-efficient upgrades. Both of these policies fit with the 
community feedback received by pursuing climate action and decarbonization policies but being 
sensitive to cost impacts, the need for flexibility, and reliability/resilience.  
Policy Option 3: Require electric equipment upon replacement ahead of BAAD’s 
Zero NOx requirements  
Description 
BAAD will require zero NOx (currently the only options for Zero NOx appliances are all-electric) 
appliances for water heating by 2027 and space heating by 2029. This policy bans the sale of 
qualifying NOx -emitting appliances in the Bay Area, requiring all-electric appliances when covered gas 
appliances are replaced. Option 3 has the greatest potential to achieve GHG reductions and the goals 
outlined in the City’s CAP by moving faster than the 2027 and 2029 timelines outlined by BAAD. 
However, it could also result in higher upfront costs especially for space heating. Under this policy the 
City would adopt and implement a local version of the BAAD zero N0x threshold ahead of the current 
schedule to require all new space and water heating to have zero NOx emissions, and therefore, zero 
GHG emissions.  
This ‘time of replacement’ ordinance would require the replacement of gas water heaters and space 
heaters with zero NOx versions when they fail or are voluntarily upgraded. This approach maximizes 
the lifespan of the existing gas appliances and reduces the cost of compliance as the increased cost 
would only be the difference between a gas appliance and the upgraded electric appliance. Focusing 
on time-of-replacement is the most cost-effective approach for the building owner. Furthermore, since 
these appliances may function for dozens of years, it also is the timeliest option to replace gas 
appliances with electric-run appliances instead of locking in emissions from natural-gas appliances 
which could potentially continue to operate past the City’s target for carbon neutrality.  
Policy Pros:  
• 
Community and workforce prepared for 2027 and 2029 BAAD requirements 
• 
On-bill savings for homes that go all-electric  
• 
Largest GHG emissions reduction potential  
Policy Cons: 
• 
Higher upfront costs for residents for home electrification 
• 
Uncertainty of incentives  
• 
Some homes may face electrical upgrades (panels or service lines) that increase costs and 
lengthen timelines for replacement  
Other Cities and Legislative Drivers 
The BAAD requirements will be implemented in 2027 and 2029 and getting ahead of the ruling offers 
an opportunity  to have the highest GHG impact. Currently, there are no jurisdictions who have adopted 
this policy for existing buildings. .  
893 of 983

--- page 894 ---
City of San Mateo 
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
13 
Greenhouse Gas Reductions Potential  
A zero NOx equipment requirement policy would have the most significant impact on greenhouse gas 
reductions with the ability to reduce emissions an average of 9,700 MT CO2e per year by 2030 
assuming a zero NOx policy was put in place for water heating and space heating in 2026 for 
residential/small commercial appliances and 2028 for larger commercial appliances.  
Community Feedback  
Feedback on this policy was largely unfavorable. In the community survey, 44% of respondents 
supported this policy option, 5% neutral, and 48% do not support. Those opposed were concerned 
about getting ahead of the Air District’s schedule and challenges with compliance and enforcement. 
Those who support this approach emphasize that this approach has the greatest GHG reduction 
potential. The Community Relations Commission was supportive of this policy pathway. 
Cost Effectiveness and Equity Considerations  
Based on the initial cost study conducted for this project, a zero NOx policy median-cost scenario could 
increase water heating incremental costs by $3,200 and space heating incremental costs by $11,778 
before rebates. However, between state, local, and federal incentives high income households could 
see an overall cost savings for water heaters and incremental costs of $5,878 dollars for space 
heating. When also considering the avoided cost of an AC unit (once it breaks down) from installing a 
two-way heat pump that acts as a furnace and an AC unit, then the incremental costs in a median-cost 
scenario for space heating would be $4,278 before rebates and incur cost savings with rebates. 
Similar conclusions were found in another cost study done for BAAD.10 However, many of the 
incentives originally modeled for this analysis have changed or have uncertain futures, especially at 
the federal level.  
Similarly to Policy Option 2, residents could utilize on-bill financing from PCE to cover up-front costs of 
electric water heater or space heating appliances, utilizing a zero percent interest loan and repaying 
that installation cost over time via their utility bill.8 The PCE program fosters equitable installation of 
electric equipment and allows renters to enjoy the same energy savings as homeowners. However, the 
availability of rebates and incentives is volatile which could impact the cost-effectiveness of an electric-
appliance mandate.  
Policy Findings  
A Zero NOx Equipment Requirements policy would achieve the highest greenhouse gas emissions 
reduction , mandating electric heat pump water heaters and furnaces ahead of the BAAD requirement. 
This policy offers a significant opportunity to reduce GHG emissions and capture appliance 
replacements over the next four years before BAAD’s mandate is fully implemented. Implementing this 
policy could potentially save residents money depending on the availability of rebates on water and 
space heating appliances. However, there are cases in which this policy could result in increased 
upfront costs for homeowners, which was the biggest concern identified in Phase II of engagement. 
The changing rebate landscape also makes it difficult to understand how costs will change over time. 
Due to the polarized community feedback, with some constituents being unsupportive of mandates, it 
may be difficult to garner public buy in for a Zero NOx Equipment Requirement policy.

Policy Option 4: Commercial Building Performance Standards  
Description  
Option 4 introduces new policy standards for commercial buildings (Building Performance Standards) 
aimed at increasing energy efficiency, and eventually, decarbonizing commercial buildings as a phased 
efficiency-focused approach. In comparison to relatively homogonous residential buildings, 
commercial buildings can be highly variable in their systems and needs. Commercial building heating 
and cooling systems are also frequently oversized, meaning replacing existing gas equipment in a like 
for like manner with electric equipment is not economical. Process loads also vary significantly across 
commercial building types. For these reasons, decarbonizing commercial buildings necessitates a 
flexible approach where building managers can first make strides towards understanding their 
buildings and cost-effective opportunities before committing to a specific approach. Typically, a BPS 
Program is implemented in phases where buildings first report energy use for several years 
(benchmarking), then perform a retro-commissioning process to identify opportunities before finally 
reducing emissions below an established threshold (performance standard).  
• 
Phase I: Benchmarking (Years 1-2): This initial phase establishes a building’s energy performance 
baseline by comparing its energy use intensity (EUI) to similar buildings. Covered Buildings 
(buildings and properties with buildings totaling the specified size threshold or larger) report their 
annual energy usage in Energy Star Portfolio Manager. The data collected in the benchmarking 
phase improves building manager and City understanding of building energy usage and will inform 
GHG-emissions performance standards. Benchmarking also identifies underperforming facilities 
and prioritizes improvements, with studies showing benchmarked buildings achieve average 
annual energy savings of 2.4 percent.11 
• 
Phase II: Retro-commissioning (Years 3-4): Retro-commissioning is a process through which a 
building’s energy consuming systems are analyzed for efficiency and proper operation. The retro-
commissioning process serves two primary purposes. The first is to uncover inefficient operations, 
increase energy efficiency, and save building owners money. The second is to identify cost-
effective opportunities to reduce building GHG emissions through electrification and energy 
efficiency. 
• 
Phase III: Performance Standard (Year 4+): Jurisdictions set phased energy or emissions targets 
for GHG reductions over time on a square feet basis. The targets are based on data collected from 
the benchmarking phase and can be met through a variety of retrofits, such as upgrades, 
electrification, or renewable energy adoption. The most cost-efficient retrofits for each building 
were identified in the retro-commissioning phase. Standards often allow flexible compliance paths, 
such as achieving specific EUI thresholds or offsetting emissions via clean energy investments.  
Policy Pros:  
• 
BPS approach addresses a high degree of variability in commercial building types by focusing on 
performance-based requirements 
• 
Phased approach allows for structured guidance for facility managers, uncovering cost savings 
over time 
• 
Focuses on worst performing buildings first 
 
11 National Association of State Energy Officials. 2025. Benchmarking and Building Performance Standards. Available: 
https://www.naseo.org/issues/buildings/benchmarking 
895 of 983

--- page 896 ---
City of San Mateo 
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
15 
• 
Reduces pressure on harder to electrify equipment types (e.g. gas stoves) through phased 
approach, longer runway for electric equipment to become widely available and cost-effective on-
market  
• 
Robust programming examples from Cities who have already implemented  
• 
Tiers off of existing State energy disclosure laws (AB 802) 
Policy Cons: 
• 
Necessitates new staff time and staff members to implement and track BPS  
• 
Does not guarantee all-electric end usage in short term until phased retro commissioning phase, 
potentially missing some appliances that could be electrified  
• 
Limited building stock reach, best suited to large commercial buildings (for example buildings 
>25,000 sq ft) 
• 
Possibility to have equity implications from increased rents in multifamily if tenant protections are 
not integrated into the policy  
Other Cities and Legislative Drivers 
Commercial Building Performance Standards are already mandated by California’s statewide building 
energy benchmarking and public disclosure law (AB 802), which require owners of commercial and 
multifamily buildings 50,000 square feet or larger to annually benchmark and report their whole-
building energy usage to the California Energy Commission. A City BPS could implement the same 
threshold as the state or have a more stringent threshold including buildings with a smaller square 
footage.  
• 
Compared to other policy approaches listed here, building performance standards are a well-
established policy mechanism that has been adopted by many local governments including 
the City of San Jose, City of San Francisco, and City of Brisbane. 13City of Brisbane Building 
Efficiency Program passed in 2019, requires annual energy and water benchmarking and 
reporting, and compliance with Beyond requirements to show that they are high performing, 
or improving performance for commercial buildings 10,000 SF and larger.14 
• 
City of San Jose’s Energy and Water Building Performance Ordinance passed in 2018 and requires 
commercial and multifamily buildings above 20,000 square feet to annually benchmark and report 
their energy and water usage. Beginning in 2023, building have been required to meet energy and 
water efficiency standards on a rolling five-year basis.15  
• 
Greenhouse Gas Reductions Potential  
Commercial building space makes up over 39 million square feet within the City with 44 percent and 
30 percent coming from space and water heating respectively. The building performance standard 
would likely not result in significant GHG emissions reductions before 2030 due to the phased in 
nature of the program. However, 83,660 MT CO2e were generated by commercial buildings in 2019 
These emissions could be reduced by 2045 depending on the final thresholds adopted by the City.17

Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
16 
Community Feedback  
Across engagement events, community members were supportive of a BPS policy. In the community 
survey, approximately 58% of respondents were supportive of a BPS policy, 12% neutral, and 29% do 
not support.  The Senior Advisory Commission, Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission, and 
Community Relations Commission were supportive of this policy pathway. 
During our direct outreach to building owners, respondents understood the importance of a BPS policy 
and made suggestions of how to set thresholds to be most efficient and minimize unnecessary costs 
like setting individual reductions targets measured against a buildings own emissions rather than a 
City average and including a vintage threshold to exclude new buildings which are already more 
efficient. Building owners also shared that limited physical space for electric equipment upgrades and 
complicated tenant lease structures would be challenges to implementation that building owners 
would need guidance navigating.  
Cost Effectiveness and Equity Considerations  
The cost of decarbonizing buildings under a Building Performance Standard (BPS) policy varies 
significantly based on factors such as building age, equipment types, and energy end uses. This 
variability necessitates a flexible, performance-based approach, allowing owners to prioritize cost-
effective solutions tailored to their assets. The BPS framework’s phased implementation mitigates 
financial burdens: 
• 
Benchmarking identifies inefficiencies (e.g., leaks, malfunctioning systems) and establishes a 
baseline for improvement. Benchmarking reporting costs approximately $3,000 annually. 
• 
Retro-commissioning pinpoints low-cost operational optimizations (e.g., HVAC adjustments, 
controls recalibration), which typically yield 16% energy savings at an average cost of $0.30/sq. 
ft., often with payback periods under two years. 
• 
Performance standards are deferred for 4+ years post-benchmarking, enabling strategic capital 
planning for equipment replacements and system upgrades. 
Electrification of commercial building electric retrofits will likely have to be paired with a suite of 
demand management, energy efficiency, or renewable energy projects in order to make building 
electrification economical for building owners. Specific cost-effective system upgrades include heat 
pump rooftop package units, which are the most promising electrification solution for commercial 
building electrification, offering a replicable model for a 1:1 equipment swap-out.18 Analysis from PCE 
found that for these easy to electrify units (packaged/split units with both heating and cooling) the 
marginal costs to upgrade to an all-electric packaged or split heat pump system were found to be 
negligible as shown in 3. 
 
18 Rocky Mountain Institute. 2022. Medium-Size Commercial Retrofits. Available: https://rmi.org/insight/economics-of-electrifying-
buildings-medium-size-commercial-retrofits/

PCE 2035 Decarbonization Plan Rooftop Package Unit Marginal Cost 
Analysis 
 
Equity is addressed by allowing building-specific compliance pathways and avoiding prescriptive 
mandates that could disproportionately burden older or under-resourced properties. There are 
additional equity implications when considering multifamily housing which could exacerbate 
disparities and lead to rent increases if not properly designed. Key considerations for equitable BPS 
implementation include anti-displacement measures for buildings that receive any City subsidy, 
incentive, or funding. Equity implications and design measures for multifamily are still being 
investigated. Due to the phased in nature of this program, those considerations can be addressed 
over time, but before thresholds for reductions are finalized (year 4+).  
Policy Findings  
A BPS policy builds on state legislation and addresses a large source of emissions reductions. There 
is overall community support and flexible and cost-efficient implementation. However, a BPS policy 
obligates a significant amount of staff time. Therefore, for efficient implementation, the City could set 
a threshold that would maximize the captured square footage while minimizing the number of 
impacted building owners. A threshold that initially only applies to buildings greater than 50,000 
square feet (which are already mandated to report benchmarking data under AB 802) would ensure 
the City’s building stock is complying with state law and allow the City to understand the benchmarking 
process without a significant staff burden.  
Policy Option 5: Municipal Electric First Policy  
Description  
This policy establishes a mechanism for local government renovations to go ‘electric first’. This means 
that new appliance purchases, renovations, and replacements should prioritize using all-electric 
equipment. This policy would also allow for technical and cost infeasibility on a project-level when an 
all-electric alternative is either not available or deemed infeasible due to cost. The main purpose of 
898 of 983

--- page 899 ---
City of San Mateo 
Electrify San Mateo: Policy Pathways Analysis 
18 
the policy is to position the City as the leader in decarbonization within the community as well as 
prepare City facilities for upcoming requirements. The City would also learn best practices through this 
approach which it can share with the community and electrified city facilities would serve as 
demonstration buildings to highlight the comfort, health, and safety benefits of going all-electric.  
Policy Pros:  
• 
Demonstrates City leadership in building electrification 
• 
Availability of significant local government incentives to electrify through Peninsula Clean Energy’s 
Gov BE and Willdan GK12 program  
• 
Existing resources available through County of San Mateo including staff report, model code 
language, and case studies 
• 
Prepares City for future requirements while incentives are available 
• 
Lower operating costs  
Policy Cons: 
• 
Limited reach as policy only covers municipal buildings  
Other Cities and Legislative Drivers 
Some other jurisdictions who have implemented this policy include: 
• 
City of Mountain View: Municipal Green Building Policy (2020): requires analysis of opportunities 
for electrification upon major upgrade of City-owned buildings, and develops a minimum of LEED 
Gold for new construction.19 
• 
City of San Diego: Zero Emissions Municipal Buildings and Operations Policy (2022): any time a 
natural gas burning system is being replaced, requires the new equipment to be electric.20 
Greenhouse Gas Reductions Potential  
In 2005 municipal natural gas use was 86,096 therms which equates to approximately 586 MT CO2e 
annually after accounting for natural gas leakage. If fully implemented, the electric first policy would 
aim to reduce these emissions over time as equipment needed replacement. The total reductions by 
2030 and 2045 would depend on the availability of all-electric replacements and the rate of 
equipment failure.  
Community Feedback  
Across community engagement events, there was broad support for this policy option. In the 
community survey, approximately 60% of respondents were supportive of this policy option, 15% 
neutral, and 24% do not support. Multiple constituents and building owners expressed a desire for the 
City to better understand the permitting process and technical implications, which would be achieved 
if the City led first by example. The Senior Advisory Commission, Sustainability and Infrastructure 
Commission, and Community Relations Commission were supportive of this policy pathway.

19 
Cost Effectiveness and Equity Considerations  
This policy enables the City to conduct a comprehensive feasibility assessment to evaluate the cost 
differentials between electrification and gas equipment replacement for municipal facilities. By 
incorporating exemptions for projects demonstrating technical or financial infeasibility, the policy 
ensures a targeted approach to identifying the most cost-effective energy upgrades, consistent with 
methodologies applied to commercial buildings. Implementation costs will vary based on building-
specific factors, including system age, infrastructure readiness, and operational requirements. 
As these facilities are City-owned, the municipality retains full decision-making authority over capital 
expenditures, eliminating potential equity concerns that might arise in privately owned housing or 
commercial properties. Furthermore, since many City owned facilities are used by the public such as 
libraries or park properties, creating healthier and more comfortable spaces while lowering operational 
costs provide an equity benefit to the community. This approach ensures alignment with both fiscal 
responsibility and the City’s broader decarbonization objectives.  
Policy Findings 
The adoption of a Municipal Electric First Policy establishes a process to systematically evaluate 
electrification opportunities during renovations and new projects for City-owned facilities. This policy 
aligns with the City’s climate goals while providing data-driven insights into cost and feasibility, 
enabling informed decision-making for future building decarbonization efforts. Furthermore, the 
municipal electrification first policy saw general support throughout the community engagement 
process.

July 28, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Amendment to Contribution Limits for City Candidate Campaigns – Ordinance Introduction and Public Financing Briefing
RECOMMENDATION:
Introduce an Ordinance to amend San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 2.80: "Contributions and Loans to City Candidate 
Campaigns” to adjust contribution limits for individuals to $1,000, thereby aligning with organizational contribution limits, 
link future contribution limits adjustments to the CPI, and enhance incentives for participation in the voluntary 
expenditure limits program, all in accordance with Council direction.  Receive an informational briefing on Public Financing 
of City Candidate Campaigns and provide direction on future action.
BACKGROUND:
In 2005, the City Council adopted Chapter 2.80 of the San Mateo Municipal Code, entitled “Contributions and Loans to City 
Candidate Campaigns.” This chapter originally established limits of $250 from individuals and $500 from organizations to 
City Council candidate campaigns, as well as a $15,000 cap on the amount a candidate could loan to their own campaign.
Subsequent amendments include:
•
2012: Section 2.80.070 was revised to state that any violation of this chapter constitutes a public offense.
•
2017: Contribution limits increased to $500 for individuals and $1,000 for organizations.
•
2021: “Contribution” revised to include goods and services received at no charge or at a discount
•
2024: Voluntary expenditure limit added
Recent Council Direction and Proposed Amendments:
On February 18, 2025, staff provided the City Council with a post-election review of candidate campaign activity from the 
November 5, 2024 general municipal election, pursuant to Section 2.80.080. Following this review, the Council directed 
staff to return with an ordinance proposing two changes:
1.
Aligning the contribution limits for individuals and organizations at $1,000.
2.
Future contribution limit adjustments to align with changes in the Consumer Price Index (CPI), rounded up to the 
nearest $10.
Voluntary Expenditure Limits:
In 2024, the City Clerk’s Office administered its first election under the newly implemented Voluntary Expenditure Limit 
(VEL) program. This program allows City Council candidates to voluntarily agree to a spending cap in exchange for specified 
benefits intended to encourage broader participation and promote transparency.
During the post-election review on February 18, 2025, the City Council noted that additional incentives may be necessary 
to increase candidate engagement with the program. In response, staff reviewed comparable programs in other 
901 of 983

--- page 902 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
jurisdictions, including the City of Redwood City’s VEL program.
Currently, candidates in San Mateo have the opportunity to publish a statement in the County’s Voter Information Guide 
and the Charter mandated newspaper publication, and the City publishes which candidates have agreed to the VEL on its 
website, in its regular digital newsletter, and in a newspaper of general circulation. The draft ordinance would include the 
additional benefit of allowing candidates who agree to the VEL to submit a 400-word written statement that would appear 
on the City’s website, exactly as submitted, and in accordance with guidelines to be provided by the City Clerk, along with 
Elections Code Section 13307(a)(1). This law includes certain technical requirements and requires a Spanish translation to 
those candidates who wish to have one.   
These changes largely mirror language in the City of Redwood City municipal code, which offers participating candidates 
the opportunity to publish a 400-word candidate statement and a photo on the City's website. Candidates who opt into 
their program are likewise prominently identified on the website, and their statements are published exactly as 
submitted—without editing by staff. If a statement exceeds the 400-word limit, it is returned to the candidate for revision 
and resubmission. Candidate statements must conform to the guidelines established by the City Clerk and comply with the 
content requirements set forth in the same Elections Code provision. Guidelines from the City Clerk could incorporate 
other provisions from the Redwood City code.  
Proposed Ordinance
The proposed ordinance (Attachment 1) includes amendments to San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 2.80 to reflect 
recent City Council direction for contribution limits, which would be increased from the current $500 to $1,000, thereby 
matching the contribution limit applicable to organizations. Future increases to the contribution limit would be adjusted 
according to the CPI, and rounded up to the nearest $10.  Staff has also included language providing for an additional 
candidate statement for candidates who opt to participate in the voluntary expenditure limits program, as noted above. If 
approved, the ordinance would take effect on January 1, 2026 in order to align with campaign reporting periods. It is 
scheduled to be considered on the consent calendar at the regular City Council meeting on August 18, 2025.
Public Financing of Candidate Campaigns: 
At the September 5, 2023 Study Session, City Council expressed general interest in further exploring a public financing 
model for City Council elections, however due to existing staff commitments at the time it was decided to pause further 
review. Subsequently, during the February 18, 2025 post-election review, City Council provided staff with further direction 
to return with a discussion on exploring public financing of election campaigns. Below is an overview of the legal 
framework, existing systems for participating in such programs and an approximate estimate of their cost for 
implementation in San Mateo.
Legal Framework:
Under current state law, public financing of candidate campaigns are prohibited for general law cities, counties, and 
special districts. However, charter cities like San Mateo may establish such programs under their “home rule” authority. A 
small number of charter cities—including San Francisco, Berkeley, Los Angeles, Oakland, and Long Beach—have adopted 
different public financing systems, most of which include expenditure limits for candidates who accept public funds, with 
the objective to reduce candidate reliance on private donations and increase participation in local elections. 
1. Matching Funds Model
Under this model, small contributions from individual donors are matched with public funds, typically at a fixed rate. San 
Francisco, Los Angeles, and Berkeley all provide a 6:1 match.
•
San Francisco: Candidates receive up to $1.2 million (mayoral) and $255,000 (supervisorial). In 2022, $1.16 million 
was disbursed, with a 46% participation rate. San Francisco’s Ethics Commission has a $4.9 million annual budget.
•
Los Angeles: Candidates must meet contribution thresholds and spending limits. In 2022, LA disbursed $12.8 
million, with a 65% participation rate.
•
Estimated Cost for San Mateo: Based on 2022 campaign activity, a 6:1 match would have cost approximately 
902 of 983

--- page 903 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
$127,000 in public funds (excluding administrative costs to operationalize the program).
2. Grant-Based Model
Candidates who meet eligibility criteria receive a lump-sum grant for campaign use.
•
Santa Fe, New Mexico: The City provides $60,000 for mayoral and $15,000 for council candidates. A total of 
$150,000 is budgeted annually. Participation in 2022 was 33%.
•
Estimated Cost for San Mateo: A grant model could require budgeting $100,000–$200,000 per cycle, depending on 
participation.
3. Reimbursement Model
Candidates are reimbursed for qualified campaign expenses.
•
Oakland’s Former Model: Provided reimbursements up to a capped amount. In 2022, $159,038 (90% of allocated 
funds) was disbursed, with a 63% participation rate.
•
Note: This program has been phased out in favor of a voucher model starting in 2024.
4. Voucher Program
Eligible residents receive vouchers (publicly funded) to donate to candidates.
•
Seattle’s Democracy Vouchers: Voters receive four $25 vouchers. In 2021, the program reached 7.6% of voting-age 
residents and cost $3 million annually (funded by property tax).
•
Estimated Cost for San Mateo: If 7.6% of voters (approx. 4,365 people) used $100 in vouchers, it would result in 
$436,500 in public funds, plus administrative costs—bringing the total to at least $500,000 per election cycle.
Table 1: Summary of California Cities Public Financing Models:
