Summary and Next Steps:
Public financing programs are designed to reduce the influence of money in local elections and encourage broader 
participation in the democratic process. However, these programs often require significant financial and administrative 
resources to implement effectively. In the City of Oakland, voters approved Measure W in 2022, which established a 
“democracy voucher” program intended to expand access to public financing, as seen in Table 1. Despite voter approval, 
the City has delayed implementation of the program due to budgetary constraints. In the interim, Oakland continues to 
offer a more limited public financing option.
Under Oakland’s Limited Public Financing (LPF) program, the amount available to candidates is determined by the total 
funding appropriated by the City Council and the number of participating candidates in the current election cycle. For the 
2024 election, approximately $177,000 was available for distribution among eligible City Council District candidates. 
Individual candidates could receive between $8,000 and $25,000, but no more than 30% of the voluntary expenditure 
ceiling for the office they are seeking.
To manage the distribution of funds, Oakland’s Public Ethics Commission oversees the program in two phases. In Phase I, 
eligible candidates opt in, and the Commission determines the initial disbursement amounts. Phase II involves a 
reallocation of remaining funds following a deadline for participants to submit required reimbursement documentation. 
Candidates who meet this deadline may receive increased distributions, depending on how many participants remain in 
the program.
Oakland’s experience illustrates both the potential benefits and practical challenges of implementing a public financing 
program. While the City has prioritized equitable access to campaign funding, limited resources have required a measured 
and adaptive approach. Notably, the administration of Oakland’s program is supported by a dedicated ethics agency with 
significant staffing and infrastructure.
In considering similar models, it is important to recognize that in San Mateo many of these responsibilities—such as 
verifying candidate eligibility, processing reimbursements, and tracking compliance—would fall to the City Clerk’s Office. 
Successful implementation would therefore require careful planning, clear procedures, and potentially additional 
administrative support to ensure the program is both effective and manageable within existing capacities. 
Direction Needed:
To guide staff’s next steps on public financing of candidate campaigns, Council is asked to provide direction on the 
following options regarding public financing of future election campaigns:
1.
Should staff actively pursue the development and implementation of a public financing program for future City 
elections, including evaluating feasibility, administrative requirements, and potential funding mechanisms?
2.
Should staff continue to monitor developments in Oakland and other jurisdictions to gather best practices and 
lessons learned, with the possibility of revisiting the topic at a later date?
904 of 983

--- page 905 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
3.
Should no further action be taken at this time?

There is no budgetary impact associated with this study session.

CITY OF SAN MATEO 
DRAFT ORDINANCE 
 
WHEREAS, on February 18, 2025, staff provided the City Council with a post-election review of 
candidate campaign activity from the November 5, 2024 general municipal election, pursuant to San Mateo 
Municipal Code Section 2.80.080; and 
WHEREAS, following this review, the Council directed staff to return with an ordinance to align 
contribution limits for individuals and organizations at $1,000, with future adjustments to follow changes in the 
Consumer Price Index (CPI), and to include additional incentives for participating in the voluntary expenditure 
limits program. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO ORDAINS AS FOLLOWS: 
Section 1. Section 2.80.030 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is amended as follows: 
2.80.030 LIMITATIONS ON CONTRIBUTIONS. 
(a) For any particular election, no individual or organization shall make, and no candidate, candidate 
committee treasurer or other person acting on behalf of a candidate committee shall solicit or accept from any 
individual or organization any contribution which will cause the total amount contributed by such individual to 
exceed $1000.00. 
(b) Beginning January 1, 2027, the City Clerk shall once biennially, on a calendar-year basis, increase the 
contribution limitation amount upon a finding that the cost of living in the immediate San Francisco Bay Area, as 
shown on the Consumer Price Index (CPI) for all items in the San Francisco Bay Area as published by the U.S. 
Department of Labor, Bureau of Statistics, has increased. The increase of the contribution limitation amount 
shall not exceed the CPI increase, using the index published in June 2025 as the index year. The adjustment shall 
be rounded up to the nearest $10.00. The City Clerk shall publish the contribution limitation amounts no later 
than February 1st of each year in which an increase occurs. 
(c) Any contribution or portion thereof that exceeds the limits in this section shall be returned to the 
contributor within 72 hours of receipt. 
(d) The limitations imposed by this section shall not apply to contribution of a candidate's personal 
funds to the candidate's own campaign, or to any loan which is personally guaranteed by the candidate or is 
secured by property owned by the candidate. 
Section 2. Subsection (c)(1) of Section 2.80.055 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is amended as follows: 
2.80.055 VOLUNTARY EXPENDITURE LIMIT. 
(c) Candidates who agree to abide by the voluntary campaign expenditure limit shall receive the 
following benefits and incentives at no cost to themselves: 
906 of 983

--- page 907 ---
(1) The City's website will prominently identify which candidates have agreed to the voluntary 
expenditure limit.  Candidates who agree to abide by the voluntary campaign expenditure limits may 
submit a written statement up to four hundred words.  The statement will appear exactly as submitted 
and shall conform to candidate statement guidelines provided by the City Clerk and Elections Code 
section 13307(a)(1), as amended. 
Section 3. Environmental Determination. In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) 
Guidelines, section 15378(b)(5), this action is not a project subject to CEQA because it will not result in direct or 
indirect physical changes in the environment. 
Section 4. Severability. In the event any section, clause or provision of this ordinance shall be determined 
invalid or unconstitutional, such section, clause or provision shall be deemed severable and all other sections or 
portions hereof shall remain in full force and effect. 
Section 5. Publication. This Ordinance shall be published in summary in a newspaper of general circulation, 
posted in the City Clerk's Office, and posted on the City's website, all in accord with Section 2.15 of the City 
Charter. 
Section 6. Legislative History and Effective Date. This ordinance was introduced on Clerk to complete., and 
adopted on Clerk to complete., and shall be effective on January 1, 2026.

Chapter 2.80 
CONTRIBUTIONS AND LOANS TO CITY CANDIDATE CAMPAIGNS 
2.80.010 
FINDINGS AND PURPOSE. 
(a) The City Council hereby finds that preserving integrity and openness in the political 
process is a matter of the highest public interest; that the people of the City of San Mateo can be 
better served through a more informed electorate; that the trust of the people is essential to 
representative government; and that regulation of certain campaign contributions and loans is 
necessary to promote public confidence in government and to protect the integrity of the electoral 
process. 
(b) The City Council further finds that regulation of campaign contributions is required 
because the costs of running political campaigns have reached levels that lead to a public 
perception that special interests and wealthy individuals may have undue influence on or access to 
elected officials. 
(c) It is the policy of the City to promote and encourage broad-based citizen involvement in 
the financing of election campaigns. 
(d) The City Council further finds that allowing candidates to lend unlimited amounts of 
their personal funds to their campaigns tends to undermine public confidence in the election 
process, because the public may perceive that candidates with large outstanding loans will seek 
contributors to repay their loan after the election, and may be predisposed to favor their post-
election contributors. 
(e) Protecting public confidence in the political process requires adequate enforcement of 
the provisions of this article. 
2.80.020 
Definitions. 
As used in this chapter, the words and phrases shall be defined in the same manner as they 
are defined in the Political Reform Act of 1974 (including any amendments thereto) as found in 
California Government Code Section 81000 et seq., unless defined otherwise in this section. For 
the purposes of this chapter, the terms below are defined as follows: 
(a) "Candidate" means any individual listed on the ballot for election to any City office, or 
who otherwise has taken affirmative action to seek election to City office, or who receives a 
contribution or makes an expenditure, or who gives his or her consent for any other person to 
receive a contribution or make an expenditure with the purpose of bringing about his or her election 
to any City office. 
(b) "Candidate committee" means a committee that is controlled directly or indirectly by a 
candidate or that acts jointly with a candidate in connection with the making of expenditures. 
908 of 983

--- page 909 ---
(c) "Contribution" means a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third 
party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent full and adequate 
consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made 
for political purposes. Except as provided herein, "contribution" includes (1) an expenditure made 
at the behest of a candidate or candidate committee unless full and adequate consideration is 
received for making the expenditure, and (2) any goods or services received by or behested by a 
candidate or candidate committee at no charge or at a discount from the fair market value, unless 
the discount is given in the regular course of business to members of the public. 
The term "contribution" does not include a payment made by the occupant of a home or 
business for costs related to any meeting or fund raising event held in the occupant's home or 
business; or volunteer personal or professional services if the services are donated without any 
understanding or agreement that they shall be directly or indirectly repaid to him or her. 
(d) "Individual" means a natural person and shall not include a partnership, corporation, 
association, firm, business entity, committee, club, or other organization. 
(e) "Organization" means a partnership, corporation, association, firm, business entity, 
committee, club, union, or company. 
2.80.030 
LIMITATIONS ON CONTRIBUTIONS. 
(a) For any particular election, no individual or organization shall make, and no candidate, 
candidate committee treasurer or other person acting on behalf of a candidate committee shall 
solicit or accept from any individual or organization any contribution which will cause the total 
amount contributed by such individual to exceed $51000.00. 
(b) Beginning January 1, 2027, the City Clerk shall once biennially, on a calendar-year basis, 
increase the contribution limitation amount upon a finding that the cost of living in the immediate 
San Francisco Bay Area, as shown on the Consumer Price Index (CPI) for all items in the San 
Francisco Bay Area as published by the U.S. Department of Labor, Bureau of Statistics, has 
increased. The increase of the contribution limitation amount shall not exceed the CPI increase, 
using the index published in June 2025 as the index year. The adjustment shall be rounded up to the 
nearest $10.00. The City Clerk shall publish the contribution limitation amounts no later than 
February 1st of each year in which an increase occurs. For any particular election, no organization 
shall make, and no candidate, candidate committee treasurer or other person acting on behalf of a 
candidate committee shall solicit or accept from any organization any contribution which will 
cause the total amount contributed by such organization to exceed $1,000.00. 
(c) Any contribution or portion thereof that exceeds the limits in this section shall be 
returned to the contributor within 72 hours of receipt. 
909 of 983

--- page 910 ---
(d) The limitations imposed by this section shall not apply to contribution of a candidate's 
personal funds to the candidate's own campaign, or to any loan which is personally guaranteed by 
the candidate or is secured by property owned by the candidate. 
2.80.040 
CANDIDATE LOANS. 
No candidate shall personally loan their campaign an amount the outstanding balance of 
which exceeds $15,000.00 at any given point in time. 
2.80.045 
Aggregation of Contributions. 
(a) To determine when contributions are aggregated under this chapter, "majority owned" 
means ownership of more than 50 percent. 
(b) If an individual directs or controls an organization's contributions, the organization's 
contributions shall be aggregated with contributions made by both of the following: 
(1) That individual. 
(2) Any other organization whose contributions that individual directs or controls. 
(c) If two or more organizations make contributions that are directed or controlled by a 
majority of the same persons, the contributions of those entities shall be aggregated. 
(d) Contributions made by organizations that are majority owned by a person shall be 
aggregated with the contributions of the majority owner and all other organizations majority owned 
by that person, unless those organizations act independently in their contribution-making 
decisions. 
(e) Aggregated contributions under this section shall be subject to the $1000 contribution 
limit. 
2.80.050 
CANDIDATE CAMPAIGNS ONLY. 
The provisions of this chapter apply to election campaigns for candidates only. The 
provisions of this chapter do not apply to any ballot measure, referendum or initiative election. 
2.80.055 
VOLUNTARY EXPENDITURE LIMIT. 
(a) Each candidate for election to the City Council in November 2024, and for each City 
Council election thereafter, shall, prior to the time they file their nomination papers with the city 
clerk, advise the city clerk in writing on a form provided by the City whether or not the candidate will 
opt to voluntarily limit their campaign expenditures in accordance with the voluntary campaign 
expenditure limits set forth in this section and by resolution. The agreement to voluntarily limit 
campaign expenditures shall pertain to all expenditures incurred by the candidate or the 
910 of 983

--- page 911 ---
candidate's committee in support of their candidacy and shall include all such expenditures that a 
candidate or candidate's committee is required to report pursuant to the California Political 
Reform Act of 1974, as amended, whether those expenditures are made before or after the filing of 
nomination papers. 
(b) Withdrawal Period: within three (3) business days after the deadline to file nomination 
papers with the city clerk, a candidate that previously accepted the voluntary campaign 
expenditure limit will have one (1) opportunity to notify the city clerk that they have decided not to 
accept the voluntary campaign expenditure limit. The candidate shall thereafter be relieved of 
abiding by the expenditure limit. 
(c) Candidates who agree to abide by the voluntary campaign expenditure limit shall 
receive the following benefits and incentives at no cost to themselves: 
(1) The City's website will clearly prominently identify which candidates have agreed 
to the voluntary expenditure limit.    Candidates who agree to abide by the voluntary 
campaign expenditure limits may submit a written statement up to four hundred words.  
The statement will appear exactly as submitted and shall conform to candidate statement 
guidelines provided by the City Clerk and Elections Code section 13307(a)(1), as amended. 
(2) The City will publish in its regular digital newsletter which candidates have 
agreed to the voluntary expenditure limit. This will be published twice (once at the next 
publishing of said newsletter after the withdrawal period and once when the ballots are 
mailed to residents.) 
(3) The City will publish notices in a newspaper of general circulation the 
candidates who agreed to the voluntary expenditure limit. 
(d) Calculation of Voluntary Campaign Expenditure Limit. 
(1) A candidate for district City Councilmember who voluntarily agrees to 
expenditure ceilings shall not make qualified expenditures exceeding two dollars and fifty 
cents ($2.50) per resident in the electoral district for each election in which the candidate is 
seeking elective office. Residency of each electoral district shall be determined by the 
latest decennial census population figures available for that district. The City Clerk shall 
publish on its website and in a newspaper of general circulation the expenditure ceiling 
amount for each district, rounded to the nearest hundred dollars ($100), no later than sixty 
(60) business days following adoption of the ordinance from which this Section is derived. 
(e) Beginning on January 1, 2026, the City Clerk shall in even-numbered years increase the 
expenditure ceiling amounts based upon a finding that the cost of living in the immediate San 
Francisco Bay Area, as shown on the CPI for all items in the San Francisco Bay Area as published 
by the U.S. Department of Labor, Bureau of Statistics, has increased. This increase shall be the 
911 of 983

--- page 912 ---
cost-of-living adjustment (COLA) computed by reference to the CPI as indexed from a base year 
that commences in November 2023 (by way of example, on January 1, 2026, the City Clerk will 
adjust the voluntary expenditure ceiling amounts by determining the increase in CPI from 
November 2023 — November 2025). The increase shall be rounded to the nearest hundred dollars 
($100). The City Clerk shall publish the adjusted expenditure ceiling amounts no later than 
February 1st of each even-numbered year. 
(1) The following shall not count toward the voluntary campaign expenditure limit: 
(A) Filing fees related to the candidacy, including fees paid to the Secretary 
of State to open or maintain a committee, and fees to translate the candidate 
statement. 
(B) Contributions returned by the candidate within thirty (30) days of receipt. 
(C) Legal fees and costs incurred in connection with litigation arising out of a 
campaign for City office. 
(D) Non-monetary contributions as defined by the Fair Political Practices 
Commission. 
(E) Post-election party expenses. 
(F) Post office box expenses. 
(f) Penalties: Any violation of this section, including the filing of false reports that entitle the 
candidate to the benefits conferred by this section, shall within seventy-two (72) hours of the City's 
knowledge of the violation: 
(1) Be posted on the City's website, and 
(2) Be posted in the City's regular digital newsletter; and 
(3) Be forwarded to newspapers of general circulation for publication. 
2.80.060 
INJUNCTION, OTHER COURT ACTION. 
In addition to all other remedies and penalties provided for violation of this chapter, the City 
Attorney may bring suit in a court of competent jurisdiction to seek an injunction or other 
appropriate relief, to halt any violation of this chapter. Such action may include seeking a temporary 
restraining order or temporary injunction and other appropriate temporary relief. Nothing in this 
chapter shall be deemed to restrict a suit for damages on behalf of the City or on behalf of any other 
person or entity. 
912 of 983

--- page 913 ---
2.80.070 
PENALTIES. 
Any person who violates the provisions of this chapter is guilty of a public offense. 
2.80.080 
Post Election Review. 
(a) No later than three (3) months after the certification of the results of a City election at 
which a City Council member is elected, the City Clerk shall calendar for City Council 
consideration a review of the provisions of this chapter. The purpose of such review is to allow the 
City Council to consider the ongoing appropriateness of the provisions of this chapter, including, 
but not limited to, the amount of the contribution and loan limits established in Sections 2.80.030 
and 2.80.040. 
(b) Notwithstanding any other provision of this code, the City Clerk's failure to calendar this 
review within the time period specified shall not subject the City Clerk to any criminal or civil 
penalty. 
2.80.090 
SEVERABILITY. 
Each section and provision of this chapter shall be considered severable and the invalidity 
of any section or provision of this chapter shall not affect the validity or enforceability of any other 
section or provision.

CORRECTION Greetings,: Campaign Financing, July 28 Council Regular Meeting Item #29

From:

To: San Mateo City Council <citycouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; clerk@cityofsanmateo.org

Subject: Campaign Financing, July 28 Council Regular Meeting Item #29
 
Honorable Mayor and Council Members,
 
I am writing regarding the July 28, 2025, Regular City Council Meeting, Agenda Item #29,
“Amendment to Contribution Limits for City Candidate Campaigns – Ordinance Introduction
and Public Financing”.
 
Ethics San Mateo has not, and most likely will not, take a position on this matter. At this time,
although campaigning financing most certainly can raise questions about ethics, I believe
taking a position could place our issue-agnostic pledge in question. Therefore, I am writing as
an individual, not on behalf of ESM.
 
The entire subject matter of how funds are raised to run a campaign is both practical and
political. No one can overlook the ever-increasing costs, and those costs can be well out of
proportion to the size of the electorate. We need smart, engaged, and selfless candidates to
select from. Many voters don’t do a deep dive into the backgrounds, qualifications, and to
seek out which candidate reflects their sentiments.
 
I believe far too often the candidates with the highest “bankroll” and thus, the ability to plaster
their name and image disproportionately, have a great advantage. While true that the more
popular a candidate is, the more contributions they may receive. It also places candidates in a
delicate position, in that high dollar donors may expect favorable treatment. This is when a
high level of ethical conduct must be fostered and maintained.
 
There are many solutions proposed at every level of government. All can be critiqued to have
both good and bad elements.
 
The public funding of campaigns with spending limits is one and is very attractive. It could
create an even playing field and no ethical challenges. However, in San Mateo there are
several aspects that would need to be resolved. The city would need to find sources of
914 of 983

--- page 915 ---
funding, and it is facing a budget deficit. Additionally, how would the funds be distributed,
proportionally by the number of voters in the district? Or, as city council members are elected
by district, they serve the entire city, split equally among all candidates? Should all candidates
receive funding? Who would administer and monitor it?
 
These are just some of the questions that need to be resolved, and it is JUST one potential
solution.
 
Therefore, I urge you to take this slowly and very carefully.
 
Regards,
 
Dave Cohen

Background on Campaign Finance Ordinance
2005: Chapter 2.80 adopted, setting contribution and loan limits
$250 from individuals; $500 from organizations
$15,000 self-loan cap for candidates
2012: Violations deemed a public offense (Sec. 2.80.070)
2017: Contribution limits increased
$500 individuals; $1,000 organizations
2021: Definition of “contribution” expanded to include in-kind support
2024: Voluntary expenditure limit introduced
2025: Council directed updates to align contribution limits at $1,000 for individuals 
and organizations and adjust for inflation

Voluntary Expenditure Limits (VEL)
5 of 11
• Self-imposed spending limits candidates choose to adhere to 
during electoral process 
• Aim: Equitable and transparent political landscape
• Mandatory expenditure limits are constitutionally 
impermissible, hence the interest from jurisdictions in 
voluntary limits
• 2024: First election administered under VEL
• 2025: Council discussed enhancing VEL incentives
• Proposed Change: Add 400-word candidate statement on City 
website.
• Modeled after Redwood City and aligned with Election Code Section 
13307 (a)(1)

Proposed Campaign Finance Ordinance – Key Updates
1. Increases contribution limits to $1,000 for both individuals and organizations
2. Future adjustments tied to CPI, rounded to the nearest $10
3. Adds optional 400-word candidate statement for VEL participants

Public Financing of Election Campaigns
7 of 11
Background: 
• September 2023: Council expressed interest in exploring public financing: review paused due to staff 
capacity
• February 2025: Council directed staff to revisit public financing discussion
Legal Context:
• Prohibited for general law cities, counties, and special districts
• Permitted for Charter Cities, like San Mateo under home rule authority
Examples: 
• San Francisco, Berkeley, Los Angeles, Long Beach
Common Objectives:
• Expenditure limits, reduced reliance on private donations, increased electoral participation
922 of 983

--- page 923 ---
Summary of Public Financing Models
8 of 11
Grant-Based Model:
• Lump-sum grant to eligible candidates
• Example: Santa Fe – $60K (Mayor), $15K (Council)
• Estimated Operational Costs for San Mateo: $100K–$200K per cycle
Reimbursement Model:
• Reimbursement for qualified campaign expenses
• Example: Oakland (pre-2024) – 63% participation; $159k disbursed
Voucher Program:
• Public funded vouchers distributed to voters
• Example: Seattle – 4 x $25 vouchers, $3M annual cost
• Estimated Operational Costs for San Mateo: $500k per cycle (administrative costs not included)
923 of 983

--- page 924 ---
Summary of Public Financing of Election Campaigns
9 of 11
• Analysis:
• Public Financing aims to reduce private influence and broaden public participation
• Parameters have been modified consistently towards this goal
• Implementation requires significant funding and administrative capacity
•  Oakland’s experience:
• Voter approved voucher program delayed due to budget constraints
• Interim Limited Public Financing (LPF) offers $8K-$25K per candidate
• Administered by a dedicated publicly funded Ethics Commission
• San Mateo context:
• City Clerk’s Office would absorb key responsibilities (eligibility verification, compliance, disbursement of funds)
• Participation rate varies across jurisdictions and model used
• Additional resources and planning would be required for success
• 2024 Election: City covered candidate statement fees in County Sample Ballot and Voter Information Guide
924 of 983

--- page 925 ---
1. Does the Council wish to proceed with introducing the draft ordinance as 
presented? 
2. Does the Council have any additional feedback or direction on the Public 
Financing research presented?
OPTION 1: staff to actively pursue the development and implementation of public financing program for future 
City elections, including evaluating feasibility, administrative requirements, and potential funding mechanisms
OPTION 2: staff continue monitoring developments in Oakland and other jurisdictions to gather best practices 
and lessons learned, with the possibility of revisiting the topic at a later date?
OPTION 3: No further action taken at this time.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  30
Section Name: NEW BUSINESS
Account Number: 34-3133
File ID: 25-10101
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
July 28, 2025 
SUBJECT:
Emergency Rental Assistance Program - Adoption and Agreement 
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to establish an Emergency Rental Assistance Program, appropriate $168,000 from the Low and Moderate 
Income Asset Fund for FY 2025-26, approve an agreement with Samaritan House to administer the program, and authorize 
the City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND: 
On April 21, 2025, the City Council held a Study Session on Tenant Protections and Anti-Displacement. This study session 
was held to support the implementation of the following policies contained in the City’s 2023-2031 Housing Element:
 

Policy H 3.4: Expand tenant protections in local ordinance to extend measures of AB 1482 related to relocation, 
documentation, and right to return policy in eviction cases. 

Policy H 3.6: Monitor rents, evictions, and tenant displacement citywide. 

Policy H 5.4.1: Establish tenant protections in local ordinance to extend measures of AB 1482 related to relocation, 
documentation, and right to return policy in eviction cases. 
 
A frequent topic that came up during community outreach and engagement, as well as at the study session, was that 
nonpayment of rent was a key factor for many of the evictions in and around San Mateo. Following public comments and 
discussion, the City Council directed staff to prioritize the establishment of an emergency rental assistance program as part 
of the City’s efforts to address tenant protection and displacement, and to meet Housing Element commitments.
 
Background Research 
Prior to the April 21 Study Session, staff met with legal service providers serving San Mateo clients to obtain data on eviction 
cases and general trends in caseloads over the last five years. These organizations provide services prior to formal eviction 
proceedings and legal aid in unlawful detainer (UD) cases. All reported a significant increase in caseloads. For example, 
Project Sentinel, a non-profit that provides services in fair housing, dispute resolution, and support to tenants facing eviction 
in San Mateo County, found a 24 percent increase in their caseload, from 346 cases in 2018 to 428 cases in 2023. Legal Aid 
Society of San Mateo County reported that their caseload on unlawful detainers went from 1,120 in 2019 to 1,520 in 2023, 
a 35 percent increase.  This trend can be attributed in part, but not entirely, to the termination of COVID-19 pandemic-
related rental assistance and eviction moratoria. The primary reason for most eviction cases in the County is related to 
nonpayment of rent. 
 
A study of court records of eviction filings in San Mateo County published in February 2025 by Mills Legal Clinic at Stanford 
Law School supports these observations. AB 1482 appeared to contribute to a significant drop in “no-cause” evictions, with 
927 of 983

--- page 928 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
most evictions due to alleged nonpayment of rent. San Mateo (city), with 141 filings, was one of the four San Mateo County 
jurisdictions with more than 100 unlawful detainers in 2019 and 2023 combined, along with Daly City, East Palo Alto, and 
Redwood City.  
 
Emergency Rental Assistance Programs 
Staff reviewed emergency rental assistance programs in nearby jurisdictions to gather more information on best practices 
and how program administrators were selected. Jurisdictions with such programs did not launch a Request for Proposals 
(RFP), but sole sourced eligible administrators who have the capacity to effectively administer an emergency rental 
assistance program. Many jurisdictions selected core service agencies who previously ran rental assistance programs during 
the COVID-19 pandemic and had previous service agreements. There are eight core service agencies within San Mateo 
County who work in collaboration with the County’s Human Service Agency to provide individuals and families with basic 
emergency and support services to stabilize their living situations. Core Service Agencies provide clients with crisis 
intervention and referrals based on an evaluation of their needs and qualifications for assistance. By partnering with a core 
service agency, San Mateo residents will have access to receive the services these agencies provide and can address other 
disparities that impact their housing stability. 
 
Cities with an Emergency Rental Assistance Program:

Samaritan House
 *Foster City’s Emergency Rental Assistance Program is only available to residents living in Below Market Rate Units
 
The City of San Mateo partnered with Samaritan House during the COVID-19 pandemic (2020-2022) to utilize $961,000 of 
Community Development Block Grant CARES Act (CDBG-CV) funds for an emergency rental assistance program. The 
successful administration of this program led to the recommendation of Samaritan House to be the administrator for the 
City’s new Emergency Rental Assistance Program. Samaritan House is part of the core service agencies in San Mateo County 
that provide safety net services to help stabilize households seeking food, clothing, and emergency housing assistance. Aside 
from the services Samaritan House provides, they are also the only core service agency in the City of San Mateo. Samaritan 
House administers an existing emergency rental assistance program that uses various funding sources to help with rent 
payments. The City’s Emergency Rental Assistance Program will supplement this existing program to expand services and 
rental assistance to more City of San Mateo residents.
 
Through Samaritan House, individuals who apply to the emergency rental assistance program can be connected to other 
resources that can help stabilize their household and finances. Samaritan House provides a variety of services such as food 
distribution, case management, shelter services, housing case management, and financial empowerment services. 
Samaritan House works in close collaboration with the County’s Human Services Agency to provide individuals and families 
with basic emergency and support services to stabilize their living situations. Core Service Agencies such as Samaritan House 
provide clients with crisis intervention and referrals based on an evaluation of their needs and qualifications for additional 
assistance. 
 
Proposed Program Description
Staff developed program guidelines to set expectations for the operation of this Emergency Rental Assistance (ERA) Program 
928 of 983

--- page 929 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
based on the City’s prior program, current programs in nearby jurisdictions and input from Samaritan House staff.  The 
program guidelines are included as Exhibit A in the Resolution (Attachment 1) and as follows:
 
Term
The ERA Program is proposed with a one-year term from July 1, 2025, to June 30, 2026, with the option to renew the service 
agreement for additional years, subject to City Council approval. Staff recommends that the initial term be for one year to 
function as a pilot for the program. If renewed, a two-year contract term may be considered.
 
Eligibility
Below are the requirements for potential applicants to be eligible for the ERA Program:

Gross Income meets the 60 percent or less of the County of San Mateo’s AMI of the Poverty Guidelines

Gross Income is determined as amount received within the previous 30 days/1 month

Verification of reason for financial assistance request (example: layoff notice, email from employer, etc.)

Other Income Verification (example: letters or statements from public assistance (CalWorks), child support, Social 
Security, unemployment, employment offer letter, bank statements for the last 2-3 months)

Proof of Address to establish residency (example: lease, utility bill, mail with client’s name received at the current 
address)

Verification of rental documents (example: Letter of Intent, Lease, Housing Voucher and/or Inspection Report with 
new address, etc.)

Proof of identification for each member of the household (example: picture of ID, birth certificate, etc.)
 
Staff received feedback that there can be barriers in meeting the documentation requirements; so Samaritan House will 
assist applicants, when needed, to support obtaining the necessary documentation.
 
Eligible Fund Uses
Assistance can be for “housing” (rent, security deposits) or both housing and utilities. If including utilities, verification of the 
utility bill must be submitted when applying. 
 
Budget – Maximum Amount Per Household
Samaritan House shared that they incorporate approximately 12 percent of the budget for personnel and overhead costs. 
To maximize the number of clients assisted, Staff determined that a budget of $168,000 would be an effective contribution 
to establish the ERA.

$150,000 for rental assistance

$18,000 for administrative expenses
 
With a $150,000 allocated for rental assistance, as directed by Council, and a maximum of $4,000 per household, at least 37 
clients will be able to receive assistance. The maximum number of times a client can receive assistance is once a year (fiscal 
year), or until they reach the $4,000 threshold.
 
Assistance Payments
Payments will be made by Samaritan House to landlords/property managers and/or utility companies. Under no 
circumstances should the client receive the payment.
 
Intake Form
The intake form (in English and Spanish) can include questions on the client’s demographics, income information, reason for 
seeking assistance and amount, and household information.
 
This is a great opportunity to add questions that can contribute to local data collection on displacement. Examples of 
questions can be if the client has ever been evicted/displaced in San Mateo, do they live in overcrowded or unsafe conditions, 
and if they would like more information on tenant protections.
 
929 of 983

--- page 930 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Program Outreach
Staff will conduct outreach on the program utilizing the Housing website, social media, eNews, printed materials at city 
facilities, targeted flyering, and email notifications as well as through partner organizations and agencies. Samaritan House 
will also conduct their own outreach and share their marketing materials with the City. Outreach will be completed in both 
English and Spanish.
 
Monitoring 
Samaritan House will submit a progress report to the City each quarter to highlight successes and challenges, and track how 
many San Mateo clients were served. Additionally, Requests for Reimbursement and Expense Summaries will be submitted 
each quarter to reimburse Samaritan House for their expenses related to the City’s ERA Program. Staff will review personnel 
documentation such as paystubs and time sheets. Samaritan House will also include copies of the checks issued with the 
intake form attached. 
 
All monitoring reports templates will be provided to Samaritan House by City staff as part of program initiation. Reports will 
be submitted to the Community Development Department via email no later than 14 days after the end of each quarter.
 
Program Administration
Samaritan House will be the administrator of the ERA program in fiscal year 2025-26. A Professional Service Agreement with 
Samaritan House (Attachment 2) was developed to outline the contractual requirements and the scope of work of the 
program. The term of this agreement will go through July 31, 2026. Since this year will function as a pilot, staff will bring this 
item back to Council during the first half of 2026 with a report on how the ERA Program did with meeting its objectives, and 
bring potential options for how the ERA Program could be funded and run on a longer-term basis.
 
Housing Element and TOC Policy Compliance 
This program supports Housing Element Goal H-3: Protection of Current Residents to Prevent Displacement.  A rising issue 
identified in the Housing Element is the displacement of low-income renters in San Mateo. The increasing rental rates, 
along with the slow progress in incomes, disproportionally impacts communities who spend more than 30 percent of their 
monthly income towards rent. And when they are displaced, households often must leave their community in San Mateo 
to seek more affordable housing elsewhere.  
 
To protect our existing communities, programs such as an emergency rental assistance program can prevent housing 
insecure households from having to leave the City entirely. With the intervention of a service operator, households can get 
more housing stability through the introduction of additional resources. Establishment of an emergency rental assistance 
program to prevent San Mateo residents from being displaced from their homes and be at-risk for homelessness supports 
implementation of Policy H 3.1: Prevent Homelessness.  
 
This program is also in compliance with the Metropolitan Transportation Commission’s (MTC) Transit-Oriented Communities 
(TOC) Policy. To meet TOC Policy requirements, jurisdictions need to establish local policies that address minimum 
residential and commercial office densities for new development; affordable housing production, preservation and 
protection, and stabilizing businesses to prevent displacement; parking management; and transit station access.
By establishing an emergency rental assistance program, the City will meet the TOC’s Affordable Housing Protection Policy 
#5: Rental Assistance Program. This Protection Policy requires jurisdictions to provide a dedicated funding source and 
program for rental assistance to low-income households. The proposed rental assistance program meets the requirements 
for TOC Policy Compliance:

Provide a total allocation of at least $300,000 over a four-year One Bay Area Grant (OBAG) cycle. At the time of 
publication of this report, the current OBAG cycle is in its third round covering the years of 2023-2026. If funded for 
four years, this program’s budget will exceed the $300,000 threshold with a total of $672,000. Since we are 
approaching the end of the third OBAG cycle, this program will continue to comply during the fourth OBAG cycle. 

The program will be funded through the Housing Successor Fund, a local funding source that allows homelessness 
programs such as rental assistance to be funded.

This policy requires the eligibility of applicants to meet the 80 percent of AMI or less threshold, to be considered 
930 of 983

--- page 931 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
low-income. This program will be eligible to residents who meet the 60 percent AMI or less threshold. 

Bilingual outreach for this program will include promotion via the City’s website and other communication channels 
as a method to make information available to City of San Mateo residents.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The Housing Successor Fund, also known as the Low and Moderate Income Asset Fund (Fund 255), is proposed as the funding 
source for the Emergency Rental Assistance Program. To fund this pilot Emergency Rental Assistance Program, staff 
recommends a budget of $168,000 for FY 2025-26 to launch this pilot program, allocating up to $150,000 for rental 
assistance and $18,000 for administrative costs. 
In addition to this program, $190,000 from Fund 255 was already allocated in the FY 2025-26 budget to support operations 
of the Police Department’s Homeless Outreach Team (HOT Team), which plays a critical role in connecting unsheltered 
individuals to housing and critical resources in San Mateo County. 
Fund 255 is a public fund used by California “successor housing agencies” to manage and utilize revenues left over from 
dissolved redevelopment agencies. The City of San Mateo receives revenues to this fund from loan repayments, lease 
payments and sales of property previously acquired with Housing Set Aside funds. These funds are regulated to be used in 
the future for housing assistance to households whose income does not exceed 80 percent of the median income, as well 
as a portion that is allowed for administrative purposes.   
Section 34176.1(a)(2)(A) of the California Health and Safety Code allows up to $500,000 of Housing Successor Funds per 
fiscal year, plus any percentage change in the cost of living, for homeless prevention and rapid rehousing services for 
individuals and families who are homeless or would be homeless but for this assistance, including the provision of short-
term or medium-term rental assistance, contributions toward the construction of local or regional homeless shelters, 
housing relocation and stabilization services including housing search, mediation, or outreach to property owners, credit 
repair, security or utility deposits, utility payments, rental assistance for a final month at a location, moving cost assistance, 
and case management, or other appropriate activities for homelessness prevention and rapid rehousing of persons who 
have become homeless. 
Together, the Emergency Rental Assistance Program and the HOT team funding represent a combined investment of 
$358,000 in FY 2025-26 in homelessness prevention efforts, which is within the $500,000 annual limit allowed under State 
law. Funding the two programs will expand services that help support individuals who are homeless or at-risk of 
homelessness.
Fund 255 has a current fund balance of $5,237,647 and received annual revenue of approximately $289,000 in FY 2024-25. 
If this proposal is approved, the fund balance is expected to be moderately drawn down by approximately $69,000 in FY 
2025-26.

RESOLUTION NO. ____ (2025) 
ESTABLISHMENT OF AN EMERGENCY RENTAL ASSISTANCE PROGRAM AND APPROVAL OF AN AGREEMENT 
WITH SAMARITAN HOUSE TO ADMINISTER THE PROGRAM 
WHEREAS, the City of San Mateo’s adopted 2023-2031 Housing Element is a plan to help address the 
region’s housing crisis, a blueprint for supporting all forms of housing, and a demonstration of the City’s 
commitment to achieving greater housing equity and access for all residents; and  
WHEREAS, the Housing Element includes Goal H-3 to “protect current residents to prevent 
displacement,” and Policy H 3.1 to “prevent homelessness;” and 
WHEREAS, the City Council held a study session on April 21, 2025 to discuss tenant protections and anti-
displacement issues to support implementation of Housing Element policies and programs; and 
WHEREAS, nonpayment of rent was identified as a major factor behind many of the evictions in and 
around San Mateo, contributing to increasing displacement rates; and  
WHEREAS, the City Council directed staff to prioritize the establishment of an emergency rental 
assistance program as part of the City’s efforts to address tenant protections and displacement, and meet 
Housing Element commitments; and  
WHEREAS, the Emergency Rental Assistance Program was developed based on the City’s prior 
emergency rental assistance program, existing programs in nearby jurisdictions and input from local service 
providers; and  
WHEREAS, the Emergency Rental Assistance Program was developed to follow the anti-displacement 
and tenant protection policies listed in the City’s 2023-2031 Housing Element and to meet the requirements in 
the Metropolitan Transportation Commission’s (MTC) Transit Oriented Communities (TOC) Policy; and  
WHEREAS, the Emergency Rental Assistance Program is an eligible activity for the City’s Housing 
Successor Funds/Low and Moderate Income Housing Fund (LMIHF), which will serve as the funding source for 
the Program; and  
WHEREAS, Samaritan House possesses the experience and capability to administer the City’s Emergency 
Rental Assistance Program, based on their role as a core services provider in the City and their previous 
experience managing a similar program from 2020-2022.   
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES 
that: 
1.
In accordance California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) Guidelines section 15378(b)(5), an
agreement award is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative
activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA
Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5)).
2.
An Emergency Rental Assistance (ERA) Program is approved and established pursuant to the
program criteria and guidelines contained in EXHIBIT A.

ERA Program for fiscal year 2025-26 is authorized.

Introduction 
On April 21, 2025, City Council directed Housing Staﬀ to develop an emergency rental assistance 
program to help San Mateo’s low-income residents from being displaced from their homes. Staﬀ 
presented to City Council the prevalence of displacement within the City of San Mateo, with 
nonpayment of rent being a key factor. As a proposal to reduce evictions and displacement, an 
emergency rental assistance program was mentioned. Multiple jurisdictions throughout San 
Mateo County (South San Francisco, Brisbane, Foster City, Redwood City) currently have 
emergency rental assistance programs, many of which started in response to the COVID-19 
pandemic. These programs have been successful in reducing displacement and keeping 
community members in their homes.  Thus, these jurisdictions continued funding these forms 
of assistance using various funding sources, including Housing Successor funds. 
Housing Element 
In connection with the City’s Housing Element, this program supports the Housing Element’s 
Goal to address the City’s housing needs through protection - Goal H-3: Protection of Current 
Residents to Prevent Displacement.  A rising issue identiﬁed in the Housing Element is the 
displacement of low-income renters in San Mateo. The increasing rental rates, along with the 
slow progress in incomes, disproportionally impacts communities who spend more than 30% of 
their monthly income towards rent. When experiencing eviction or displacement, households 
often have to leave their community in San Mateo to ﬁnd more aﬀordable housing.  
To protect our existing communities, programs such as an emergency rental assistance program 
can aid housing insecure households from having to leave the City entirely. With the 
intervention of a service operator, households can get more housing stability through the 
introduction of additional resources. Therefore, based on the data that shows nonpayment of 
rent as being a key driver of displacement, and to support implementation of Policy H 3.1: 
Prevent Homelessness, establishing an emergency rental assistance program will be an eﬀective 
tool to help reduce San Mateo residents from being displaced from their homes and at-risk for 
homelessness.  
Metropolitan Transportation Commission – Transit Oriented 
Communities (TOC) Grant 
This program is also in compliance with the Metropolitan Transportation Commission’s (MTC) 
Transit-Oriented Communities (TOC) Policy. To meet TOC Policy requirements, jurisdictions need 
to establish local policies that address minimum residential and commercial oﬃce densities for 
new development; aﬀordable housing production, preservation and protection, and stabilizing 
businesses to prevent displacement; parking management; and transit station access. By 
establishing an emergency rental assistance program, the City will meet the TOC’s Aﬀordable 
937 of 983

--- page 938 ---
3 
Housing Protection Policy #5: Rental Assistance Program. This Protection Policy requires 
jurisdictions to provide a dedicated funding source and program for rental assistance to low-
income households. The proposed rental assistance program meets the requirements for TOC 
Policy Compliance: 
•
Provide a total allocation of at least $300,000 over a four-year One Bay Area Grant
(OBAG) cycle. At the time of publication of this report, the current OBAG cycle is in its 
third round covering the years of 2023-2026. If funded for four years, this program’s 
budget will exceed the $300,000 threshold with a total of $672,000. Since we are 
approaching the end of the third OBAG cycle, this program will continue to comply 
during the fourth OBAG cycle.
•
The program will be funded through the Housing Successor Fund, a local funding 
source that allows homelessness programs such as rental assistance to be funded 
through.
•
This policy requires the eligibility of applicants to meet the 80 percent of AMI or less 
threshold, to be considered low-income. This program will be eligible to residents who 
meet the 60 percent AMI or less threshold.
•
Outreach for this program will include promotion via the City’s website and other 
communication channels as a method to make information available to City of San 
Mateo residents.
Funding 
The City will provide up to $168,000 from the Housing Successor Fund, also known as the Low 
and Moderate Income Housing Asset Fund (LMIHAF), to the emergency rental assistance 
program to support eligible City of San Mateo residents for FY 2025-26. Funding may be 
renewed annually upon City Council approval. The program administrator, Samaritan House, 
will provide assistance of up to $4,000 per household and per ﬁscal year for rental and utility 
payments to prevent tenants from being evicted and displaced from San Mateo.

used in the future for housing assistance to households whose income does not exceed 80% of 
the median income, as well as a portion that is allowed for administrative purposes.

$4,000 
25 individuals  
Samaritan 
House 
*Foster City’s Emergency Rental Assistance Program is only available to residents living in Below Market Rate
Units
It is understood that there are multiple factors that can prevent a tenant from making their 
monthly rent and/or utility payments such as employment loss and emergencies (i.e. medical), 
among other factors. These unforeseen circumstances can lead to a household getting evicted, 
and a resource such as an emergency rental assistance program can help prevent that.  
Samaritan House 
The City of San Mateo partnered with Samaritan House during the COVID-19 pandemic (2020-
2022) to utilize $961,000 of Community Development Block Grant CARES Act (CDBG-CV) funds 
for an emergency rental assistance program. The success of this program led to the 
recommendation of Samaritan House to be the operator for the City’s new emergency rental 
assistance program. Samaritan House is part of the core service agencies in San Mateo County 
that provide safety net services to help stabilize households seeking food, clothing, and 
emergency housing assistance. Aside from the services Samaritan House provides, they are also 
the only core service agency in the City of San Mateo. Samaritan House administers an existing 
emergency rental assistance program that uses various funding sources to help with rent 
payments. The City’s Emergency Rental Assistant Program will supplement this existing program 
to expand services and rental assistance to more City of San Mateo residents. 
Through Samaritan House, individuals who apply to the emergency rental assistance program 
can be connected to other resources that can help stabilize their household and ﬁnances. 
Samaritan House provides a variety of services such as food distribution, case management, 
shelter services, housing case management, and even ﬁnancial empowerment services. 
Samaritan House works in close collaboration with the County’s Human Services Agency to 
provide individuals and families with basic emergency and support services to stabilize their 
living situations. Core Service Agencies such as Samaritan House provide clients with crisis 
intervention and referrals based on an evaluation of their needs and qualiﬁcations for additional 
assistance.  Through Samaritan House, a household experiencing certain socio-economic 
challenges can be addressed and increase their housing and ﬁnancial stability.  
940 of 983

--- page 941 ---
6 
Purpose of These Guidelines 
The purpose of these guidelines is to deﬁne program rules and requirements, and to provide 
information to potential applicants as well as the community. Since guidelines can never 
address every type of administrative/accounting problem or situation that may arise during the 
contract period, the City’s Housing Division staﬀ is available to respond to requests for 
clariﬁcation or additional information. If new procedures or policies are implemented during the 
contract period, Housing staﬀ will provide timely notiﬁcation and technical assistance.  
Note that these Guidelines are neither intended to be all inclusive or so restrictive that 
they cannot be amended. Minor modiﬁcations to these Guidelines can be made with 
approval of the Community Development Department Director. Major modiﬁcations 
(such as those that would signiﬁcantly aﬀect funding levels, potential applicants or 
recipients) must be approved by the City Council.

Application 
Eligibility 
To be eligible to receive rental assistance, applicants must meet the following requirements: 
•
Live in the City of San Mateo
•
Household gross income at 60% AMI or lower of the San Mateo County Income
Guidelines
•
Not related to Landlord
•
Have a signed lease agreement or application to a rental unit
•
Utility bill must be under the applicant’s name
Income Guidelines 
60% AMI – San Mateo County Income Guidelines

Gross income is determined as the amount received within the previous 30 days/1 month. 
Household income is determined by all household members who are 18 years or older. For 
cases where a roommate needs ﬁnancial assistance, they are considered separately from the 
main household if they are not related to the main household.  
Income guidelines are updated on an annual basis; therefore, the most recent income 
guidelines will be included in the grant agreement if extended for an additional year.  
Amount and Type of Assistance 
The City will provide up to three months of rental and/or utilities assistance in an amount not to 
exceed $4,000 per household per ﬁscal year. Security deposits are also an eligible expense if the 
applicant got displaced. Applicants can seek ﬁnancial assistance multiple times until they hit the 
$4,000 limit. However, each time they return to request additional assistance, they must show 
proof of the latest critical need. 
942 of 983

--- page 943 ---
8 
Upon ﬁnalizing an application, Samaritan House will introduce the client to additional resources 
that can increase their housing stability to prevent the risk of displacement after the $4,000 
limit. In the case that an applicant has already reached their $4,000 limit, but demonstrates an 
urgent need to receive assistance, Samaritan House will contact the Community Development 
Department for approval.  
Payments will be made directly to the applicant’s landlord, property management ﬁrm, and/or 
utility company. Under no circumstances will payments be made directly to the applicant. 
Samaritan House will process all ﬁnancial assistance payments.  
Duplication of Beneﬁts 
Best practices for rental assistance are recommended for Samaritan House to check that the 
applicant is not receiving duplicative beneﬁts to cover rental costs. To ensure that there is no 
duplication of beneﬁts, applicants must inform Samaritan House about other ﬁnancial 
assistance or applications to other rental assistance programs. Receiving ﬁnancial aid does not 
disqualify the applicant from receiving emergency rental assistance but must be noted in their 
application. 
Samaritan House must also ensure that the applicant is not receiving rental assistance through 
another source of funding through their rental assistance program.

•
Complete intake form
•
Proof of rent due, including one of the following:
o
Eviction notice
o
Notice of Delinquent Rent
o
3-Day Pay or Quit
•
Proof of utility bill due
•
Proof of Identiﬁcation for each member of the household. One of the following:
o

o

o
Social Security
•

o

•
Rental/lease agreement
Application Process 
Applicants must complete an application, gather the required documentation noted above, and 
set an appointment with Samaritan House by calling (650) 347-3648 or visiting their oﬃce at 
4031 Paciﬁc Boulevard, San Mateo, CA 94403. During their appointment time, applicants will 
meet with a case manager to ensure their application is complete, and all required 
documentation is secured. Incomplete applications or missing documentation will result in a 
delay of services. Once the appointment is done, the case manager will advise the client of the 
application review process. Clients are contacted about the status of their application within 
one week of their appointment date. 
In the event that an application for rental assistance is denied, the applicant is eligible to submit 
an appeal to Samaritan House and the Community Development Department and the Housing 
Division. Applicant must express to Samaritan House their interest in submitting an appeal, and 
Samaritan House will provide the applicant with the Appeal Request forms and notify City staﬀ. 
Appeals will be responded within a week of receiving an appeal request. 
Language Access Plan 
To increase accessibility to our community members, intake forms will be available in English 
and Spanish. For other languages, translation and interpretation services can be available 
throughout the application process.

--- page 945 ---

Outreach 
To promote the availability of the program, City staﬀ will announce the implementation of the 
program through their Housing and Policy Updates newsletter and through the City’s website 
and social media channels. Information on the eligibility requirements and on how to apply will 
be added to the Housing and Neighborhood Services website. Samaritan House promotes its 
services to the community at large through a host of means. These include ﬂyers, social media, 
tabling events, presentations, and sharing information with other community-based 
organizations and governmental entities.

Grant Administration 
Reimbursements 
Reimbursements requests are to be prepared by Samaritan House containing invoices for 
eligible expenses. Reimbursement requests will be prepared on City-provided templates and are 
due quarterly via email. The following documentation is required to support each listed 
expense: 
Personnel/Administrative Costs: 
1. Timesheets (signed by staﬀ and supervisor)
2. Paycheck stubs
3. Fringe beneﬁt documentation
Rental Assistance: 
1. Copy of check made to landlord or property management company
2. Copy of applicant intake form
Leveraging Funds 
In the case that Samaritan House is in a budget deﬁcit for the emergency rental assistance 
program, City staﬀ must be notiﬁed. Samaritan House is expected to ﬁrst utilize the $168,000 
award for City of San Mateo residents prior to leveraging other funding sources. 
Performance Progress Reports 
Samaritan House will submit a progress report to the City each quarter to highlight any success 
or challenges and track how many San Mateo clients were served. City staﬀ will provide a 
template to Samaritan House to track their progress each quarter. Submissions will be accepted 
through email.  
Staﬀ will utilize the data shared in the performance progress report to prepare an annual 
progress report for the City Council and will be presented on an annual basis at a City Council 
meeting. 
Recordkeeping 
Recordkeeping and Record Retention requirements must be in compliance with 24 CFR 92.508. 
Records must be retained for ﬁve years after the period of rental assistance ends or from the 
time the project is closed, whichever is longer. Records 
shall include documentation of compliance with the requirements stated in these guidelines, 
including: 
•
Tenant eligibility
•
No duplication of beneﬁts
946 of 983

--- page 947 ---
12 
•
Lease requirements
Documentation will be available for review by City’s Housing staﬀ. Samaritan House is 
responsible for ensuring that all records are maintained for the appropriate period for all 
households receiving emergency rental assistance. 
Additional Program Information 
Additional requirements include but are not limited to: 
1. Samaritan House will develop and utilize a standard program application form. Each
household must complete the standard application form. If necessary, the City will aid in
preparing the form; especially to the elderly, handicapped, non-English speaking
persons, and persons who are unable to read and/or write.
2. All households deemed ineligible for assistance must be notiﬁed by Samaritan House in
writing (or via their preferred method of communication) of such determination and the
reasons for such determination. The household should also be notiﬁed of other
potential resources.
3. Samaritan House should document their eﬀorts to ensure clients are complying with the
requirements.
4. Conﬂict of Interest: Samaritan House must disclose all real, potential, or perceived
conﬂicts of interest to City as outlined in 2 CFR Part 200, as applicable, regarding the
receipt of, assistance provided with, or expenditure of City funds. All conﬂicts of interest
must be disclosed and resolved prior to providing emergency rental assistance to the
household.
5. Conﬂicts are applicable to both City staﬀ and Samaritan House staﬀ as well as their
immediate family members. This assistance would not be permitted when such conﬂict
existed.
a. Any exceptions to the Conﬂict of Interest policy can only be granted directly by
the City Manager, and no assistance will be issued without approval.
6. Samaritan House has access to technical assistance through the City’s Housing Division.
Compliance Monitoring 
The City is responsible for conducting monitoring reviews of the Emergency Rental Assistance 
program.  
1. The City will utilize a risk assessment tool to determine the relative risk among funded
entities and projects.
2. Samaritan House will receive on-site, desk, or remote monitoring reviews based on the
risk assessment.

SECTION 18 - MEDIATION 
 
 
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw. 
 
 
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

EXHIBIT A 
 
SCOPE OF SERVICES 
 
1. PROJECT SUMMARY: Provide emergency financial assistance in the form of rent, security deposit, 
and/or utility payments to individuals and families to stabilize their housing and prevent 
displacement. This will be accomplished by assessing client eligibility according to the City’s 
Emergency Rental Assistance Program guidelines. Upon approval, rent payments will be disbursed 
directly to vendors (i.e. landlords, owners, property managers, etc.).  Samaritan House will serve 37 
households in the City of San Mateo this program year (2025-2026). 
 
2. TIMELINE: Samaritan House’s Emergency Rental Assistance program is a continuing program. 
Therefore, we do not foresee any obstacles to implementing our project workplan as described, 
fully achieving our projected goals, and fully expending our contracted funds by the end of the 
fiscal year ending July 31, 2026.  
 
3. RECIPIENT ELIGIBILITY: Households who receive rental assistance shall be residents of the City of 
San Mateo and are at-risk of displacement. Their income shall not exceed the 2025 60% AMI of the 
San Mateo County Income Guidelines, as updated from time to time (Attachment 1). 
 
4. DETERMINATION OF ASSISTANCE AMOUNT: Samaritan House will use its standard procedures for 
determining the amount of rental assistance for each household. In no event shall the total 
assistance to a household exceed $4,000 per fiscal year. 
 
5. INCOME VERIFICATION: Each client who arrives at Samaritan House must be screened for income 
eligibility prior to receiving services. Each client has an intake session in which they fill out the 
intake questionnaire packet with an Intake Coordinator. The packet includes screening forms, 
which gather information on demographics, household information, income and expenses, history 
of program utilization, and other relevant information. Supporting documentation is required to 
support eligibility and verify income including pay stubs, bank statements, lease agreements, 
benefit letters and other documents as appropriate as stated in the program’s guidelines. Other 
verification (i.e. direct staff contact with landlord) may be required. This third-party documentation 
and/or client signatures on certification forms are always available on file for review. 
 
6. DUPLICATION OF BENEFITS: Samaritan House will check that the applicant is not receiving 
duplicative benefits to cover rental costs. To ensure that there is no duplication of benefits, 
applicants must inform Samaritan House about additional financial assistance or applications to 
other rental assistance programs. Receiving financial aid does not disqualify the applicant from 
receiving emergency rental assistance but must be noted in their application. Samaritan House will 
also ensure that the applicant is not receiving rental assistance through another source of funding 
through their existing rental assistance program.

EXHIBIT C 
 
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS 
 
 
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE  
 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as: 
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate limit 
applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or the 
general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit. 
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if 
ORGANIZATION has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit 
no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage. 
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury 
or disease.  
If the ORGANIZATION maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and 
shall be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the ORGANIZATION. 
Other Insurance Provisions 
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions: 
Additional Insured Status 
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the ORGANIZATION; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the ORGANIZATION including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the ORGANIZATION’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used). 
Primary Coverage 
For any claims related to this contract, the ORGANIZATION’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the ORGANIZATION’s insurance and shall not contribute with it. 
 
 
Notice of Cancellation 
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City.

Waiver of Subrogation 
ORGANIZATION hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said 
ORGANIZATION may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such 
insurance.  ORGANIZATION agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this 
waiver of subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a 
waiver of subrogation endorsement from the insurer.   
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions 
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the ORGANIZATION to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide 
proof of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses 
within the retention.   
Acceptability of Insurers 
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City. 
Verification of Coverage 
ORGANIZATION shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the ORGANIZATION’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

958 of 983

--- page 959 ---
From:
Thomas Morgan II
To:
City Council (San Mateo); Alex Khojikian
Subject:
Agenda item No. 30
Date:
Friday, July 25, 2025 7:46:28 AM
Dear Council,
I am writing to express my opposition to Agenda Item No. 30 in its current form. While I
understand the intent behind the proposal, I believe it introduces significant uncertainty
for housing providers. This uncertainty could ultimately increase the overall cost of
providing housing, rather than reduce it.
Additionally, while the policy aims to support tenants facing financial hardship, it may
unintentionally enable the transfer of problematic tenants from one landlord to another.
This could create further strain on housing providers and diminish the quality of housing
communities.
The proposal reminds me of an observation by Morgan Housel in one of his books, who
once explained why people buy lottery tickets despite the odds: “When you can’t afford
to put groceries in the refrigerator, buying a lottery ticket feels like a small risk for a
potentially life-changing reward. If you win, your situation improves dramatically. If you
lose, you’re no worse off.” Similarly, this policy may incentivize tenants not to pay rent at
all believing that partial payment offers no benefit and full payment is out of reach.
To improve the proposal, I recommend requiring tenants who access these funds to
waive relocation benefits and/or other non-essential tenant rights . In addition, I would
also recommend a faster pathway for eviction in the event the tenant is legitimately a
bad tenant trying to game the system. This would help ensure that the support is used
responsibly and does not create further imbalance between tenant protections and
landlord rights.
Thank you for your time and consideration.
Sincerely,
Thomas Morgan

City Council Direction – April 21, 2025
• Prioritize the establishment of an emergency rental assistance program and 
amending the City’s Tenant Relocation Ordinance to protect tenants from 
unsafe living conditions.
• $150,000 for assistance plus administrative costs.
• Extend AB 1482 provisions: relocation assistance for no-fault evictions for 
tenants with a tenure of less than one year (11 months) and the right-to-
return to substantially rehabilitated or demolished units.

how to build on existing City data collection to monitor rental rates and 
evictions citywide.
•  Emphasis on tenant and landlord education on current policies, such as AB 
1482

•
Gap between incomes and rents is a known driver of displacement
•
Renters constitute ~ 50%* of San Mateo residents, and ~ 49%* of renters are in 
lower-income category (50% AMI - 80% AMI)
•
Rents in older (and often smaller) buildings significantly lower than “market”
•
Ownership type varies significantly across housing type (individual vs. entity)
•
Exact number of units not known:
•
1,110 rental properties (excl. 
single-family homes) on CoStar 
vs. 1,028 registered rental 
properties in City records
•
Significant older/smaller housing 
stock
*2023 5-yr American Community Survey/HUD Comp. Housing Afford. Survey 2019
977 of 983

--- page 978 ---
Evictions Data – Overall Trends
• Providers reported increase in caseloads after COVID-19 pandemic
• Termination of rental assistance and eviction moratoria contributing factors
• AB 1482 associated with decrease in “no-cause” eviction rates 
• Non-payment of rent is the main cause for evictions
• Rents and areamedian income (AMI) are increasing, but many lower-income tenants’ 
incomes static
• Does not cover “soft” or “functional” evictions. Full extent of displacement is 
unknown.
• Data from legal aid providers is only a portion of the total number of eviction 
cases in San Mateo County.
978 of 983

--- page 979 ---
Mills Legal Clinic – Stanford Law School: Evictions 
in San Mateo County 2019/2023
• Analysis to identify underlying basis for unlawful detainers in San Mateo County 
by reviewing eviction cases in San Mateo County through a court order
• Compared cases from 2019 and 2023 (pre- and post- COVID-19)
Findings:
• San Mateo had 141 unlawful detainer filings in 2019 and 2023 combined
• One of the four jurisdictions (Daly City, East Palo Alto, and Redwood City) with more 
than 100 unlawful detainer cases
• Most unlawful detainer cases are due to non-payment of rent 
• AB 1482 has shown apparent success in reducing “no-cause” evictions
• Disparity between tenant and landlord representation
979 of 983

--- page 980 ---
Public Feedback:
• Concern about displacement 
• Concerns about the cost of living and 
overcrowding
• Support for more protections against unsafe 
living conditions, such as mold or pests
• Support for improved tenant and landlord 
education
• Support for City rental assistance program
• Support for, and opposition to, a rental 
registry
• Concerns about risk to mom-and-pop 
landlords from expanded tenant protections 
beyond AB 1482

Public Feedback:
• Support for anti-harassment and rental registry policies
• Insurance and maintenance expenses on rental 
properties are increasing
• Consistency of relocation assistance amount across City 
Code provisions
• Fairness of relocation assistance amount in substantial 
rehab scenario
• Limiting no-fault evictions during the school year
• Support for more education on current policies and 
resources
• Additional restrictions/policies on landlords can add 
administrative burden
• Support for just-cause protections, regardless of tenure
• Support for rent registries to support policymaking
Community Workshop #2

981 of 983

--- page 982 ---
Focus Group Meetings 
• Focus groups in December 2024 and January 2025 with tenant/property owner groups, nonprofit 
service providers, developers, and general public. Feedback included:
• Rent increases as driver of displacement
• Outreach and education on existing laws, especially AB 1482
• Impact of escalating external costs on rents (e.g., utilities)
• Relationship between tenant protections and new housing production/maintenance of existing 
units
• “Functional evictions” (lack of reasonable accommodations, substantial rehab, etc.)
• Relocation assistance and right to return after substantial rehab (incl. amount of assistance; rent 
upon return to unit; means-testing of recipient household)
• Rent registries
• Language barriers in outreach and policy-making
• Negative effects of eviction, especially on school-age children
• Rental assistance and mediation as anti-displacement tools
